#### **E. The TEST Instruction**

**Operation:** Performs `AND` but **discards the result** — only **flags** are updated.

**General Form:** `TEST destination, source`

**Logic Steps:**
- **Why?** To **test a condition without destroying the operand** — perfect for conditional jumps like `JNZ`.
- **How?** Identical hardware to `AND`; only the write-back to destination is suppressed.

#### **F. Shift Instructions**

| Mnemonic | Full Name | Empty Bit Fill | Bit Lost → |
|:--------:|:---------:|:--------------:|:----------:|
| `SHL` / `SAL` | Shift Left / Shift Arithmetic Left | Always `0` | `CF` |
| `SHR` | Shift Right Logical | Always `0` | `CF` |
| `SAR` | Shift Right Arithmetic | **Sign bit** (preserves sign) | `CF` |

**Logic Steps:**
- `SHL` and `SAL` are **functionally identical** for 8086 (the assembler distinguishes them only for documentation).
- **Multi-bit shifts** by a constant use an immediate count `1`; shifts by `CL` use the count in `CL` register.
- **`SAR` vs `SHR` distinction:** This is a **favourite KTU question**. `SHR` always fills with `0` (treats as unsigned); `SAR` replicates the **sign bit** (treats as signed, performs arithmetic division by powers of 2).

#### **G. Rotate Instructions**

| Mnemonic | Full Name | Vacated Bit Fill |
|:--------:|:---------:|:----------------:|
| `ROL` | Rotate Left | MSB → LSB |
| `ROR` | Rotate Right | LSB → MSB |
| `RCL` | Rotate Through Carry Left | MSB → CF, CF → LSB |
| `RCR` | Rotate Through Carry Right | LSB → CF, CF → MSB |

**Logic Steps:**
- Rotations **preserve all bits** — useful for **endian-swap routines** and **CRC computation** in embedded communication protocols.

#### **H. The CMP Instruction**

**Operation:** Computes `destination - source` and updates flags, but **discards the result**.

**General Form:** `CMP destination, source`

**Logic Steps:**
- **Why?** Always followed immediately by a conditional jump (`JE`, `JNE`, `JG`, `JL`, etc.).
- **How?** Internally uses the subtractor; therefore CF, OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF are all updated exactly as for `SUB`.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Logical Instructions

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — it covers ~70% of all KTU logical-instruction questions.**

| Instruction | Syntax | Operands | Operation | CF | OF | SF, ZF, PF | AF | Typical Use |
|:-----------:|:------:|:--------:|:---------:|:--:|:--:|:----------:|:--:|:-----------:|
| `AND` | `AND dst, src` | Reg, Imm / Reg, Reg / Mem, Imm / Mem, Reg | dst ← dst AND src | **0** | **0** | result | undef | Bit masking |
| `OR` | `OR dst, src` | Same as AND | dst ← dst OR src | **0** | **0** | result | undef | Bit setting |
| `XOR` | `XOR dst, src` | Same as AND | dst ← dst XOR src | **0** | **0** | result | undef | Bit toggle, clear reg |
| `NOT` | `NOT dst` | Reg / Mem | dst ← complement(dst) | unchanged | unchanged | unchanged | unchanged | One's complement |
| `TEST` | `TEST dst, src` | Same as AND | flags ← dst AND src | **0** | **0** | result | undef | Non-destructive test |
| `SHL/SAL` | `SHL dst, cnt` | Reg/Mem, 1 / Reg/Mem, CL | Shift left, fill `0` | last bit out | 1 (multi-bit: undef) | result | undef | Multiply unsigned by 2 |
| `SHR` | `SHR dst, cnt` | Same | Shift right, fill `0` | last bit out | 1 (multi-bit: undef) | result | undef | Divide unsigned by 2 |
| `SAR` | `SAR dst, cnt` | Same | Shift right, fill sign bit | last bit out | 1 (multi-bit: undef) | result | undef | Divide signed by 2 |
| `ROL` | `ROL dst, cnt` | Same | Rotate left within operand | last bit out (=MSB) | 1 (multi-bit: undef) | result | undef | Bit rotation |
| `ROR` | `ROR dst, cnt` | Same | Rotate right within operand | last bit out (=LSB) | 1 (multi-bit: undef) | result | undef | Bit rotation |
| `RCL` | `RCL dst, cnt` | Same | 17-bit rotate through CF | MSB | 1 (multi-bit: undef) | result | undef | 17-bit rotation |
| `RCR` | `RCR dst, cnt` | Same | 17-bit rotate through CF | LSB | 1 (multi-bit: undef) | result | undef | 17-bit rotation |
| `CMP` | `CMP dst, src` | Same as SUB | dst − src (discard) | borrow | signed overflow | result | result | Conditional jump setup |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded control:** Configuring microcontroller peripherals (e.g., setting bit 7 of a GPIO direction register to make a pin output uses `OR`).
- **Communication protocols:** UART bit-stuffing, SPI clock polarity (CPOL/CPHA bits in CR1 register of STM32) — these are accessed via `AND`/`OR` bit-masking.
- **DSP / Signal Processing:** `SAR` is the basis of fast division-by-power-of-2 in fixed-point DSP filters.
- **Cryptography:** `XOR` is the fundamental operator in stream ciphers (e.g., Vernam cipher).
- **Operating Systems:** Setting/clearing/toggling individual flag bits in a process control block.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Worked Examples (KTU Exam Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Methodology:** Every problem below follows the official KTU valuation key steps: **(1) Write the instruction, (2) Show the binary representation of operands, (3) Apply the bit-wise operation column by column, (4) Determine the result, (5) Explicitly state the flag map.**

---

#### **Example 1: AND Instruction with Flag Analysis**

**Problem:** Execute `AND AX, 0F0FH` when `AX = 3C5AH`. Show the result and all flags.

**Step 1 — Convert operands to binary (16-bit):**

$$\begin{aligned}
AX &= 3C5A_{H} = 0011\,1100\,0101\,1010_{B} \\
\text{Mask} &= 0F0F_{H} = 0000\,1111\,0000\,1111_{B}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply bit-wise AND column by column:**

$$\begin{aligned}
&0011\,1100\,0101\,1010 \\
\text{AND}\quad &0000\,1111\,0000\,1111 \\
\hline
&0000\,1100\,0000\,1010
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert result back to hex:**

$$\text{Result} = 0C0A_{H}$$

**Step 4 — Flag Calculation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CF} &= 0 \quad \text{(AND always clears CF)} \\
\text{OF} &= 0 \quad \text{(AND always clears OF)} \\
\text{ZF} &= 0 \quad \text{(result } \neq 0\text{)} \\
\text{SF} &= 0 \quad \text{(MSB of result is 0)} \\
\text{PF} &= 1 \quad \text{(count of 1's in low byte } 0A_{H} = 0000\,1010\text{ has two 1's — even parity)} \\
\text{AF} &= \text{undefined}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** `AX = 0C0AH`, flags = `CF=0, OF=0, ZF=0, SF=0, PF=1, AF=?`

---

#### **Example 2: XOR Instruction — Register Clearing Trick**

**Problem:** Show that `XOR BX, BX` effectively clears `BX`. Assume `BX = 9A4CH` initially.

**Step 1 — Apply XOR (same operand):**

$$\begin{aligned}
&1001\,1010\,0100\,1100 \\
\text{XOR}\quad &1001\,1010\,0100\,1100 \\
\hline
&0000\,0000\,0000\,0000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Since `X ⊕ X = 0` for every bit, the result is identically zero.**

$$\text{Result} = 0000_{H}$$

**Step 3 — Flags:** `CF=0, OF=0, ZF=1, SF=0, PF=1` (zero is even-parity)

**Engineering Insight:** This 3-byte instruction (`33 DB` in machine code) is shorter and faster than `MOV BX, 0` (4 bytes: `BB 00 00`). Compiler optimizers universally use this trick.

---

#### **Example 3: SHR vs SAR — Signed vs Unsigned Division**

**Problem:** Execute both `SHR AL, 1` and `SAR AL, 1` on `AL = 1001 0100` (= `-108` in signed 8-bit, `= 148` unsigned). Compare results.

**Step 1 — SHR AL, 1 (Logical, fills with 0):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Before:} \quad &1\,0010\,100\quad \text{(MSB in carry)} \\
\text{After:} \quad &0\,1001\,010_{B} = 4A_{H} = 74_{10}
\end{aligned}$$

The result `74` = `148 / 2`, correctly performing **unsigned** division by 2.

**Step 2 — SAR AL, 1 (Arithmetic, fills with sign bit):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Before:} \quad &1\,0010\,100 \\
\text{After:} \quad &1\,1001\,010_{B} = CA_{H} = -54_{10} \quad \text{(in 8-bit signed)}
\end{aligned}$$

The result `-54` = `-108 / 2`, correctly performing **signed** division by 2.

**Step 3 — Compare:**
- `SHR` would give `0100 1010 = +74` — **wrong** for signed division because the sign bit flipped.
- `SAR` preserves the sign by replicating the MSB.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students frequently assume `SHR` and `SAR` are identical. In 8086, they differ **only for right shifts of negative numbers**. KTU regularly tests this with a question like: *"What is the result of `SAR AL, 1` when `AL = 80H`?"* — the answer is `C0H`, **not** `40H`.

---

#### **Example 4: TEST Instruction for Bit Testing**

**Problem:** Write an 8086 instruction sequence to test whether bit 3 of register `BL` is set, without altering `BL`.

**Solution:**
```
TEST BL, 08H      ; 08H = 0000 1000B isolates bit 3
JNZ  BIT_IS_SET   ; jump if ZF=0 (bit was 1)
```

**Flag Mechanism:** `TEST BL, 08H` performs the AND and sets `ZF=1` if bit 3 was `0`, `ZF=0` if bit 3 was `1`. The value of `BL` is **not modified**.

---

#### **Example 5: Multi-bit Shift through Carry (RCR)**

**Problem:** Execute `RCR AX, 1` when `AX = 8001H` and `CF = 0`. Show the full 17-bit rotation.

**Step 1 — Form the 17-bit circular buffer:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CF} + \text{AX} = 0 \mid 1000\,0000\,0000\,0001
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Rotate right by 1 (LSB moves to CF position; CF moves to MSB):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{New CF} &= \text{old LSB} = 1 \\
\text{New AX[15]} &= \text{old CF} = 0 \\
\text{Rotated buffer} &= 1 \mid 0100\,0000\,0000\,0000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Final state:**

$$AX = 4000H, \quad CF = 1$$

This 17-bit rotation pattern is fundamental in **multi-word shift routines** used in 32-bit/64-bit arithmetic on an 8086.

---

#### **Example 6: CMP Instruction Analysis**

**Problem:** Execute `CMP AX, BX` when `AX = 1234H`, `BX = 1234H`. State which conditional jumps will be taken.

**Step 1 — Internal subtraction:** `1234H - 1234H = 0`

**Step 2 — Flags:** `ZF=1, CF=0, OF=0, SF=0`

**Step 3 — Conditional jumps triggered:**
- `JE` / `JZ` → **taken** (equal)
- `JNE` / `JNZ` → not taken
- `JA` / `JG` → not taken (only valid for signed compare when SF=OF AND ZF=0, but here ZF=1)

> [!NOTE]
> **`AX` is unchanged** after `CMP`. This is why KTU considers `CMP` part of the "logical/test" family in Module 1 questions.

---

### 3.2 Quick Reference — Bit Mask Cheat Patterns

| Goal | Operation | Mask |
|:----:|:---------:|:----:|
| Set bit *n* | `OR reg, (1 << n)` | 1 at position n |
| Clear bit *n* | `AND reg, ~(1 << n)` | 0 at position n, all others 1 |
| Toggle bit *n* | `XOR reg, (1 << n)` | 1 at position n |
| Test bit *n* | `TEST reg, (1 << n)` | 1 at position n |
| Multiply by 2 | `SHL reg, 1` | — |
| Divide unsigned by 2 | `SHR reg, 1` | — |
| Divide signed by 2 | `SAR reg, 1` | — |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification of 8086 Logical Instructions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Logical Instructions in 8086]:::root --> B[Boolean Logic]:::grp
    A --> C[Test Operation]:::grp
    A --> D[Shift Operations]:::grp
    A --> E[Rotate Operations]:::grp
    A --> F[Comparison]:::grp

    B --> B1[AND]
    B --> B2[OR]
    B --> B3[XOR]
    B --> B4[NOT]

    C --> C1[TEST]

    D --> D1[SHL or SAL]
    D --> D2[SHR]
    D --> D3[SAR]

    E --> E1[ROL]
    E --> E2[ROR]
    E --> E3[RCL]
    E --> E4[RCR]

    F --> F1[CMP]

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2c4a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef grp fill:#d9e8f5,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#0b2c4a
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of 8086 ALU (Logical Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REGFILE[General Registers\nAX BX CX DX]:::reg --> MUX1[Operand Select MUX]:::mux
    IMM[Immediate or Memory Operand]:::mem --> MUX1
    MUX1 --> ALU[16-bit ALU\nLogical Unit]:::alu
    ALU --> FLAG[Flags Register\nCF ZF SF OF PF AF]:::flag
    ALU --> RESULT[Result Bus]:::res
    RESULT --> REGFILE

    ALU -.AND gate array.-> ANDX[AND Logic]
    ALU -.OR gate array.-> ORX[OR Logic]
    ALU -.XOR gate array.-> XORX[XOR Logic]
    ALU -.Inverter array.-> NOTX[NOT Logic]
    ALU -.Barrel shifter.-> SHIFT[Shift / Rotate Logic]

    classDef reg fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
    classDef mux fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235
    classDef mem fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c65911
    classDef alu fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2c4a,color:#ffffff
    classDef flag fill:#d9e8f5,stroke:#1f4e79
    classDef res fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235
```

### 4.3 Bit-Wise Operation Flow (Truth Table Style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Bit A from Destination] --> GATE{Logical\nGate}:::gate
    B[Bit B from Source] --> GATE
    GATE --> Y[Result Bit Y]:::out

    GATE -.selection.-> SEL[Decoder driven by opcode]:::sel

    classDef gate fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2c4a,color:#ffffff
    classDef out fill:#d9e8f5,stroke:#1f4e79
    classDef sel fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
```

### 4.4 Shift vs Rotate — Conceptual Distinction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[8-bit Operand]:::reg --> Q{Operation?}:::dec
    Q -->|SHL or SAL| SHL[MSB goes to CF\nLSB filled with 0\nBits shift left]:::shr
    Q -->|SHR| SHR[LSB goes to CF\nMSB filled with 0\nBits shift right]:::shr
    Q -->|SAR| SAR[LSB goes to CF\nMSB filled with sign bit]:::sar
    Q -->|ROL| ROL[MSB wraps to LSB\nBits rotate within operand]:::rot
    Q -->|ROR| ROR[LSB wraps to MSB\nBits rotate within operand]:::rot
    Q -->|RCL| RCL[MSB to CF, CF to LSB\n17-bit rotation]:::rot
    Q -->|RCR| RCR[LSB to CF, CF to MSB\n17-bit rotation]:::rot

    SHL --> OUT[Result in Operand and Flags]:::out
    SHR --> OUT
    SAR --> OUT
    ROL --> OUT
    ROR --> OUT
    RCL --> OUT
    RCR --> OUT

    classDef reg fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
    classDef dec fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2c4a,color:#ffffff
    classDef shr fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c65911
    classDef sar fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235
    classDef rot fill:#d9e8f5,stroke:#1f4e79
    classDef out fill:#d9e8f5,stroke:#1f4e79
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Explain the difference between the `SHL` and `SAR` instructions in 8086 with a suitable example. How do they affect the Carry Flag?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Both `SHL` (Shift Left) and `SAR` (Shift Arithmetic Right) are bit-shifting instructions in 8086, but they differ in direction and bit-fill behaviour.

| Feature | `SHL` | `SAR` |
|:-------:|:-----:|:-----:|
| Direction | Left | Right |
| Vacated bit fill | Always `0` | Sign bit (MSB replicated) |
| Effect on signed number | Treats as multiplication by 2 | Performs signed division by 2 |
| Effect on unsigned | Works correctly for unsigned left shift | Not intended for unsigned |

**Example:** If `AL = 80H` (= `-128` signed, `= 128` unsigned):
- `SHL AL, 1` → `AL = 00H` (`CF=1`, the `1` bit shifts into carry; result overflows)
- `SAR AL, 1` → `AL = C0H` (= `-64` in signed 8-bit; correct arithmetic right shift)

**Carry Flag:** In both, the **last bit shifted out** goes into `CF`. For `SHL`, the MSB shifted out becomes the new `CF`. For `SAR`, the LSB shifted out becomes the new `CF`.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is the purpose of the `TEST` instruction in 8086? How is it different from the `AND` instruction?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `TEST` instruction performs a **bit-wise logical AND** between its two operands but **discards the result** — it updates **only the Flags register**, leaving both operands unchanged. It is the **non-destructive** counterpart of `AND`.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | `AND` | `TEST` |
|:------:|:-----:|:------:|
| Result stored in destination | **Yes** | **No** |
| Destination operand modified | **Yes** | **No** |
| Flags updated | Yes (`CF=0`, `OF=0`, plus SF/ZF/PF) | Yes (same flag pattern) |
| Use case | Masking and extracting bits | Testing a condition without altering data |

**Typical use:**
```
TEST AL, 01H    ; check whether AL is odd (LSB = 1)
JNZ  ODD_NUMBER
```
This is the most memory-efficient way to test a single bit without modifying the operand — a fundamental idiom in embedded firmware.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1 [KTU ESE – 2024 Scheme Model]**

**(a)** Explain the following 8086 logical instructions with one example each: `AND`, `OR`, `XOR`, `NOT`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Execute `AND BX, 00FFH` when `BX = A5C3H`. Show the binary operation, the result, and the status of all flags (CF, OF, SF, ZF, PF, AF). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**`AND destination, source`** — Performs bit-wise logical AND. Result is `1` only if **both** bits are `1`. The result is stored in the destination operand. It is used primarily for **bit masking** (extracting specific bits by zeroing out the others). Flags affected: `CF=0`, `OF=0`, `SF`, `ZF`, `PF` are set according to the result; `AF` is undefined.

*Example:* `AND AL, 0FH` keeps only the lower nibble of `AL` and clears the upper nibble.

**`OR destination, source`** — Performs bit-wise logical OR. Result is `1` if **at least one** bit is `1`. Used for **setting specific bits** to 1. Flags affected: same as `AND`.

*Example:* `OR AL, 80H` sets the MSB of `AL` to `1`, leaving other bits untouched.

**`XOR destination, source`** — Performs bit-wise exclusive-OR. Result is `1` if the bits **differ**. Used for **bit toggling** and the **idiom `XOR reg, reg`** to clear a register efficiently. Flags affected: same as `AND`.

*Example:* `XOR AL, 0FFH` inverts all bits of `AL` (logical NOT via XOR with all-ones mask).

**`NOT destination`** — One's complement (bit-wise inversion). Operates on a **single operand** (unary). It flips every bit (0→1, 1→0). **No flags are affected** by `NOT`.

*Example:* `NOT AL` when `AL = 3CH` (`0011 1100`) results in `AL = C3H` (`1100 0011`).

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Express operands in 16-bit binary:**

$$\begin{aligned}
BX &= A5C3_{H} = 1010\,0101\,1100\,0011_{B} \\
\text{Mask} &= 00FF_{H} = 0000\,0000\,1111\,1111_{B}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Bit-wise AND operation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
&1010\,0101\,1100\,0011 \\
\text{AND}\quad &0000\,0000\,1111\,1111 \\
\hline
&0000\,0000\,1100\,0011
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Result in hexadecimal:**

$$\text{Result} = 00C3_{H}$$

So `BX` becomes `00C3H` — only the lower 8 bits of the original are preserved (the upper byte is masked to zero).

**Step 4 — Flag computation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CF} &= 0 \quad \text{(AND clears carry)} \\
\text{OF} &= 0 \quad \text{(AND clears overflow)} \\
\text{SF} &= 0 \quad \text{(MSB of result } = 0\text{)} \\
\text{ZF} &= 0 \quad \text{(result } \neq 0\text{)} \\
\text{PF} &= 1 \quad \text{(low byte } C3_{H} = 1100\,0011\text{ has four 1's — even parity)} \\
\text{AF} &= \text{undefined (don't care)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final answer:** `BX = 00C3H`, Flags = `CF=0, OF=0, SF=0, ZF=0, PF=1, AF=?`

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 [KTU ESE – 2024 Scheme Model]**

**(a)** Explain the 8086 shift and rotate instructions in detail. Differentiate between `SHL`, `SHR`, and `SAR`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A register `AX` contains `C400H` and the Carry Flag is `1`. Execute the following sequence and show the state of `AX` and `CF` after each instruction: **(7 marks)**
```
(i)   SAR AX, 1
(ii)  ROL AX, 1
(iii) RCR AX, 1
```

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Shift Instructions** shift the bits of an operand left or right by a specified count. The vacated positions are filled according to the instruction type.

- **`SHL` (Shift Left):** Shifts bits left by `1` (immediate) or by `CL` register positions. The LSB is filled with `0`, and the MSB shifted out goes into `CF`. Operationally identical to `SAL`. Effect: **multiplication by 2** for each shift (unsigned/signed left shift).

- **`SHR` (Shift Right Logical):** Shifts bits right. The MSB is filled with `0`, and the LSB shifted out goes into `CF`. Used for **unsigned division by 2**. If the operand is signed-negative, the sign bit is lost (replaced by `0`).

- **`SAR` (Shift Arithmetic Right):** Shifts bits right but **fills the MSB with the original sign bit**, preserving the sign of the operand. Used for **signed division by 2**.

**Rotate Instructions** shift bits within the operand; the bit that "falls off" one end **wraps around** to the other end (no bits are lost). Variants:

- **`ROL`** — Rotate Left within the 8/16-bit operand.
- **`ROR`** — Rotate Right within the 8/16-bit operand.
- **`RCL`** — Rotate Left through Carry; the CF becomes part of a 17-bit/9-bit rotation loop.
- **`RCR`** — Rotate Right through Carry; similar 17-bit rotation in the opposite direction.

**Comparative Table:**

| Instruction | Direction | Vacated Bit Fill | Use |
|:-----------:|:---------:|:----------------:|:--:|
| `SHL` | Left | 0 | Multiply by 2 |
| `SHR` | Right | 0 | Unsigned divide by 2 |
| `SAR` | Right | Sign bit (MSB) | Signed divide by 2 |
| `ROL` | Left | MSB→LSB | Bit rotation |
| `ROR` | Right | LSB→MSB | Bit rotation |
| `RCL` | Left | MSB→CF, CF→LSB | 17-bit rotate |
| `RCR` | Right | LSB→CF, CF→MSB | 17-bit rotate |

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Initial state:** `AX = C400H = 1100\,0100\,0000\,0000`, `CF = 1`

---

**(i) `SAR AX, 1`** — Arithmetic right shift, sign bit (`1`) replicated on the left.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Before:} \quad &1\,100\,0100\,0000\,0000 \quad CF=? \\
\text{After:} \quad &1\,1100\,0100\,0000\,0000 \quad \text{(MSB stays 1, LSB moves to CF)} \\
\text{Result:} \quad &AX = E200H, \quad CF = 0
\end{aligned}$$

---

**(ii) `ROL AX, 1`** — Rotate left within AX. MSB (which is `1`) wraps to LSB.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Before:} \quad &AX = E200H = 1110\,0010\,0000\,0000,\quad CF=0 \\
\text{After:} \quad &\text{MSB }= 1 \rightarrow \text{LSB},\ \text{CF receives old MSB}=1 \\
\text{Result:} \quad &AX = C400H,\quad CF = 1
\end{aligned}$$

(Notice we return to the original state — this is a hallmark of 16-bit rotation by 16.)

---

**(iii) `RCR AX, 1`** — Rotate right through carry (17-bit rotation).

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{17-bit buffer:}\quad &CF\,|\,AX = 1\mid 1100\,0100\,0000\,0000 \\
\text{Rotate right by 1:}\quad &\text{LSB }=0 \rightarrow CF,\ \text{old CF }=1 \rightarrow \text{MSB} \\
\text{New buffer:}\quad &0\mid 1110\,0010\,0000\,0000 \\
\text{Result:}\quad &AX = E200H, \quad CF = 0
\end{aligned}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting `CF=0` for `AND`/`OR`/`XOR`** — Examiners specifically test this; missing it costs ½ to 1 mark per instance.
> 2. **Confusing `SHR` with `SAR`** — If the operand is `80H`, `SHR` gives `40H` (wrong for signed), `SAR` gives `C0H` (correct). Examiners set questions with negative numbers precisely to trap this.
> 3. **Treating `NOT` as affecting flags** — `NOT` affects **no flags**. Writing "`NOT` clears CF" is a guaranteed zero for that sub-question.
> 4. **Confusing destination vs source for `XOR`** — Students write `XOR BX, AX` thinking it swaps registers; in 8086, `XOR` only writes back to the **destination**. Use `XCHG` for swap.
> 5. **Skipping binary representation** — Always show the bit-wise operation column-by-column. Even if the hex calculation is correct, full marks require visible binary work.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Logical Instructions (Module 1):**

- ✅ **Logical instructions = bit-wise operations**, NOT numerical arithmetic.
- ✅ **`AND` → mask** (zero unwanted bits), **`OR` → set** (force 1s), **`XOR` → toggle** (flip bits or clear register).
- ✅ **`TEST` = non-destructive `AND`**; use it for conditional jumps without altering data.
- ✅ **`NOT` is the ONLY logical instruction that affects NO flags.**
- ✅ **`AND`, `OR`, `XOR`, `TEST` always clear `CF` and `OF`;** `SF`, `ZF`, `PF` reflect the result.
- ✅ **`SHL` ≡ `SAL`** — identical machine encoding; the assembler distinguishes them for readability.
- ✅ **`SHR` fills with `0` (unsigned-friendly);** `SAR` fills with the **sign bit** (signed-friendly).
- ✅ **`ROL`/`ROR`** rotate within the operand (no bit lost, no carry involvement).
- ✅ **`RCL`/`RCR`** are **17-bit rotations** — the carry flag is part of the rotation loop.
- ✅ **`CMP` performs subtraction internally** but stores nothing — used only to set flags for the next conditional jump.
- ✅ **Common exam idiom:** `XOR reg, reg` to clear a register (faster and shorter than `MOV reg, 0`).
- ✅ **Parity Flag (PF) = 1** when the low byte of the result has an **even number of 1-bits**.

**Memory aid — Flag Map Mnemonic:**
> *"**A**ll **L**ogical **O**ps **X**-out **C**arry and **O**verflow: **ALOX-CO**."*
> (AND, OR, NOT — wait, NOT doesn't — so use: **"AND-OR-XOR-TEST zero CF & OF"**.)

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Branching Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEET504 - MICROPROCESSORS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Branching Instructions ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)** > *Branching Instructions* in the 8086 microprocessor are a class of control transfer instructions that alter the sequential flow of program execution by modifying the Instruction Pointer (IP), and in the case of intersegment transfers, the Code Segment (CS) register. They enable the processor to skip, repeat, or conditionally execute blocks of code, forming the fundamental building blocks of decision-making logic, loops, and procedure calls in assembly language programming. ### 🔑 Intuitive Analogy — The "Railway Signal & Switchman" Imagine a train moving on a single railway track. By default, it continues straight along the main line. However, at a junction, a **signal post** (think: *status flags*) indicates whether the train should continue or divert. A **switchman** stands ready to flip the tracks if a particular signal is GREEN for a different route. - **The Train** = the Program Counter (CS:IP) - **The Main Track** = sequential execution - **The Signal Post** = Flag Register (ZF, CF, SF, etc.) - **The Switchman** = Branching Instruction - **The Diverging Track** = Target address of the jump When the Switchman reads a signal, he either **flips the track (conditional branch)** or **always flips it (unconditional branch)**. This is exactly how 8086 branching works. ### 📋 Classification of Branching Instructions (KTU High-Yield) | Category | Purpose | Examples | |----------|---------|----------| | **Unconditional Jumps** | Always transfer control | `JMP` | | **Conditional Jumps** | Transfer only if a flag condition is TRUE | `JZ`, `JNZ`, `JC`, `JNC`, `JE`, `JNE`, `JG`, `JL`, `JA`, `JB`, etc. | | **Iteration / Loop Control** | Repeat a block of code with CX counter | `LOOP`, `LOOPE`, `LOOPNE` | | **Procedure Calls** | Transfer + save return address on stack | `CALL` and `RET` | | **Software Interrupts** | Vector-based control transfer | `INT n`, `INTO`, `IRET` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Out of all the above, **Conditional Jumps** and the **Flag Register** are the most heavily tested areas in Module 1. You must memorize the mnemonics and the exact flag bit they test. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Flag Register — The Heart of Branching The **Flag Register** (a **16-bit** register) is the decision-making engine. Six of its bits are **status flags** that reflect the outcome of the last arithmetic/logic operation, and three are **control flags**. Branching instructions consult the status flags. $$ \text{Flag Register} = 15\ \text{bits (status + control)} $$ | Bit | Flag | Symbol | What Branching Tests | |-----|------|--------|----------------------| | 0 | Carry Flag | **CF** | Unsigned overflow / borrow | | 1 | Reserved | — | — | | 2 | Parity Flag | **PF** | Even parity in low 8 bits | | 3 | Reserved | — | — | | 4 | Auxiliary Carry | **AF** | BCD adjustment | | 5 | Reserved | — | — | | 6 | Zero Flag | **ZF** | Result is exactly **zero** | | 7 | Sign Flag | **SF** | MSB is 1 (negative result) | | 8 | Trap Flag | **TF** | (Control) — single stepping | | 9 | Interrupt Enable | **IF** | (Control) | | 10 | Direction Flag | **DF** | (Control) — string ops | | 11 | Overflow Flag | **OF** | Signed arithmetic overflow | ### 2.2 Unconditional Jump — `JMP` > [!NOTE] > **Definition:** `JMP target` unconditionally loads the IP (and CS, if intersegment) with the target address. No flag is consulted. It is the assembly equivalent of `goto`. **Three Operand Variants (KTU 2024 Critical):** | Variant | Size | Displacement | Address Range | Syntax | |---------|------|--------------|---------------|--------| | **Short Jump** | 2 bytes | 8-bit signed | −128 to +127 bytes from IP | `JMP SHORT label` | | **Near Jump** | 3 bytes | 16-bit signed | ±32 KB from IP (within segment) | `JMP NEAR PTR label` | | **Far Jump** | 5 bytes | 16:16 segment:offset | Anywhere in 1 MB | `JMP FAR PTR label` | ### 2.3 Conditional Jumps — The High-Yield Table All conditional jumps in 8086 are **SHORT jumps** (only 8-bit displacement). They come in pairs (opposite polarity). The KTU board loves testing the **mnemonic ↔ condition** mapping. | Mnemonic | Tested Flag / Condition | Meaning | |----------|--------------------------|---------| | `JZ` / `JE` | ZF = 1 | Jump if Zero / Equal | | `JNZ` / `JNE` | ZF = 0 | Jump if Not Zero / Not Equal | | `JC` | CF = 1 | Jump if Carry (unsigned < or ≥) | | `JNC` | CF = 0 | Jump if No Carry | | `JS` | SF = 1 | Jump if Sign (negative) | | `JNS` | SF = 0 | Jump if No Sign | | `JO` | OF = 1 | Jump if Overflow | | `JNO` | OF = 0 | Jump if No Overflow | | `JP` / `JPE` | PF = 1 | Jump if Parity Even | | `JNP` / `JPO` | PF = 0 | Jump if Parity Odd | | `JA` / `JNBE` | CF = 0 AND ZF = 0 | Above (unsigned) | | `JAE` / `JNB` | CF = 0 | Above or Equal | | `JB` / `JNAE` | CF = 1 | Below (unsigned) | | `JBE` / `JNA` | CF = 1 OR ZF = 1 | Below or Equal | | `JG` / `JNLE` | ZF=0 AND SF=OF | Greater (signed) | | `JGE` / `JNL` | SF = OF | Greater or Equal (signed) | | `JL` / `JNGE` | SF ≠ OF | Less (signed) | | `JLE` / `JNG` | ZF=1 OR SF≠OF | Less or Equal (signed) | | `JCXZ` | CX = 0 | Jump if CX register is zero | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > Use `\vert` instead of `|` inside this table to avoid breaking markdown. | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |----------|---------|---------------| | New IP (near, intra-segment) | $\text{IP}_{\text{new}} = \text{IP}_{\text{current}} + \text{disp16} + \text{instruction\_size}$ | bytes | | New IP (short) | $\text{IP}_{\text{new}} = \text{IP}_{\text{current}} + \text{disp8} + \text{instruction\_size}$ | bytes | | New CS:IP (far) | $(\text{CS:IP})_{\text{new}} = \text{segment\_target} : \text{offset\_target}$ | direct | | Physical Address (any) | $\text{PA} = \text{CS} \times 16 + \text{IP}$ | bytes (0 to 1,048,575) | | Displacement for short jump | $\text{disp8} = \text{offset\_target} - \text{IP}_{\text{after\_fetch}}$ | signed, range $-128$ to $+127$ | | Unsigned comparison result | $A > B \iff CF = 0 \land ZF = 0$ | bitwise | | Signed comparison result | $A > B \iff SF = OF \land ZF = 0$ | bitwise | | LOOP iterations | $\text{CX}_{\text{new}} = \text{CX}_{\text{old}} - 1$; jumps if $CX \neq 0$ | unsigned | | CALL stack push (near) | $[\text{SP}-1] \leftarrow \text{IP}_{\text{high}}$; $[\text{SP}-2] \leftarrow \text{IP}_{\text{low}}$; $\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} - 2$ | bytes | | CALL stack push (far) | pushes CS then IP | bytes | ### 2.5 Engineering Utility Branching instructions are the **CPU's decision-making machinery**. They are the foundation of: - **if-else constructs** in C → translated to CMP + conditional JMP - **for / while loops** → translated to LOOP / CMP + Jcc - **Function calls** in C → translated to CALL + RET - **Operating system interrupts** → INT n + IRET - **Finite State Machines** in embedded controllers Without branching, a CPU would be a useless sequential ROM player. Branching is what makes the 8086 a **Turing-complete general-purpose processor**. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Physical Address from CS:IP The 8086 has a **20-bit address bus** but only **16-bit segment registers**. To form a 20-bit physical address, the segment register is shifted left by 4 (i.e., multiplied by 16) and added to the offset. $$ \text{PA} = (\text{CS} \times 16) + \text{IP} $$ **Exhaustive Step-by-Step Example:** Given: `CS = 2000H`, `IP = 4030H`. Find the physical address of the next instruction. $$ \text{PA} = (\text{CS} \times 16) + \text{IP} $$ **Step 1** — Convert CS to decimal form for clarity (or do it in hex by appending a 0): $$ \text{CS} = 2000\text{H} \implies \text{CS} \times 16 = 20000\text{H} $$ (Because shifting a hex digit left by one position is equivalent to multiplying by 16.) **Step 2** — Add IP: $$ \text{PA} = 20000\text{H} + 4030\text{H} $$ **Step 3** — Perform the hex addition: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{PA} &= 20000\text{H} + 04030\text{H} \\ &= 24030\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4** — Verify range: `24030H = 147,504` decimal, which is < 1,048,576 (`FFFFF`+1), so it is a valid physical address in the 1 MB memory space. > **Result:** The next instruction will be fetched from physical address **24030H**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Displacement Calculation for a Short Jump **Problem:** A `JMP SHORT TARGET` instruction is located at physical address `10200H`. The IP of the next instruction (after fetching the 2-byte JMP) is `0202H`. The label `TARGET` resides at `CS:01F8H`. Compute the 8-bit signed displacement byte. **Step 1** — Determine the new IP after JMP is fetched: $$ \text{IP}_{\text{after\_fetch}} = \text{IP}_{\text{current}} + 2 = 0200\text{H} + 0002\text{H} = 0202\text{H} $$ **Step 2** — Apply the displacement formula: $$ \text{disp8} = \text{offset}_{\text{target}} - \text{IP}_{\text{after\_fetch}} $$ **Step 3** — Substitute the values: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{disp8} &= 01\text{F}8\text{H} - 0202\text{H} \\ &= 01\text{F}8\text{H} + (-\,0202\text{H}) \\ &= 01\text{F}8\text{H} + \text{FDFEH}\ (\text{two's complement of 0202H}) \\ &= \text{FFF6H} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4** — Truncate to 8 bits: $$ \text{disp8} = \text{F6H} $$ **Step 5** — Verify signed interpretation: $$ \text{F6H as signed 8-bit} = -10_{10} $$ So we are jumping **10 bytes backward** from `0202H`: $$ 0202\text{H} - 000\text{AH} = 01\text{F}8\text{H} \ \checkmark $$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — CALL/RET Stack Mechanics (Near CALL) **Problem:** SP = `2000H`, IP = `1500H`, and a near `CALL` to a subroutine is executed. Show the state of the stack and IP afterwards. Then, on `RET`, show restoration. **Step 1 — CALL Execution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SP}_{\text{new}} &= \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 2 = 2000\text{H} - 0002\text{H} = 1\text{FFE H} \\ \text{Stack}[1\text{FFF H}] &\leftarrow \text{IP}_{\text{high byte}} = 15\text{H} \\ \text{Stack}[1\text{FFE H}] &\leftarrow \text{IP}_{\text{low byte}} = 00\text{H} \\ \text{IP} &\leftarrow \text{subroutine offset} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — RET Execution (pop IP from stack):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{IP}_{\text{low}} &\leftarrow \text{Stack}[1\text{FFE H}] = 00\text{H} \\ \text{IP}_{\text{high}} &\leftarrow \text{Stack}[1\text{FFF H}] = 15\text{H} \\ \text{IP} &= 1500\text{H} \\ \text{SP}_{\text{new}} &= 1\text{FFE H} + 2 = 2000\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ The IP is now restored to its pre-CALL value, and the stack pointer is back to its original location. The CALL/RET pair is **stack-balanced**. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Jump | Goal in C | 8086 Mnemonic Sequence | |-----------|------------------------| | `if (A == B)` | `CMP A, B` → `JE target` | | `if (A < B)` (signed) | `CMP A, B` → `JL target` | | `if (A < B)` (unsigned) | `CMP A, B` → `JB target` | | `while (CX > 0)` | `top: ...` → `DEC CX` → `JNZ top` OR use `LOOP top` | | `for(i=0; i<10; i++)` | `MOV CX, 10` → `top: ...` → `LOOP top` | | `switch(x)` | chain of `CMP` + `JE` jumps | | Function call | `CALL proc` + `RET` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **`CMP` does not change the operands** — it only performs an internal subtraction and sets the flags. This is why it always precedes a conditional jump. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Branching Instruction Decision Tree The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete classification hierarchy** of branching instructions in 8086 — a high-yield KTU exam diagram. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[BRANCHING INSTRUCTIONS] --> B[UNCONDITIONAL] A --> C[CONDITIONAL] A --> D[ITERATION] A --> E[PROCEDURE] A --> F[INTERRUPT] B --> B1[JMP SHORT
8-bit disp] B --> B2[JMP NEAR
16-bit disp] B --> B3[JMP FAR
16:16 target] C --> C1[SINGLE FLAG TESTS] C --> C2[UNSIGNED COMPARE] C --> C3[SIGNED COMPARE] C1 --> C11[JZ JE
ZF equals 1] C1 --> C12[JNZ JNE
ZF equals 0] C1 --> C13[JC
CF equals 1] C1 --> C14[JNC
CF equals 0] C1 --> C15[JS JNS
Sign flag] C1 --> C16[JO JNO
Overflow flag] C1 --> C17[JP JNP
Parity flag] C2 --> C21[JA JNBE
CF0 and ZF0] C2 --> C22[JAE JNB
CF0] C2 --> C23[JB JNAE
CF1] C2 --> C24[JBE JNA
CF1 or ZF1] C3 --> C31[JG JNLE
SF equals OF and ZF0] C3 --> C32[JGE JNL
SF equals OF] C3 --> C33[JL JNGE
SF not equal OF] C3 --> C34[JLE JNG
ZF1 or SF not equal OF] D --> D1[LOOP
CX minus 1 then jump if CX not 0] D --> D2[LOOPE LOOPZ
jump if CX not 0 and ZF equals 1] D --> D3[LOOPNE LOOPNZ
jump if CX not 0 and ZF equals 0] D --> D4[JCXZ
jump if CX equals 0] E --> E1[CALL NEAR
intra-segment] E --> E2[CALL FAR
inter-segment] E --> E3[RET optional pop] F --> F1[INT n
type n interrupt] F --> F2[INTO
interrupt on overflow] F --> F3[IRET
interrupt return] ``` ### 4.2 Execution Flow Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR FETCH[Fetch Instruction] --> DECODE[Decode Opcode] DECODE --> Q1{Is it a
branching
instruction?} Q1 -- NO --> EXEC[Execute Normally] Q1 -- YES --> Q2{Unconditional
or Conditional?} Q2 -- UNCONDITIONAL --> UPD_IP[Update IP
and CS if needed] Q2 -- CONDITIONAL --> CHK_FLAG{Check Status
Flag Condition} CHK_FLAG -- CONDITION TRUE --> UPD_IP CHK_FLAG -- CONDITION FALSE --> SEQ[Continue Sequential
Execution] UPD_IP --> FETCH EXEC --> FETCH SEQ --> FETCH ``` ### 4.3 Memory Layout for a Short Jump (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Address: 0100
Opcode EB] --> B[Address: 0101
Displacement F6] B --> C[Address: 0102
NEXT INSTR
IP equals 0102] C --> D[Address 01F8
TARGET LABEL
reached after jump] B -. disp8 equals minus 10 .-> D ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert** > 1. Mixing up **signed** vs **unsigned** comparison mnemonics (`JL` vs `JB`) — you will lose 2 to 3 marks in a 7-mark sub-question. > 2. Forgetting that **`LOOP` decrements CX first, then checks if CX ≠ 0**. Many students wrongly assume it checks CX > 0 before decrementing. > 3. For **CALL/RET**, students often forget that **near CALL pushes only IP**, while **far CALL pushes both CS and IP**. The stack grows **downward** (toward lower addresses). > 4. In a displacement calculation, students frequently use the **wrong reference point** — the displacement is calculated from the **IP of the next instruction**, NOT the IP of the current JMP opcode. > 5. The destination of a conditional jump is encoded as a **signed 8-bit** number. A naive unsigned conversion will give a wrong forward/backward direction. --- ### 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** *Explain the difference between a NEAR jump and a FAR jump in 8086 with suitable examples.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **NEAR jump** is an **intrasegment** jump where only the **IP** is modified. The CS register remains unchanged. The displacement can be 8-bit (short near) or 16-bit (full near), allowing the target to be within the same 64 KB code segment. Example: `JMP 2000H` will set IP to 2000H while CS remains as it is. A **FAR jump** is an **intersegment** jump where **both CS and IP** are loaded with new values. The target can lie anywhere in the 1 MB memory space. It is encoded with 5 bytes: a 16-bit offset followed by a 16-bit segment. Example: `JMP 2000H:0500H` sets CS = 2000H and IP = 0500H. | Feature | Near Jump | Far Jump | |---------|-----------|----------| | Registers modified | IP only | CS and IP | | Size of instruction | 2 or 3 bytes | 5 bytes | | Range | Within segment | Anywhere in 1 MB | | Example | `JMP NEAR PTR LABEL` | `JMP FAR PTR LABEL` | --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** *List any six conditional jump instructions of 8086 and state the flag condition tested by each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | # | Mnemonic | Condition Tested | |---|----------|---------------------| | 1 | `JZ` / `JE` | ZF = 1 (result is zero / equal) | | 2 | `JNZ` / `JNE` | ZF = 0 (result is not zero / not equal) | | 3 | `JC` | CF = 1 (carry generated) | | 4 | `JNC` | CF = 0 (no carry) | | 5 | `JS` | SF = 1 (result is negative) | | 6 | `JO` | OF = 1 (signed overflow) | --- ### 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 ESE):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`** **(a)** Explain the **Flag Register** of 8086 in detail with a neat diagram. Highlight the status flags that are used by conditional jump instructions. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The 8086 **Flag Register** is a 16-bit register, but only **9 bits are actively used**. The lower 8 bits behave like 8085 flags; the upper byte contains three control flags and one status flag. **Bit-wise Layout:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Bit 15} &\to \text{Reserved} \\ \text{Bit 14} &\to \text{NT (Nested Task)} \\ \text{Bit 13-12} &\to \text{IOPL (I/O Privilege Level)} \\ \text{Bit 11} &\to \text{OF (Overflow Flag)} \\ \text{Bit 10} &\to \text{DF (Direction Flag — control)} \\ \text{Bit 9} &\to \text{IF (Interrupt Flag — control)} \\ \text{Bit 8} &\to \text{TF (Trap Flag — control)} \\ \text{Bit 7} &\to \text{SF (Sign Flag)} \\ \text{Bit 6} &\to \text{ZF (Zero Flag)} \\ \text{Bit 5} &\to \text{Reserved} \\ \text{Bit 4} &\to \text{AF (Auxiliary Carry Flag)} \\ \text{Bit 3} &\to \text{Reserved} \\ \text{Bit 2} &\to \text{PF (Parity Flag)} \\ \text{Bit 1} &\to \text{Reserved} \\ \text{Bit 0} &\to \text{CF (Carry Flag)} \end{aligned} $$ **Status flags used by conditional jumps:** - **CF (Carry)** → tested by `JC`, `JNC`, `JB`, `JNB`, `JA`, `JBE` - **ZF (Zero)** → tested by `JZ`, `JNZ`, `JE`, `JNE`, `JA`, `JBE`, `JG`, `JLE` - **SF (Sign)** → tested by `JS`, `JNS`, `JG`, `JGE`, `JL`, `JLE` - **OF (Overflow)** → tested by `JO`, `JNO`, `JG`, `JGE`, `JL`, `JLE` - **PF (Parity)** → tested by `JP`, `JNP` Control flags (TF, IF, DF) are not used by conditional jump instructions. **(b)** A near jump `JMP NEAR PTR TARGET` instruction is located at offset `1500H` within segment `2000H`. The 16-bit displacement stored in the instruction is `4050H`. Find: (i) the IP after the instruction is fetched, (ii) the new IP, and (iii) the physical address of the target. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) IP after instruction fetch:** A near jump is 3 bytes (opcode E9 + 2 displacement bytes), so: $$ \text{IP}_{\text{after}} = 1500\text{H} + 0003\text{H} = 1503\text{H} $$ **(ii) New IP after applying the displacement:** $$ \text{IP}_{\text{new}} = \text{IP}_{\text{after}} + \text{disp16} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{IP}_{\text{new}} &= 1503\text{H} + 4050\text{H} \\ &= 5553\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Physical address of the target:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{PA} &= (\text{CS} \times 16) + \text{IP}_{\text{new}} \\ &= 2000\text{H} \times 16 + 5553\text{H} \\ &= 20000\text{H} + 5553\text{H} \\ &= 25553\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Target Physical Address} = 25553\text{H} }$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`** **(a)** Explain the **CALL and RET** instructions in 8086. Differentiate between near and far CALL with proper stack diagrams. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The **CALL** instruction transfers control to a subroutine while saving the return address on the stack so that execution can resume at the instruction following the CALL. The **RET** instruction pops the saved return address from the stack and transfers control back. **Near CALL (intrasegment):** - Modifies **IP only**. - Pushes the 16-bit IP of the next instruction onto the stack. - **SP decreases by 2.** ``` Memory (Stack) +-----------+ Low Addr| IP Low | <- SP after CALL +-----------+ | IP High | +-----------+ High Addr| ... | <- SP before CALL +-----------+ ``` **Far CALL (intersegment):** - Modifies **both CS and IP**. - Pushes **CS first, then IP** onto the stack. - **SP decreases by 4.** ``` Memory (Stack) +-----------+ Low Addr| IP Low | <- SP after CALL +-----------+ | IP High | +-----------+ | CS Low | +-----------+ High Addr| CS High | <- SP before CALL +-----------+ ``` | Feature | Near CALL | Far CALL | |---------|-----------|----------| | Size of instruction | 3 bytes | 5 bytes | | Registers saved | IP only | IP and CS | | Stack usage | 2 bytes | 4 bytes | | Range | Within segment | Anywhere | **RET** pops the saved return address in **reverse order** (IP first for far, IP only for near) and increments SP by the same number of bytes it was decremented by CALL. **(b)** Write an 8086 assembly program to **find the largest of N numbers** stored in memory using the branching instructions concept. Explain the logic. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Logic:** Initialize a counter N in CX, load the first number as the "current maximum" in AL, and loop through the remaining numbers. For each number, compare with AL using `CMP`; if the new number is greater (unsigned, use `JA`), update AL. At the end, AL holds the maximum. ```asm ASSUME CS:CODE CODE SEGMENT START: MOV CX, COUNT ; CX = N MOV SI, OFFSET LIST ; SI -> first element LODSB ; AL = first element, SI++ DEC CX ; one element already loaded AGAIN: CMP AL, [SI] ; compare max with next JAE NEXT ; if AL >= [SI], skip MOV AL, [SI] ; else AL = [SI] (new max) NEXT: INC SI LOOP AGAIN ; CX--, jump if CX != 0 MOV MAX, AL ; store result HLT LIST DB 34H, 12H, 7FH, 22H, 0A5H COUNT EQU 5 MAX DB ? CODE ENDS END START ``` **Explanation of branching instructions used:** - `JAE NEXT` — conditional jump based on **CF = 0** (unsigned comparison). - `LOOP AGAIN` — decrements CX and jumps to AGAIN if CX ≠ 0. - `LODSB` automatically updates SI and AL — it is not a branch, but is used here to traverse the array. The maximum of `{34H, 12H, 7FH, 22H, 0A5H}` is `0A5H`, which will be stored in `MAX`. --- ### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Branching instructions** modify the flow of execution by changing IP (and CS for far/intersegment transfers). - The **Flag Register** holds 6 status flags and 3 control flags. Only the **status flags** are tested by conditional jumps. - **Unconditional JMP** has 3 variants: short (8-bit disp, ±128), near (16-bit disp, ±32 KB), far (16:16, anywhere in 1 MB). - **Conditional jumps** are always **SHORT** (2 bytes, signed 8-bit disp). - **`CMP` is essential** before a conditional jump — it sets the flags without altering operands. - **Unsigned comparisons** use `JA`, `JAE`, `JB`, `JBE`; **Signed comparisons** use `JG`, `JGE`, `JL`, `JLE`. Mixing them is the #1 source of bugs. - **LOOP** instructions use the **CX register** as the counter. `LOOP` decrements CX *first*, then jumps if CX ≠ 0. - **CALL/RET** work as a stack-balanced pair. Near CALL/RET saves/restores **IP only**; far saves/restores **CS and IP**. - The **physical address** is always $\text{PA} = \text{Segment} \times 16 + \text{Offset}$. - The **displacement** in a short jump is calculated from the **IP after the JMP opcode is fetched**, not from the current IP. - **INT n** is a branching instruction that uses the **Interrupt Vector Table** at `0000:0000` to find the target address.

topics.title: Opcodes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET504 - MICROPROCESSORS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Opcodes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **OPCODE (Operation Code):** An **opcode** (operation code) is the portion of a machine language instruction that specifies the operation to be performed by the microprocessor. It is a binary code (typically **8 bits** in 8-bit processors like the **8085** and **8086**, and **6 bits** in the ARM Cortex-M architecture) that the processor's **Instruction Decoder** interprets to activate the corresponding micro-operations in the **Control Unit (CU)**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, the term *opcode* is often taught in conjunction with the **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)** of the target processor. The opcode is the *verb* of the instruction — it tells the CPU **what to do**, while the *operand* tells it **what to do it with**.

### 1.2 Instruction Format Anatomy

A standard machine instruction is divided into two major fields:

$$\text{Machine Instruction} = \underbrace{\text{Opcode Field}}_{\text{What to do}} \; + \; \underbrace{\text{Operand Field}}_{\text{What to use}}$$

For the **Intel 8085 microprocessor**, an instruction can be **1 byte, 2 bytes, or 3 bytes** long. The **first byte is always the opcode**.

$$T_n = 1 + N_{op}$$

Where $T_n$ is the total instruction size in bytes and $N_{op}$ is the number of operand bytes.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: A Restaurant Kitchen Order**
> 
> Imagine you are a chef (the **CPU**) in a busy kitchen. A waiter hands you a printed order ticket:
> - The **action word** (e.g., "FRY", "BOIL", "CHOP") is the **Opcode** — it tells you the *operation*.
> - The **ingredients** listed below (e.g., "Potato, 200g") are the **Operands** — they tell you *what data* to apply the operation to.
> 
> You, as the chef, don't need the full recipe book. Just the *command* (opcode) and the *data* (operands) is enough. Similarly, the microprocessor's Control Unit only needs the opcode bits to fire the right sequence of control signals.

### 1.4 Why Opcodes Matter in Embedded Systems

In embedded systems (the focus of PBEET504), the opcode width directly determines:
- **Code density** (how much program fits in Flash/ROM)
- **Decoding complexity** (more opcode bits = larger decoder = slower or costlier hardware)
- **Power consumption** (ARM Thumb opcodes are 16-bit specifically to reduce fetch energy)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the 2024 scheme, students are expected to distinguish between *opcode*, *operand*, and *mnemonic*. The mnemonic (e.g., `MVI A, 32H`) is the *human-readable* form; the assembler translates it into a binary opcode (e.g., `3E 32` for `MVI A, data`).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Opcode Classification in the 8085

The 8085 has a **8-bit opcode** field, which means it can support a theoretical maximum of:

$$N_{max} = 2^8 = 256 \text{ distinct opcodes}$$

However, not all 256 codes are used. The 8085 has approximately **246 implemented opcodes** (with 10 unused/illegal opcodes acting as NOPs in the NMOS version, and 12 unused in CMOS 80C85).

### 2.2 The Three Instruction Formats

| Format Type | Byte Structure | 8085 Example | Hex Code |
|-------------|----------------|--------------|----------|
| **1-Byte Instruction** | `[Opcode]` | `MOV A,B` (Copy register B to A) | `78` |
| **2-Byte Instruction** | `[Opcode][Operand]` | `MVI A, 32H` (Move immediate 32H to A) | `3E 32` |
| **3-Byte Instruction** | `[Opcode][Operand-Low][Operand-High]` | `LXI H, 2000H` (Load H-L pair with 2000H) | `21 00 20` |

### 2.3 Opcode Categories by Function

The 8085 opcodes fall into **5 functional groups**:

1. **Data Transfer Group** — `MOV`, `MVI`, `LXI`, `LDA`, `STA`, `LHLD`, `SHLD`, `XCHG`
2. **Arithmetic Group** — `ADD`, `ADI`, `SUB`, `SUI`, `INR`, `DCR`, `INX`, `DCX`, `DAD`
3. **Logical Group** — `ANA`, `ANI`, `ORA`, `ORI`, `XRA`, `XRI`, `CMA`, `CMP`, `CPI`
4. **Branch Control Group** — `JMP`, `JC`, `JNC`, `JZ`, `JNZ`, `CALL`, `RET`, `RST`
5. **Machine/I/O Control Group** — `NOP`, `HLT`, `DI`, `EI`, `IN`, `OUT`, `RIM`, `SIM`

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Formulas for Opcode Analysis**

| Formula / Rule | Expression | Description |
|----------------|------------|-------------|
| Maximum distinct opcodes | $N = 2^{W}$ | Where $W$ = opcode width in bits |
| 8085 total opcodes | $N = 2^8 = 256$ | Theoretical maximum |
| Instruction size | $S = 1 + O$ | $O$ = operand bytes (0, 1, or 2 for 8085) |
| Program size | $P = \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i$ | Sum of all instruction sizes in bytes |
| Code density | $D = \dfrac{I_{count}}{P}$ | Instructions per byte (higher = denser) |
| Decoder combinational depth | $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ | Gate levels in PLA/ROM decoder |
| Opcode prefix (8086) | $W \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ bytes | Multi-byte opcode escape for complex instructions |

### 2.5 Encoding Strategy: Why Hex Opcodes?

The 8085 uses **hexadecimal notation** as a shorthand for the 8-bit binary opcode, because one hex digit represents 4 binary bits:

$$\text{Binary: } \underbrace{0111}_{7} \; \underbrace{1000}_{8} \rightarrow \text{Hex: } 78\text{H}$$

This makes opcode tables (like the 8085 datasheet) compact and human-readable. A KTU question often asks: *"Given the opcode `3E 32`, identify the instruction."* — You decode `3E` = `MVI A,` and `32` is the immediate data.

### 2.6 Opcode Decoding in Hardware

The opcode byte enters the **Instruction Register (IR)**, which feeds the **Instruction Decoder** — a combinational logic network (often implemented as a PLA — Programmable Logic Array). The decoder outputs one *active* line for each unique opcode, which then enables the corresponding micro-operation sequence in the Control Unit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The same opcode-decode concept is used today in **RISC-V**, **ARM**, and even **custom ASIC controllers**. The principles of fixed-width vs. variable-width opcodes (CISC vs. RISC) trace directly back to opcode field design.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Number of Opcodes

**Problem:** Calculate the theoretical maximum number of distinct opcodes for processors with different opcode widths.

**Step 1 — General Formula:**
Every distinct opcode corresponds to a unique binary pattern. For a $W$-bit opcode field:

$$N_{max} = 2^W$$

**Step 2 — Apply to 8085 (W = 8):**

$$N_{max} = 2^8 = 256$$

**Step 3 — Apply to 8086 (Variable-width, 1–2 bytes):**
The 8086 uses a more complex scheme. The first byte has 256 possibilities. If the first byte is an *escape* (e.g., `0FH` for 2-byte opcodes), the second byte adds 256 more:

$$N_{max,8086} = 2^8 + 2^8 = 2 \times 256 = 512 \text{ primary opcodes}$$
(plus more escape prefixes yielding ~$2{,}000$ total in practice)

**Step 4 — Apply to ARM Cortex-M4 (Thumb-2, 16-bit fixed):**

$$N_{max} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ 16-bit opcodes}$$
(ARM uses ~$500$ defined instructions in Thumb-2)

**Step 5 — Summary Table:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{8085} &= 2^8 = 256 \\
N_{8086,1B} &= 2^8 = 256 \\
N_{8086,2B} &= 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \\
N_{ARM,16b} &= 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \\
N_{ARM,32b} &= 2^{32} = 4.29 \times 10^9
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The opcode width $W$ is the single most important design knob in an ISA. Increasing $W$ adds expressiveness but also increases instruction fetch bandwidth and decoder hardware cost.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Program Memory Size from Opcode Sizes

**Problem:** Calculate the total ROM/Flash required for an 8085 program given the instruction mix.

**Given:** A program consists of:
- 20 instructions of type `MOV A,B` (1-byte opcodes)
- 15 instructions of type `MVI A, data` (2-byte opcodes)
- 10 instructions of type `LXI H, addr` (3-byte opcodes)

**Step 1 — Define total program size:**

$$P = \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the byte counts:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= (20 \times 1) + (15 \times 2) + (10 \times 3) \\
  &= 20 + 30 + 30 \\
  &= 80 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to hexadecimal (typical in KTU):**

$$P = 80_{10} = 50\text{H}$$

**Result:** The program occupies **80 decimal / 50H bytes** of code memory.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Decoder Hardware Depth

**Problem:** How many logic-gate levels are required in a ROM-based opcode decoder for the 8085?

**Step 1 — The decoder is an $N$-to-$1$ line decoder where $N = 2^8 = 256$:**

$$\text{Inputs} = 8 \text{ bits}, \quad \text{Output lines} = 256$$

**Step 2 — In a PLA/ROM implementation, the worst-case path is a single $8$-input AND gate:**

$$\text{Depth} = 1 \text{ gate level (the AND plane)}$$

followed by the OR plane for shared minterms.

**Step 3 — For a diode-matrix ROM, the access time is:**

$$t_{acc} = t_{AND} + t_{OR} + t_{sense}$$

**Step 4 — Generalization to a $W$-bit opcode:**

$$\text{Decoder inputs} = W, \quad \text{Decoder outputs} = 2^W$$

For RISC-V (32-bit base integer opcodes), a fully decoded PLA would have $2^{32}$ output lines — clearly infeasible, which is why modern CPUs use *predecoding* and *caching* of opcode classes.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common 8085 Opcodes

| Mnemonic | Opcode (Hex) | Bytes | Machine Cycles | T-States | Operation |
|----------|--------------|-------|----------------|----------|-----------|
| `NOP` | `00` | 1 | 1 | 4 | No operation |
| `MOV A,B` | `78` | 1 | 1 | 4 | A ← B |
| `ADD B` | `80` | 1 | 1 | 4 | A ← A + B |
| `MVI A,32H` | `3E 32` | 2 | 2 | 7 | A ← 32H |
| `LXI H,2000H` | `21 00 20` | 3 | 3 | 10 | HL ← 2000H |
| `JMP 2000H` | `C3 00 20` | 3 | 3 | 10 | PC ← 2000H |
| `CALL 2000H` | `CD 00 20` | 3 | 5 | 18 | Stack & jump |
| `RET` | `C9` | 1 | 3 | 10 | Pop & return |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Opcode Bit-Position Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Machine Instruction Word] --> B{Byte 1}
    B --> C[Opcode Field]
    B --> D[Operand Field 1]
    B --> E[Operand Field 2]
    C --> C1[Bit 7]
    C --> C2[Bit 6]
    C --> C3[Bit 5]
    C --> C4[Bit 4]
    C --> C5[Bit 3]
    C --> C6[Bit 2]
    C --> C7[Bit 1]
    C --> C8[Bit 0]
    C1 --> F1[Decoder AND Plane]
    C2 --> F1
    C3 --> F1
    C4 --> F1
    C5 --> F1
    C6 --> F1
    C7 --> F1
    C8 --> F1
    F1 --> G[Active Micro-operation Line]
    G --> H[Control Unit Activates Datapath]
```

### 4.2 Instruction Fetch & Decode Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PC points to next instruction] --> B[Fetch Opcode Byte from Memory]
    B --> C[Load into Instruction Register IR]
    C --> D[Opcode Decoder PLA Activates]
    D --> E{Opcode Class?}
    E -->|Data Transfer| F1[Enable MOV or MVI micro-ops]
    E -->|Arithmetic| F2[Enable ADD or SUB micro-ops]
    E -->|Logical| F3[Enable AND or OR micro-ops]
    E -->|Branch| F4[Enable JMP or CALL micro-ops]
    E -->|Control| F5[Enable HLT or NOP micro-ops]
    F1 --> G[Fetch Operands if any]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> H[Calculate Target Address]
    F5 --> I[Special Cycle like Halt]
    G --> J[Execute Micro-operations]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Update Flags and PC]
    K --> L[Next Instruction Cycle]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Opcode Execution

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPU[8085 Microprocessor]
        PC[Program Counter]
        IR[Instruction Register]
        DEC[Opcode Decoder PLA]
        CU[Control Unit]
        REG[Register Bank A B C D E H L]
        ALU[Arithmetic Logic Unit]
        FLAG[Flag Register Z S P CY AC]
    end
    MEM[External Memory ROM or RAM]
    BUS[8-bit Data Bus and 16-bit Address Bus]
    PC -->|Address| BUS
    BUS -->|Opcode Byte| IR
    IR -->|8 bits| DEC
    DEC -->|Active Line| CU
    CU -->|Control Signals| REG
    CU -->|Control Signals| ALU
    REG -->|Operands| ALU
    ALU -->|Result| REG
    ALU -->|Status| FLAG
    CU -->|Increment| PC
```

### 4.4 Opcode Decode Timing (Machine Cycle View)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Opcode Fetch and Decode Timeline
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Fetch Phase
    Opcode Fetch (T1-T4) :a1, 0, 4
    section Decode Phase
    Instruction Decode (Internal) :a2, 4, 5
    section Execute Phase
    Operand Fetch (if any) :a3, 5, 7
    ALU Operation :a4, 7, 9
    Result Writeback :a5, 9, 10
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define the term "Opcode". How is it different from a Mnemonic? Give an example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Opcode (Operation Code):** It is the binary-coded portion of a machine instruction that specifies the operation the microprocessor must perform. It is stored as a fixed-width binary number (e.g., 8 bits in 8085) and is decoded by the Control Unit.
>
> **Mnemonic** is the human-readable alphanumeric symbol used by the programmer/assembler to represent the opcode (e.g., `ADD`, `MOV`, `MVI`).
>
> **Example:**
> - Opcode (hex) = `80H` → Mnemonic = `ADD B` (Adds the content of register B to Accumulator A)
> - Opcode (hex) = `3EH` → Mnemonic = `MVI A, data` (Moves immediate data to A)
>
> The assembler translates the mnemonic into the binary opcode during assembly.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, S4 B.Tech EEE]**
**An 8085 instruction has the hex code `21 35 10`. Identify the opcode, the operand, and the equivalent assembly mnemonic.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$$\text{Hex Code: } \underbrace{21}_{\text{Opcode}} \; \underbrace{35}_{\text{Low Byte}} \; \underbrace{10}_{\text{High Byte}}$$

- **Opcode byte** = `21H`
- **Operand (16-bit data)** = `1035H` (Low byte first, then High byte — *little-endian* order)
- **Assembly mnemonic** = `LXI H, 1035H`
- **Operation:** Loads the HL register pair with the 16-bit address `1035H`. (1 Mark for opcode identification, 1 Mark for operand and endianness, 1 Mark for mnemonic and operation)

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the role of the opcode in the instruction execution cycle of the 8085 microprocessor. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Classify the 8085 instruction set into functional groups with at least two example opcodes (with hex codes) for each group. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Instruction Execution Cycle (2 Marks):**
The 8085 executes every instruction through a sequence of machine cycles:
1. **Opcode Fetch (OF)** — Reads the opcode byte from memory into the Instruction Register (IR).
2. **Memory Read/Write (if operands exist)** — Fetches operand bytes.
3. **Execute (EX)** — Performs the operation.

**Step 2 — Role of Opcode (2 Marks):**
- The opcode byte is the **first byte** of every instruction.
- It is fetched in the **T1–T4 states** of the first machine cycle.
- It is latched into the **Instruction Register (IR)**.
- The IR feeds the **Instruction Decoder** (a PLA), which produces a unique *one-hot* enable signal.

**Step 3 — Control Unit Activation (2 Marks):**
- The decoded opcode activates the **Control Unit's** micro-operation sequencer.
- The CU then generates the precise timing signals to move data on the buses, gate registers, and trigger the ALU.
- The Program Counter (PC) is incremented to point to the next instruction.

**Step 4 — Diagram (1 Mark):**
Refer to the architecture diagram in Section 4.3 above. The student should draw the path: `Memory → IR → Decoder → CU → Datapath`.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

| Group | Example 1 (Mnemonic + Hex) | Example 2 (Mnemonic + Hex) | Function |
|-------|----------------------------|----------------------------|----------|
| **Data Transfer** | `MOV A,B` → `78H` | `MVI A,32H` → `3E 32` | Move data between registers/memory |
| **Arithmetic** | `ADD B` → `80H` | `SUI 05H` → `D6 05` | Add/Subtract operations |
| **Logical** | `ANA B` → `A0H` | `ORI 0FH` → `F6 0F` | AND, OR, XOR operations |
| **Branch Control** | `JMP 2000H` → `C3 00 20` | `CALL 2050H` → `CD 50 20` | Unconditional/conditional jumps and calls |
| **Machine Control** | `NOP` → `00H` | `HLT` → `76H` | CPU control (halt, no-op, interrupts) |

**Explanation (1 Mark):**
- Data transfer group copies data between registers and memory **without changing flags**.
- Arithmetic group updates the **CY, Z, S, P, AC** flags based on the result.
- Logical group performs bitwise operations and updates flags.
- Branch group alters the **sequential flow** of execution via PC manipulation.
- Machine control group affects the CPU's operating mode (interrupts, halt, etc.).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, S4 B.Tech EEE – Model]**
> **(a)** A processor has a 12-bit opcode field. Calculate the maximum number of distinct opcodes it can support. If three opcodes are reserved for future expansion, how many usable opcodes remain? **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A program contains 25 one-byte instructions, 30 two-byte instructions, and 15 three-byte instructions. Calculate: (i) the total program size in bytes, (ii) the equivalent in kilobytes (KB), and (iii) the percentage of program memory saved if a redesign converts all 3-byte instructions to 2-byte ones. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Maximum opcodes (3 Marks):**

$$N_{max} = 2^W = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ distinct opcodes}$$

**Step 2 — Usable opcodes after reservation (2 Marks):**

$$N_{usable} = N_{max} - N_{reserved} = 4096 - 3 = 4093$$

**Step 3 — Verification (2 Marks):**
The processor's instruction decoder would need $4096$ output lines from a $12$-input AND-plane PLA. The 3 reserved opcodes can be left unconnected or used as *trap* codes for fault detection.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Original total size (2 Marks):**

$$P_{orig} = (25 \times 1) + (30 \times 2) + (15 \times 3)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{orig} &= 25 + 60 + 45 \\
         &= 130 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent in KB (2 Marks):**

$$P_{orig,KB} = \frac{130 \text{ bytes}}{1024 \text{ bytes/KB}} = 0.1270 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 3 — Redesigned total size (1 Mark):**
Converting all 3-byte → 2-byte (saves 1 byte per such instruction):

$$P_{new} = 25 + 60 + (15 \times 2) = 25 + 60 + 30 = 115 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage memory saved (2 Marks):**

$$\text{Saved \%} = \frac{P_{orig} - P_{new}}{P_{orig}} \times 100$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Saved \%} &= \frac{130 - 115}{130} \times 100 \\
                &= \frac{15}{130} \times 100 \\
                &= 11.54\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** The redesign saves **15 bytes** of program memory, equivalent to a **11.54% reduction** in code footprint — a significant saving in resource-constrained embedded systems.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Endianness Confusion:** Forgetting that 8085 stores the **low byte first**, then the high byte. For `LXI H, 2000H`, the hex code is `21 00 20` — **not** `21 20 00`. (Loses 1–2 marks)
> 
> 2. **Opcode vs Operand Mistake:** Treating the *entire* instruction (e.g., `3E 32`) as the opcode. Only the **first byte** is the opcode; `32` is the operand. (Loses 1 mark)
> 
> 3. **Flag Register Ignorance:** Stating that data transfer instructions like `MOV` modify flags. They **do not**. Only arithmetic, logical, and compare instructions modify flags. (Loses 1 mark)
> 
> 4. **T-States vs Machine Cycles:** Confusing *T-states* (clock ticks) with *machine cycles* (fetch/decode phases). The 8085 has 4 T-states per machine cycle by default. (Loses 1 mark)
> 
> 5. **Mnemonic–Opcode Reversal:** Writing the mnemonic in the "opcode" column of a table. Always write the **hex code** as opcode. (Loses 1 mark)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist: Opcodes**
> 
> ✅ **Opcode = Operation Code** = the binary field that tells the CPU *what to do*.
> 
> ✅ **First byte of every 8085 instruction is the opcode.**
> 
> ✅ **Mnemonic** (e.g., `ADD B`) is the *human-readable* form; **Opcode** (e.g., `80H`) is the *machine-readable* form.
> 
> ✅ **8085 opcode width = 8 bits → 256 possible codes → ~246 defined instructions.**
> 
> ✅ **Instruction sizes in 8085:** 1-byte (`MOV`), 2-byte (`MVI`), 3-byte (`LXI`, `JMP`, `CALL`).
> 
> ✅ **Five functional groups:** Data Transfer, Arithmetic, Logical, Branch, Machine Control.
> 
> ✅ **Endianness:** 8085 uses **little-endian** for 16-bit operands (low byte first).
> 
> ✅ **Decoder hardware:** Opcode → Instruction Register → PLA Decoder → Control Unit micro-operations.
> 
> ✅ **Data transfer opcodes do NOT modify flags.** Only arithmetic/logical/compare opcodes modify the flag register.
> 
> ✅ **T-States per machine cycle = 4** (except for WAIT, HOLD, or special cycles).
> 
> ✅ **CISC processors (8085, 8086)** have variable-length opcodes; **RISC processors (ARM, RISC-V)** have fixed-length opcodes for faster pipelined decode.
> 
> ✅ **ARM Thumb-2 uses 16-bit opcodes** to halve code size vs. 32-bit ARM mode — critical for embedded Flash memory.
> 
> ✅ **Maximum opcodes formula:** $N = 2^W$ where $W$ = opcode width in bits.
> 
> ✅ **Program size formula:** $P = \sum S_i$ (sum of byte-lengths of all instructions).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hand Coding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET504 - MICROPROCESSORS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hand Coding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hand Coding** (also called **Hand Assembly** or **Manual Encoding**) is the technique of manually translating an assembly language program into its equivalent **machine code (object code in hex/binary)** by referring to the instruction set reference table of the microprocessor/microcontroller — *without the use of an assembler software*.

In the context of the **Intel 8051 family** (the canonical MCU for the KTU PBEET504 syllabus), every assembly mnemonic maps to a fixed **opcode byte**, with 0, 1, or 2 additional operand bytes appended. Hand coding demands that the programmer:
1. Knows the exact opcode for every mnemonic.
2. Assigns starting memory addresses to each instruction.
3. Computes the **byte size** of each instruction (1-byte, 2-byte, or 3-byte).
4. Calculates **relative jump offsets** (a two's complement 8-bit signed value) for branch instructions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** Hand coding directly supports the Course Outcome *CO1 — "Write assembly language programs for 8051 and explain the internal architecture"*. Board examiners frequently ask students to hand-code short programs and calculate the *code size in bytes* and *execution time in machine cycles*.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are writing a **letter in English** (assembly mnemonics) to a friend who **only understands Morse code** (the CPU's machine language). Normally, you would use a translation app (an *assembler*). **Hand coding is like translating the letter yourself letter-by-letter using a Morse code reference book.**

- The **mnemonic** `MOV A, #25H` is your English sentence.
- The **opcode `74 25`** is the Morse code your CPU "hears."
- The **opcode table** is your Morse code book.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why bother hand coding if assemblers exist?**
> 1. It forces deep understanding of instruction formats and addressing modes.
> 2. In resource-constrained embedded bootloaders, you may need to write tiny machine code routines by hand.
> 3. KTU examiners love it — it tests genuine understanding rather than IDE familiarity.

## 1.3 Standardized Metrics Used in Hand Coding

| Metric | 8051 Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Program memory (ROM) | **4 KB** (8051), expandable to 64 KB | Bytes |
| Instruction length | **1, 2, or 3 bytes** | Bytes |
| Program Counter size | **16-bit** (PC = 0000H to FFFFH) | Bits |
| Relative jump range (SJMP) | **−128 to +127** bytes from PC+2 | Bytes (signed) |
| Oscillator frequency (typical) | **11.0592 MHz** | MHz |
| Machine cycle period | **12 oscillator periods** = **1.085 µs** | Microseconds |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 The Anatomy of an 8051 Instruction

Every 8051 instruction consists of:

- **Byte 1 (Opcode byte):** Contains the operation code. For register-based instructions (e.g., `MOV A, R0`), the **lowest 3 bits** encode the register number `Rn` (n = 0–7).
- **Byte 2 (optional operand):** Direct address, 8-bit immediate data, or relative offset.
- **Byte 3 (optional operand):** High byte of a 16-bit address, or second operand byte.

### Instruction Length Classification

| Length | When Used | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **1-byte** | Operations on accumulator or implied addressing | `NOP`, `CPL A`, `INC A`, `RL A` |
| **2-byte** | 8-bit immediate data OR direct + 1 operand | `MOV A, #25H`, `MOV A, 30H`, `ADD A, #05H` |
| **3-byte** | 16-bit address OR direct,data pair | `LJMP 2000H`, `MOV 30H, #45H` |

## 2.2 The 8051 Opcode Encoding Logic (Register Operand)

For instructions of the form `OPCODE A, Rn` or `OPCODE Rn, A`, the 8051 uses a clever 3-bit field within the opcode to embed the register number. Example for `ADD A, Rn`:

$$\text{Opcode Byte} = \text{Base Opcode} + n$$

Where the base opcode for `ADD A, Rn` is `0x28`. So:
- `ADD A, R0` → `28H`
- `ADD A, R1` → `29H`
- ...
- `ADD A, R7` → `2FH`

The same rule applies to `MOV A, Rn` (base `E8H`), `MOV Rn, A` (base `F8H`), `INC Rn` (base `08H`), `DJNZ Rn, rel` (base `D8H`), and many others.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does 8051 do this?** It saves 1 byte per register instruction (no extra byte needed to specify the register). This was a brilliant design choice by Intel in 1980 to make 8051 code compact for embedded systems of that era.

## 2.3 The Relative Jump Offset Formula

For 2-byte branch instructions (e.g., `SJMP`, `JZ`, `JNC`, `DJNZ`), the second byte is a **signed 8-bit offset** (range −128 to +127) added to the current Program Counter. The PC used in the calculation is the address of the **next instruction** (PC after the branch instruction is fetched).

$$\text{Offset} = \text{Target Address} - (\text{Current PC} + 2)$$

Equivalently, since the branch instruction itself occupies 2 bytes:

$$\text{Offset} = \text{Target Address} - \text{Address of next instruction}$$

If the offset is **negative** (backward jump), the magnitude is stored in **two's complement form**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Students often forget that the PC after a 2-byte branch points to the instruction *after* the branch, not the branch itself. This causes off-by-2 errors in every manual offset calculation.

## 2.4 Critical Opcode Reference Table (Most Frequently Tested)

| Mnemonic | Opcode (Hex) | Bytes | Cycles |
|---|---|---|---|
| `NOP` | 00 | 1 | 1 |
| `AJMP addr11` | 01, 21, 41, 61, 81, A1, C1, E1 | 2 | 2 |
| `LJMP addr16` | 02 LL HH | 3 | 2 |
| `RR A` | 03 | 1 | 1 |
| `INC A` | 04 | 1 | 1 |
| `INC direct` | 05 | 2 | 1 |
| `INC @R0/@R1` | 06, 07 | 1 | 1 |
| `INC Rn` | 08–0F | 1 | 1 |
| `JBC bit,rel` | 10 | 3 | 2 |
| `ACALL addr11` | 11, 31, 51, 71, 91, B1, D1, F1 | 2 | 2 |
| `LCALL addr16` | 12 LL HH | 3 | 2 |
| `RRCA / RRC A` | 13 | 1 | 1 |
| `DEC A` | 14 | 1 | 1 |
| `DEC direct` | 15 | 2 | 1 |
| `DEC @R0/@R1` | 16, 17 | 1 | 1 |
| `DEC Rn` | 18–1F | 1 | 1 |
| `JB bit,rel` | 20 | 3 | 2 |
| `RET` | 22 | 1 | 2 |
| `RL A` | 23 | 1 | 1 |
| `ADD A,#data` | 24 | 2 | 1 |
| `ADD A,direct` | 25 | 2 | 1 |
| `ADD A,@R0/@R1` | 26, 27 | 1 | 1 |
| `ADD A,Rn` | 28–2F | 1 | 1 |
| `JNB bit,rel` | 30 | 3 | 2 |
| `RETI` | 32 | 1 | 2 |
| `RLC A` | 33 | 1 | 1 |
| `JC rel` | 40 | 2 | 2 |
| `ORL direct,A` | 42 | 2 | 1 |
| `ORL direct,#data` | 43 | 3 | 2 |
| `ORL A,#data` | 44 | 2 | 1 |
| `ORL A,direct` | 45 | 2 | 1 |
| `ORL A,@R0/@R1` | 46, 47 | 1 | 1 |
| `ORL A,Rn` | 48–4F | 1 | 1 |
| `JNC rel` | 50 | 2 | 2 |
| `ANL direct,A` | 52 | 2 | 1 |
| `ANL direct,#data` | 53 | 3 | 2 |
| `ANL A,#data` | 54 | 2 | 1 |
| `ANL A,direct` | 55 | 2 | 1 |
| `ANL A,@R0/@R1` | 56, 57 | 1 | 1 |
| `ANL A,Rn` | 58–5F | 1 | 1 |
| `JZ rel` | 60 | 2 | 2 |
| `XRL direct,A` | 62 | 2 | 1 |
| `XRL direct,#data` | 63 | 3 | 2 |
| `XRL A,#data` | 64 | 2 | 1 |
| `XRL A,direct` | 65 | 2 | 1 |
| `XRL A,@R0/@R1` | 66, 67 | 1 | 1 |
| `XRL A,Rn` | 68–6F | 1 | 1 |
| `JNZ rel` | 70 | 2 | 2 |
| `ORL C,bit` | 72 | 2 | 2 |
| `MOV A,#data` | 74 | 2 | 1 |
| `MOV direct,#data` | 75 | 3 | 2 |
| `MOV @R0/@R1,#data` | 76, 77 | 2 | 1 |
| `MOV Rn,#data` | 78–7F | 2 | 1 |
| `SJMP rel` | 80 | 2 | 2 |
| `ANL C,bit` | 82 | 2 | 2 |
| `MOVC A,@A+DPTR` | 93 | 1 | 2 |
| `MOVC A,@A+PC` | 83 | 1 | 2 |
| `DIV AB` | 84 | 1 | 4 |
| `MOV direct,direct` | 85 | 3 | 2 |
| `MOV direct,@R0/@R1` | 86, 87 | 2 | 2 |
| `MOV direct,Rn` | 88–8F | 2 | 2 |
| `MOV DPTR,#data16` | 90 LL HH | 3 | 2 |
| `MOV bit,C` | 92 | 2 | 2 |
| `SUBB A,Rn` | 98–9F | 1 | 1 |
| `MOV C,bit` | A2 | 2 | 1 |
| `INC DPTR` | A3 | 1 | 2 |
| `MOV @R0/@R1,direct` | A6, A7 | 2 | 2 |
| `MOV Rn,direct` | A8–AF | 2 | 2 |
| `XCHD A,@R0/@R1` | D6, D7 | 1 | 1 |
| `DJNZ direct,rel` | D5 | 3 | 2 |
| `XCH A,@R0/@R1` | C6, C7 | 1 | 1 |
| `XCH A,Rn` | C8–CF | 1 | 1 |
| `CLR bit` | C2 | 2 | 1 |
| `CLR C` | C3 | 1 | 1 |
| `SWAP A` | C4 | 1 | 1 |

| `CPL C` | B3 | 1 | 1 |
| `CJNE @R0/@R1,#data,rel` | B6, B7 | 3 | 2 |
| `CJNE Rn,#data,rel` | B8–BF | 3 | 2 |
| `POP direct` | D0 | 2 | 2 |
| `SETB C` | D3 | 1 | 1 |
| `SETB bit` | D2 | 2 | 1 |

| `DJNZ Rn,rel` | D8–DF | 2 | 2 |
| `MOVX A,@R0/@R1` | E2, E3 | 1 | 2 |
| `CLR A` | E4 | 1 | 1 |
| `MOV A,direct` | E5 | 2 | 1 |
| `MOV A,@R0/@R1` | E6, E7 | 1 | 1 |
| `MOV A,Rn` | E8–EF | 1 | 1 |
| `MOVX A,@DPTR` | E0 | 1 | 2 |
| `PUSH direct` | C0 | 2 | 2 |
| `MOVX @R0/@R1,A` | F2, F3 | 1 | 2 |
| `CPL A` | F4 | 1 | 1 |
| `MOV direct,A` | F5 | 2 | 1 |
| `MOV @R0/@R1,A` | F6, F7 | 1 | 1 |
| `MOV Rn,A` | F8–FF | 1 | 1 |
| `MOVX @DPTR,A` | F0 | 1 | 2 |
| `MUL AB` | A4 | 1 | 4 |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hand coding is used in:
- **Bootloader development** for custom MCUs.
- **Reverse engineering** of firmware (modifying binary images).
- **Real-time ISR routines** where exact cycle counting is critical.
- **Smart card / SIM card programming** where space is limited to a few hundred bytes.
- **Bug fix patches** shipped as raw binary "hotfix" hex records.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Worked Examples (Hand Coding Walkthroughs)

## 3.1 Example 1 — Simple Register-to-Register Move (Trivial Warm-Up)

**Assembly program:**
```assembly
        MOV  A, R5       ; Copy R5 into Accumulator
        MOV  R3, A       ; Copy Accumulator into R3
        END
```

### Step 1: Address Assignment

| Line | Instruction | Address |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `MOV A, R5` | 2000H |
| 2 | `MOV R3, A` | 2001H |

### Step 2: Look Up Opcodes

From the opcode table:
- `MOV A, Rn` → base opcode `E8H`, with `+n`. So `MOV A, R5` → `E8 + 5 = EDH`.
- `MOV Rn, A` → base opcode `F8H`, with `+n`. So `MOV R3, A` → `F8 + 3 = FBH`.

### Step 3: Final Machine Code

| Address | Hex Code | Size |
|---|---|---|
| 2000H | `ED` | 1 byte |
| 2001H | `FB` | 1 byte |

**Total code size: 2 bytes. Total cycles: 2.**

---
