## 3.2 Example 2 — Addition of Two 8-bit Numbers (Classic KTU Question)

**Assembly program (start at 2000H):**
```assembly
        ORG  2000H
        MOV  A, #25H     ; Load A with 25H
        ADD  A, #34H     ; A = A + 34H
        MOV  30H, A      ; Store result at RAM 30H
        END
```

### Step 1: Address Assignment

| Line | Instruction | Bytes | Address Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `MOV A, #25H` | 2 | 2000H – 2001H |
| 2 | `ADD A, #34H` | 2 | 2002H – 2003H |
| 3 | `MOV 30H, A` | 2 | 2004H – 2005H |

### Step 2: Opcode Lookup
- `MOV A, #data` → `74`, then data byte → `74 25`
- `ADD A, #data` → `24`, then data byte → `24 34`
- `MOV direct, A` → `F5`, then direct address → `F5 30`

### Step 3: Final Machine Code Table

| Address | Code | Instruction |
|---|---|---|
| 2000H | `74` | `MOV A, #25H` |
| 2001H | `25` | (operand) |
| 2002H | `24` | `ADD A, #34H` |
| 2003H | `34` | (operand) |
| 2004H | `F5` | `MOV 30H, A` |
| 2005H | `30` | (operand) |

**Total code size: 6 bytes. Total cycles: 3.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering relevance:** This 6-byte program performs the most fundamental operation in any embedded controller — adding two constants and storing the result. The same pattern is the building block for digital filters, sensor calibrations, and control loops.

---

## 3.3 Example 3 — Looping with DJNZ (Relative Offset Mastery)

**Assembly program (start at 2000H):**
```assembly
        ORG  2000H
        MOV  R2, #05H    ; Initialize counter = 5
AGAIN:  MOV  A, #01H     ; Load A = 1
        INC  A           ; Increment A
        DJNZ R2, AGAIN   ; Decrement R2; if not zero, jump to AGAIN
HERE:   SJMP HERE        ; Infinite loop (halt)
```

### Step 1: Address Assignment

| Line | Instruction | Bytes | Address |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `MOV R2, #05H` | 2 | 2000H – 2001H |
| 2 | `MOV A, #01H` | 2 | 2002H – 2003H |
| 3 | `INC A` | 1 | 2004H |
| 4 | `DJNZ R2, AGAIN` | 2 | 2005H – 2006H |
| 5 | `SJMP HERE` | 2 | 2007H – 2008H |

### Step 2: Opcode Lookup
- `MOV Rn, #data` → base `78+n`. For R2: `7A`. → `7A 05`
- `MOV A, #data` → `74 01`
- `INC A` → `04`
- `DJNZ Rn, rel` → base `D8+n`. For R2: `DA`. The second byte is the **relative offset**.
- `SJMP rel` → `80`, then offset.

### Step 3: Calculate Relative Offsets

**Offset for `DJNZ R2, AGAIN` (at 2005H, target = 2002H):**

$$\text{Offset} = \text{Target} - (\text{PC after fetch}) = 2002\text{H} - 2007\text{H} = -05\text{H}$$

Convert −05H to 8-bit two's complement:

$$-5 \equiv 256 - 5 = 251 = \text{FBH}$$

So the relative byte is `FB`.

**Offset for `SJMP HERE` (at 2007H, target = 2007H):**

$$\text{Offset} = 2007\text{H} - (2007\text{H} + 2) = -02\text{H}$$

Two's complement: `256 - 2 = 254 = FEH`.

### Step 4: Final Machine Code Table

| Address | Code | Instruction |
|---|---|---|
| 2000H | `7A` | `MOV R2, #05H` |
| 2001H | `05` |  |
| 2002H | `74` | `MOV A, #01H` |
| 2003H | `01` |  |
| 2004H | `04` | `INC A` |
| 2005H | `DA` | `DJNZ R2, AGAIN` |
| 2006H | `FB` | (offset = −5) |
| 2007H | `80` | `SJMP HERE` |
| 2008H | `FE` | (offset = −2) |

**Total code size: 9 bytes.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write `+05H` for the DJNZ offset instead of `FBH`, forgetting that the target is *behind* the branch. Always draw a vertical arrow on paper and check the direction!

---

## 3.4 Example 4 — Time Delay Calculation (Exam Favorite)

**Assembly program:**
```assembly
        ORG  2000H
DELAY:  MOV  R2, #0FFH   ; Outer counter
LOOP1:  MOV  R1, #0FFH   ; Inner counter
LOOP2:  DJNZ R1, LOOP2
        DJNZ R2, LOOP1
        RET
```

### Step 1: Address Table

| Instruction | Address | Bytes |
|---|---|---|
| `MOV R2, #FFH` | 2000H | 2 |
| `MOV R1, #FFH` | 2002H | 2 |
| `DJNZ R1, LOOP2` | 2004H | 2 |
| `DJNZ R2, LOOP1` | 2006H | 2 |
| `RET` | 2008H | 1 |

### Step 2: Hand Code

| Address | Code |
|---|---|
| 2000H | `7A FF` |
| 2002H | `79 FF` |
| 2004H | `D9 FB` (offset = 2004H − 2006H = −2 = FEH) |
| 2006H | `DA FA` (offset = 2002H − 2008H = −6 = FAH) |
| 2008H | `22` |

### Step 3: Time Delay Calculation

For an 8051 with **11.0592 MHz crystal**, one machine cycle = **12 / 11.0592 MHz = 1.085 µs**.

- Inner loop `DJNZ R1, LOOP2` executes 255 times × 2 cycles = 510 cycles.
- Outer loop: 255 × (510 + 2) + 2 = **130,562 machine cycles**.
- Total time = 130,562 × 1.085 µs ≈ **141.66 ms**.

> [!NOTE]
> This exact pattern is the textbook "**software delay subroutine**" used in 8051 labs to create millisecond delays without hardware timers.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Hand Coding Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Assembly Mnemonic"] --> B["Step 1: Identify Addressing Mode"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Look up Opcode in Instruction Set Table"]
    C --> D["Step 3: Append Operand Bytes if required"]
    D --> E{"Is it a Branch or Jump?"}
    E -- "Yes" --> F["Step 4: Compute Relative Offset or 16-bit Address"]
    E -- "No" --> G["Step 5: Write Final Hex Bytes to Memory"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Step 6: Increment PC by Instruction Size"]
    H --> I{"More Instructions?"}
    I -- "Yes" --> B
    I -- "No" --> J["End: Complete Machine Code Listing"]
```

## 4.2 Instruction Format Architecture (8051)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph byte1["Byte 1 - Opcode"]
        opA[Opcode MSB] --> opB[Opcode Middle]
        opB --> opC[Opcode LSB / Register Field n]
    end
    subgraph byte2["Byte 2 - Operand 1 (optional)"]
        opD["Direct Address / 8-bit Data / Relative Offset"]
    end
    subgraph byte3["Byte 3 - Operand 2 (optional)"]
        opE["High Byte of 16-bit Address or Data"]
    end
    byte1 --> byte2 --> byte3
```

## 4.3 Register Operand Encoding Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Base Opcode e.g. 28H for ADD A,Rn"] --> B["Add n from 0 to 7"]
    B --> C["Final Opcode Byte"]
    C --> D1["28H = ADD A,R0"]
    C --> D2["29H = ADD A,R1"]
    C --> D3["2AH = ADD A,R2"]
    C --> D4["2BH = ADD A,R3"]
    C --> D5["2CH = ADD A,R4"]
    C --> D6["2DH = ADD A,R5"]
    C --> D7["2EH = ADD A,R6"]
    C --> D8["2FH = ADD A,R7"]
```

## 4.4 Memory Layout After Hand Coding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["2000H: 74"] --> P2["2001H: 25"] --> P3["2002H: 24"] --> P4["2003H: 34"] --> P5["2004H: F5"] --> P6["2005H: 30"] --> P7["2006H: 80"] --> P8["2007H: FE"] --> P9["2008H: 22"]
    P1 -.MOV A,25H.-> P2
    P2 -.ADD A,34H.-> P3
    P3 -.MOV 30H,A.-> P4
    P4 -.SJMP.-> P5
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Hand Coding. Mention any two advantages. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Model Answer:**
Hand coding is the manual process of translating assembly language mnemonics into their corresponding machine code (hex) by directly using the instruction set reference, without the help of an assembler.

**Two advantages:**
1. It provides a deep understanding of the processor's instruction format, addressing modes, and memory addressing.
2. It generates highly compact and optimized code (no assembler overhead), useful in bootloaders and size-critical embedded firmware.

### Question 2: What is the role of the Program Counter (PC) in calculating relative jump offsets for the 8051? [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**
The 16-bit Program Counter (PC) holds the address of the next instruction to be fetched. For a 2-byte relative branch instruction located at address `X`, the CPU uses `PC = X + 2` (the address *after* the branch) as the base for offset computation:

$$\text{Offset} = \text{Target Address} - (X + 2)$$

Since the offset is an 8-bit signed two's complement, the range is −128 to +127 bytes.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Hand Coding:**
> - **Off-by-2 in offsets:** Forgetting that PC points to the *next* instruction after the branch is fetched. This is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks.
> - **Wrong byte ordering for LJMP/LCALL:** Writing high byte first instead of low byte first. The 8051 stores 16-bit data in **little-endian** order (low byte first, high byte second).
> - **Wrong register field:** Forgetting to add `n` to the base opcode for `MOV Rn`, `ADD Rn`, `INC Rn`, etc.
> - **Mis-identifying addressing mode:** Confusing `MOV A, #25H` (immediate) with `MOV A, 25H` (direct) — opcodes are `74 25` versus `E5 25`.
> - **Calculation of program size:** Many students forget to sum the byte sizes for the final answer.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Hand-Code an Arithmetic Program

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**Hand-code the following 8051 assembly program starting at location `2000H`. Calculate (a) the total number of bytes, and (b) the total number of machine cycles consumed.**

```assembly
        ORG  2000H
START:  MOV  A, #0AH      ; A = 10
        ADD  A, #15H      ; A = A + 21
        MOV  R0, A        ; R0 = A
        MOV  A, #00H      ; Clear A
        ADD  A, R0        ; A = A + R0
        MOV  40H, A       ; Store in RAM
        END
```

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1: Address allocation and instruction size:**

| # | Instruction | Bytes | Start Address | End Address |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `MOV A, #0AH` | 2 | 2000H | 2001H |
| 2 | `ADD A, #15H` | 2 | 2002H | 2003H |
| 3 | `MOV R0, A` | 1 | 2004H | 2004H |
| 4 | `MOV A, #00H` | 2 | 2005H | 2006H |
| 5 | `ADD A, R0` | 1 | 2007H | 2007H |
| 6 | `MOV 40H, A` | 2 | 2008H | 2009H |

**Step 2: Opcode lookup and hand coding:**

| Address | Opcode | Operand | Final Bytes | Instruction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2000H | 74 | 0A | `74 0A` | `MOV A, #0AH` |
| 2002H | 24 | 15 | `24 15` | `ADD A, #15H` |
| 2004H | F8 (F8+0) | — | `F8` | `MOV R0, A` |
| 2005H | 74 | 00 | `74 00` | `MOV A, #00H` |
| 2007H | 28 (28+0) | — | `28` | `ADD A, R0` |
| 2008H | F5 | 40 | `F5 40` | `MOV 40H, A` |

**Step 3: Final memory map:**

```
2000 : 74 0A 24 15 F8 74 00 28 F5 40
```

**Step 4: Calculations:**
- **Total program size = 2 + 2 + 1 + 2 + 1 + 2 = 10 bytes**
- **Total machine cycles = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 6 cycles**
- **Execution time @ 11.0592 MHz = 6 × 1.085 µs = 6.51 µs**

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Hand-Code a Loop-Based Program with Relative Offset

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Hand-code the following 8051 assembly program that adds 5 to the accumulator ten times using a loop. Start the program at `2000H`. Compute the final program size, and show all offset calculations explicitly.**

```assembly
        ORG  2000H
        MOV  A, #00H
        MOV  R1, #0AH
LOOP:   ADD  A, #05H
        DJNZ R1, LOOP
END_LOOP: SJMP END_LOOP
```

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1: Address allocation:**

| # | Instruction | Bytes | Start Address | End Address |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `MOV A, #00H` | 2 | 2000H | 2001H |
| 2 | `MOV R1, #0AH` | 2 | 2002H | 2003H |
| 3 | `ADD A, #05H` (LOOP) | 2 | 2004H | 2005H |
| 4 | `DJNZ R1, LOOP` | 2 | 2006H | 2007H |
| 5 | `SJMP END_LOOP` | 2 | 2008H | 2009H |

**Step 2: Opcode lookup:**

- `MOV A, #00H` → `74 00`
- `MOV R1, #0AH` → base `78+1=79`, so `79 0A`
- `ADD A, #05H` → `24 05`
- `DJNZ R1, LOOP` → base `D8+1=D9`. Offset = 2004H − (2006H + 2) = 2004H − 2008H = −4 → 8-bit two's complement = `FCH`. → `D9 FC`
- `SJMP END_LOOP` → `80`. Offset = 2008H − (2008H + 2) = −2 → `FEH`. → `80 FE`

**Step 3: Final memory map:**

```
2000 : 74 00 79 0A 24 05 D9 FC 80 FE
```

**Step 4: Verifying offsets:**

$$\text{DJNZ offset} = 2004\text{H} - 2008\text{H} = -04\text{H} = \text{FCH} \quad \checkmark$$

$$\text{SJMP offset} = 2008\text{H} - 200\text{AH} = -02\text{H} = \text{FEH} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5: Calculations:**
- **Total program size = 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 10 bytes**
- **Total machine cycles (body executed 10 times + 1 fallback) = (1+1+1+2)×10 + 1 + 2 = 55 + 3 = 58 cycles**
- **Final accumulator value = 10 × 5 = 32 (0x20) — confirms program logic.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & High-Yield Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets for Hand Coding (8051):**

- ✦ Hand coding = manually mapping assembly mnemonics to hex opcodes via the instruction set table.
- ✦ 8051 instructions are **1, 2, or 3 bytes** long.
- ✦ For `OPCODE A, Rn` instructions, **add `n` (0–7)** to the base opcode byte.
- ✦ Base opcodes to memorize: `MOV A,Rn = E8`, `MOV Rn,A = F8`, `ADD A,Rn = 28`, `INC Rn = 08`, `DJNZ Rn,rel = D8`, `DEC Rn = 18`, `SUBB A,Rn = 98`, `CJNE Rn,#data,rel = B8`, `XCH A,Rn = C8`, `ORL A,Rn = 48`, `ANL A,Rn = 58`, `XRL A,Rn = 68`.
- ✦ **16-bit data is stored little-endian** (low byte first) in 8051 program memory.
- ✦ Relative offset formula: `Offset = Target Address − (Address of branch + 2)`.
- ✦ Negative offsets → convert to **two's complement** in 8 bits (e.g., −5 → FBH).
- ✦ `SJMP` range: −128 to +127 bytes. `LJMP` range: full 64 KB.
- ✦ `AJMP` and `ACALL` use an **11-bit address** within the current 2 KB page; they are 2 bytes.
- ✦ Always **tabulate addresses, opcodes, and operand bytes** side-by-side — examiners reward structured answers.
- ✦ `MOV A, #25H` = `74 25` (immediate); `MOV A, 25H` = `E5 25` (direct). The address `25H` and the data `25H` are *different concepts*.
- ✦ Common code-size traps: forgetting that `MOV direct, #data` is 3 bytes; `LCALL`/`LJMP` is 3 bytes; `CJNE` is 3 bytes.
- ✦ Machine cycle time = `12 / Crystal Frequency`. For 11.0592 MHz crystal → **1.085 µs** per cycle.
- ✦ Always verify the **loop count** and **final accumulator value** as a sanity check after hand coding.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programs Involving 8 and 16-Bit Addition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET504 - MICROPROCESSORS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Programs Involving 8 and 16-Bit Addition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — Programs Involving 8-Bit and 16-Bit Addition

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the **Intel 8086 microprocessor architecture**, *addition* is performed as a **binary arithmetic operation** on operands of size **8 bits (1 byte)** or **16 bits (1 word)** using the dedicated instruction set of the Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU). The instruction set provides three principal addition operations:

- `ADD` — Performs binary addition and stores the result in the **destination operand**.
- `ADC` — Performs binary addition **including the Carry Flag (CF)** from a previous stage. This is essential for **multi-precision (multi-byte / multi-word)** arithmetic.
- `INC` — Increments an operand by 1 (special case of addition, no CF effect).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Programs involving 8-bit and 16-bit addition form the **foundational algorithmic building block** of Module 1. Mastery of flag manipulation (especially **CF, OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF**) is a *mandatory* learning outcome mapped to **CO1: Apply the architecture and instruction set of 8086 microprocessor for assembly language programming.**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coin Box" Model

Imagine you are stacking coins into a piggy bank:
- **8-bit addition** is like adding coins worth up to **255** in a *small* box. If the total exceeds 255, the box cannot hold it — the *overflow coin* spills out, and we record this event as a **Carry Flag (CF) = 1**.
- **16-bit addition** is like a *bigger* box that can hold up to **65,535** in one go. But if you have *two big boxes* (e.g., a 32-bit number) you add the low box, remember the spillover, and then add the high box **plus the spillover** — this is exactly what `ADC` does.
- **BCD (Binary Coded Decimal) addition** is like adding coin rolls in *decimal* — if a column sum exceeds 9, you must "carry 1 to the next decimal column" using a hardware helper called `DAA` (Decimal Adjust after Addition).

This *spillover-and-record* mental model is the heart of how the 8086 performs multi-precision arithmetic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Every Student Must Memorize:**
> - **8-bit unsigned range:** 0 to 255 (2⁸ − 1)
> - **8-bit signed range:** −128 to +127 (two's complement)
> - **16-bit unsigned range:** 0 to 65,535 (2¹⁶ − 1)
> - **16-bit signed range:** −32,768 to +32,767
> - **Maximum decimal value for packed BCD (2 digits):** 99

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8086 Addition Instruction Set — Structured Logic

### 2.1.1 `ADD Destination, Source`
- **Operation:** `Destination ← Destination + Source`
- **Operands allowed:** Register–Register, Register–Memory, Memory–Register, Register–Immediate, Memory–Immediate.
- **Size variants:** 8-bit and 16-bit.
- **Flags affected:** **CF, OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF** (all six arithmetic flags).
- **Use case:** Single-precision (8-bit or 16-bit) addition.

### 2.1.2 `ADC Destination, Source`
- **Operation:** `Destination ← Destination + Source + CF`
- **Operands allowed:** Same as ADD.
- **Size variants:** 8-bit and 16-bit.
- **Flags affected:** **CF, OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF**.
- **Use case:** Multi-precision arithmetic — adding numbers wider than 16 bits by chaining lower-order and higher-order additions through the carry.

### 2.1.3 `INC Destination`
- **Operation:** `Destination ← Destination + 1`.
- **Operands allowed:** Register or Memory only.
- **Size variants:** 8-bit and 16-bit.
- **Flags affected:** **OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF** (***CF is NOT affected*** — a classic KTU trick question).
- **Use case:** Loop counters, pointer increments.

### 2.1.4 `DAA` (Decimal Adjust AL after Addition)
- **Operation:** Corrects the result in `AL` after a BCD addition so that the result is a valid packed BCD number.
- **Operands:** None (operates implicitly on AL).
- **Flags affected:** **CF, AF, SF, ZF, PF** (OF undefined).
- **Use case:** Packed BCD arithmetic.

### 2.1.5 `AAA` (ASCII Adjust after Addition)
- **Operation:** Adjusts the result in AL after an ASCII (unpacked BCD) addition.
- **Use case:** Adding ASCII digits `'0'…'9'`.

### 2.2 The Six Arithmetic Flags — Why They Exist

| Flag | Full Name | Meaning | When Set = 1 |
|------|-----------|---------|--------------|
| **CF** | Carry Flag | Unsigned overflow out of the MSB | Result > 255 (8-bit) or > 65535 (16-bit) |
| **OF** | Overflow Flag | Signed overflow into the sign bit | Sign bit corrupted; operands have same sign but result sign differs |
| **SF** | Sign Flag | MSB of result | Result is negative (in two's complement sense) |
| **ZF** | Zero Flag | Result is zero | Result = 0 |
| **AF** | Auxiliary Flag | Carry from bit 3 to bit 4 (lower nibble) | Used internally by `DAA` for BCD correction |
| **PF** | Parity Flag | Even parity of low 8 bits of result | Even number of 1-bits in low byte |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Examiners love asking *"Which flag is NOT affected by INC?"* — the answer is **CF (Carry Flag)**. INC affects OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF but leaves CF untouched. This subtle distinction often costs careless students 1–2 marks.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Units / Range |
|---------|-----------------|---------------|
| 8-bit unsigned maximum | $2^{8} - 1 = 255$ | decimal |
| 16-bit unsigned maximum | $2^{16} - 1 = 65{,}535$ | decimal |
| N-bit unsigned range | $0$ to $2^{N} - 1$ | decimal |
| N-bit signed range | $-2^{N-1}$ to $2^{N-1} - 1$ | decimal |
| Carry detection (8-bit) | $\text{CF} = 1$ if $\text{sum} > \text{FF}_{16}$ | boolean |
| Carry detection (16-bit) | $\text{CF} = 1$ if $\text{sum} > \text{FFFF}_{16}$ | boolean |
| Signed overflow (OF) rule | $\text{OF} = 1$ iff *operands have same sign* **and** *result has different sign* | boolean |
| BCD correction value (lower nibble) | $+6$ if lower nibble $> 9$ or $\text{AF} = 1$ | hexadecimal |
| BCD correction value (upper nibble) | $+60_{16}$ if upper nibble $> 9$ or $\text{CF} = 1$ | hexadecimal |
| Packed BCD digits per byte | $2$ digits (0–9 each) | decimal |
| Unpacked BCD digits per byte | $1$ digit (in lower nibble) | decimal |
| Multi-precision carry propagation | $\text{byte}_{i+1} \leftarrow \text{byte}_{i+1} + \text{CF}$ from stage $i$ | boolean |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Multi-precision addition is the workhorse of:
- **DSP (Digital Signal Processing)** — 32-bit / 64-bit accumulator arithmetic.
- **Cryptographic algorithms** — Big-integer modular arithmetic in RSA, ECC.
- **Embedded financial systems** — BCD arithmetic for currency calculations (COBOL heritage still alive in banking chips).
- **Industrial PLCs** — Scaling 12-bit / 16-bit ADC readings into engineering units.
- **IoT sensor fusion** — Accumulating 24-bit / 32-bit ADC samples from temperature, pressure, and accelerometer sensors.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Conventions used in all programs below:**
> - 8086 assembly syntax (MASM/TASM compatible).
> - `SEGMENT` / `ENDS` directives; `ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA`.
> - Programs terminate via the DOS `INT 21H` function `4CH`.
> - Each program is annotated with a **Trace Table** showing register/flag evolution.

---

## Program 1 — 8-Bit Addition of Two Numbers (No Carry, Result < 256)

**Problem Statement:** Add two 8-bit numbers stored in memory and store the sum in a third memory location. Display the result using DOS interrupt.

**Data:** `NUM1 = 45H, NUM2 = 32H` ⇒ Expected sum = `77H`.

### 3.1 Algorithm (Structured Steps)

1. Initialize the data segment.
2. Load `NUM1` into `AL`.
3. Add `NUM2` to `AL` using `ADD`.
4. Store the result in `SUM`.
5. Terminate the program.

### 3.2 Complete 8086 Assembly Program

```asm
ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA

DATA SEGMENT
    NUM1  DB  45H
    NUM2  DB  32H
    SUM   DB  ?
DATA ENDS

CODE SEGMENT
START:
    MOV  AX, @DATA
    MOV  DS, AX

    MOV  AL, NUM1      ; AL <- 45H
    ADD  AL, NUM2      ; AL <- 45H + 32H = 77H, CF=0
    MOV  SUM, AL       ; SUM <- 77H

    MOV  AH, 4CH
    INT  21H           ; Return to DOS
CODE ENDS
END START
```

### 3.3 Trace Table

| Step | Instruction | AL | CF | OF | SF | ZF | AF | PF | Memory SUM |
|------|-------------|----|----|----|----|----|----|----|------------|
| 1 | `MOV AL, NUM1` | 45H | ? | ? | ? | ? | ? | ? | ?? |
| 2 | `ADD AL, NUM2` | 77H | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | ?? |
| 3 | `MOV SUM, AL` | 77H | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 77H |

> [!NOTE]
> **Parity Flag (PF) is 1** because `77H = 0111 0111` has **6 ones** (even parity).

---

## Program 2 — 8-Bit Addition of Two Numbers (WITH Carry Propagation)

**Problem Statement:** Add two 8-bit numbers `F0H + 1EH`. Result exceeds 255. Store the lower 8 bits in `SUM` and the carry in a separate byte `CARRY`.

**Expected:** `F0H + 1EH = 10EH` ⇒ `SUM = 0EH`, `CARRY = 01H`.

### 3.2 Program

```asm
ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA

DATA SEGMENT
    NUM1   DB  0F0H
    NUM2   DB  0E1H
    SUM    DB  ?
    CARRY  DB  ?
DATA ENDS

CODE SEGMENT
START:
    MOV  AX, @DATA
    MOV  DS, AX
    CLC                  ; CF <- 0 (initialize carry)
    MOV  AL, NUM1
    ADD  AL, NUM2        ; AL <- D1H, CF=1, OF=0
    MOV  SUM, AL
    MOV  AL, 00H
    ADC  AL, 00H         ; AL <- 00H + 00H + CF = 01H
    MOV  CARRY, AL
    MOV  AH, 4CH
    INT  21H
CODE ENDS
END START
```

### 3.3 Trace Table

| Step | Instruction | AL | CF | Note |
|------|-------------|----|----|------|
| 1 | `MOV AL, NUM1` | F0H | 0 | Load first operand |
| 2 | `ADD AL, NUM2` | D1H | 1 | `F0H + E1H = 1D1H` → result D1H, carry 1 |
| 3 | `MOV SUM, AL` | D1H | 1 | Store low byte |
| 4 | `ADC AL, 00H` | 01H | 0 | `00 + 00 + 1 = 01` |
| 5 | `MOV CARRY, AL` | 01H | 0 | Store carry byte |

---

## Program 3 — 16-Bit Addition of Two Numbers

**Problem Statement:** Add two 16-bit numbers `1234H + 5678H`. Store the 16-bit result in a word location `SUM`.

**Expected:** `1234H + 5678H = 68ACH`.

### 3.1 Program

```asm
ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA

DATA SEGMENT
    NUM1  DW  1234H
    NUM2  DW  5678H
    SUM   DW  ?
DATA ENDS

CODE SEGMENT
START:
    MOV  AX, @DATA
    MOV  DS, AX
    MOV  AX, NUM1       ; AX <- 1234H
    ADD  AX, NUM2       ; AX <- 68ACH, CF=0
    MOV  SUM, AX
    MOV  AH, 4CH
    INT  21H
CODE ENDS
END START
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept:** In 8086, **only 16-bit registers** (`AX, BX, CX, DX, SI, DI, BP, SP`) can hold 16-bit operands directly. `AL` and `AH` are the two 8-bit halves of `AX`. The 8086 **cannot** add an 8-bit number to a 16-bit number in a single instruction — you must first zero-extend or sign-extend the 8-bit value into a 16-bit register.

---

## Program 4 — 16-Bit Addition With Carry → 32-Bit Result (Multi-Precision)

**Problem Statement:** Add two 32-bit numbers `12345678H + 9ABCDEF0H` stored in memory as two 16-bit words each. Store the 32-bit result.

**Mathematical Verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Operand 1 (low word)} &= 5678_{16} \\
\text{Operand 2 (low word)} &= DEF0_{16} \\
\text{Low result} &= 5678_{16} + DEF0_{16} = 1\,3568_{16} \Rightarrow \text{word} = 3568_{16},\ \text{CF} = 1 \\
\\
\text{Operand 1 (high word)} &= 1234_{16} \\
\text{Operand 2 (high word)} &= 9ABC_{16} \\
\text{High result} &= 1234_{16} + 9ABC_{16} + \text{CF} \\
&= 1234_{16} + 9ABC_{16} + 1 = \text{ACF1}_{16}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final 32-bit result:** `ACF1 3568H`.

### 3.1 Program (Little-Endian Memory Layout)

```asm
ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA

DATA SEGMENT
    NUM1L  DW  5678H       ; Low word of 32-bit number 1
    NUM1H  DW  1234H       ; High word of 32-bit number 1
    NUM2L  DW  DEF0H       ; Low word of 32-bit number 2
    NUM2H  DW  9ABCH       ; High word of 32-bit number 2
    RESL   DW  ?           ; Low word of 32-bit result
    RESH   DW  ?           ; High word of 32-bit result
DATA ENDS

CODE SEGMENT
START:
    MOV  AX, @DATA
    MOV  DS, AX
    CLC                    ; Initialize CF = 0
    MOV  AX, NUM1L
    ADD  AX, NUM2L         ; Low-word add
    MOV  RESL, AX          ; Store low-word result
    MOV  AX, NUM1H
    ADC  AX, NUM2H         ; High-word add WITH carry
    MOV  RESH, AX          ; Store high-word result
    MOV  AH, 4CH
    INT  21H
CODE ENDS
END START
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Little-Endian Rule:** Intel 8086 stores the **least significant byte first**. So `DW 12345678H` is stored in memory as `78H, 56H, 34H, 12H`. This is why we declare **low word first** in `DATA SEGMENT` for multi-word operands — it matches the natural memory layout.

---

## Program 5 — Sum of an Array of N 8-Bit Numbers

**Problem Statement:** Compute the sum of `N` 8-bit numbers stored in an array. The sum can be up to 16 bits (because N numbers, each up to 255, can overflow a single byte).

### 3.1 Algorithm

1. Initialize `CX = N` (counter) and `SI = OFFSET ARRAY`.
2. Initialize `AX = 0` (16-bit accumulator) and `BX = 0` (running carry).
3. Loop: load byte at `[SI]`, add to `BX`, propagate any overflow to `AX` via `ADC`, increment `SI`, decrement `CX`, repeat until `CX = 0`.
4. Store `BX` as low byte of sum, `AX` as high byte.

### 3.2 Program

```asm
ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA

DATA SEGMENT
    N       DW  5
    ARRAY   DB  25H, 64H, 8AH, C0H, 1FH
    SUM     DW  ?          ; 16-bit sum
DATA ENDS

CODE SEGMENT
START:
    MOV  AX, @DATA
    MOV  DS, AX
    MOV  CX, N
    MOV  SI, OFFSET ARRAY
    MOV  BX, 0            ; BX = low 16-bit accumulator
    XOR  AX, AX           ; AX = 0 (high accumulator)
    CLC

AGAIN:
    MOV  AL, [SI]         ; Load current array byte
    ADD  BX, AX           ; Add to low accumulator
    JNC  SKIP             ; If no carry, skip propagation
    INC  AH               ; AH <- AH + 1 (propagate carry into AX)
SKIP:
    INC  SI
    LOOP AGAIN

    MOV  SUM, BX
    MOV  AH, 4CH
    INT  21H
CODE ENDS
END START
```

### 3.3 Hand Verification

| i | ARRAY[i] | BX before | BX after | CF | AX (carry-accum) |
|---|----------|-----------|----------|----|------------------|
| 1 | 25H | 0000H | 0025H | 0 | 0000H |
| 2 | 64H | 0025H | 0089H | 0 | 0000H |
| 3 | 8AH | 0089H | 0113H | 0 | 0000H |
| 4 | C0H | 0113H | 01D3H | 0 | 0000H |
| 5 | 1FH | 01D3H | 01F2H | 0 | 0000H |

**Final 16-bit sum = 01F2H** (= 498 decimal). ✓

---

## Program 6 — Packed BCD Addition Using `DAA`

**Problem Statement:** Add two packed BCD numbers `45H` (i.e., decimal 45) and `38H` (i.e., decimal 38). The result should be `83H` (decimal 83), and if a BCD overflow occurs (sum > 99), the carry should be stored.

### 3.1 Program

```asm
ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA

DATA SEGMENT
    BCD1   DB  45H
    BCD2   DB  38H
    BCDSUM DB  ?
    BCDCY  DB  ?
DATA ENDS

CODE SEGMENT
START:
    MOV  AX, @DATA
    MOV  DS, AX
    MOV  AL, BCD1
    ADD  AL, BCD2        ; AL <- 7DH, AF=1
    DAA                  ; AL <- 83H, CF=0
    MOV  BCDSUM, AL
    MOV  AL, 00H
    ADC  AL, 00H         ; Capture CF
    MOV  BCDCY, AL
    MOV  AH, 4CH
    INT  21H
CODE ENDS
END START
```

### 3.2 The DAA Internal Logic (For Exam Reference)

The 8086's `DAA` instruction applies this conditional correction to `AL`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } (\text{AL} \ \& \ \text{0FH}) > 9 \text{ OR } \text{AF} = 1 \text{ :} \quad &\text{AL} \leftarrow \text{AL} + 6 \\
\text{If } (\text{AL} \ \& \ \text{F0H}) > 90\text{H} \text{ OR } \text{CF} = 1 \text{ :} \quad &\text{AL} \leftarrow \text{AL} + 60\text{H},\ \text{CF} \leftarrow 1
\end{aligned}
$$

For our example, `7DH` has lower nibble `D = 13 > 9`, so `DAA` adds 6, yielding `7DH + 6 = 83H`. ✓

---

## Program 7 — 16-Bit Addition Using Direct & Indirect Addressing Modes

**Problem Statement:** Demonstrate **register indirect addressing** in 16-bit addition. Add `NUM1` (16-bit) to a value pointed to by `BX` register.

```asm
ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA

DATA SEGMENT
    NUM1   DW  4000H
    NUM2   DW  1234H
    SUM    DW  ?
DATA ENDS

CODE SEGMENT
START:
    MOV  AX, @DATA
    MOV  DS, AX
    MOV  BX, OFFSET NUM2  ; BX points to NUM2 in memory
    MOV  AX, NUM1         ; AX <- 4000H
    ADD  AX, [BX]         ; AX <- 4000H + 1234H = 5234H
    MOV  SUM, AX
    MOV  AH, 4CH
    INT  21H
CODE ENDS
END START
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Pattern — Addressing Mode Identification:** The instruction `ADD AX, [BX]` is a textbook example of **Register Indirect Addressing Mode**. The square brackets `[BX]` mean *"the memory location whose address is inside BX"*. This is the most-asked addressing mode in KTU exams.

---

## 3.X Analytical Matrix — When To Use Which Instruction

| Scenario | Correct Instruction | Reason |
|----------|---------------------|--------|
| Add two 8-bit numbers | `ADD AL, BL` | Single instruction, both operands same size |
| Add two 16-bit numbers | `ADD AX, BX` | Single instruction, both operands same size |
| Add array of N bytes | `ADD BX, AX` + `JNC` skip + `INC AH` | Manually propagate byte-overflow to 16-bit accumulator |
| Add 32-bit numbers (two 16-bit halves) | `ADD` for low word + `ADC` for high word | Use CF propagation to chain |
| Increment a counter | `INC CX` | More efficient than `ADD CX, 1` and preserves CF |
| BCD addition (packed) | `ADD` then `DAA` | DAA corrects to valid BCD |
| BCD addition (unpacked) | `ADD` then `AAA` | AAA adjusts for ASCII digits |
| Sign-extend 8-bit to 16-bit before adding | `MOVSX` (in 80386) or `CBW` | 8086 uses `CBW` (Convert Byte to Word) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Flow of an 8086 Addition Program

The diagram below shows the canonical structure every KTU student should follow for any 8086 addition program.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Program Entry] --> B[Initialize DS with @DATA]
    B --> C[Load Operands into Registers]
    C --> D{Choose Instruction Type}
    D -->|Single Precision| E[Execute ADD or ADC]
    D -->|Multi Precision| F[Clear CF via CLC]
    F --> G[ADD low word and store]
    G --> H[ADC high word with carry]
    H --> I[Store high result]
    E --> I
    I --> J[Examine Flags if needed]
    J --> K[Terminate via INT 21H Function 4CH]
    K --> L[End]
```

## 4.2 Multi-Precision (32-bit) Addition Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start] --> B[Load NUM1L into AX]
    B --> C[Execute ADD AX, NUM2L]
    C --> D[Store low result in RESL]
    D --> E[Load NUM1H into AX]
    E --> F[Execute ADC AX, NUM2H]
    F --> G[Store high result in RESH]
    G --> H[Optional: check CF for 33rd bit]
    H --> I[INT 21H Exit]
    I --> J[End]
```

## 4.3 Array Summation Loop Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start] --> B[Initialize CX = N]
    B --> C[Initialize SI = OFFSET ARRAY]
    C --> D[Initialize BX = 0, AX = 0]
    D --> E{CX = 0?}
    E -->|No| F[Load AL with SI]
    F --> G[ADD BX, AX]
    G --> H{CF = 1?}
    H -->|Yes| I[INC AH to propagate carry]
    H -->|No| J[Skip propagation]
    I --> K[Increment SI]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Decrement CX via LOOP]
    L --> E
    E -->|Yes| M[Store SUM = BX]
    M --> N[INT 21H Exit]
    N --> O[End]
```

## 4.4 Flag-Update Decision Block for ADD Instruction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ADD instruction executes] --> B[Compute binary sum]
    B --> C[Update all 6 flags]
    C --> D{CF = 1?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Store carry byte separately]
    D -->|No| F[Proceed to next step]
    E --> F
    F --> G{OF = 1?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Report signed overflow]
    G -->|No| I[Result is valid]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Continue program]
```

## 4.5 8086 ALU Internal Data Path (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[General Purpose Register] -->|Operand 1| MUX1
    B[Memory or Immediate] -->|Operand 2| MUX1
    MUX1 --> ALU[ALU: ADD or ADC]
    ALU --> TEMP[Temporary Result Register]
    TEMP --> FLAG[Flag Register: CF OF SF ZF AF PF]
    TEMP --> DEST[Destination Register or Memory]
    FLAG --> CTRL[Control Unit reads CF for next ADC]
    CTRL -.->|feedback for multi-precision| ALU
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The dashed feedback arrow from the **Flag Register** back to the **ALU** is the *physical mechanism* by which multi-precision arithmetic works in 8086. The `CF` bit stored in the flags register after the first `ADD` is fed back as an extra input to the ALU on the next `ADC` instruction. This is the **hardware basis** for assembling numbers of arbitrary width out of 16-bit additions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting `CLC` (Clear Carry) before the first `ADD` in a multi-precision program.** If the previous operation left CF = 1, the low-word `ADD` will be *wrong* and every byte after it will cascade the error. Always clear CF explicitly with `CLC` at the start of any multi-precision add.
> 2. **Using 8-bit register (e.g., `AL`) for 16-bit addition.** The 8086 *will* flag an assembler error "operand size mismatch". KTU evaluators deduct 1 mark per such compile-time error.
> 3. **Forgetting the `ASSUME` directive** or omitting `MOV AX, @DATA` and `MOV DS, AX` — without this, `DS` points to the wrong segment and the program reads garbage values.
> 4. **Confusing `INC` with `ADD` flag effects.** `INC` does **NOT** affect CF. If a KTU question asks *"After INC, which flag is unchanged?"* — the answer is **CF**.
> 5. **Little-endian byte order errors** when declaring `DW` constants for multi-word numbers. Always declare the low word first.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the difference between the `ADD` and `ADC` instructions of the 8086 microprocessor. When would you prefer `ADC` over `ADD`?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `ADD destination, source` instruction performs binary addition of the destination and source operands and stores the result in the destination. It updates six flags: **CF, OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF**.

The `ADC destination, source` instruction performs the same operation but **additionally adds the current value of the Carry Flag (CF)** to the sum: `destination ← destination + source + CF`.

`ADC` is preferred over `ADD` when performing **multi-precision (multi-byte or multi-word) addition**, where the carry generated from the addition of lower-order words must be propagated into the higher-order words. For example, to add two 32-bit numbers using two 16-bit additions, we use `ADD` for the low word and `ADC` for the high word to incorporate the carry.

> **Mark Distribution:** Instruction syntax (1) + Flag behavior (1) + Use case for ADC (1) = 3 marks.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the flags affected by the `ADD` instruction in 8086. Why is the Carry Flag (CF) not affected by the `INC` instruction?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `ADD` instruction affects **all six arithmetic flags**: Carry Flag (CF), Overflow Flag (OF), Sign Flag (SF), Zero Flag (ZF), Auxiliary Carry Flag (AF), and Parity Flag (PF).

The `INC` instruction is designed to increment a counter or memory location by 1 without disturbing the carry state of an ongoing multi-precision calculation. The 8086 designers intentionally made `INC` update only **OF, SF, ZF, AF, and PF**, but **leave CF unchanged**. This allows a programmer to write `INC CX` inside a loop or to advance a pointer without inadvertently corrupting the carry that was set by a prior `ADD` instruction in a multi-precision addition chain.

> **Mark Distribution:** List of six flags (1.5) + INC design rationale and CF preservation (1.5) = 3 marks.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Write an 8086 assembly language program to add **two 16-bit numbers** stored in memory. Store the 16-bit result and the carry (if any) in separate memory locations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a suitable example, how the **Carry Flag (CF)** and **Overflow Flag (OF)** differ in the 8086 microprocessor. Show the flag values for the following operations: `3CH + 6FH` and `0B5H + 7EH`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Algorithm:**
1. Load first 16-bit number into `AX`.
2. Add second 16-bit number using `ADD AX, mem`.
3. Store result in `SUM` (16-bit word).
4. Use a separate `ADD AL, 00H` + `ADC` trick (or simply check CF via `LAHF`/`PUSHF`) to capture the carry.
5. Terminate.

**Program:**

```asm
ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA

DATA SEGMENT
    NUM1   DW  89ABH
    NUM2   DW  6789H
    SUM    DW  ?
    CARRY  DB  ?
DATA ENDS

CODE SEGMENT
START:
    MOV  AX, @DATA
    MOV  DS, AX
    MOV  AX, NUM1          ; AX <- 89ABH
    ADD  AX, NUM2          ; AX <- F134H, CF=0
    MOV  SUM, AX           ; Store result
    MOV  AL, 00H
    ADC  AL, 00H           ; Capture CF
    MOV  CARRY, AL         ; CARRY = 00H (since sum < 10000H)
    MOV  AH, 4CH
    INT  21H
CODE ENDS
END START
```

**Verification:**

$$
89AB_{16} + 6789_{16} = F134_{16} \quad \text{(decimal: } 35211 + 26505 = 61716 \text{)}
$$

Since $61716 < 65535$, no carry. `CARRY = 00H`, `SUM = F134H`. ✓

**Mark Distribution:** Algorithm (1) + Program syntax (3) + Verification (1.5) + Termination code (1.5) = 7 marks.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Theoretical Distinction (3 marks):**

- **Carry Flag (CF)** indicates **unsigned overflow** — a carry out of the most significant bit of the result.
- **Overflow Flag (OF)** indicates **signed overflow** — a corruption of the sign bit when two same-signed operands produce a result of the opposite sign. OF is meaningful only for *signed* arithmetic.

**Rule:** $\text{OF} = 1$ **iff** *both operands have the same sign* **and** *the result has a different sign*.

**Operation 1: `3CH + 6FH` (4 marks)**

Binary addition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
3C_{16} &= 0011\,1100 \\
6F_{16} &= 0110\,1111 \\
\hline
\text{Sum} &= 1010\,1011 = AB_{16}
\end{aligned}
$$

- Bit 7 (sign bit): operand 1 = 0, operand 2 = 0 (both positive), result = 1 (negative!). 
- Therefore **OF = 1** (signed overflow).
- MSB carry out: $C_{out} = 0$ (no carry out of bit 7).
- Therefore **CF = 0**.

**Operation 2: `0B5H + 7EH`**

Binary addition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B5_{16} &= 1011\,0101 \\
7E_{16} &= 0111\,1110 \\
\hline
\text{Sum} &= 1\,0011\,0011 = 133_{16}
\end{aligned}
$$

- Result = `33H` with carry out of bit 7.
- **CF = 1** (unsigned overflow: $181 + 126 = 307 > 255$).
- Sign bit: operand 1 (negative) and operand 2 (positive) have *different* signs, so signed overflow is **not** possible.
- Therefore **OF = 0**.

**Summary Table:**

| Operation | Hex Sum | CF | OF |
|-----------|---------|----|----|
| `3CH + 6FH` | ABH | 0 | 1 |
| `0B5H + 7EH` | 33H (with carry 1) | 1 | 0 |

> **Mark Distribution:** CF vs OF definition (2) + Operation 1 flag derivation (2) + Operation 2 flag derivation (2) + Summary table (1) = 7 marks.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks]` — *ALTERNATIVE CHOICE*

**(a)** Write an 8086 assembly language program to compute the **sum of N 8-bit numbers** stored in an array. The sum may be a 16-bit value, so the program must handle carries. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an 8086 assembly language program to add **two packed BCD numbers** stored in memory. Use the `DAA` instruction. Show the contents of AL and the flags after each step for the inputs `45H` and `38H`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Program:**

```asm
ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA

DATA SEGMENT
    N       DW  5
    ARRAY   DB  80H, 90H, 70H, 60H, 50H
    SUM     DW  ?
DATA ENDS

CODE SEGMENT
START:
    MOV  AX, @DATA
    MOV  DS, AX
    MOV  CX, N
    MOV  SI, OFFSET ARRAY
    XOR  AX, AX            ; AX = 0  (high 8 bits of result)
    MOV  BX, 0             ; BX = low 16-bit accumulator

NEXT:
    MOV  DL, [SI]          ; DL <- current byte
    ADD  BX, DX            ; BX <- BX + DL
    JNC  SKIP              ; If CF=0, skip carry propagation
    INC  AL                ; Carry into AL
SKIP:
    INC  SI
    LOOP NEXT

    MOV  SUM, BX           ; Store 16-bit result
    MOV  AH, 4CH
    INT  21H
CODE ENDS
END START
```

**Verification:**

| i | ARRAY[i] | BX (hex) | CF | AL (carry accumulator) |
|---|----------|----------|----|------------------------|
| 1 | 80H | 0080H | 0 | 00H |
| 2 | 90H | 0110H | 0 | 00H |
| 3 | 70H | 0180H | 0 | 00H |
| 4 | 60H | 01E0H | 0 | 00H |
| 5 | 50H | 0230H | 0 | 00H |

**Final sum = 0230H = 560 decimal.** ✓

**Mark Distribution:** Counter and pointer init (1.5) + Loop body (3) + Carry propagation logic (1.5) + Termination (1) = 7 marks.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Program:**

```asm
ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA

DATA SEGMENT
    BCD1   DB  45H
    BCD2   DB  38H
    BCDSUM DB  ?
    BCDCY  DB  ?
DATA ENDS

CODE SEGMENT
START:
    MOV  AX, @DATA
    MOV  DS, AX
    MOV  AL, BCD1          ; AL = 45H
    ADD  AL, BCD2          ; AL = 7DH, AF=1, CF=0
    DAA                    ; AL = 83H, CF=0
    MOV  BCDSUM, AL
    MOV  AL, 00H
    ADC  AL, 00H           ; Capture CF
    MOV  BCDCY, AL
    MOV  AH, 4CH
    INT  21H
CODE ENDS
END START
```

**Step-by-Step Flag Trace (4 marks):**

| Step | Instruction | AL (Hex) | AL (Decimal) | CF | AF |
|------|-------------|----------|--------------|----|----|
| 1 | `MOV AL, BCD1` | 45H | 45 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | `ADD AL, BCD2` | 7DH | 125 | 0 | 1 |
| 3 | `DAA` | 83H | 83 | 0 | 0 |
| 4 | `MOV BCDSUM, AL` | 83H | 83 | 0 | 0 |
| 5 | `ADC AL, 00H` | 00H | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 6 | `MOV BCDCY, AL` | 00H | 0 | 0 | 0 |

**Explanation of DAA Action (3 marks):**

After `ADD AL, BCD2`, `AL = 7DH`. The lower nibble is `D = 13 > 9`, so the condition for BCD correction is satisfied. The `DAA` instruction internally checks `((AL & 0FH) > 9) OR (AF = 1)`. Since `AF = 1`, the lower nibble is corrected by adding 6:

$$
7D_{16} + 6_{16} = 83_{16}
$$

The result `83H` is a valid packed BCD representation of decimal 83. Since no upper-nibble overflow occurred, `CF` remains 0 and `BCDCY = 00H`. ✓

> **Mark Distribution:** Program (2) + Step-by-step trace table (2) + DAA internal logic explanation (3) = 7 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **8086 has three principal addition instructions:** `ADD`, `ADC`, and `INC`. `ADD` and `ADC` affect **all six flags**; `INC` affects only **five** (it does NOT touch CF).
- **CF = 1** means unsigned overflow. **OF = 1** means signed overflow. Always check both when validating an addition.
- For **multi-precision (multi-byte) addition**, the pattern is always: `CLC` → `ADD` low word → store → `ADC` high word → store. Carry from the low addition is automatically consumed by `ADC`.
- The 8086's **little-endian** byte order means the *low* word of a multi-word number is stored at the *lower* memory address. Declare `DW` constants accordingly.
- `DAA` is a **single-operand** instruction that works *implicitly* on `AL`. It must be invoked **immediately after** an `ADD` or `ADC` of two packed BCD values — do not place any other instruction between them, or the correction will be wrong.
- The `INC` instruction is the **preferred choice for loop counters** because it preserves CF and is typically faster (smaller opcode) than `ADD reg, 1`.
- **Address mode identification** is a high-yield KTU exam topic: `ADD AX, [BX]` is *register indirect*; `ADD AX, BX` is *register*; `ADD AX, NUM1` is *direct*; `ADD AX, 4000H` is *immediate*.
- **DOS termination idiom:** Always end the program with `MOV AH, 4CH` followed by `INT 21H` to return cleanly to the operating system.
- **Always initialize DS** at the start of the program: `MOV AX, @DATA` then `MOV DS, AX`. Forgetting this is the #1 reason KTU practical/lab programs fail.
- The **Auxiliary Carry Flag (AF)** is rarely used by application code but is **critical** for `DAA` to perform correct decimal correction of the lower nibble.
- For **mixed 8-bit/16-bit addition**, first zero-extend the 8-bit value: `MOV BH, 0` then `ADD AX, BX` — or use `CBW` to sign-extend `AL` into `AX`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Subtraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET504 - MICROPROCESSORS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Subtraction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PBEET504 - Module 1)**
> Subtraction in the **8086 microprocessor** is an arithmetic operation performed internally using **2's complement addition**. The instruction set provides dedicated mnemonics (`SUB`, `SBB`, `DEC`, `NEG`, `CMP`) that allow the programmer to perform 8-bit and 16-bit subtraction, with or without borrow, on registers, memory locations, and immediate operands.

### 1.1 What is Subtraction in 8086?
The **8086 microprocessor** does **not** have a dedicated hardware subtractor. Instead, it cleverly reuses its adder circuit. To compute `A - B`, the processor internally converts the operation into:

$$A - B \;\equiv\; A + (\bar{B} + 1)$$

where $(\bar{B} + 1)$ is the **2's complement** of $B$. The result is identical to true binary subtraction, and all status flags (Carry/Borrow, Overflow, Sign, Zero, Auxiliary, Parity) are automatically updated.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Odometer" Method
> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a car's odometer rolling backward. If the odometer reads `00015` km and you "subtract" `00030` km, the meter rolls over backward to `99985`. That weird number `99985` is exactly the **10's complement** of `00030` added to `00015`. The processor uses this same trick with binary numbers — the weird wrap-around value is the **2's complement**. The Carry Flag tells you whether the odometer rolled over (no borrow) or did not (borrow occurred).

### 1.3 The Subtraction Instruction Family
The 8086 instruction set provides **five** primary instructions that perform or use subtraction:

| Mnemonic | Full Name | Operation Performed | Stores Result? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `SUB` | Subtract | `dest ← dest − src` | Yes |
| `SBB` | Subtract with Borrow | `dest ← dest − src − CF` | Yes |
| `DEC` | Decrement by 1 | `dest ← dest − 1` | Yes |
| `NEG` | Negate (2's Complement) | `dest ← 0 − dest` | Yes |
| `CMP` | Compare | `dest − src` (flags only) | **No** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The `CMP` instruction is the **most frequently tested** instruction in this family because it is the gateway to all conditional jumps (`JE`, `JNE`, `JL`, `JG`, `JB`, `JA`, etc.) — a guaranteed question in KTU exams on Module 1.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- **Operand size:** **8-bit** (byte) or **16-bit** (word)
- **Unsigned 8-bit range:** $0$ to $255$ ($2^8 - 1$)
- **Signed 8-bit range:** $-128$ to $+127$ ($-2^7$ to $2^7 - 1$)
- **Unsigned 16-bit range:** $0$ to $65{,}535$ ($2^{16} - 1$)
- **Signed 16-bit range:** $-32{,}768$ to $+32{,}767$ ($-2^{15}$ to $2^{15} - 1$)
- **Status Flags affected:** **CF, OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF** (all six arithmetic flags)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Internal Mechanism — How 8086 Subtracts
The 8086 ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit) has **only an adder circuit**. Subtraction is faked via the following identity:

$$A - B = A + \bar{B} + 1$$

The steps the hardware performs for `SUB dest, src`:

1. **Invert every bit of `src`** (1's complement) using NOT gates.
2. **Set the carry-in (CF) to 1** of the lowest bit position of the adder.
3. **Feed `dest` and inverted `src` into the adder.**
4. **The sum produced is `dest - src`** with the correct flag updates.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the reason the **Carry Flag doubles as the Borrow Flag** after a subtraction — it is literally the carry output of the internal addition of the 2's complement.

### 2.2 Borrow Flag (CF) Logic After Subtraction
- **CF = 1** → An **unsigned borrow occurred**, i.e., unsigned `dest < src`.
- **CF = 0** → **No borrow**; unsigned `dest ≥ src`.

For multi-word subtraction (e.g., 32-bit subtraction using two 16-bit `SUB`s), the borrow from the **low-word** subtraction must be subtracted from the **high-word** subtraction. This is precisely why `SBB` exists.

### 2.3 Overflow Flag (OF) Logic After Subtraction
- **OF = 1** → A **signed overflow** occurred; the true mathematical result cannot fit in 8/16 bits.
- **OF = 0** → Result fits in the signed range.

For 8-bit signed: overflow if `dest` and `src` have **opposite signs** AND the result has a sign **different from `dest`**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Flags Affected | Units / Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Internal operation of `SUB` | $A - B = A + \bar{B} + 1$ | CF, OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF | 8 or 16 bits |
| 2's complement of $B$ | $\bar{B} + 1$ | — | n-bit number |
| Borrow (CF) | $\text{CF} = 1 \iff \text{dest} < \text{src}$ (unsigned) | — | Boolean |
| Signed overflow (OF) | OF = 1 if signs of operands differ AND sign of result differs from dest | — | Boolean |
| `SBB` operation | $\text{dest} \leftarrow \text{dest} - \text{src} - \text{CF}$ | CF, OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF | 8 or 16 bits |
| `DEC` operation | $\text{dest} \leftarrow \text{dest} - 1$ | OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF (**CF NOT affected**) | 8 or 16 bits |
| `NEG` operation | $\text{dest} \leftarrow 0 - \text{dest}$ | CF, OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF | 8 or 16 bits |
| `CMP` operation | Performs `dest - src`, discards result | CF, OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF | 8 or 16 bits |
| Unsigned 8-bit borrow check | If result $>$ dest, then **borrow = 1** | — | $0 \le \text{val} \le 255$ |
| Signed 8-bit overflow check | If result has wrong sign vs. expectation, OF = 1 | — | $-128 \le \text{val} \le +127$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Trap:** `DEC` does **NOT** modify the Carry Flag. This is a classic 2-mark KTU question. Students often write "DEC affects all flags" — that loses marks.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of These Instructions
- **Array indexing:** `DEC SI` is used to traverse an array backward.
- **Loop counters:** `DEC CX` is the heart of the `LOOP` instruction in 8086.
- **Multi-precision arithmetic:** `SBB` is mandatory for subtracting 32-bit, 64-bit, or BCD numbers in finance and embedded controllers.
- **Decision making:** `CMP` + conditional jump forms the **if-else** structure of assembly.
- **DSP & Embedded:** Subtraction is the basis of **delta modulation**, **PID error terms** ($e(t) = \text{setpoint} - \text{measured}$), and **digital filter** computations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example 1 — Unsigned 8-bit Subtraction (No Borrow)

**Problem:** Compute `SUB AL, BL` with `AL = 30H` and `BL = 15H`. State the result in AL and all flags.

**Step 1 — Write the operation:**
$$\text{AL} \leftarrow \text{AL} - \text{BL} = 30\text{H} - 15\text{H}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the 8086 internal identity $A - B = A + \bar{B} + 1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A - B &= 30\text{H} + \overline{15\text{H}} + 1 \\
&= 0011\,0000_2 + 1110\,1010_2 + 1 \\
&= 0011\,0000_2 + 1110\,1011_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Add bit-by-bit using binary addition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phantom{+}&\;0011\,0000 \\
+\;&\;1110\,1011 \\
\hline
=\;&\;1\,0001\,1011
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Discard the carry-out (9th bit). The 8-bit result is `0001 1011` = `1B H` = 27 decimal.**

**Step 5 — Update flags:**
- **CF (Carry/Borrow) = 0** → No borrow (carry out of MSB is 1 → CF = 0 in unsigned subtraction logic)
- **OF (Overflow) = 0** → Operands are same sign (both positive unsigned), no overflow possible
- **SF (Sign) = 0** → MSB of result is 0
- **ZF (Zero) = 0** → Result is not zero
- **AF (Auxiliary) = 0** → No borrow from bit 4
- **PF (Parity) = 1** → Result `1B H = 0001 1011` has five 1's (odd parity, but Intel convention: PF=1 means **even** parity → actually 5 ones = odd → **PF = 0**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parity Flag subtlety:** PF = 1 means **even** number of 1's in the low byte. `1B H` = `0001 1011` has 5 ones (odd) → **PF = 0**. This is a frequently mis-marked KTU answer.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example 2 — Unsigned 8-bit Subtraction (With Borrow)

**Problem:** Compute `SUB AL, BL` with `AL = 15H` and `BL = 30H`. State the result in AL and all flags.

**Step 1:** $15\text{H} - 30\text{H} = 21_{10} - 48_{10} = -27_{10}$ (negative, so borrow must occur).

**Step 2 — Internal 2's complement method:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A - B &= 15\text{H} + \overline{30\text{H}} + 1 \\
&= 0001\,0101_2 + 1101\,1111_2 + 1 \\
&= 0001\,0101_2 + 1110\,0000_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Add:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phantom{+}&\;0001\,0101 \\
+\;&\;1110\,0000 \\
\hline
=\;&\;1111\,0101
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — 8-bit result: `1111 0101` = `F5 H` = 245 unsigned, or $-11\text{H} = -17_{10}$ in signed.**

**Step 5 — Flags:**
- **CF = 1** → Borrow occurred (carry out of MSB is 0)
- **OF = 0** → No signed overflow (operands same sign, result negative — consistent)
- **SF = 1** → MSB is 1
- **ZF = 0** → Non-zero
- **AF = 1** → Borrow from bit 4
- **PF = 0** → 6 ones (even) → **PF = 1**

### 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example 3 — 16-bit Subtraction With Borrow (`SBB`)

**Problem:** Subtract the 32-bit number `[BX+2]:[BX]`` from `[DX+2]:[DX]`. Assume `DX = 0000H`, `DX+2 = 0001H`, `BX = FFFFH`, `BX+2 = 0000H`.

**Step 1 — First, the low word (no incoming borrow, so CF is cleared beforehand):**
```asm
CLC              ; CF = 0
SUB  [BX], DX    ; low 16-bit subtraction
```

**Step 2 — Then the high word with the borrow from the low word:**
```asm
SBB  [BX+2], DX  ; high 16-bit subtraction minus borrow
```

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation of the 32-bit subtraction:**
- High 32-bit value = $0001\;0000\text{H} = 65{,}536$
- Low 32-bit value = $0000\;\text{FFFF}\text{H} = 65{,}535$
- Difference = $65{,}536 - 65{,}535 = 1$

**Step 4 — Resulting flag after `SUB` (low word):**
$0000\text{H} - \text{FFFFH} = 1$ (with borrow)
- **CF = 1**, result = `0001H`, ZF = 0, OF = 0

**Step 5 — Resulting flag after `SBB` (high word):**
$0001\text{H} - 0000\text{H} - 1 = 0000\text{H}$
- **CF = 0**, result = `0000H`, **ZF = 1** (the 32-bit result is exactly zero!)

### 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example 4 — Signed Subtraction & Overflow Detection

**Problem:** Compute `SUB AL, BL` with `AL = 7FH` and `BL = 01H` (signed: $+127 - 1 = +126$, fits in range). Then try `AL = 7FH`, `BL = FFH` (signed: $+127 - (-1) = +128$ → overflow!).

**Case 1 — `7F H - 01 H`:**
$$\text{Result} = 7\text{EH} = +126$$
- **OF = 0** (no signed overflow, operands same sign, result same sign as dest)
- **CF = 0**, **SF = 0**, **ZF = 0**

**Case 2 — `7F H - FF H`:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
7\text{FH} - \text{FFH} &= 0111\,1111_2 + 0000\,0000_2 + 1 \quad (\text{2's comp of FFH} = 01\text{H}) \\
&= 0111\,1111 + 0000\,0001 = 1000\,0000 = 80\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$
- **OF = 1** (true result +128 cannot fit in signed 8-bit, MSB flipped from 0 to 1)
- **CF = 0** (no unsigned borrow, since 127 unsigned > 1 unsigned)
- **SF = 1**, **ZF = 0**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Rule for OF in SUB:** OF = 1 only when subtracting operands of **opposite signs** produces a result with the **same sign as the subtrahend** (or vice-versa). If both operands have the **same sign**, OF = 0 for SUB.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of 8086 Subtraction

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the 8086 ALU processes a `SUB` instruction internally.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User issues SUB dest, src] --> B[Instruction Decoder identifies SUB opcode]
    B --> C[ALU Control Unit sends invert signal]
    C --> D[Bit-wise NOT applied to src operand]
    D --> E[Forced Carry-In Cin set to 1]
    E --> F[Full Adder performs dest + NOT src + 1]
    F --> G[8-bit or 16-bit Sum produced]
    G --> H[Result written back to dest]
    G --> I[Flag Update Unit]
    I --> J[CF updated from Cout of MSB]
    I --> K[OF updated from XOR of carries into and out of MSB]
    I --> L[SF, ZF, AF, PF updated from result bits]
    J --> M[Final Status Flags visible to programmer]
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

### 4.2 Subtraction Instruction Family — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to subtract?] --> Q1{Result to be stored?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Borrow from previous subtraction?}
    Q1 -- No --> CMP[CMP dest, src — flags only]
    Q2 -- Yes --> SBB[SBB dest, src — subtract with borrow]
    Q2 -- No --> SUB[SUB dest, src — basic subtract]
    Start --> Q3{Negate a number?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> NEG[NEG dest — 2's complement]
    Start --> Q4{Decrement by 1?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> DEC[DEC dest — note CF not affected]
```

### 4.3 Borrow Propagation in Multi-Word Subtraction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LOW[Low Word: SUB LowA, LowB] --> CFMid[Borrow CF generated]
    CFMid --> HIGH[High Word: SBB HighA, HighB]
    HIGH --> CFOut[Final Borrow CF out]
    LOW --> ResultLow[Result Low Word]
    HIGH --> ResultHigh[Result High Word]
    ResultLow --> Final[Complete N-bit Result]
    ResultHigh --> Final
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading aid for the diagrams:** Each rectangular node is a hardware/functional block inside the 8086. The arrows represent data and control signal flow. The flag update unit runs **in parallel** with the result write-back, so flags are available immediately for the next conditional instruction.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"Explain the difference between the SUB and SBB instructions of the 8086 microprocessor with a suitable example."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- `SUB dest, src` performs simple binary subtraction: `dest ← dest − src`. It updates all six arithmetic flags based on the result. (1 mark)
- `SBB dest, src` performs subtraction **including the current value of the Carry Flag** (which acts as a borrow flag): `dest ← dest − src − CF`. (1 mark)
- **Example:** To subtract a 32-bit number in `DX:BX` from `CX:AX`, we use `SUB AX, BX` first, then `SBB CX, DX`. The borrow produced by the low-word subtraction is automatically subtracted from the high-word subtraction. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Why does the DEC instruction not modify the Carry Flag? Justify with an example."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The 8086 designers left CF unchanged by `DEC` so that the Carry Flag from a **preceding arithmetic operation** is preserved for use in multi-precision arithmetic chains. (1 mark)
- If `DEC` altered CF, the borrow status from a prior `SBB` would be lost, breaking multi-word subtraction. (1 mark)
- **Example:** After `SBB AX, BX` sets CF=1, executing `DEC CX` leaves CF=1 intact, allowing the next `SBB DX, CX` to correctly subtract the propagated borrow. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain how the 8086 microprocessor performs the subtraction operation internally. Discuss the role of the Carry Flag as a Borrow Flag. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Subtract the unsigned 16-bit number `B479H` from `7F20H` using the 8086 subtraction logic. Show all intermediate steps and determine the state of the Carry, Sign, Zero, and Overflow flags after the operation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Internal subtraction mechanism (7 marks):**

The 8086 ALU does not have a dedicated subtractor. It exploits the identity:

$$A - B = A + \bar{B} + 1$$

**Working steps:**
1. The instruction decoder recognizes the `SUB` opcode. (1 mark)
2. The ALU control unit activates the **invert** line, which bit-wise NOTs the source operand. (1 mark)
3. The carry-in (Cin) of the lowest bit of the adder is **forced to 1**. (1 mark)
4. The full adder computes `dest + (~src) + 1`, which mathematically equals `dest - src`. (1 mark)
5. The result is written back to `dest`, and the flag update unit refreshes CF, OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF in parallel. (1 mark)

**Role of CF as Borrow Flag:** In the identity, the carry-out of the MSB is the carry-out of the internal addition. If the unsigned `dest < src`, no carry is generated → **CF = 1 (borrow)**. If `dest ≥ src`, a carry is generated → **CF = 0 (no borrow)**. Thus CF is **inverted in meaning** compared to addition: it flags borrow rather than overflow. (2 marks — for the explanation linking CF=1 to unsigned borrow)

*Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 for the visual architecture.*

---

**(b) 16-bit subtraction: `7F20H - B479H` (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert to binary:**
$$7\text{F}20\text{H} = 0111\,1111\,0010\,0000_2$$
$$\text{B}479\text{H} = 1011\,0100\,0111\,1001_2$$

**Step 2 — Internal operation: $A - B = A + \bar{B} + 1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{B} &= 0100\,1011\,1000\,0110_2 \\
\bar{B} + 1 &= 0100\,1011\,1000\,0111_2 \quad (\text{2's complement of B479H})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Add `7F20H` to the 2's complement of `B479H`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phantom{+}&\;0111\,1111\,0010\,0000 \\
+\;&\;0100\,1011\,1000\,0111 \\
\hline
=\;&\;1100\,1010\,1010\,0111
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Result:** `1100 1010 1010 0111` = `CAA7H`

The decimal check: $32{,}544 - 46{,}201 = -13{,}657$. Unsigned wrap-around: $65{,}536 - 13{,}657 = 51{,}879$ → `CAA7H` ✓

**Step 5 — Flag determination:**
- **CF (Borrow) = 1** (no carry out of MSB → borrow occurred, since `7F20H < B479H` unsigned)
- **SF = 1** (MSB of result is 1)
- **ZF = 0** (result is non-zero)
- **OF = 0** (operands have different signs in signed interpretation: +32,544 and −46,201; result `CAA7H` interpreted signed is negative, consistent — no signed overflow)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):**
> Students commonly write **"OF = 1 because the result looks negative"**. This loses 1 mark. OF = 0 here because the **true mathematical result is −13,657**, which **does** fit in signed 16-bit range (−32,768 to +32,767). OF = 1 only if the result falls outside the signed range. The correct justification: operands have opposite signs → no signed overflow is possible for SUB.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the instructions `SUB`, `SBB`, `CMP`, `NEG`, and `DEC` of the 8086 microprocessor with their syntax, operation, and flag effects in a tabular form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 32-bit unsigned number is stored in registers `DX:CX` (DX = high word, CX = low word). Another 32-bit number is stored in `BX:AX` (BX = high word, AX = low word). Write an 8086 assembly language program to subtract the second 32-bit number from the first, storing the result in `DX:CX`. Show the step-by-step flag analysis for the case where `DX:CX = 0001 0000H` and `BX:AX = 0000 FFFFH`. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Instruction table (7 marks):**

| Instruction | Syntax | Operation | Flags Affected |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `SUB` | `SUB dest, src` | `dest ← dest − src` | CF, OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF |
| `SBB` | `SBB dest, src` | `dest ← dest − src − CF` | CF, OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF |
| `CMP` | `CMP dest, src` | `dest − src` (result discarded) | CF, OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF |
| `NEG` | `NEG dest` | `dest ← 0 − dest` (2's complement) | CF, OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF |
| `DEC` | `DEC dest` | `dest ← dest − 1` | OF, SF, ZF, AF, PF (CF **unchanged**) |

(Marking: 1 mark per row for correct syntax + operation; bonus 1 mark for explicitly noting CF is preserved in `DEC`)

---

**(b) 32-bit subtraction program (7 marks):**

**Assembly program:**

```asm
; Subtract (BX:AX) from (DX:CX), result in (DX:CX)
        CLC              ; 1. Clear CF before low-word subtraction
        SUB  CX, AX      ; 2. Low word: CX <- CX - AX
        SBB  DX, BX      ; 3. High word: DX <- DX - BX - borrow
        HLT              ; 4. Stop (or jump to result handler)
```

**Step-by-step execution for `DX:CX = 0001 0000H`, `BX:AX = 0000 FFFFH`:**

**Step 1 — `CLC`:** CF = 0 (no incoming borrow). (0.5 mark)

**Step 2 — `SUB CX, AX`:** `0000H − FFFFH` (1 mark)

Internal: $0000\text{H} + 0000\text{H} + 1 = 0001\text{H}$ (with carry-out of 0 → CF=1). (1 mark)

Result: **CX = 0001H**, flags: CF=1, ZF=0, SF=0, OF=0. (0.5 mark)

**Step 3 — `SBB DX, BX`:** `0001H − 0000H − 1 (borrow)` (1 mark)

Computation: $0001\text{H} - 0001\text{H} = 0000\text{H}$. Carry-out = 0 → CF = 1. (1 mark)

Result: **DX = 0000H**, flags: **ZF = 1** (entire 32-bit result is zero), CF = 1. (1 mark)

**Step 4 — Final 32-bit result:** `DX:CX = 0000:0001H` = `1` decimal. (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):**
> A common mistake is writing `SUB DX, BX` instead of `SBB DX, BX` for the high word. This loses 2 marks. Another frequent error is forgetting `CLC` before the first subtraction — this is acceptable here only if the problem explicitly states the initial CF state, but examiners usually expect to see `CLC` for safety. Always add `CLC` in your first line of multi-precision subtraction code.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Subtraction**

- ✅ 8086 has **no hardware subtractor**; subtraction is performed as $A + \bar{B} + 1$.
- ✅ The **Carry Flag acts as Borrow Flag** after `SUB`/`SBB` — CF = 1 means unsigned borrow.
- ✅ `SUB dest, src` → simple subtraction, affects all 6 arithmetic flags.
- ✅ `SBB dest, src` → subtraction with borrow; **mandatory for multi-word subtraction**.
- ✅ `CMP dest, src` → performs subtraction but **discards result**; **used before conditional jumps**.
- ✅ `NEG dest` → computes 2's complement; CF = 1 except when operand is zero.
- ✅ `DEC dest` → subtracts 1; **does NOT modify CF** (KTU classic trap).
- ✅ `SUB` and `SBB` support addressing modes: Register, Immediate, Direct, Register Indirect, Indexed.
- ✅ Signed overflow (OF) for `SUB` = 1 only when operands of **opposite signs** produce a result with sign different from expected.
- ✅ `JC` (Jump if Carry) = `JB` (Jump if Below) tests CF=1 for unsigned below.
- ✅ `JS` (Jump if Sign) tests SF=1; `JNS` tests SF=0; `JO`/`JNO` test OF.
- ✅ For 16-bit subtraction: range $0$ to $65{,}535$ (unsigned) or $-32{,}768$ to $+32{,}767$ (signed).
- ✅ Always use `CLC` before initiating a multi-precision subtraction chain to guarantee correct borrow propagation.
- ✅ A common **2-mark** KTU question: "Why does `DEC` not affect CF?" — Answer: to preserve the borrow for chained multi-word operations.
- ✅ Result wrap-around: if `dest < src` unsigned, the result is `dest - src + 2^n` and CF = 1.
- ✅ `SUB` and `CMP` set flags **identically** — the only difference is whether the result is stored.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory Reading and Writing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET504 - MICROPROCESSORS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Memory Reading and Writing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In a microprocessor-based system, **Memory Reading** and **Memory Writing** are the two fundamental bus operations through which the CPU exchanges data with external memory devices (RAM/ROM). These operations are governed by a synchronized sequence of signals on the system buses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Read Operation:** A bus cycle in which the CPU fetches data *from* a memory location by placing its address on the address bus, asserting the **Read (RD-bar)** control signal, and receiving the data through the bidirectional data bus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Write Operation:** A bus cycle in which the CPU stores data *into* a memory location by placing both the address and the data word simultaneously on the address bus and data bus respectively, then asserting the **Write (WR-bar)** control signal to latch the data.

The standard metrics associated with memory operations are:
- **Memory Cycle Time (T<sub>MC</sub>)** — typically **200 ns to 500 ns** for legacy 8085/8086 systems.
- **Address Bus Width** — **16 bits** for 8086, **20 bits** for 8086 (segmented), **16 bits** for 8085.
- **Data Bus Width** — **8 bits** for 8085, **16 bits** for 8086.
- **Memory Access Time (T<sub>ACC</sub>)** — the time within which memory must place valid data after address is stable.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a **massive post-office warehouse (the Memory)** containing millions of labelled pigeonholes (memory locations). A **postal clerk (the CPU)** needs to either **drop a letter into** a specific pigeonhole (WRITE) or **retrieve a letter from** it (READ).

| Postal Analogy | Microprocessor Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Pigeonhole number | **Address** placed on address bus |
| Letter to deliver / collect | **Data word** on data bus |
| Authorization slip for withdrawal | **RD-bar** control signal (active LOW) |
| Authorization slip for deposit | **WR-bar** control signal (active LOW) |
| Warehouse floor number (which section) | **Chip Select (CS-bar)** signal |
| Office hours / time-stamp slip | **Clock (CLK)** and timing signals |

For the clerk to act, three things must be in order:
1. The **pigeonhole number (address)** must be announced clearly.
2. The correct **floor (chip)** must be unlocked.
3. The correct **authorization slip (RD/WR)** must be presented.

If any of these three is missing, the operation is invalid — the same way a CPU aborts a memory cycle if signals are not synchronized.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the 8085 architecture, the lower 8 bits of the address (AD0–AD7) are **multiplexed** with the data bus, requiring the use of an external **Address Latch (74LS373)** controlled by the **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** signal to demultiplex them.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Bus Architecture Recap

Every memory operation requires the coordinated use of three buses:

- **Address Bus (Unidirectional, CPU → Memory):** Carries the unique binary address of the target memory cell. Width determines maximum addressable memory (2<sup>N</sup> locations).
- **Data Bus (Bidirectional):** Carries the actual data being transferred. For 8085, this is 8-bit; for 8086, 16-bit.
- **Control Bus:** Carries timing and direction signals (RD-bar, WR-bar, IO/M-bar, MREQ-bar, etc.).

### 2.2 Memory Read Operation — Step-by-Step Logic

The bus cycle for reading data from memory proceeds as follows:

1. **T1 State:** CPU places the **16-bit address** on AD0–AD7 and A8–A15. For multiplexed buses, ALE goes HIGH to latch the lower address into an external latch.
2. **T2 State:** 
   - ALE goes LOW (latch is now holding the address).
   - **RD-bar** control signal is asserted (driven LOW).
   - **IO/M-bar** (for 8086) or **MREQ-bar** (for Z80/8085-like) is asserted to select memory over I/O.
   - CPU's data bus drivers are placed in **HIGH-Z (input mode)** to receive data.
3. **T3 State (Wait States if needed):** 
   - Memory device's access time elapses.
   - Memory places the requested data byte on the data bus.
   - READY signal is sampled; if LOW, CPU inserts **Wait States (T<sub>W</sub>)**.
4. **T4 State:** 
   - CPU **latches** the data from the data bus into its internal register.
   - **RD-bar** is de-asserted (driven HIGH).
   - Bus cycle completes; data bus returns to HIGH-Z.

### 2.3 Memory Write Operation — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **T1 State:** CPU places the **address** on the address bus. ALE is pulsed HIGH then LOW to latch the low-order address.
2. **T2 State:** 
   - CPU places the **data byte** to be written on the data bus.
   - **WR-bar** control signal is asserted (driven LOW).
   - **IO/M-bar** or **MREQ-bar** is asserted for memory selection.
3. **T3 State:** 
   - Memory's internal write circuitry activates.
   - Data on the data bus is latched into the addressed memory cell.
   - Wait states may be inserted based on READY signal.
4. **T4 State:** 
   - **WR-bar** is de-asserted (driven HIGH).
   - CPU removes data from the data bus.
   - Write cycle terminates.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and routinely tested in KTU University Exams.

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Addressable Memory Capacity | $C = 2^{N}$ where $N$ = number of address lines | Bytes (if data bus = 8 bits) |
| 2 | Number of Address Lines for given capacity | $N = \log_{2} C$ | Dimensionless integer |
| 3 | Total Memory Space (16-bit address bus) | $C_{total} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 = 64\ KB$ | Bytes |
| 4 | Memory Addressing Range | $\text{Range} = \text{Base Address} \rightarrow (\text{Base} + \text{Size} - 1)$ | Hexadecimal |
| 5 | Number of Memory Chips required | $n = \dfrac{C_{total}}{C_{chip}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Address Lines for Chip + Address Lines for Chip Select | $A_{total} = A_{chip} + A_{CS}$ | $A_{CS} = \log_{2}(\text{number of chips})$ |
| 7 | Memory Access Time Constraint | $T_{ACC} \leq T_{cycle} - T_{decode} - T_{propagation}$ | Nanoseconds (ns) |
| 8 | Wait States Required | $T_{W} = \left\lceil \dfrac{T_{ACC,required} - T_{ACC,available}}{T_{CLK}} \right\rceil$ | Integer number of clock cycles |
| 9 | End Address Calculation | $E = S + C - 1$ | Hexadecimal |
| 10 | Decoder Selection | $\text{Decoder size} = 2^{A_{CS}}$ | e.g., 3-to-8, 4-to-16 |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Memory read/write operations are the **backbone of every embedded system**:
- **Firmware Boot:** The CPU reads its first instruction (typically a JMP) from a fixed ROM address (e.g., FFFF0h in 8086 reset vector).
- **Sensor Data Logging:** Microcontroller writes ADC values to SRAM in real time.
- **Display Driving:** Constant memory reads fetch pixel data from frame buffers.
- **EEPROM/Flash Storage:** Non-volatile memory writes preserve configuration across power cycles.
- **DMA Transfers:** Direct Memory Access controllers perform block memory read/write operations without CPU intervention, increasing throughput.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Memory Capacity from Address Lines

**Problem:** A memory chip has 14 address lines and an 8-bit data bus. Find its capacity in bytes.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of addressable locations} &= 2^{A} \\
&= 2^{14} \\
&= 16{,}384 \text{ locations}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Capacity (in bytes)} &= \text{Number of locations} \times \text{Data width (bits)} \div 8 \\
&= 16{,}384 \times 8 \div 8 \\
&= 16{,}384 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 16\ KB
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** The memory chip has a capacity of **16 KB**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Number of Address Lines for Given Capacity

**Problem:** A memory system must store 32 KB of data. How many address lines are required?

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 32\ KB = 32 \times 1024 = 32{,}768 \text{ bytes} \\
2^{N} &= 32{,}768 \\
N &= \log_{2}(32{,}768) \\
N &= \log_{2}(2^{15}) \\
N &= 15
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** **15 address lines** are required to address 32 KB of memory.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Address Range of a Memory Chip (Chip Select Decoding)

**Problem:** Interface four 8 KB RAM chips to an 8085 microprocessor (16-bit address bus). Determine the address range of each chip using absolute decoding with a 3-to-8 line decoder.

**Given:**
- Total memory space = $2^{16} = 64\ KB$
- Each chip size = $8\ KB = 2^{13}\ \text{bytes}$
- Number of chips = 4

**Step 1 — Identify the lines used for chip-internal addressing:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{chip} &= \log_{2}(8\ KB) = \log_{2}(2^{13}) = 13 \\
\text{These are the LOW-order address lines: } A_0 \text{ to } A_{12}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the lines used for chip selection:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{CS} &= \log_{2}(4) = 2 \\
\text{These are the HIGH-order address lines: } A_{13} \text{ and } A_{14} \\
\text{(}A_{15} \text{ is reserved/ignored in this scheme, or used for full decoding)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Build the truth table and assign ranges:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Chip 1:} \quad A_{15}A_{14}A_{13} &= 000 \Rightarrow 0000\text{H} \rightarrow 1\text{FFFH} \\
\text{Chip 2:} \quad A_{15}A_{14}A_{13} &= 001 \Rightarrow 2000\text{H} \rightarrow 3\text{FFFH} \\
\text{Chip 3:} \quad A_{15}A_{14}A_{13} &= 010 \Rightarrow 4000\text{H} \rightarrow 5\text{FFFH} \\
\text{Chip 4:} \quad A_{15}A_{14}A_{13} &= 011 \Rightarrow 6000\text{H} \rightarrow 7\text{FFFH}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the end address of Chip 1 (example):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1 &= S_1 + C - 1 \\
&= 0000\text{H} + (8 \times 1024) - 1 \\
&= 0000\text{H} + 1\text{FFFH} \\
&= 1\text{FFFH} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Address Map:**

| Chip | A15 | A14 | A13 | Start Address | End Address | Size |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| RAM 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0000H | 1FFFH | 8 KB |
| RAM 2 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 2000H | 3FFFH | 8 KB |
| RAM 3 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 4000H | 5FFFH | 8 KB |
| RAM 4 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 6000H | 7FFFH | 8 KB |

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Memory Read Cycle Timing Analysis

**Problem:** An 8085 system uses a clock frequency of **3 MHz**. The memory device has an access time of **450 ns**. The address decode and propagation delay totals **100 ns**. Determine the number of wait states required.

**Given:**
- Clock period: $T_{CLK} = 1/f = 1 / (3 \times 10^{6}) = 333.33\ ns$
- Memory access time required: $T_{ACC,req} = 450\ ns$
- Decode + propagation delay: $T_{DP} = 100\ ns$
- Available time per T-state: $T_{CLK} = 333.33\ ns$
