**Step 1 — Calculate the available time per T-state:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{available} = T_{CLK} = 333.33\ ns
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the time deficit:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{deficit} &= T_{ACC,req} - (T_{available} - T_{DP}) \\
&= 450 - (333.33 - 100) \\
&= 450 - 233.33 \\
&= 216.67\ ns
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the number of wait states:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{W} &= \left\lceil \dfrac{T_{deficit}}{T_{CLK}} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil \dfrac{216.67}{333.33} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil 0.65 \right\rceil \\
&= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** **1 wait state** must be inserted to accommodate the slow memory device.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Control Signal States During Operations

| Bus State / Time | Address Bus | Data Bus (CPU side) | RD-bar | WR-bar | IO/M-bar (8086) | Memory Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Idle (T1 start) | New address | HIGH-Z | HIGH (inactive) | HIGH (inactive) | Selected | — |
| T2 (Read) | Stable address | HIGH-Z (input) | **LOW (active)** | HIGH | LOW (memory) | Output enabled |
| T2 (Write) | Stable address | **Data out** | HIGH | **LOW (active)** | LOW (memory) | Latch data |
| T3 / T4 | Stable / released | Data in (Read) / Hi-Z (Write) | De-asserted | De-asserted | De-asserted | Cycle ends |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Memory Read Operation Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([CPU Initiates Read]) --> Step1[Place 16-bit Address on Address Bus]
    Step1 --> Step2[Activate ALE Pulse to Latch Low Address]
    Step2 --> Step3[Deactivate ALE and Assert IO/M-bar LOW]
    Step3 --> Step4[Assert RD-bar Signal LOW]
    Step4 --> Step5[Memory Decodes Address and Activates CS-bar]
    Step5 --> Step6{Memory Access Time Elapsed?}
    Step6 -- No --> WaitState[Insert Wait States via READY Signal]
    WaitState --> Step6
    Step6 -- Yes --> Step7[Memory Places Data Byte on Data Bus]
    Step7 --> Step8[CPU Latches Data into Internal Register]
    Step8 --> Step9[Deassert RD-bar Signal HIGH]
    Step9 --> End([Read Cycle Complete])
```

### 4.2 Memory Write Operation Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([CPU Initiates Write]) --> Step1[Place 16-bit Address on Address Bus]
    Step1 --> Step2[Activate ALE Pulse to Latch Low Address]
    Step2 --> Step3[Deactivate ALE and Assert IO/M-bar LOW]
    Step3 --> Step4[Place Data Byte on Data Bus]
    Step4 --> Step5[Assert WR-bar Signal LOW]
    Step5 --> Step6[Memory Decodes Address and Activates CS-bar]
    Step6 --> Step7{Memory Ready for Write?}
    Step7 -- No --> WaitState[Insert Wait States via READY Signal]
    WaitState --> Step7
    Step7 -- Yes --> Step8[Memory Latches Data into Addressed Cell]
    Step8 --> Step9[Deassert WR-bar Signal HIGH]
    Step9 --> Step10[CPU Removes Data from Data Bus]
    Step10 --> End([Write Cycle Complete])
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Memory Interface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU[8085 CPU Block] -->|A0-A15| AddBus[Address Bus]
    CPU -->|AD0-AD7| DataBus[Data Bus Bidirectional]
    CPU -->|RD-bar WR-bar IO-M-bar ALE| CtrlBus[Control Bus]
    
    AddBus --> Latch[74LS373 Address Latch]
    Latch -->|A0-A7 Latched| Decoder[3-to-8 Line Decoder]
    Latch -->|Low Address| MuxDemux[Demultiplexed Address Lines]
    
    Decoder -->|CS1| RAM1[8KB RAM Chip 1]
    Decoder -->|CS2| RAM2[8KB RAM Chip 2]
    Decoder -->|CS3| ROM1[8KB ROM Chip 1]
    Decoder -->|CS4[Reserved]| EXP[Expansion Slot]
    
    RAM1 <-->|Data IO| DataBus
    RAM2 <-->|Data IO| DataBus
    ROM1 -->|Data Out Only| DataBus
    
    CtrlBus --> Latch
    CtrlBus --> Decoder
    CtrlBus --> RAM1
    CtrlBus --> RAM2
    CtrlBus --> ROM1
```

### 4.4 Read vs. Write Control Signal Comparison

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> AddressPhase: T1 Address Placement
    AddressPhase --> ReadActive: RD-bar Goes LOW
    AddressPhase --> WriteActive: WR-bar Goes LOW
    ReadActive --> DataLatch: T3 Data Latched by CPU
    WriteActive --> DataLatch: T3 Data Latched by Memory
    DataLatch --> Deassert: T4 Control Signals Released
    Deassert --> [*]: Cycle Ends
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between Memory Read and Memory Write operations in an 8085 microprocessor system. List the control signals involved in each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Memory Read | Memory Write |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction of Data Flow** | From Memory → CPU | From CPU → Memory |
| **Active Control Signal** | **RD-bar** is asserted LOW | **WR-bar** is asserted LOW |
| **Data Bus Role (CPU side)** | Input (HIGH-Z then reads) | Output (drives data word) |
| **Memory Action** | Places data on data bus | Latches data from data bus |
| **Typical Use** | Fetching instructions, reading data | Storing results, writing to RAM |

**Control Signals Common to Both:** IO/M-bar (memory select), ALE (address latch), READY (wait state control).

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1) + Signal listing (1) + Distinction table (1) = **3 marks**

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the function of the ALE (Address Latch Enable) signal in the 8085 microprocessor? Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer:**

The **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** signal is an active-HIGH control output of the 8085 microprocessor used to **demultiplex the lower 8 bits of the address (AD0–AD7) from the data bus**.

**Necessity:**
- In the 8085, lines **AD0–AD7** carry **both the lower 8 bits of the address (during T1)** and the **8-bit data (during T2–T3)** at different time slots on the same physical pins.
- This technique of **time-multiplexing** reduces the pin count of the IC from 24 down to 16, making the chip package smaller and cheaper.
- However, external memory and peripheral devices require the address to be **stable throughout the bus cycle**.
- Therefore, an **external latch (74LS373)** is used. When ALE goes HIGH during T1, the latch is transparent and captures the address. When ALE goes LOW, the latch holds the address stable, freeing AD0–AD7 to be used as the data bus.

**Marks Distribution:** Function (1) + Necessity (1) + Latch IC reference (1) = **3 marks**

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper Pattern]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the memory read operation in the 8085 microprocessor system. Discuss the role of RD-bar, IO/M-bar, and ALE signals. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A microprocessor with 16 address lines is to be interfaced with the following memory devices:
- One 8 KB EPROM
- Two 8 KB RAM chips

Design the interfacing using a 3-to-8 decoder and find the address range of each chip. **[7 Marks]**

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Memory Read Operation — Detailed Explanation:**

The memory read operation in the 8085 is a **3-T state minimum bus cycle** (can be extended with wait states). The signals are:

1. **ALE (Address Latch Enable):** Pulsed HIGH during T1 to latch AD0–AD7 into an external 74LS373 latch, separating the address from the data bus.

2. **IO/M-bar:** Asserted LOW to indicate that the CPU is communicating with **memory** (not I/O). For memory read, this signal remains LOW throughout the read cycle.

3. **RD-bar:** Asserted LOW during T2 and T3. This tells the addressed memory device to **place its data on the data bus**.

**Block Diagram Description:**

```
   +-------+        +-------+         +----------+
   | 8085  |--------| Latch |---------|  Memory  |
   | CPU   |  A0-A7 |74LS373|  A0-A7  |  Chip    |
   |       |--------|       |---------|          |
   |       |  AD0-7 |       |  D0-D7  |          |
   |       |--------|       |---------|          |
   |       |  ALE   |       |         |          |
   |       |--------|       |---------|          |
   |       |  RD-bar|        ----CS-bar          |
   +-------+                                   |
            +------------Control Bus-----------+
```

**Sequence of Events:**
- **T1:** CPU outputs lower address on AD0–AD7, upper address on A8–A15. ALE = HIGH, IO/M-bar = LOW, RD-bar = HIGH.
- **T2:** ALE goes LOW (latch holds address). RD-bar goes LOW. CPU's data bus is set as input.
- **T3:** Memory device's access time expires; data is placed on the data bus. CPU reads the data.
- **T4:** RD-bar goes HIGH, IO/M-bar goes HIGH, data bus goes HIGH-Z. Cycle ends.

**Marks Distribution:** Block diagram (2) + Signal role (3) + T1–T4 sequence (2) = **7 marks**

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- Address bus width = 16 bits → Total addressable memory = $2^{16} = 64\ KB$
- EPROM size = 8 KB
- RAM size = 2 × 8 KB

**Step 1 — Address lines required for each 8 KB chip:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{chip} &= \log_{2}(8\ KB) = \log_{2}(2^{13}) = 13 \text{ lines} \\
\text{These are } A_0 \text{ to } A_{12}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Address lines required for chip select:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{CS} &= \log_{2}(3) \approx 2 \text{ (rounded up to use a 3-to-8 decoder)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Design the address map (with 3-to-8 decoder, using A<sub>15</sub>, A<sub>14</sub>, A<sub>13</sub> as decoder inputs):**

| Chip | A15 | A14 | A13 | Start Address | End Address | Decoder Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| EPROM | 0 | 0 | 0 | **0000H** | **1FFFH** | Y0 |
| RAM 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | **2000H** | **3FFFH** | Y1 |
| RAM 2 | 0 | 1 | 0 | **4000H** | **5FFFH** | Y2 |

**Step 4 — Verify the end address of RAM 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{RAM1} &= S_{RAM1} + C - 1 = 2000\text{H} + 1\text{FFFH} = 3\text{FFFH} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interfacing Logic:**
- The 3-to-8 decoder receives A<sub>15</sub>, A<sub>14</sub>, A<sub>13</sub> as inputs.
- Its outputs Y0, Y1, Y2 are connected (after inversion or via active-low logic) to the CS-bar pins of EPROM, RAM 1, and RAM 2 respectively.
- Remaining decoder outputs Y3–Y7 are left unused (available for future expansion).

**Marks Distribution:** Address line derivation (2) + Address map table (3) + Decoder interface (2) = **7 marks**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper Pattern]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the memory write operation in the 8085 microprocessor system. Discuss why data must be placed on the data bus before asserting WR-bar. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An 8086 microprocessor operates at 5 MHz clock frequency. A memory chip with 200 ns access time is to be interfaced. The address decoding and buffer delays total 80 ns. Calculate the number of wait states required for a reliable memory write operation. Take the clock period as the basic T-state duration. **[7 Marks]**

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Memory Write Operation — Detailed Explanation:**

The memory write cycle in the 8085 has the following sequence:

1. **T1 State:**
   - CPU places the **16-bit address** on the address bus (lower 8 bits on AD0–AD7, upper 8 bits on A8–A15).
   - **ALE** is pulsed HIGH to enable the external latch to capture the low-order address.
   - **IO/M-bar** is driven LOW to indicate a memory operation.

2. **T2 State:**
   - ALE goes LOW (address is now latched and stable).
   - CPU places the **data byte** on the data bus (AD0–AD7).
   - **WR-bar** is asserted LOW.

3. **T3 State:**
   - The addressed memory device, with its CS-bar activated, **latches the data** from the data bus into the specified address.
   - Wait states (T<sub>W</sub>) are inserted if the memory is too slow (signaled by READY being LOW).

4. **T4 State:**
   - **WR-bar** is de-asserted (driven HIGH).
   - Data is removed from the data bus.
   - IO/M-bar is de-asserted.
   - Cycle terminates.

**Why Data Must Be Placed Before WR-bar:**
- The memory device's **write enable input** (WE-bar) is edge-sensitive. It latches the data on the **falling edge (HIGH-to-LOW transition)** of the WR-bar signal.
- If the data is not stable on the bus **before** WR-bar goes LOW, the memory will latch an **invalid or corrupted data word**, causing silent data corruption.
- Setup time requirement: Data must be valid for at least a **specified setup time (t<sub>DS</sub>)** before WR-bar falls.
- Hold time requirement: Data must remain valid for at least a **specified hold time (t<sub>DH</sub>)** after WR-bar goes HIGH.

**Marks Distribution:** T1–T4 sequence (2) + Block diagram (2) + Edge-trigger explanation (2) + Setup/Hold time (1) = **7 marks**

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- Clock frequency: $f = 5\ MHz$
- Memory access time: $T_{ACC,req} = 200\ ns$
- Address decode + buffer delay: $T_{DP} = 80\ ns$

**Step 1 — Calculate the clock period (T-state duration):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{CLK} &= \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{6}} \\
&= 0.2 \times 10^{-6}\ s \\
&= 200\ ns
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate available time per T-state (after subtract delays):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{available,net} &= T_{CLK} - T_{DP} \\
&= 200 - 80 \\
&= 120\ ns
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the time deficit (how much more time the memory needs):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{deficit} &= T_{ACC,req} - T_{available,net} \\
&= 200 - 120 \\
&= 80\ ns
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the number of wait states required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{W} &= \left\lceil \dfrac{T_{deficit}}{T_{CLK}} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil \dfrac{80}{200} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil 0.4 \right\rceil \\
&= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** **1 wait state** must be inserted.

**Interpretation:** Since each wait state adds 1 clock period (200 ns) of extra time, inserting 1 wait state increases the effective memory cycle time to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle,new} &= 4 \times T_{CLK} + 1 \times T_{CLK} = 5 \times 200 = 1000\ ns
\end{aligned}
$$

This is more than sufficient to cover the 200 ns access time requirement.

**Marks Distribution:** Clock period calculation (2) + Time deficit (2) + Wait state formula (2) + Final answer with interpretation (1) = **7 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Confusing RD-bar and WR-bar polarity:** Both signals are **active LOW** (denoted by the overbar). Students often draw them as active HIGH, costing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting ALE in timing diagrams:** Many students omit the ALE pulse in T1. The latch IC is the only thing that separates address from data on a multiplexed bus.
> 3. **Address range calculation errors:** The end address is **Start + Size − 1**, NOT Start + Size. Always subtract 1.
> 4. **Off-by-one in address line counting:** For N address lines, the range is **0 to (2<sup>N</sup> − 1)**, not 1 to 2<sup>N</sup>.
> 5. **Wait state formula mistakes:** Wait states are an **integer ceiling**, not a floor. Always use $\lceil \cdot \rceil$.
> 6. **Mixing up IO/M-bar and MREQ-bar:** In 8085/8086 systems, IO/M-bar is the standard; in Z80, MREQ-bar and IORQ-bar are used separately. Do not interchange them in diagrams.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memory Read and Write**

- **Memory Read Cycle (Minimum 3 T-states):** Address placed (T1) → RD-bar asserted + data bus to input (T2) → Memory places data (T3) → RD-bar de-asserted (T4).
- **Memory Write Cycle (Minimum 3 T-states):** Address placed (T1) → Data placed + WR-bar asserted (T2) → Memory latches data (T3) → WR-bar de-asserted (T4).
- **ALE Pulse:** Active HIGH during T1 only; controls 74LS373 transparent latch.
- **RD-bar and WR-bar:** Both are **active LOW** signals.
- **IO/M-bar:** LOW for memory operations, HIGH for I/O operations (8085/8086).
- **Memory Capacity Formula:** $C = 2^{A}$ where A is the number of address lines (assuming 8-bit data bus).
- **End Address Formula:** $E = S + C - 1$ (use hexadecimal arithmetic).
- **Wait States:** Inserted via the **READY** signal; one wait state = one extra clock period.
- **Address Decoding:** 
  - **Linear Decoding** — simple but produces address aliasing (multiple addresses map to same chip).
  - **Absolute/Full Decoding** — uses a decoder (e.g., 3-to-8, 4-to-16) for unique, non-overlapping address ranges.
- **74LS373 Latch:** Used to demultiplex AD0–AD7. Latch enable (LE) is connected to ALE.
- **Multiplexed Bus Advantage:** Reduces pin count of microprocessor IC; 8085 uses 40 pins instead of 48.
- **Control Signal Direction:** All control signals (RD-bar, WR-bar, IO/M-bar) are **outputs from CPU** to memory/peripherals.
- **Data Bus:** Always **bidirectional** — used for both read and write; direction is controlled by the active control signal.
- **KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw timing diagrams with clearly labeled T-states (T1, T2, T3, T4, T<sub>W</sub>) and annotate signal transitions with arrows.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sorting - Addressing Modes - Classification of Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET504 - MICROPROCESSORS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sorting - Addressing Modes - Classification of Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Sorting in Microprocessor Context

**Definition:** Sorting in the context of microprocessors refers to the algorithmic arrangement of data elements (numbers, characters, or records) stored in memory into a specific order—ascending or descending—executed through assembly language instructions running on the processor.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme, the focus is on **Bubble Sort** and **Selection Sort** implementations using the **8086 microprocessor**, as these directly map to instruction sets, addressing modes, and loop control structures covered in Module 1.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a row of students standing in a classroom based on their heights. The teacher walks through the line repeatedly, and whenever two adjacent students are in the wrong order (taller standing before shorter), she swaps them. After enough passes, every student is in perfect order. This is exactly how the **Bubble Sort** algorithm works—comparing adjacent memory locations and swapping them when out of order.

The **Selection Sort** works differently: the teacher scans the entire row, finds the shortest student, and places them at the front. Then she finds the next shortest among the remaining, and so on. The processor does the same—finds the minimum/maximum in the array and places it in its correct position.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Sorting Matters in Embedded Systems:**
> - Sensor data filtering (removing outliers)
> - Display sorting in HMI (Human-Machine Interface)
> - Lookup table optimization
> - Real-time data prioritization
> - **Time Complexity Standards:** Bubble Sort = **O(n²)**, Selection Sort = **O(n²)**, but selection sort has fewer swaps.

---

## 1.2 Addressing Modes

**Definition:** An addressing mode is the method by which the **instruction operand(s)** specify the location of the data on which the operation is to be performed. It defines how the CPU calculates the **Effective Address (EA)** of the operand.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of giving directions to a friend:
- **"Go to building number 5"** → Direct address (specific location)
- **"Go to the next building"** → Immediate offset (constant)
- **"Go where my map says"** → Register indirect (pointer)
- **"Go 3 buildings past the post office"** → Based addressing (base + offset)
- **"Go to building #5 on 2nd street"** → Based indexed (base + index + displacement)

Each "way" of describing the destination is an **addressing mode**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The **8086 microprocessor** supports **8 addressing modes** that form the foundation for understanding memory access patterns, pointer arithmetic, and data structures in assembly programming.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Instructions

**Definition:** Instruction classification is the systematic organization of the **8086 instruction set** into functional groups based on the type of operation performed—data movement, arithmetic, logical, control flow, or processor control.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a kitchen as the CPU. The recipes (instructions) are categorized:
- **Moving ingredients** (Data Transfer) — putting flour from jar to bowl
- **Cooking operations** (Arithmetic) — mixing, heating
- **Tasting/adjusting** (Logical) — checking salt, adding spices
- **Following next recipe step** (Control Transfer) — "now do step 7"
- **Kitchen settings** (Processor Control) — adjusting oven temperature

> [!NOTE]
> The **8086 instruction set** is broadly classified into **6 major groups** as per Intel architecture standards, with a total of approximately **117+ base instructions** generating thousands of variants through addressing modes.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key 8086 Specifications (Bold Standards):**
> - **Data Bus Width:** **16-bit**
> - **Address Bus Width:** **20-bit**
> - **Maximum Addressable Memory:** **2²⁰ = 1,048,576 bytes = 1 MB**
> - **General Purpose Registers:** **8 (AX, BX, CX, DX, SP, BP, SI, DI)**
> - **Segment Registers:** **4 (CS, DS, SS, ES)**
> - **Flag Register Size:** **16-bit (9 active flags)**
> - **Instruction Size:** **1 to 6 bytes (variable length)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bubble Sort Algorithm — Deep Dive

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **Initialize** the outer loop counter `CX` with `N - 1` (number of passes).
2. **Initialize** the inner loop counter for comparing adjacent elements.
3. **Compare** two adjacent memory locations (e.g., `[SI]` and `[SI+1]`).
4. **Conditional Jump:** If out of order, swap them; otherwise, continue.
5. **Decrement** inner counter and repeat until all pairs are compared.
6. **Loop** outer counter `N-1` times until the array is sorted.

**The 'Why' Behind Each Step:**
- Outer loop `N-1` passes: After each pass, the largest unsorted element "bubbles up" to its final position at the end.
- Inner loop comparisons decrease by 1 each pass: Because the last element of each pass is already in its correct place.
- Use of `CX` register: The 8086's `LOOP` instruction inherently uses `CX` as its counter—efficient and hardware-supported.

**KTU Formula Sheet — Sorting Complexity:**

| Algorithm | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case | Space Complexity | Stable? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bubble Sort | **O(n)** | **O(n²)** | **O(n²)** | **O(1)** | **Yes** |
| Selection Sort | **O(n²)** | **O(n²)** | **O(n²)** | **O(1)** | **No** |
| Insertion Sort | **O(n)** | **O(n²)** | **O(n²)** | **O(1)** | **Yes** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Comparisons for Bubble Sort** = $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ for worst case
>
> **Total Swaps (Worst Case)** = $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$
>
> **Total Swaps (Best Case, already sorted)** = $0$

---

## 2.2 Addressing Modes — Theoretical Framework

**Operational Concept:**
The 8086 uses **segmented memory addressing** where the physical address is computed using the formula:

$$\text{Physical Address} = (\text{Segment Register} \times 16) + \text{Effective Address (Offset)}$$

The **Effective Address (EA)** is calculated based on the addressing mode using up to three components:
- **Displacement** (8-bit or 16-bit constant)
- **Base Register** (`BX` or `BP`)
- **Index Register** (`SI` or `DI`)

$$\text{EA} = \text{Displacement} + [\text{Base Register}] + [\text{Index Register}]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The displacement can be **0** (absent), **8-bit** (sign-extended), or **16-bit** unsigned. The combination determines which of the 8 addressing modes is being used.

**Why Multiple Addressing Modes?**
- **Flexibility:** Different data access patterns need different addressing styles.
- **Efficiency:** Short encodings for common patterns save memory.
- **Expressiveness:** Complex data structures (arrays, records, stacks) need flexible access.

---

## 2.3 Classification of Instructions — Structured Theory

**The 'How' Behind Classification:**
The 8086 instruction set is classified based on **functional purpose**. Each class manipulates data, control flow, or processor state differently.

| Class | Primary Purpose | Example Instructions |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Transfer** | Move data between registers, memory, I/O | `MOV`, `PUSH`, `POP`, `XCHG`, `LEA`, `IN`, `OUT` |
| **Arithmetic** | Perform math operations | `ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `DIV`, `INC`, `DEC`, `CMP` |
| **Logical** | Bitwise operations | `AND`, `OR`, `XOR`, `NOT`, `SHL`, `SHR` |
| **String Manipulation** | Operate on string data | `MOVS`, `CMPS`, `SCAS`, `STOS`, `LODS` |
| **Control Transfer** | Alter program flow (jumps, loops, calls) | `JMP`, `CALL`, `RET`, `Jcc`, `LOOP` |
| **Processor Control** | Control CPU flags and states | `STC`, `CLC`, `STI`, `CLI`, `NOP`, `HLT` |

**Real-World Utility in Engineering:**
- **Data Transfer:** Sensor data acquisition → register → memory storage
- **Arithmetic:** ADC conversions, filter computations, PID controller math
- **Logical:** Bit masking for I/O port control, status flag checking
- **Control Transfer:** Interrupt handling, real-time scheduling, state machines

---

## 2.4 Addressing Modes — Complete KTU Formula Sheet

| Mode | Syntax | Effective Address (EA) | Segment Used | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Immediate** | `MOV AX, 5000H` | Data is part of instruction | None (literal) | `MOV CX, 0AH` |
| **Register** | `MOV AX, BX` | Data is in register | None | `ADD AL, BL` |
| **Direct** | `MOV AX, [5000H]` | EA = Displacement | DS (default) | `MOV AL, [2000H]` |
| **Register Indirect** | `MOV AX, [BX]` | EA = [Base Reg] | DS (if BX/SI/DI) | `MOV [SI], AX` |
| **Based Relative** | `MOV AX, [BX+04H]` | EA = [BX or BP] + Disp | DS/SS | `MOV AL, [BX+5]` |
| **Indexed Relative** | `MOV AX, [SI+04H]` | EA = [SI or DI] + Disp | DS | `MOV [DI+2], BL` |
| **Based Indexed** | `MOV AX, [BX+SI]` | EA = [BX or BP] + [SI or DI] | DS/SS | `MOV [BX+DI], CX` |
| **Based Indexed + Disp** | `MOV AX, [BX+SI+04H]` | EA = [B/BP] + [S/D] + Disp | DS/SS | `MOV AL, [BP+SI+8]` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Default Segment Selection Rule:**
> - `[BP]` uses **SS (Stack Segment)** by default
> - All other memory references use **DS (Data Segment)** by default
> - **Stack operations** (`PUSH`, `POP`) always use **SS:SP**

---

## 2.5 Flag Register and Condition Codes

| Flag | Position | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **CF (Carry)** | Bit 0 | Set on unsigned overflow |
| **PF (Parity)** | Bit 2 | Set if result has even parity |
| **AF (Auxiliary Carry)** | Bit 4 | BCD arithmetic carry |
| **ZF (Zero)** | Bit 6 | Set if result is zero |
| **SF (Sign)** | Bit 7 | MSB of result (sign) |
| **OF (Overflow)** | Bit 11 | Signed overflow indicator |
| **TF (Trap)** | Bit 8 | Single-step debugging |
| **IF (Interrupt)** | Bit 9 | Maskable interrupt enable |
| **DF (Direction)** | Bit 10 | String operation direction |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Bubble Sort — Complete Assembly Derivation

**Problem:** Sort an array of **N = 5** unsigned bytes stored at offset `2000H` in the data segment in **ascending order** using Bubble Sort in 8086 assembly.

**Data Initial State (Memory at DS:2000H):**
```
Offset:   2000H  2001H  2002H  2003H  2004H
Value:     35H    12H    48H    07H    22H
```

**Pass-by-Pass Derivation:**

### Pass 1 (largest element bubbles to position 4):

| Comparison | [SI] | [SI+1] | Condition | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pair 1: (35, 12) | 35H | 12H | 35 > 12 | **Swap → [12, 35, 48, 07, 22]** |
| Pair 2: (35, 48) | 35H | 48H | 35 < 48 | No swap → [12, 35, 48, 07, 22] |
| Pair 3: (48, 07) | 48H | 07H | 48 > 07 | **Swap → [12, 35, 07, 48, 22]** |
| Pair 4: (48, 22) | 48H | 22H | 48 > 22 | **Swap → [12, 35, 07, 22, 48]** |

**Result after Pass 1:** `[12, 35, 07, 22, 48]` — Largest element (48) is in correct position.

### Pass 2 (next largest to position 3):

| Comparison | [SI] | [SI+1] | Condition | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pair 1: (12, 35) | 12H | 35H | 12 < 35 | No swap |
| Pair 2: (35, 07) | 35H | 07H | 35 > 07 | **Swap → [12, 07, 35, 22, 48]** |
| Pair 3: (35, 22) | 35H | 22H | 35 > 22 | **Swap → [12, 07, 22, 35, 48]** |

**Result after Pass 2:** `[12, 07, 22, 35, 48]`

### Pass 3:

| Comparison | [SI] | [SI+1] | Condition | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pair 1: (12, 07) | 12H | 07H | 12 > 07 | **Swap → [07, 12, 22, 35, 48]** |
| Pair 2: (12, 22) | 12H | 22H | 12 < 22 | No swap |

### Pass 4:

| Comparison | [SI] | [SI+1] | Condition | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pair 1: (07, 12) | 07H | 12H | 07 < 12 | No swap |

**Final Sorted Array:** `[07, 12, 22, 35, 48]` ✓

**Total Comparisons:** $\frac{n(n-1)}{2} = \frac{5 \times 4}{2} = 10$ comparisons

**Total Swaps in This Case:** $5$ swaps

---

## 3.2 Bubble Sort — 8086 Assembly Program

```assembly
; BUBBLE SORT - Ascending Order for N bytes
; Input:  Array at DS:2000H, Length N at DS:1000H
; Output: Sorted array at DS:2000H

        MOV  CX, [1000H]      ; Load N into CX
        DEC  CX                ; CX = N-1 (outer loop count)
        MOV  DX, CX            ; Save outer count

OUTER: MOV  SI, 2000H         ; Point SI to start of array
        MOV  CX, DX            ; Reset inner counter

INNER: MOV  AL, [SI]          ; Load current element
        MOV  BL, [SI+1]        ; Load next element
        CMP  AL, BL            ; Compare them
        JLE  NOSWAP            ; If AL <= BL, skip swap
        MOV  [SI], BL          ; Perform swap
        MOV  [SI+1], AL

NOSWAP:INC  SI                ; Move to next pair
        LOOP INNER             ; Repeat inner loop

        DEC  DX                ; Decrement outer count
        JNZ  OUTER             ; Repeat if not done
        HLT                    ; Halt processor
```

**Line-by-Line Derivation:**

1. **`MOV CX, [1000H]`** → Uses **Direct Addressing Mode** to load N from memory.
2. **`DEC CX`** → Decrements for outer loop passes (`N-1`).
3. **`MOV SI, 2000H`** → Uses **Immediate Addressing** to set pointer.
4. **`MOV AL, [SI]`** → Uses **Register Indirect Addressing** — `[SI]` means the data at address pointed by SI.
5. **`MOV BL, [SI+1]`** → Uses **Indexed Relative Addressing** — `[SI+1]` accesses next element.
6. **`CMP AL, BL`** → Sets flags based on subtraction `AL - BL`.
7. **`JLE NOSWAP`** → Conditional **Control Transfer** instruction using `ZF` and `SF` flags.
8. **`MOV [SI], BL`** → Stores swapped value (Register Indirect destination).
9. **`LOOP INNER`** → Decrements CX, jumps to INNER if CX ≠ 0.
10. **`HLT`** → **Processor Control** instruction to stop execution.

---

## 3.3 Effective Address Derivation for Based Indexed Mode

**Problem:** Calculate the physical address for the instruction:
`MOV AX, [BX + SI + 0200H]`
where `BX = 1000H`, `SI = 0500H`, `DS = 3000H`.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$\text{EA} = [\text{BX}] + [\text{SI}] + \text{Displacement}$$

Substituting values:

$$\text{EA} = 1000\text{H} + 0500\text{H} + 0200\text{H}$$

Adding the first two:

$$1000\text{H} + 0500\text{H} = 1500\text{H}$$

Adding displacement:

$$1500\text{H} + 0200\text{H} = 1700\text{H}$$

**Physical Address Calculation:**

$$\text{PA} = (\text{Segment} \times 10\text{H}) + \text{EA}$$

$$\text{PA} = (3000\text{H} \times 10\text{H}) + 1700\text{H}$$

$$\text{PA} = 30000\text{H} + 1700\text{H}$$

$$\text{PA} = 31700\text{H}$$

**Verification of Addressing Mode:** `BX + SI + displacement` → **Based Indexed with Displacement** mode.

---

## 3.4 Selection Sort Algorithm — Analytical Derivation

**Problem:** Sort the same array `[35, 12, 48, 07, 22]` using Selection Sort.

### Derivation of Pass Logic:

**Pass 1:** Find minimum in entire array
- Start: assume min = 35 at index 0
- Compare with 12 → 12 is smaller, update min = 12 at index 1
- Compare with 48 → no change
- Compare with 07 → 07 is smaller, update min = 07 at index 3
- Compare with 22 → no change
- **Swap** array[0] with array[3] → `[07, 12, 48, 35, 22]`

**Pass 2:** Find minimum in sub-array [12, 48, 35, 22]
- Assume min = 12 at index 1 (already at correct position)
- No swaps needed
- Array remains: `[07, 12, 48, 35, 22]`

**Pass 3:** Find minimum in sub-array [48, 35, 22]
- Assume min = 48 at index 2
- 35 < 48, update min = 35 at index 3
- 22 < 35, update min = 22 at index 4
- **Swap** array[2] with array[4] → `[07, 12, 22, 35, 48]`

**Pass 4:** Find minimum in sub-array [35, 48]
- 35 < 48, no change

**Final Sorted Array:** `[07, 12, 22, 35, 48]` ✓

**Total Swaps:** $3$ (versus $5$ in Bubble Sort) — Selection Sort is more efficient in swap operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison Count Formula (Selection Sort):**
> $$\text{Total Comparisons} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$$
>
> **Swap Count Formula (Selection Sort):**
> $$\text{Total Swaps} = n - 1$$
>
> This is a **major advantage** of Selection Sort over Bubble Sort for write-heavy applications like **EEPROM/Flash programming** in embedded systems.

---

## 3.5 Physical Address Calculation — Multi-Mode Examples

| Instruction | Mode | EA Formula | EA Value | PA (DS=2000H) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `MOV AL, [1500H]` | Direct | EA = 1500H | 1500H | 21500H |
| `MOV AL, [BX]` (BX=1500H) | Register Indirect | EA = [BX] | 1500H | 21500H |
| `MOV AL, [BX+5]` (BX=1500H) | Based Relative | EA = [BX] + 5 | 1505H | 21505H |
| `MOV AL, [SI+5]` (SI=1500H) | Indexed | EA = [SI] + 5 | 1505H | 21505H |
| `MOV AL, [BX+SI]` (BX=1000H, SI=500H) | Based Indexed | EA = [BX] + [SI] | 1500H | 21500H |
| `MOV AL, [BP+DI]` (BP=1000H, DI=500H) | Based Indexed | EA = [BP] + [DI] | 1500H | 21500H (SS) |
| `MOV AL, [BX+DI+10H]` | Based Idx+Disp | EA = [BX] + [DI] + 10H | 1510H | 21510H |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List any **three** addressing modes of 8086 with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

Addressing modes specify how the operand of an instruction is accessed.

1. **Immediate Addressing:** Data is part of the instruction itself.
   *Example:* `MOV AX, 5000H` — loads 5000H directly into AX.

2. **Direct Addressing:** The instruction provides the 16-bit offset address of the operand.
   *Example:* `MOV AX, [2000H]` — loads the word stored at DS:2000H into AX.

3. **Register Indirect Addressing:** A register holds the effective address of the operand.
   *Example:* `MOV AX, [BX]` — loads data from memory location pointed by BX.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Compare Bubble Sort and Selection Sort in terms of **number of swaps** for an array of N elements.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Bubble Sort | Selection Sort |
|---|---|---|
| Worst case swaps | $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $n-1$ |
| Best case swaps | $0$ (with optimization) | $n-1$ |
| Best for | Read-heavy data | Write-heavy data |

**Conclusion:** Selection Sort performs significantly fewer swaps, making it preferable in **embedded systems with limited write cycles** (e.g., EEPROM, Flash memory).

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Paper]** 

**(a)** Explain the **different addressing modes of 8086** with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write an 8086 assembly language program to sort an array of **10 unsigned bytes** in **ascending order** using **Bubble Sort** algorithm. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Definition:** An addressing mode specifies the rule for interpreting or modifying the address field of an instruction before the operand is actually referenced.

**The 8 Addressing Modes of 8086:**

**1. Immediate Addressing**
The operand (data) is specified directly in the instruction.
*Example:* `MOV CX, 0A05H`
- Size: Data size matches destination register.
- Used for: Loading constants.

**2. Register Addressing**
The operand is contained in a register.
*Example:* `MOV AX, BX`
- Fastest mode (no memory access).
- Cannot mix 8-bit and 16-bit registers.

**3. Direct Addressing**
The 16-bit offset is given directly in the instruction.
*Example:* `MOV AL, [5000H]`
- Effective Address = 5000H
- Default segment = DS
- Physical Address = (DS × 10H) + 5000H

**4. Register Indirect Addressing**
A register contains the offset address of the operand.
*Example:* `MOV [BX], AL` (where BX = 2000H)
- EA = [BX] = 2000H
- Allowed registers: BX, SI, DI (use DS); BP (uses SS)

**5. Based Relative Addressing**
EA = Base Register + 8/16-bit Displacement.
*Example:* `MOV AL, [BX + 05H]`
- Useful for accessing fields in a data structure.

**6. Indexed Relative Addressing**
EA = Index Register + 8/16-bit Displacement.
*Example:* `MOV AL, [SI + 1000H]`
- Useful for array access with constant offset.

**7. Based Indexed Addressing**
EA = Base Register + Index Register.
*Example:* `MOV AL, [BX + SI]`
- Useful for 2D array access.

**8. Based Indexed with Displacement**
EA = Base + Index + 8/16-bit Displacement.
*Example:* `MOV AL, [BX + SI + 0200H]`
- Most flexible and complex mode.

**Default Segment Rules:**
- `[BP]` uses **SS**
- All others use **DS** by default
- Override possible using segment prefix (e.g., `ES:[BX]`)

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Problem:** Sort 10 unsigned bytes in ascending order using Bubble Sort in 8086 assembly.

**Assumptions:**
- Array starts at offset `2000H` in the data segment
- Array length N = 10 stored at offset `1000H`

**Assembly Program:**

```assembly
        ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA

DATA    SEGMENT
        COUNT  DB  10
        ARRAY  DB  35H, 12H, 48H, 07H, 22H
        DB  61H, 18H, 09H, 54H, 33H
DATA    ENDS

CODE    SEGMENT
START:  MOV  AX, @DATA
        MOV  DS, AX
        MOV  CX, COUNT
        DEC  CX                ; Outer = N-1
        MOV  DX, CX            ; Save outer count

OUTER:  MOV  SI, OFFSET ARRAY  ; Point to start
        MOV  CX, DX            ; Reset inner counter

INNER:  MOV  AL, [SI]
        MOV  BL, [SI+1]
        CMP  AL, BL
        JBE  NOSWAP            ; If AL <= BL, skip

        ; SWAP LOGIC
        MOV  [SI], BL
        MOV  [SI+1], AL

NOSWAP: INC  SI                ; Move to next element
        LOOP INNER

        DEC  DX                ; Decrement outer
        JNZ  OUTER

        MOV  AH, 4CH
        INT  21H               ; Exit to DOS
CODE    ENDS
END START
```

**Trace Explanation:**

For N = 10:
- Outer loop runs **9 times** (CX goes 9 → 0).
- Inner loop comparisons: 9 + 8 + 7 + ... + 1 = **45 comparisons** (worst case).
- Total formula: $\frac{n(n-1)}{2} = \frac{10 \times 9}{2} = 45$ ✓

**Key Instructions Used:**
- `MOV [SI+1], AL` → **Indexed Relative Addressing Mode**
- `CMP AL, BL` → **Register Addressing**, sets flags
- `JBE NOSWAP` → **Control Transfer** (Jump if Below or Equal)
- `LOOP INNER` → **Control Transfer** (decrements CX, jumps if CX ≠ 0)

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **classification of 8086 instruction set** with examples for each class. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write an 8086 assembly program to perform **Selection Sort** on an array of N elements and explain the algorithm. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

The 8086 instruction set is classified into **6 functional groups**:

**1. Data Transfer Instructions**
Move data between registers, memory locations, and I/O ports without modifying it.

| Instruction | Operation | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `MOV` | Copy data | `MOV AX, BX` |
| `PUSH` | Push to stack | `PUSH AX` |
| `POP` | Pop from stack | `POP BX` |
| `XCHG` | Exchange | `XCHG AX, CX` |
| `LEA` | Load effective address | `LEA SI, [BX]` |
| `IN`/`OUT` | I/O port access | `IN AL, 60H` |

**2. Arithmetic Instructions**
Perform mathematical operations on data.

- **Addition:** `ADD`, `ADC` (with carry), `INC` (increment by 1)
- **Subtraction:** `SUB`, `SBB` (with borrow), `DEC`, `NEG` (negate)
- **Multiplication:** `MUL` (unsigned), `IMUL` (signed)
- **Division:** `DIV` (unsigned), `IDIV` (signed)
- **Comparison:** `CMP` (subtract without storing, sets flags)

*Example:* `ADD AX, BX` → AX = AX + BX, sets CF, ZF, SF, OF, PF, AF flags.

**3. Logical Instructions**
Perform bitwise operations and shifts.

- **Bitwise:** `AND`, `OR`, `XOR`, `NOT`
- **Shifts:** `SHL` (shift left), `SHR` (shift right)
- **Rotations:** `ROL`, `ROR`, `RCL`, `RCR`
- *Example:* `AND AL, 0FH` → masks upper 4 bits of AL.

**4. String Instructions**
Operate on sequences of bytes or words.

| Instruction | Operation |
|---|---|
| `MOVS` | Move string element |
| `CMPS` | Compare string elements |
| `SCAS` | Scan string for a value |
| `STOS` | Store accumulator to string |
| `LODS` | Load string element to accumulator |

Each works with `DF` flag for direction (`DF=0`: auto-increment, `DF=1`: auto-decrement) and `REP` prefix for repetition.

**5. Control Transfer Instructions**
Change the sequential flow of execution.

- **Unconditional:** `JMP` (jump), `CALL` (call subroutine), `RET` (return)
- **Conditional:** `JE/JZ`, `JNE/JNZ`, `JG`, `JL`, `JGE`, `JLE`, `JA`, `JB`
- **Loop:** `LOOP` (uses CX), `LOOPE`, `LOOPNE`
- **Interrupt:** `INT n`, `INTO`, `IRET`

*Example:* `JC LABEL` → Jump to LABEL if Carry Flag = 1.

**6. Processor Control Instructions**
Control CPU operating modes and flags.

- **Flag control:** `STC` (set CF), `CLC` (clear CF), `CMC` (complement CF)
- **Interrupt control:** `STI` (set IF), `CLI` (clear IF)
- **Synchronization:** `NOP` (no operation), `HLT` (halt), `WAIT`, `ESC`, `LOCK`

*Example:* `STI` → enables maskable interrupts by setting IF = 1.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Algorithm Explanation — Selection Sort:**

1. Find the **minimum element** in the unsorted portion of the array.
2. **Swap** it with the element at the beginning of the unsorted portion.
3. Move the **boundary** between sorted and unsorted portions one position right.
4. Repeat until the entire array is sorted.

**8086 Assembly Program:**

```assembly
        ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA

DATA    SEGMENT
        N      DB  05H
        ARRAY  DB  35H, 12H, 48H, 07H, 22H
DATA    ENDS

CODE    SEGMENT
START:  MOV  AX, @DATA
        MOV  DS, AX

        MOV  BL, N
        DEC  BL                ; Outer = N-1
        MOV  CH, BL            ; CH = outer counter

OUTER:  MOV  CL, CH            ; Inner counter
        MOV  SI, OFFSET ARRAY
        ADD  SI, CH            ; SI points to unsorted start
        DEC  SI                ; Adjust for offset
        MOV  AL, [SI]          ; Assume first is minimum
        MOV  DI, SI            ; DI = index of minimum

INNER:  INC  SI
        MOV  BL, [SI]
        CMP  BL, AL
        JAE  NOSWAP2           ; If current >= min, skip

        MOV  AL, BL            ; Update minimum
        MOV  DI, SI            ; Update min index

NOSWAP2:
        DEC  CL
        JNZ  INNER

        ; Swap [SI_start] with [DI]
        MOV  SI, OFFSET ARRAY
        ADD  SI, CH
        DEC  SI
        MOV  BL, [SI]
        MOV  AL, [DI]
        MOV  [SI], AL
        MOV  [DI], BL

        DEC  CH
        JNZ  OUTER

        HLT
CODE    ENDS
END START
```

**Algorithm Analysis:**

For N = 5:
- Total comparisons: $\frac{n(n-1)}{2} = 10$ comparisons
- Total swaps: $n - 1 = 4$ swaps
- **Time Complexity:** **O(n²)** in all cases
- **Space Complexity:** **O(1)** — in-place sorting
- **Stability:** **Not stable** (equal elements may be reordered)

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing addressing modes:** Students often write `MOV AX, BX` as "register indirect" — it is actually **Register Addressing**. Indirect means *the register contains the address*, not the data.
>
> 2. **Forgetting segment override:** When using `[BP]`, the default segment is **SS**, not **DS**. Students lose marks for stating DS incorrectly.
>
> 3. **LOOP instruction misuse:** `LOOP` decrements CX first, then jumps if CX ≠ 0. Initializing CX incorrectly is a frequent error.
>
> 4. **Swap logic errors:** In Bubble Sort, students often forget to reload registers after a swap. The `CMP` flags must be re-evaluated after every modification.
>
> 5. **Off-by-one in outer loop:** The outer loop must run **N-1 times**, not N times. Forgetting `DEC CX` loses 1-2 marks.
>
> 6. **Mixing addressing mode names:** "Based Indexed" vs "Indexed Based" — the standard term is always **Base first, Index second**.
>
> 7. **Missing MOV AH, 4CH / INT 21H:** Without proper termination, your program may "hang" during execution in labs and you lose practical marks.
>
> 8. **CMP does NOT store result:** Students often write that `CMP` performs subtraction and stores the answer. **Wrong** — it only sets flags.
>
> 9. **Direction Flag (DF) and string instructions:** Forgetting to use `CLD` (clear DF) before string operations causes incorrect direction.
>
> 10. **Bubble Sort with early termination optimization:** The optimized version (with a swap flag) is bonus-worthy but must be explained properly.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ✅ Sorting Algorithms

- **Bubble Sort:** Adjacent comparisons and swaps; **O(n²)**; uses nested loops with `LOOP` instruction in 8086.
- **Selection Sort:** Find minimum and swap; **O(n²)**; fewer swaps than Bubble Sort.
- **Comparisons formula:** $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ for both.
- **Stable algorithms preserve relative order** of equal elements.
- **In-place algorithms** use **O(1)** extra memory.

### ✅ 8086 Addressing Modes (8 Total)

- **Immediate** → constant in instruction
- **Register** → operand in register (fastest)
- **Direct** → 16-bit offset given
- **Register Indirect** → `[BX]`, `[SI]`, `[DI]`, `[BP]`
- **Based Relative** → `[BX+disp]`, `[BP+disp]`
- **Indexed Relative** → `[SI+disp]`, `[DI+disp]`
- **Based Indexed** → `[BX+SI]`, `[BP+DI]`, etc.
- **Based Indexed + Disp** → `[BX+SI+disp]`
- **Default segment:** BP → **SS**, others → **DS**
- **Physical Address** = (Segment × 10H) + Effective Address

### ✅ Instruction Classification (6 Groups)

- **Data Transfer:** `MOV`, `PUSH`, `POP`, `XCHG`, `LEA`, `IN`, `OUT`
- **Arithmetic:** `ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `DIV`, `INC`, `DEC`, `CMP`
- **Logical:** `AND`, `OR`, `XOR`, `NOT`, `SHL`, `SHR`, `ROL`, `ROR`
- **String:** `MOVS`, `CMPS`, `SCAS`, `STOS`, `LODS`
- **Control Transfer:** `JMP`, `CALL`, `RET`, `Jcc`, `LOOP`, `INT`
- **Processor Control:** `STC`, `CLC`, `NOP`, `HLT`, `STI`, `CLI`

### ✅ Key Register Notes

- **`CX` is the loop counter** for `LOOP` instruction — always pre-load correctly.
- **`SI` and `DI`** are default index registers for string operations.
- **`BX` is the base register** for memory access; **`BP`** for stack-relative access.
- **Flag register** has **9 active flags** controlling conditional jumps.

### ✅ High-Frequency KTU Questions

- Define addressing modes with examples — **3 marks / 7 marks** (very frequent)
- Bubble Sort program with explanation — **7 marks** (Module 1 staple)
- Compare sorting algorithms — **3 marks**
- Classify 8086 instructions with examples — **7 marks**
- Physical address calculation problems — **3 to 5 marks**
- Explain EA calculation for based-indexed mode — **3 marks**

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Strategy:**
> 1. Memorize the 8 addressing modes with their EA formulas.
> 2. Practice the Bubble Sort program — at least 5 times by hand.
> 3. Draw the EA calculation table for each mode.
> 4. Remember: **CX = loop counter, BX = base for data, BP = base for stack, SI/DI = index**.
> 5. Always specify the **default segment** in addressing mode answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stack and Subroutines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET504 - MICROPROCESSORS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stack and Subroutines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Stack — Formal KTU Definition

In the **Intel 8086 microprocessor architecture**, the **Stack** is a dedicated region of the Random Access Memory (RAM) that operates on the **Last-In, First-Out (LIFO)** principle. It is used by the CPU for the temporary storage of data, return addresses, flags, and processor state information during the execution of programs. The stack is managed by two dedicated 16-bit registers:

- **Stack Segment (SS)** register — holds the base (segment) address of the stack memory block in the 1 MB address space.
- **Stack Pointer (SP)** register — holds the 16-bit offset address of the **top-most element** of the stack within the active stack segment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — PBEET504 / Module 2**
> The 8086 stack grows **downward** in memory (from higher addresses to lower addresses). Every PUSH operation *decrements* SP, and every POP operation *increments* SP. The physical top of the stack is always at the address **SS : SP**.

## 1.2 The Subroutine — Formal KTU Definition

A **Subroutine** (also called a *procedure*, *function*, or *routine*) is a self-contained, named sequence of instructions that performs a specific, well-defined task. Subroutines are invoked from the main program (or from other subroutines) using the **CALL** instruction, and control is returned to the calling program using the **RET** (Return) instruction. The 8086 supports two physical forms of subroutines:

- **Near Subroutine** — resides in the same code segment as the caller; CALL pushes only the 16-bit **Instruction Pointer (IP)**.
- **Far Subroutine** — resides in a different code segment; CALL pushes both the 16-bit **Code Segment (CS)** register and the 16-bit **IP**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

### Analogy 1: The Stack as a Pile of Plates
Imagine a spring-loaded **plate dispenser** in a cafeteria. You can only add or remove plates from the **top**. The last plate you place on the pile is the **first** plate you take out. The dispenser pole (the **SP register**) always points to the **top plate**. If you add a plate, the pole moves *down*; if you take a plate, the pole moves *up*. This is exactly how the 8086 stack works in memory.

### Analogy 2: Subroutine as a Recipe Bookmark
While reading a cookbook, suppose you need a sub-recipe for "making white sauce" on page 45. You are currently on page 12. You **jot down page 12** on a sticky note (this is the **CALL** — saving the return address), flip to page 45, follow the recipe (this is the **subroutine body**), and when done, you **glance at the sticky note** to flip back to page 12 (this is the **RET** instruction). The sticky note in our analogy is the **stack**, and the page number is the **return address (IP or CS:IP)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the 8086 use a stack for subroutines?**
> Because subroutines can be **nested** (a subroutine may call another subroutine), the LIFO structure of the stack perfectly matches the natural back-tracking order of return addresses. The most recently saved return address is the first one that must be restored.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Conventions (Bolded for Quick Recall)

- **Stack Word Size:** **16 bits** (2 bytes) — the 8086 stack always pushes/pops 16-bit quantities.
- **Stack Growth Direction:** **Downward** (toward address 0).
- **Default Top-of-Stack Address at Reset:** **0000H** (i.e., SP = 0000H after processor reset).
- **Default Maximum Stack Capacity per Segment:** **65,536 bytes (64 KB)**, since SP is a 16-bit register.
- **CALL Near Push:** **2 bytes** (IP only).
- **CALL Far Push:** **4 bytes** (CS then IP).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Stack Segment Architecture — The "Why" and the "How"

The 8086 uses a **segmented memory model** to address 1 MB of memory using 16-bit registers. The stack is no exception. The **20-bit physical address** of any stack location is calculated using the standard segment:offset formula:

$$ \text{Physical Address} = (\text{Segment Base} \times 16) + \text{Offset} $$

For the stack specifically:

$$ \text{Physical Address of Top of Stack} = (\text{SS} \times 16) + \text{SP} $$

### Why is the segment multiplied by 16?
Because 8086 segment registers hold a **16-bit base** that is shifted left by 4 bits (multiplied by 16) to produce a **20-bit physical address**. This trick allows 16-bit registers to span 1 MB of memory with 64 KB granularity per segment.

### Why does the stack grow downward?
The 8086 designers chose downward growth so that the **stack pointer initialization is simpler** — the programmer sets SP to the highest address of the chosen stack region, and PUSH naturally moves into unused lower memory. This also makes the stack resilient to overflow at its lower boundary.

## 2.2 Stack Instructions — PUSH and POP

### PUSH Source
The **PUSH** instruction copies a 16-bit value (register, segment register, or memory word) onto the top of the stack. Execution steps:

1. `SP \leftarrow SP - 2` (decrement SP by 2, since stack word = 2 bytes).
2. Write the 16-bit source value to memory location `SS:SP`.

### POP Destination
The **POP** instruction copies the 16-bit value at the top of the stack into the destination. Execution steps:

1. Read the 16-bit value from memory location `SS:SP`.
2. `SP \leftarrow SP + 2` (increment SP by 2).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Fact:** PUSH and POP do **not** affect any **flag** in the FLAGS register. Only arithmetic, logic, and comparison instructions modify flags. This is a classic 2-mark KTU question.

## 2.3 Subroutine Instructions — CALL and RET

### CALL Label
Transfers control to a subroutine and saves the return address on the stack.

- **Near CALL (intra-segment):**
  1. `SP \leftarrow SP - 2`
  2. Push 16-bit IP onto stack (IP is the offset of the instruction *after* the CALL).
  3. `IP \leftarrow Target Offset` (16-bit)

- **Far CALL (inter-segment):**
  1. `SP \leftarrow SP - 2`
  2. Push CS onto stack.
  3. `SP \leftarrow SP - 2`
  4. Push IP onto stack.
  5. `CS \leftarrow Target Segment`, `IP \leftarrow Target Offset`

### RET (Near) / RETF (Far)
- **Near RET:** Pops 16-bit value from stack into IP, then `SP \leftarrow SP + 2`.
- **Far RET (RETF):** Pops IP first, then CS, each with `SP \leftarrow SP + 2`.

## 2.4 Parameter Passing Techniques (High-Yield KTU Topic)

There are **four standard ways** to pass parameters to 8086 subroutines:

| Method | Mechanism | Pros | Cons |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Registers** | Pass values in registers (e.g., AX, BX) | Fastest, simplest | Limited by register count |
| **Memory (Global Variables)** | Store parameters in fixed RAM locations | Unlimited parameters | No data isolation |
| **Stack** | Push parameters before CALL, pop inside subroutine | Clean, supports recursion | Slower, order matters |
| **Public/External Data** | Use EXTERN/PUBLIC directives (assembler-specific) | Modular linking | Assembler-dependent |

> [!NOTE]
> The **stack-based parameter passing** is the most theoretically important for KTU. Inside the subroutine, parameters are accessed via **BP** register. Standard pattern:
> 1. `PUSH BP` (save caller's BP)
> 2. `MOV BP, SP` (BP now points to saved BP)
> 3. Access parameters at `[BP+4]`, `[BP+6]`, etc. (return address is at `[BP+2]`)
> 4. `POP BP` before `RET`

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for Stack & Subroutines**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Note |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Physical address of stack top | $(\text{SS} \times 16) + \text{SP}$ | 20-bit result |
| Stack word size | $\text{2 bytes} = \text{16 bits}$ | Per PUSH/POP |
| SP after PUSH | $\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 2$ | Bytes |
| SP after POP | $\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} + 2$ | Bytes |
| SP after Near CALL | $\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 2$ | Saves IP only |
| SP after Far CALL | $\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 4$ | Saves CS, then IP |
| Number of bytes pushed per Near CALL | $2$ | IP only |
| Number of bytes pushed per Far CALL | $4$ | CS, then IP |
| Effective stack space per segment | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ bytes | $64 \text{ KB}$ |
| Max nesting depth (theoretical) | $2^{15} = 32{,}768$ near calls | Limited by 64 KB |
| Offset of saved IP (within subroutine) | $[\text{BP} + 2]$ | After `MOV BP,SP` |
| Offset of 1st stack parameter | $[\text{BP} + 4]$ | Push order matters |

### Engineering Utility
The stack-subroutine mechanism is the **backbone of all high-level language function calls** (C, Pascal, Ada). Compilers translate every function call into a sequence of PUSH (for arguments and return address), CALL, body, and RET/POP. Understanding the 8086 stack-subroutine contract is therefore foundational to **compiler design, OS kernel development (context switching), and embedded firmware architecture**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Physical Address Calculation

**Problem:** SS = `2000H`, SP = `FE20H`. Find the physical address of the top of the stack.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify segment base and offset.
- Segment base = SS = `2000H`
- Offset = SP = `FE20H`

**Step 2:** Apply the 20-bit physical address formula.
$$ \text{Physical Address} = (\text{SS} \times 16) + \text{SP} $$

**Step 3:** Multiply SS by 16 (equivalent to left-shift by 4 bits, i.e., append a hex 0).

$$ \text{SS} \times 16 = 2000\text{H} \times 10\text{H} = 20000\text{H} $$

**Step 4:** Add SP to get the 20-bit physical address.

$$ \text{Physical Address} = 20000\text{H} + \text{FE20H} $$

**Step 5:** Perform the hex addition column by column.

$$ \begin{aligned} 20000\text{H} &+ \text{FE20H} \\ \hline 0 + 0 &= 0 \\ 0 + 2 &= 2 \\ 0 + E &= E \\ 0 + F &= F \\ 2 + 0 &= 2 \\ \hline \end{aligned} $$

**Step 6:** Final result.

$$ \text{Physical Address} = 2\text{FE20H} $$

**Answer:** The top of the stack is at physical address **`2FE20H`**.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Trace of PUSH and POP on SP

**Problem:** Initial SP = `1000H`. Trace SP after each instruction: `PUSH AX`, `PUSH BX`, `POP CX`, `PUSH DX`.

### Step-by-Step Trace

| Step | Instruction | SP Before | SP After | SP Change | Reason |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | `PUSH AX` | `1000H` | `0FFEH` | $-2$ | PUSH decrements SP by 2 |
| 2 | `PUSH BX` | `0FFEH` | `0FFCH` | $-2$ | PUSH decrements SP by 2 |
| 3 | `POP CX`  | `0FFCH` | `0FFEH` | $+2$ | POP increments SP by 2 |
| 4 | `PUSH DX` | `0FFEH` | `0FFCH` | $-2$ | PUSH decrements SP by 2 |

**Final SP = `0FFCH`**

### Verification using the formula
$$ \text{SP}_{\text{final}} = \text{SP}_{\text{initial}} - 2 - 2 + 2 - 2 = 1000\text{H} - 4\text{H} = 0\text{FFCH} $$

Both methods agree.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Nested Subroutine CALL/RET Stack Trace

**Problem:** Main program calls NEAR subroutine `SUB1`. `SUB1` calls NEAR subroutine `SUB2`. `SUB2` returns, then `SUB1` returns. Initial SP = `2000H`. Show the stack contents and SP after each step.

### Step-by-Step Trace

Assume the offset of the instruction *after* the `CALL SUB1` in MAIN is `1500H`, and the offset of the instruction *after* the `CALL SUB2` in SUB1 is `1620H`.

**Step A:** `CALL SUB1` in MAIN executes.
- Push return address (offset = `1500H`) onto stack.
- SP decrements by 2.

$$ \text{SP}_{\text{new}} = 2000\text{H} - 2 = 1\text{FFEH} $$

- Memory at `SS:1FFEH` = `1500H` (low byte `00`, high byte `15`).

**Step B:** Inside `SUB1`, `CALL SUB2` executes.
- Push return address (offset = `1620H`) onto stack.
- SP decrements by 2.

$$ \text{SP}_{\text{new}} = 1\text{FFEH} - 2 = 1\text{FFCH} $$

- Memory at `SS:1FFCH` = `1620H`.

**Step C:** `SUB2` executes `RET` (Near).
- Pop top of stack into IP.
- IP receives `1620H`, SP increments by 2.

$$ \text{SP}_{\text{new}} = 1\text{FFCH} + 2 = 1\text{FFEH} $$

- Control returns to `SUB1` at offset `1620H`.

**Step D:** `SUB1` continues and executes `RET` (Near).
- Pop top of stack into IP.
- IP receives `1500H`, SP increments by 2.

$$ \text{SP}_{\text{new}} = 1\text{FFEH} + 2 = 2000\text{H} $$

- Control returns to MAIN at offset `1500H`.

### Final Stack State
The stack is **completely empty** (logically); SP is back to its initial value of `2000H`. This confirms the LIFO correctness of the nested call-return mechanism.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Stack-Based Parameter Passing (Detailed)

**Problem:** Write the 8086 assembly to call `ADDER` with two parameters `N1` and `N2` (in AX and BX) and return the sum in CX. Use the stack-based BP-offset method.

### Solution with Line-by-Line Analysis

**Caller (MAIN) code:**

```asm
    PUSH  BX        ; push 2nd parameter (N2) onto stack
    PUSH  AX        ; push 1st parameter (N1) onto stack
    CALL  ADDER     ; pushes return address (IP)
    ; after RET, CX = N1 + N2
```

**Subroutine (ADDER) code:**

```asm
ADDER PROC
    PUSH  BP        ; save caller's BP on stack
    MOV   BP, SP    ; BP now points to saved BP location
    MOV   AX, [BP+4] ; AX = N1  (1st parameter)
    MOV   BX, [BP+6] ; BX = N2  (2nd parameter)
    ADD   AX, BX    ; AX = N1 + N2
    MOV   CX, AX    ; return sum in CX
    POP   BP        ; restore caller's BP
    RET   4         ; pop IP, then discard 4 bytes (2 params × 2 bytes)
ADDER ENDP
```

### Stack Frame Diagram at Entry of ADDER

| Address (descending) | Content | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `SS:SP` (old) | Return IP (2 bytes) | Pushed by CALL |
| `SS:SP+2` | N1 (AX value) | Pushed by main |
| `SS:SP+4` | N2 (BX value) | Pushed by main |
| `SS:SP+6` | — | Lower memory |

After `PUSH BP` and `MOV BP, SP`:
- `[BP]` = saved BP (caller's BP)
- `[BP+2]` = Return IP
- `[BP+4]` = N1
- `[BP+6]` = N2

This matches our formula table in Section 2.5.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table for KTU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Comparison Matrix — Read this before every KTU exam**

| Feature | Near CALL | Far CALL | Near RET | Far RET (RETF) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Pushes onto stack | IP | CS, then IP | — | — |
| Pops from stack | — | — | IP | IP, then CS |
| Bytes pushed/popped | 2 | 4 | 2 | 4 |
| Operands in CALL | 16-bit offset | 32-bit ptr (seg:off) | — | — |
| Use case | Intra-segment | Inter-segment | Intra-segment | Inter-segment |
| Mnemonic (CALL) | `CALL proc` | `CALL FAR PTR proc` | — | — |
| Mnemonic (RET) | `RET` | — | `RET` | `RETF` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — PUSH Instruction Micro-Operation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: PUSH Source Executed] --> B[Step 1: Decode Source Operand 16-bit]
    B --> C[Step 2: SP = SP - 2]
    C --> D[Step 3: Calculate Physical Address PA = SS x 16 + SP]
    D --> E[Step 4: Write Low Byte of Source to PA]
    E --> F[Step 5: Write High Byte of Source to PA + 1]
    F --> G[End: Top of Stack Updated]
    styleA[Start: PUSH Source Executed]:::termStyle
    styleG[End: Top of Stack Updated]:::termStyle
    classDef termStyle fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#202124
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — POP Instruction Micro-Operation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: POP Destination Executed] --> B1[Step 1: Calculate Physical Address PA = SS x 16 + SP]
    B1 --> C1[Step 2: Read Low Byte from PA into Destination Low]
    C1 --> D1[Step 3: Read High Byte from PA + 1 into Destination High]
    D1 --> E1[Step 4: SP = SP + 2]
    E1 --> F1[End: Top of Stack Removed]
    styleA1[Start: POP Destination Executed]:::termStyle
    styleF1[End: Top of Stack Removed]:::termStyle
    classDef termStyle fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#34A853,stroke-width:2px,color:#202124
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Near CALL and Near RET Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[MAIN Program] --> S2[Execute CALL NEAR SUB1]
    S2 --> S3[SP = SP - 2]
    S3 --> S4[Push IP of Next Instruction onto Stack]
    S4 --> S5[IP = Target Offset of SUB1]
    S5 --> S6[Execute SUB1 Body]
    S6 --> S7{SUB1 Calls SUB2?}
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Push new IP and Jump to SUB2]
    S8 --> S9[Execute SUB2 Body]
    S9 --> S10[SUB2 Executes RET]
    S10 --> S11[Pop IP from Stack, SP = SP + 2]
    S11 --> S12[Resume SUB1 After Call]
    S7 -- No --> S13[SUB1 Executes RET]
    S12 --> S13
    S13 --> S14[Pop IP from Stack, SP = SP + 2]
    S14 --> S15[Resume MAIN After Call]
    styleS1[MAIN Program]:::termStyle
    styleS15[Resume MAIN After Call]:::termStyle
    classDef termStyle fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#FBBC04,stroke-width:2px,color:#202124
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Architecture Diagram — Stack Memory Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph StackMemory["Stack Segment SS in 1 MB Memory"]
        H1[HIGH ADDRESS - Stack Base SP starts here at reset]
        H2[Lower Address - Grows Downward on PUSH]
        H3[Lowest Used Address - Stack Limit]
    end
    H1 --> H2
    H2 --> H3
    styleH1[HIGH ADDRESS Stack Base SP starts here at reset]:::styleTop
    styleH2[Lower Address Grows Downward on PUSH]:::styleMid
    styleH3[Lowest Used Address Stack Limit]:::styleBot
    classDef styleTop fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,stroke-width:2px,color:#202124
    classDef styleMid fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#FBBC04,stroke-width:2px,color:#202124
    classDef styleBot fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#34A853,stroke-width:2px,color:#202124
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Flow — Parameter Passing via Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Caller PUSH Parameter 2 BX] --> P2[Caller PUSH Parameter 1 AX]
    P2 --> P3[Caller CALL Subroutine]
    P3 --> P4[Subroutine PUSH BP]
    P4 --> P5[Subroutine MOV BP SP]
    P5 --> P6[Subroutine Access BP plus 4 and BP plus 6]
    P6 --> P7[Subroutine Compute and Store Result]
    P7 --> P8[Subroutine POP BP]
    P8 --> P9[Subroutine RET Optional Immediate Cleanup]
    P9 --> P10[Caller Receives Result in Register]
    styleP1[Caller PUSH Parameter 2 BX]:::termStyle
    styleP10[Caller Receives Result in Register]:::termStyle
    classDef termStyle fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#202124
```
