topics.title: Machine Cycles, Instruction Cycle and T States
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET504 - MICROPROCESSORS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Machine Cycles, Instruction Cycle and T States

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Hierarchy of Execution in the 8086 Microprocessor

In the **Intel 8086 microprocessor**, every operation performed by the CPU is governed by a strict hierarchical timing framework. Understanding the relationship between the **Instruction Cycle**, **Machine Cycle**, and **T-State** is fundamental for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations in Microprocessors and Embedded Systems, as questions frequently test the calculation of execution time for given instruction sequences.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 2)**
> The **Instruction Cycle** is the complete sequence of events executed by the microprocessor to fetch, decode, and execute a single machine instruction. It is composed of one or more **Machine Cycles**, and each Machine Cycle is further subdivided into a fixed number of **T-States** (clock periods).

---

## 1.2 Formal Definitions Aligned with KTU Syllabus

### A. T-State (Clock Cycle / Clock Period)
The **T-State** is the smallest unit of time recognized by the microprocessor. It is defined as **one clock period**, i.e., the time interval between two consecutive rising (or falling) edges of the system clock signal. All internal and external operations of the 8086 are synchronized to this fundamental time quantum.

### B. Machine Cycle (MC / Bus Cycle)
A **Machine Cycle** represents the elementary bus operation required to access a memory location, an I/O port, or an internal register. It consists of a fixed number of T-States during which the CPU performs a single specific bus activity such as *memory read*, *memory write*, *I/O read*, *I/O write*, or *instruction fetch*. The 8086 BIU initiates a new machine cycle whenever it needs to interact with external buses.

### C. Instruction Cycle (IC)
The **Instruction Cycle** is the highest-level operation encompassing the complete processing of one instruction. It begins with the **fetch** of the opcode byte(s), includes the **decode** phase, and concludes with the **execute** phase. An instruction cycle is composed of one or more machine cycles, which in turn consist of multiple T-States.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Office Worker and His Day

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: Reading a Recipe Book**
> Imagine a chef reading a recipe book to prepare a dish:
>
> - The **T-State** is like the *tick of a clock* — the chef can only perform one micro-action per tick (e.g., read one word, stir once).
> - The **Machine Cycle** is like reading one *complete page* of the recipe — a self-contained action with a defined start and end.
> - The **Instruction Cycle** is the act of *cooking the entire dish* — it involves reading multiple pages (machine cycles) before the dish is ready to serve.

This analogy perfectly captures the nested relationship:

$$\text{Instruction Cycle} \;\supset\; \text{Machine Cycles} \;\supset\; \text{T-States}$$

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics for the 8086 Microprocessor

The following metrics are **exam-critical** and must be memorized for KTU valuations:

| Parameter | Standard Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| System Clock Frequency | **5 MHz** |
| Clock Period (T-State) | **200 ns** |
| Memory Read/Write MC | **4T** |
| I/O Read/Write MC | **4T** |
| Opcode Fetch MC | **4T** |
| Opcode Fetch with Segment Override | **6T** |
| Wait State Duration | **1T** per inserted state |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners expect students to clearly distinguish between the *Internal Execution Time* (handled by the EU) and the *External Bus Activity Time* (handled by the BIU). Internal EU operations do not consume machine cycles when instructions are pre-fetched in the queue.

---

## 1.5 The 8086 Dual-Unit Architecture (Prefetching Insight)

Unlike the 8085, the 8086 incorporates a **6-byte instruction queue** managed by the **Bus Interface Unit (BIU)**, while the **Execution Unit (EU)** decodes and executes instructions. This parallelism causes the Instruction Cycle to overlap with subsequent instruction fetches — a phenomenon known as **pipelining** (specifically, *instruction look-ahead*).

- The **BIU** is responsible for fetching machine cycles (bus cycles).
- The **EU** is responsible for execution, often without requiring the bus.

This means that for short instructions, the actual time taken is the time for the *fetch* machine cycle plus the *execute* operation time, frequently overlapping with the next instruction's fetch.

---

## 1.6 Quick Reference: KTU Terminology Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terms for KTU 2024 Examinations:**
> - **T-State = Clock Period = T**
> - **Machine Cycle = Bus Cycle = MC**
> - **Instruction Cycle = Fetch + Decode + Execute**
> - **Clock = Oscillator input to CLK pin**
> - **Wait State = Extra T-State inserted via READY pin LOW**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Analysis: T-State → Machine Cycle → Instruction Cycle

Let us now break down the three levels of CPU timing rigorously, so each concept can be quantified for KTU numerical problems.

---

### Level 1: T-State (The Atomic Time Unit)

The T-State is a direct derivative of the system clock signal feeding the **CLK** pin of the 8086.

$$T \;=\; \frac{1}{f_{\text{CLK}}}$$

For the standard 8086 with a **5 MHz crystal**, the T-state duration is:

$$T \;=\; \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}} \;=\; 200 \text{ ns}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why does T-State matter?**
> Every signal transition on the address, data, and control buses occurs at a T-State boundary. KTU questions on **bus timing diagrams** require you to mark signal states at each T-edge precisely.

---

### Level 2: Machine Cycle (The Bus Transaction Unit)

A machine cycle corresponds to a single bus transaction. The 8086 BIU performs the following types of machine cycles:

- **Opcode Fetch (OF):** Reading instruction bytes from memory.
- **Memory Read (MR):** Reading operand data from memory.
- **Memory Write (MW):** Writing operand data to memory.
- **I/O Read (IOR):** Reading from an I/O port.
- **I/O Write (IOW):** Writing to an I/O port.
- **Interrupt Acknowledge (INTA):** Reading interrupt vector.
- **Bus Request / Hold Acknowledge:** DMA coordination.

Each machine cycle consumes a **fixed number of T-States** based on its type and operating conditions (e.g., wait states).

---

### Level 3: Instruction Cycle (The Complete Operation)

An instruction cycle includes:

1. **Fetch Phase:** BIU reads opcode (and operands if needed) from memory.
2. **Decode Phase:** EU's instruction decoder interprets the opcode.
3. **Execute Phase:** EU performs arithmetic/logic/data movement.
4. **Store Phase:** If required, results are written back.

For example, the instruction `MOV AX, BX` (opcode 8B C3, 2 bytes) requires:
- **1 Opcode Fetch Machine Cycle** (BIU) = 4T
- **0 Bus Machine Cycles** for execution (register-to-register operation in EU)

The total time is dominated by the fetch, with internal execution time being relatively negligible compared to bus activity.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **mandatory** for KTU 2024 ESE numerical problems. Memorize the column boundaries and units.

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Clock Period | $T \;=\; \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{CLK}}}$ | ns, µs, ms | $f_{\text{CLK}}$ in Hz |
| One T-State Time | $T$ | ns | For 5 MHz, $T$ = 200 ns |
| Machine Cycle Time | $T_{\text{MC}} \;=\; n \times T$ | ns | $n$ = T-states in that MC |
| Total Instruction Time | $T_{\text{IC}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{m} (n_i \times T)$ | ns | Sum over all machine cycles |
| With Wait States | $T_{\text{MC}} \;=\; (n + w) \times T$ | ns | $w$ = number of wait states |
| Instruction Throughput | $\eta \;=\; \dfrac{N_{\text{inst}}}{T_{\text{IC}}}$ | inst/µs | Useful for benchmarks |
| Instruction Byte Fetched | $B$ | bytes | 8086 fetches 2 bytes per queue load |
| Queue Refill Trigger | When $\geq 2$ bytes free | – | For even-aligned code |

---

## 2.3 The 8086 Internal Architecture and Timing Interaction

The 8086 has a **striated architecture** with two cooperating units:

- **BIU (Bus Interface Unit):** Handles all external bus operations, instruction prefetching, queue management, address generation, and segment registers.
- **EU (Execution Unit):** Performs decoding, ALU operations, register management, and flag updates.

This separation is critical because:

1. While the EU is busy executing the *current* instruction, the BIU is simultaneously *fetching the next* instruction into the queue.
2. The 8086 reads **2 bytes** (1 word) per bus cycle when fetching instruction bytes — this is called a **word fetch** at even addresses.
3. At **odd addresses**, the 8086 requires **two consecutive byte fetches**, effectively doubling the bus activity.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Understanding machine cycles and T-states is critical for designing real-time embedded systems. Engineers must guarantee that CPU response to an interrupt occurs within bounded time — this requires precise knowledge of instruction cycle durations.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Application

In **embedded automotive ECUs**, **aerospace flight controllers**, and **medical devices**, deterministic timing is paramount. The 8086 (or its modern derivatives) executes time-critical control loops where:

- A sensor interrupt must be serviced within **N microseconds**.
- The interrupt handler's instruction sequence has a *predictable* execution time only if every machine cycle's T-state count is known.

This is why the formula:

$$T_{\text{critical}} \;=\; \sum_{j} \bigl( n_j + w_j \bigr) \cdot T$$

is evaluated for the *worst-case* path (maximum wait states, maximum memory access time, alignment penalties).

---

## 2.5 Detailed Machine Cycle Breakdown for the 8086

The 8086 BIU uses **status lines S2̄, S1̄, S0̄** to encode the current machine cycle type:

| S2̄ | S1̄ | S0̄ | Machine Cycle Type |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 0 | 0 | 0 | Interrupt Acknowledge |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | I/O Read |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | I/O Write |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | Halt |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | Instruction Fetch |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | Memory Read |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | Memory Write |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | Passive (no bus cycle) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Examiners often ask students to identify the bus cycle type from the **status line pattern** or from a timing diagram. Memorize this 3-bit encoding carefully.

---

## 2.6 Wait States and Bus Cycle Extension

When the addressed memory or I/O device is **too slow** to respond within the standard 4T-states, it must assert the **READY** pin LOW. This inserts **wait states** — extra T-states during which the CPU waits for the device to complete its operation.

The extended machine cycle becomes:

$$T_{\text{MC,extended}} \;=\; (n + w) \cdot T$$

For KTU problems, if a problem specifies *"2 wait states per memory access"*, you must add **2T** to every memory/I/O machine cycle before computing total execution time.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Clock Period from Frequency

**Problem Context:** Given an 8086 system operating with a clock frequency of 5 MHz, derive the duration of one T-State.

### Step 1: Recall the fundamental relationship
The clock period is the reciprocal of the clock frequency:

$$T \;=\; \frac{1}{f_{\text{CLK}}}$$

### Step 2: Substitute the given frequency value
The given frequency is 5 MHz:

$$T \;=\; \frac{1}{5 \,\text{MHz}}$$

### Step 3: Expand the unit explicitly
Express MHz in Hz:

$$T \;=\; \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{6} \,\text{Hz}}$$

### Step 4: Perform the division

$$T \;=\; 2 \times 10^{-7} \,\text{seconds}$$

### Step 5: Convert to nanoseconds for clarity

$$T \;=\; 200 \,\text{ns}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,T \;=\; 200 \,\text{ns}\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Execution Time for an Instruction Sequence

**Problem Context:** Compute the total time taken by the 8086 to execute the following instruction sequence. Assume the clock frequency is **5 MHz** and **1 wait state** is inserted for every memory access.

```text
MOV AL, [2000H]    ; Memory Read  (3 bytes, 1 MR machine cycle)
ADD AL, BL         ; Register add (2 bytes, 0 MR MC)
MOV [3000H], AL    ; Memory Write (3 bytes, 1 MW machine cycle)
```

### Step 1: Identify each machine cycle type and its T-state count

For 8086 (without wait states):
- Opcode Fetch (OF) = 4T
- Memory Read (MR) = 4T
- Memory Write (MW) = 4T

### Step 2: For the first instruction `MOV AL, [2000H]`
The 8086 must:
- Fetch 3 bytes (one OF cycle of 4T for the first 2 bytes + a second OF cycle for the 3rd byte, but since the queue pre-fetches, we count **1 OF = 4T** for the first 2 bytes and consider the 3rd as part of the next fetch if aligned).
- Perform 1 Memory Read (4T).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** When 8086 fetches a 3-byte instruction, the BIU performs 1 OF (4T) for the first 2 bytes from the queue, then a 2nd OF (4T) for the 3rd byte. For simplicity in KTU problems, assume each OF = 4T.

**Number of Machine Cycles:**
- 2 × OF (4T each) = 8T
- 1 × MR (4T + 1 wait = 5T)

**Subtotal for Instruction 1:**

$$T_1 \;=\; 8T + 5T \;=\; 13T$$

### Step 3: For the second instruction `ADD AL, BL`
This is a register-to-register ALU operation. No bus activity is required once the opcode is fetched.
- 1 × OF = 4T (2-byte instruction fits in one word fetch)

**Subtotal for Instruction 2:**

$$T_2 \;=\; 4T$$

### Step 4: For the third instruction `MOV [3000H], AL`
This requires:
- 2 × OF (4T each) for fetching 3 bytes = 8T
- 1 × MW (4T + 1 wait = 5T)

**Subtotal for Instruction 3:**

$$T_3 \;=\; 8T + 5T \;=\; 13T$$

### Step 5: Sum all subtotals

$$T_{\text{total}} \;=\; T_1 + T_2 + T_3 \;=\; 13T + 4T + 13T \;=\; 30T$$

### Step 6: Substitute T = 200 ns

$$T_{\text{total}} \;=\; 30 \times 200 \,\text{ns} \;=\; 6000 \,\text{ns} \;=\; 6 \,\mu s$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,T_{\text{total}} \;=\; 6 \,\mu\text{s}\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Computing Throughput

**Problem Context:** An 8086 system executes **10,000 instructions per second** at 5 MHz. Each instruction consumes on average **4 machine cycles** and each machine cycle consumes **4 T-states**. Verify the consistency of this throughput.

### Step 1: Compute time per instruction
Average machine cycles per instruction = 4
Average T-states per instruction:

$$N_T \;=\; 4 \times 4 \;=\; 16 \;T\text{-states}$$

### Step 2: Compute time per instruction in nanoseconds
With T = 200 ns:

$$T_{\text{inst}} \;=\; 16 \times 200 \,\text{ns} \;=\; 3200 \,\text{ns} \;=\; 3.2 \,\mu\text{s}$$

### Step 3: Compute instructions per second (throughput)

$$\eta \;=\; \frac{1}{3.2 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 312{,}500 \,\text{instructions/second}$$

### Step 4: Compare with the given throughput

The claimed throughput (10,000 inst/s) corresponds to a much longer instruction time:

$$T_{\text{claimed}} \;=\; \frac{1}{10{,}000} \;=\; 100 \,\mu\text{s} \;=\; 100{,}000 \,\text{ns}$$

This means each instruction actually takes:

$$N_T^{\text{claimed}} \;=\; \frac{100{,}000}{200} \;=\; 500 \,T\text{-states}$$

**Conclusion:** The claimed throughput implies an average of **500 T-states per instruction**, indicating the workload must be heavily memory-bound with many wait states and complex addressing. The hypothetical 16 T-state average is unrealistic for instruction-rich code.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: 8086 Machine Cycle Counts

> [!TIP]
> **Use this matrix as the master reference for KTU 2024 Module 2 problems.** Each cell shows T-state count for a given access.

| Operation Type | T-States (No Wait) | With 1 Wait State | Notes |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Opcode Fetch (OF) — Even Address | 4T | 5T | Standard word fetch |
| Opcode Fetch (OF) — Odd Address | 8T (2 cycles) | 10T | Penalty for misalignment |
| OF with Segment Override Prefix | 6T | 7T | e.g., `MOV AX, CS:[BX]` |
| Memory Read (MR) | 4T | 5T | Operand fetch |
| Memory Write (MW) | 4T | 5T | Operand store |
| I/O Read (IOR) | 4T | 5T | IN instruction |
| I/O Write (IOW) | 4T | 5T | OUT instruction |
| Interrupt Acknowledge (INTA) | 6T | – | Hardwired by 8086 |
| HLT / Bus Hold | Idle T | – | No bus cycle active |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Identifying T-States from a Timing Diagram Question

**Problem:** A KTU question provides a timing diagram showing:
- T1: ALE high, address valid, status lines encode "Memory Read"
- T2: ALE low, address latched
- T3: RD̄ goes low
- T4: Data sampled by CPU, RD̄ goes high

**Step 1:** Identify machine cycle boundary:
- T1 to T4 represents **one Memory Read Machine Cycle = 4T**.

**Step 2:** Calculate total time:

$$T_{\text{MC}} \;=\; 4 \times 200 \,\text{ns} \;=\; 800 \,\text{ns}$$

**Step 3:** If a `READY` LOW signal is shown during T3 (stretching to T5), then:

$$T_{\text{MC,extended}} \;=\; (4 + 1) \times 200 \,\text{ns} \;=\; 1000 \,\text{ns}$$

**Step 4:** Compare to expected memory access time:
- If the memory device requires **600 ns** access, then wait states are needed because 4T = 800 ns > 600 ns? Wait — 800 ns > 600 ns, so NO wait states are needed.
- Conversely, if memory requires **900 ns**, then **1 wait state** is needed because (4T = 800 ns) < 900 ns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget that wait states are inserted when memory is **slower** than the standard 4T bus cycle. A 5 MHz 8086 (T = 200 ns) requires memory with access time ≤ **800 ns** to avoid wait states.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Flow of CPU Timing (T-State → Machine Cycle → Instruction Cycle)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the nested relationship and the BIU/EU interaction that drives the 8086 timing model:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["System Clock CLK"] --> B["T-State One Clock Period"]
    B --> C["Machine Cycle Bus Transaction"]
    C --> D["Instruction Cycle Fetch Decode Execute"]
    D --> E["Instruction Complete Result in Memory or Register"]
    F["BIU Bus Interface Unit"] --> C
    G["EU Execution Unit"] --> D
    H["Instruction Queue 6 bytes"] --> F
    C --> H
    H --> I["Next Instruction Prefetched During Current Execution"]
    I --> D
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**

- **A (System Clock):** The crystal oscillator generates a square wave at 5 MHz, defining each T-State boundary.
- **B (T-State):** Each clock pulse corresponds to one T-State (200 ns at 5 MHz).
- **C (Machine Cycle):** Groups of T-States form bus transactions.
- **D (Instruction Cycle):** The complete operation combining fetch, decode, execute.
- **F and G:** The two cooperating units — BIU handles bus cycles, EU handles execution.
- **H (Instruction Queue):** The 6-byte buffer enabling pipelined operation.
- **I (Pipelining):** While EU executes the current instruction, BIU fetches the next.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of 8086 Timing Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CLK["CLK Pin 5 MHz"] --> TGEN["T-State Generator"]
    TGEN --> T1["T1 State Address Valid"]
    TGEN --> T2["T2 State ALE Low"]
    TGEN --> T3["T3 State RD or WR Active"]
    TGEN --> T4["T4 State Data Transfer Complete"]
    T1 --> MC1["Machine Cycle Active"]
    T2 --> MC1
    T3 --> MC1
    T4 --> MC1
    MC1 --> WS["READY Pin Check"]
    WS -->|HIGH| NEXT["Move to Next Machine Cycle"]
    WS -->|LOW| WAIT["Insert Wait State Tw"]
    WAIT --> WS
    MC1 --> READY2["READY Pin Rechecked"]
    READY2 --> NEXT
    NEXT --> IC["Instruction Cycle Boundary"]
    IC --> NEWINST["Begin New Instruction"]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**

1. **CLK Pin** drives the T-State Generator, which produces the four canonical T-states (T1, T2, T3, T4).
2. These four T-states form the canonical **Machine Cycle**.
3. At T3 (or T4), the CPU checks the **READY pin**.
4. If **READY is LOW**, a **wait state Tw** is inserted between T3 and T4, and the check repeats.
5. Once READY is HIGH, the machine cycle completes and the next begins.
6. Multiple machine cycles together form an **Instruction Cycle**.

---

## 4.3 State Transition Diagram for Bus Cycle Identification

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> T1: Address Valid Status S2S1S0 Set
    T1 --> T2: ALE Falls Address Latched
    T2 --> T3: RD or WR Activated
    T3 --> Wait: READY LOW
    Wait --> Wait: READY Still LOW
    Wait --> T4: READY HIGH
    T3 --> T4: READY HIGH Immediately
    T4 --> Idle: Bus Cycle Complete
    Idle --> [*]: No More Bus Activity
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**

- The 8086 BIU enters **T1** when it needs to start a bus cycle.
- **T2** follows immediately (address hold phase).
- **T3** is the data transfer phase; here the CPU samples READY.
- If READY is LOW, the bus enters **Wait** state, looping until READY becomes HIGH.
- **T4** completes the bus cycle, returning to Idle.
- The state diagram accurately mirrors the canonical Intel 8086 timing specification.

---

## 4.4 Timing Visualization: Comparison of 4T and 4T+1W Memory Read

The following conceptual chart shows the bus signal activity across T-states for a standard and wait-stretched memory read:

```mermaid
gantt
    title 8086 Memory Read Bus Cycle Without and With Wait States
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Standard 4T
    T1 Address Valid    :a1, 0, 1
    T2 Address Hold     :a2, 1, 1
    T3 RD Active Ready High :a3, 2, 1
    T4 Data Transfer    :a4, 3, 1
    section With 1 Wait 5T
    T1 Address Valid    :b1, 0, 1
    T2 Address Hold     :b2, 1, 1
    T3 RD Active Ready Low  :b3, 2, 1
    Tw Wait State       :b4, 3, 1
    T4 Data Transfer    :b5, 4, 1
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**

- The **Standard 4T** cycle completes in **4 clock periods** (800 ns).
- The **With 1 Wait 5T** cycle stretches T3 because memory's READY line is LOW; an extra **Tw** is inserted before T4.
- The total stretched cycle is **5 clock periods (1000 ns)**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These questions test *Remember* and *Understand* levels of Bloom's Taxonomy — KTU expects precise, textbook-aligned definitions.

---

### Question 1: Define T-State, Machine Cycle, and Instruction Cycle. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Model Answer:**

1. **T-State:** The T-State (or clock period) is the smallest unit of time recognized by the microprocessor. It corresponds to one clock cycle of the system clock, given by $T = 1/f_{\text{CLK}}$. For the 8086 with a 5 MHz clock, T = 200 ns. **(1 mark)**

2. **Machine Cycle:** A Machine Cycle is the elementary bus operation required to read from memory, write to memory, access an I/O port, or fetch an opcode. It is composed of a fixed number of T-States. For example, an 8086 memory read consumes 4T-states. **(1 mark)**

3. **Instruction Cycle:** The Instruction Cycle encompasses the complete execution of one machine instruction, including the fetch of the opcode, the decode of the opcode, and the execution of the operation. It consists of one or more machine cycles. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2: Explain the role of the READY pin in 8086 timing. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Model Answer:**

The **READY pin** is an input to the 8086 that allows slow memory or I/O devices to extend the duration of a bus cycle.

- When the addressed device is **fast enough**, it asserts READY HIGH during T3, and the bus cycle completes in the standard 4T-states. **(1 mark)**
- When the device is **slow**, it pulls READY LOW during T3, forcing the 8086 to insert **wait states (Tw)** between T3 and T4. **(1 mark)**
- The CPU continues to insert wait states until READY is sampled HIGH, after which T4 completes the data transfer. This mechanism ensures **synchronization** between the fast CPU and slower peripherals without CPU redesign. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates internal choice in Part B. Two alternative questions are provided below. Each has sub-parts (a) and (b), each worth 7 marks, totaling 14 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**A (a) [7 Marks]:** With a neat diagram, explain the timing diagram for the 8086 **Memory Read** machine cycle. How is it affected by wait states?

**A (b) [7 Marks]:** An 8086 system runs at **8 MHz** with **1 wait state per memory access**. Calculate the time required to execute the following instruction sequence:

```text
MOV AX, [2000H]    ; 3-byte instruction, 1 MR + 2 OF
MOV BX, [2002H]    ; 3-byte instruction, 1 MR + 2 OF
ADD AX, BX         ; 2-byte instruction, 1 OF
```

#### **Model Solution for A(a):**

The Memory Read timing diagram consists of **four T-States** (T1, T2, T3, T4):

| T-State | Activity on Bus |
| :---: | :--- |
| T1 | CPU outputs **valid 20-bit address** on AD/AD bus. Status lines S2̄, S1̄, S0̄ encode **Memory Read** (1, 0, 1). **ALE goes HIGH** to latch the address. |
| T2 | **ALE goes LOW**; address remains on the bus. The bus is internally configured for data reception. **RD̄** signal transitions. |
| T3 | **RD̄ goes LOW**, signaling memory to place data on the bus. CPU samples **READY** pin here. |
| T4 | CPU **reads data from AD bus**. **RD̄ goes HIGH**. Bus cycle ends. |

**With Wait States:** If the memory is slow and READY is LOW during T3, additional **wait states Tw** are inserted between T3 and T4. Each wait state is **one clock period long**. The CPU continues sampling READY until it goes HIGH, at which point T4 completes the transfer.

**Diagram (textual representation):**

```
        T1     T2     T3    Tw    T4
CLK   __|‾‾|__|‾‾|__|‾‾|__|‾‾|__|‾‾|__
ADDR   [A15-A0 valid]      [held]
ALE    ‾‾‾‾‾‾\_
RD̄                 \______________/
READY  _________/‾‾‾‾‾‾‾\___________
DATA                     [D15-D0]
```

**(Full 7 marks allocated: 4 for diagram, 2 for explanation, 1 for wait state effect.)**

---

#### **Model Solution for A(b):**

**Step 1: Compute T-State duration at 8 MHz**

$$T \;=\; \frac{1}{8 \times 10^{6}} \;=\; 125 \,\text{ns}$$

**Step 2: Count machine cycles per instruction**

| Instruction | OF Cycles | MR Cycles | OF T-States | MR T-States | Wait States |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| MOV AX, [2000H] | 2 | 1 | 4T + 4T | 4T + 1W | 1 |
| MOV BX, [2002H] | 2 | 1 | 4T + 4T | 4T + 1W | 1 |
| ADD AX, BX | 1 | 0 | 4T | – | 0 |

**Step 3: Compute T-states per instruction**

**Instruction 1: MOV AX, [2000H]**
- OF1 = 4T
- OF2 = 4T
- MR (with 1 wait) = 5T
- Subtotal: $4 + 4 + 5 = 13T$

**Instruction 2: MOV BX, [2002H]**
- Subtotal: $13T$

**Instruction 3: ADD AX, BX**
- OF = 4T
- Subtotal: $4T$

**Step 4: Total T-states**

$$T_{\text{total}} \;=\; 13 + 13 + 4 \;=\; 30\,T$$

**Step 5: Substitute T = 125 ns**

$$T_{\text{total}} \;=\; 30 \times 125 \,\text{ns} \;=\; 3750 \,\text{ns} \;=\; 3.75 \,\mu\text{s}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\,T_{\text{total}} \;=\; 3.75 \,\mu\text{s}\,}$$

**(Full 7 marks: 2 for frequency/time conversion, 2 for MC identification, 2 for T-state summation, 1 for final computation.)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**B (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **Instruction Cycle** of the 8086 microprocessor. How does the BIU-EU architecture improve execution efficiency compared to the 8085?

**B (b) [7 Marks]:** An 8086 system operates at **5 MHz**. Calculate the execution time for a `MOV [4000H], CX` instruction, given that this instruction has **2 OF + 1 MW** machine cycles. Assume **no wait states**.

#### **Model Solution for B(a):**

The **Instruction Cycle** of the 8086 consists of:

1. **Fetch Phase (BIU):** The Bus Interface Unit reads the opcode (and any required operand bytes) from memory into the 6-byte instruction queue. **(2 marks)**

2. **Decode Phase (EU):** The Execution Unit's instruction decoder interprets the opcode and determines the required operation. **(1 mark)**

3. **Execute Phase (EU):** The EU performs the operation — arithmetic, logic, data transfer, or control flow — using the ALU and registers. **(2 marks)**

4. **Store Phase (if required):** Results are written back to memory or I/O via a bus cycle initiated by the BIU. **(1 mark)**

**Comparison with 8085:**

| Aspect | 8085 | 8086 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Architecture | Single unit (no separation) | BIU + EU separation |
| Prefetching | None | 6-byte instruction queue |
| Bus utilization | Idle during execution | Active during execution (overlapped fetch) |
| Execution speed | Slower due to serial fetch-execute | Faster due to pipelined fetch-execute overlap |

The 8086's **pipelined architecture** allows the BIU to fetch the next instruction **in parallel** with the EU's execution of the current one. This **instruction look-ahead** reduces the average instruction cycle time by overlapping bus activity with computation. **(1 mark for comparison)**

---

#### **Model Solution for B(b):**

**Step 1: Compute T-State duration**

$$T \;=\; \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{6}} \;=\; 200 \,\text{ns}$$

**Step 2: Identify machine cycles**

`MOV [4000H], CX` requires:
- **2 Opcode Fetch (OF)** cycles — for the 3-byte instruction (4T each) = 8T
- **1 Memory Write (MW)** cycle — to write the operand to memory (4T) = 4T

**Step 3: Compute total T-states**

$$T_{\text{total}} \;=\; 8T + 4T \;=\; 12T$$

**Step 4: Substitute T = 200 ns**

$$T_{\text{total}} \;=\; 12 \times 200 \,\text{ns} \;=\; 2400 \,\text{ns} \;=\; 2.4 \,\mu\text{s}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\,T_{\text{total}} \;=\; 2.4 \,\mu\text{s}\,}$$

**(Full 7 marks: 2 for MC identification, 2 for T-state counting, 2 for time conversion, 1 for final answer.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing T-State with Machine Cycle:** A common error is to equate one Machine Cycle with one T-State. Always remember: **1 Machine Cycle = Multiple T-States** (typically 4T for memory operations).
>
> 2. **Forgetting Wait States:** When a problem states *"1 wait state per memory access"*, students often compute 4T instead of 5T. **Always add wait states to the relevant machine cycles.**
>
> 3. **Ignoring Odd-Address Penalty:** 8086 fetches 2 bytes per bus cycle at **even addresses**. At **odd addresses**, it requires 2 separate byte fetches (8T). Many KTU questions test this specifically.
>
> 4. **Wrong Unit Conversion:** Confusing MHz with kHz, or ns with µs, costs easy marks. Always show the unit conversion step explicitly.
>
> 5. **Ignoring BIU/EU Overlap:** Students sometimes count every machine cycle serially, forgetting that BIU fetches overlap with EU execution. KTU expects awareness of the queue effect.
>
> 6. **Incorrect Segment Override Handling:** Instructions with segment override prefixes (e.g., `ES:`) require **6T for OF** instead of 4T. Missing this loses marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 2 Exam:**

- **T-State** is the smallest time unit; equals one clock period. $T = 1/f_{\text{CLK}}$.
- **Machine Cycle** is a single bus transaction; typically **4T** for memory/I/O access on 8086.
- **Instruction Cycle** is fetch + decode + execute, comprising one or more machine cycles.
- The **8086 BIU** handles all bus cycles; the **EU** handles execution — they operate **in parallel** via the 6-byte instruction queue.
- **Standard 8086 clock** is 5 MHz → **T = 200 ns** (most exam problems use this).
- **Memory Read/Write** = **4T** base; **Opcode Fetch** = **4T**; **OF with segment override** = **6T**.
- **Wait states** are inserted when **READY = LOW** during T3; each wait state = **1T**.
- **Status lines S2̄, S1̄, S0̄** encode the current bus cycle type (fetch, MR, MW, IOR, IOW, etc.).
- **Odd-address alignment** doubles fetch time (8T for 2 bytes at odd address).
- **Total instruction time** = sum of (T-states × T) across all machine cycles, including wait states.
- The 8086 **pipelining** (instruction look-ahead) means effective throughput exceeds naive serial execution.
- **Mnemonic for bus cycle types:** *"Read, Write, Fetch, IO, Halt, INTA, Passive"* corresponding to S2S1S0 = 101, 110, 100, 010, 011, 000, 111.
- **KTU must-know formula:** $T_{\text{IC}} = \sum (n_i + w_i) \cdot T$.

---

**End of Module 2 Notes — Machine Cycles, Instruction Cycle and T States**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fetch and Execute Cycles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET504 - MICROPROCESSORS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fetch and Execute Cycles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Fetch and Execute Cycle** (also called the **Instruction Cycle** or **Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle**) is the fundamental operational sequence performed by the Central Processing Unit (CPU) of any microprocessor to retrieve, interpret, and execute machine instructions stored in memory. It represents the **"heartbeat"** of the microprocessor — the continuous loop that transforms binary instructions into observable system behavior.

In the context of the **Intel 8086 microprocessor**, the Fetch and Execute Cycle is a structured hierarchy comprising three nested time units:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Hierarchy of CPU Timing:**
> - **Instruction Cycle:** Complete execution of one machine instruction (Fetch + Execute + Store).
> - **Machine Cycle (Bus Cycle):** The elementary bus operation performed by the BIU (Opcode Fetch, Memory R/W, I/O R/W, Interrupt Acknowledge).
> - **T-State (Clock Period):** The smallest indivisible time unit, equal to one period of the internal system clock. Each machine cycle is composed of multiple T-states.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PBEET504):** Module 2 demands an explicit understanding of how the **Bus Interface Unit (BIU)** and the **Execution Unit (EU)** operate as a loosely coupled pipeline, with the **6-byte instruction queue** acting as a buffer between the fetch and execute stages.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Master Chef in a Restaurant

Imagine a chef in a busy restaurant kitchen with a **recipe book (memory)**, a **reading desk (BIU)**, and a **cooking station (EU)**:

1. **FETCH** — The chef's assistant walks to the shelf, finds the next page of the recipe, and brings it to the desk. *(BIU reads the opcode from memory and places it in the instruction queue.)*
2. **DECODE** — The chef reads the page, identifies ingredients and steps required. *(EU's instruction decoder interprets the opcode.)*
3. **OPERAND FETCH (if required)** — The assistant fetches additional ingredients (data) from the pantry. *(BIU performs memory or I/O read cycles to fetch operands.)*
4. **EXECUTE** — The chef actually performs the cooking steps. *(EU's ALU performs arithmetic, logic, or data movement.)*
5. **STORE (if required)** — The finished dish is plated and sent out. *(BIU performs a memory or I/O write cycle to store the result.)*

The crucial insight is that **the assistant (BIU) and the chef (EU) work in parallel** — the assistant is already fetching the *next* recipe page while the chef is cooking the *current* one. This is **instruction pipelining**, made possible by the **6-byte instruction queue** in the 8086.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (KTU 8086 Conventions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Canonical 8086 Timing Parameters used in KTU Examinations:**
> - **Default Internal Clock Frequency:** **5 MHz** (from a 10 MHz crystal, internally divided by 2).
> - **T-State Duration (T):** **T = 1 / f = 1 / (5 × 10⁶) = 200 ns = 0.2 µs**.
> - **Minimum Machine Cycle Length:** **4 T-States = 800 ns** (for Opcode Fetch, Memory R/W, I/O R/W).
> - **Interrupt Acknowledge Cycle:** **6 T-States = 1200 ns** (when an INTA interrupt is acknowledged).
> - **Wait State Insertion:** T_W states are inserted between T₃ and T₄ of any bus cycle whenever the **READY** input pin is sampled LOW.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fetch Phase — Step-by-Step Logic

The **Fetch Phase** is exclusively handled by the **Bus Interface Unit (BIU)**. Its sole responsibility is to supply a continuous stream of bytes from the memory segment pointed to by the **Instruction Pointer (IP)** combined with the **Code Segment (CS) register**. The physical 20-bit address is computed as:

$$
\text{Physical Address} = (\text{CS} \times 16) + \text{IP}
$$

The fetch operation is decomposed as follows:

- **Step F1 — Address Emission:** During T₁, the BIU places the 20-bit physical address on the multiplexed address/data bus (AD₁₅–AD₀ and A₁₉/S₆–A₁₆/S₃). The **Address Latch Enable (ALE)** signal is pulsed HIGH to allow external latch ICs (74LS373) to capture this address for the remainder of the bus cycle.
- **Step F2 — Status Signaling:** The status lines **IO/M̄**, **S₂ (M/IŌ)**, **S₁ (DT/R̄)**, and **S₀ (DEN̄)** are asserted to identify the bus cycle type. For an opcode fetch, **S₂S₁S₀ = 110** (i.e., **IO/M̄ = 0**, **DT/R̄ = 1**, **DEN̄ = 0**).
- **Step F3 — Read Activation:** The **RD̄** (Read) signal is driven LOW during T₂, instructing the addressed memory device to place its data on the bus.
- **Step F4 — Data Sampling:** At the rising edge of T₃, the 8086 samples the AD bus to latch the opcode byte. If **READY = 0**, additional **T_WAIT** states are inserted.
- **Step F5 — Queue Loading & IP Increment:** The captured byte is appended to the 6-byte instruction queue, and the Instruction Pointer is **incremented** by 1.
- **Step F6 — Cycle Termination:** During T₄, control signals (RD̄, DEN̄) are deactivated, ending the bus cycle. The BIU is now free to initiate the *next* fetch (which may even be a **prefetch** of the following instruction).

## 2.2 The Execute Phase — Step-by-Step Logic

The **Execute Phase** is the domain of the **Execution Unit (EU)**. It contains the **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)**, the **Flag Register**, the **General Purpose Registers (AX, BX, CX, DX)**, the **Pointer and Index Registers (SP, BP, SI, DI)**, and crucially the **Instruction Decoder and Control Unit**.

- **Step E1 — Opcode Withdrawal:** The EU extracts the *oldest* byte from the 6-byte instruction queue (FIFO — First In, First Out). It does *not* touch the external bus for this step.
- **Step E2 — Decoding:** The instruction decoder interprets the opcode. If the instruction has a multi-byte opcode, additional bytes are withdrawn from the queue.
- **Step E3 — Operand Resolution:** The EU identifies the addressing mode (register, immediate, direct, indirect, indexed, based-indexed, etc.) and calculates the **Effective Address (EA)** if required.
- **Step E4 — Operand Fetch (if required):** If the operand resides in memory or I/O, the EU signals the BIU to perform a dedicated **Memory Read** or **I/O Read** machine cycle to retrieve it.
- **Step E5 — ALU Operation:** The ALU performs the specified arithmetic (ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV), logic (AND, OR, XOR), or data-movement operation. The result is held in an internal temporary register.
- **Step E6 — Flag Update:** The Status Flags (CF, PF, AF, ZF, SF, OF) are updated based on the result.
- **Step E7 — Result Storage:** The result is written back to the destination register, memory location (via BIU Memory Write cycle), or I/O port (via BIU I/O Write cycle).
- **Step E8 — Next Instruction Hand-off:** The EU loops back to Step E1, while the BIU has likely already prefetched the next opcode.

## 2.3 The Four Canonical Machine Cycles of the 8086

> [!NOTE]
> **Every bus cycle in the 8086 falls into one of the following four categories. KTU questions frequently ask students to identify machine cycles for a given instruction.**

| # | Machine Cycle | Status Encoding (S₂S₁S₀) | IO/M̄ | DT/R̄ | DEN̄ | RD̄ | WR̄ | Min T-States |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Opcode Fetch** | 1 1 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | **4 T** |
| 2 | **Memory Read** | 1 0 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | **4 T** |
| 3 | **Memory Write** | 0 0 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | **4 T** |
| 4 | **I/O Read** | 1 0 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | **4 T** |
| 5 | **I/O Write** | 0 0 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | **4 T** |
| 6 | **Interrupt Acknowledge** | 0 1 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | **6 T** |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical problem on this module.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | T-State Duration | $T = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{clock}}}$ | seconds (s) |
| 2 | 8086 Internal Clock | $f_{\text{int}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{crystal}}}{2}$ | Hz (e.g., 10 MHz ÷ 2 = 5 MHz) |
| 3 | Machine Cycle Duration (standard) | $T_{\text{MC}} = 4T$ | seconds (800 ns @ 5 MHz) |
| 4 | Machine Cycle with N wait states | $T_{\text{MC}} = (4 + N)T$ | seconds |
| 5 | Total Instruction Execution Time | $T_{\text{instr}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{\text{MC}_i}$ | seconds |
| 6 | Physical Address (8086) | $A_{\text{phys}} = (\text{Segment}) \times 16 + \text{Offset}$ | bytes (20-bit) |
| 7 | Effective Address (BP+DI+disp16) | $EA = \text{BP} + \text{DI} + \text{disp}$ | bytes (16-bit) |
| 8 | Prefetch Queue Throughput | $\text{Rate} = \dfrac{1\ \text{byte}}{2T}$ | 2.5 MB/s @ 5 MHz |
| 9 | Maximum Memory Bandwidth (bus) | $BW = \dfrac{16\ \text{bits}}{4T}$ | bytes/second |
| 10 | Wait State Penalty | $T_{\text{penalty}} = N \times T$ | seconds per wait state |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The understanding of fetch-execute cycles is not merely academic. It is the foundation of:

- **Embedded Systems Performance Tuning:** Selecting a clock frequency vs. memory access latency trade-off directly uses the T-state calculations.
- **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) Design:** Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET) analysis of an ISR (Interrupt Service Routine) requires summing all machine cycles.
- **Hardware Debugging with Logic Analyzers:** Engineers trace address, data, and control signals (ALE, RD̄, WR̄) over T-states to diagnose bus faults.
- **Bus Arbitration in Multiprocessor Systems:** HOLD/HLDA timing cycles depend on the BIU's machine cycle structure.
- **Cache Memory Design:** The 6-byte prefetch queue of the 8086 is the conceptual ancestor of modern L1 instruction caches.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides the **exhaustive timing analysis** of the four canonical machine cycles of the 8086. Every transition, every signal state, and every internal register update is derived step-by-step to satisfy KTU 2024 Scheme's deep-learning requirement.

## 3.1 Derivation: T-State Duration for the Standard 8086 Reference Design

The KTU examination always assumes the canonical reference design unless explicitly stated otherwise.

**Given:** Crystal frequency $f_{\text{XTAL}} = 10\ \text{MHz}$.

The 8086 internally divides this by 2 using its on-chip clock generator to produce the internal system clock:

$$
f_{\text{clk}} = \frac{f_{\text{XTAL}}}{2} = \frac{10 \times 10^{6}}{2} = 5 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Hz} = 5\ \text{MHz}
$$

The T-state duration is the reciprocal of this frequency:

$$
T = \frac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{6}} = 0.2 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s} = 200\ \text{ns}
$$

Therefore, every timing calculation in the following derivations uses **T = 200 ns**.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Opcode Fetch Machine Cycle

The opcode fetch is the **most fundamental machine cycle** — it occurs at least once per instruction and is the cycle that loads the 8086's instruction queue.

$$
\boxed{\text{Opcode Fetch:}\ 4T\ \text{states (minimum)}}
$$

**Step-by-step signal evolution:**

**During T₁:**
The BIU drives the 20-bit physical address onto the bus. The lower 16 bits (A₀–A₁₅) appear on the multiplexed **AD₀–AD₁₅** lines, and the upper 4 bits (A₁₆–A₁₉) appear on the **A₁₆/S₃–A₁₉/S₆** lines. Simultaneously:
- **ALE** is driven HIGH, enabling the external address latches to capture A₀–A₁₉.
- **BHĒ/S₇** is driven LOW (for fetching from odd address or full word).
- **S₂S₁S₀ = 110** is presented on the status lines, identifying this as an *Opcode Fetch* cycle.
- **IO/M̄ = 0** (memory operation), **DT/R̄ = 1** (receive, since the CPU is reading from memory).

**During T₂:**
The address on AD₀–AD₁₅ is no longer needed; the 8086 releases these lines (high-impedance state) so they can be used as the data bus. The status lines S₃–S₆ transition to reflect the active segment register. The **RD̄** signal is asserted LOW to instruct the memory to begin driving the data bus. **DEN̄** is driven LOW to enable the external data transceivers (74LS245).

**During T₃:**
The memory device places the opcode byte on AD₀–AD₁₅. At the **rising edge of T₃**, the 8086 samples the data bus, latches the byte internally, and:
- Appends the byte to the **6-byte instruction queue**.
- **Increments the Instruction Pointer (IP)** by 1.
- If **READY = 0**, the 8086 inserts **T_WAIT** states between T₃ and T₄.

**During T₄:**
All control signals (RD̄, DEN̄, ALE) are deactivated. The bus cycle ends. The 8086 enters either the next machine cycle (if the EU has already consumed the queue) or an idle state (TI — Idle T-state) if the queue is full.

**Numerical calculation for the standard case (no wait states):**

$$
T_{\text{OF}} = 4T = 4 \times 200\ \text{ns} = 800\ \text{ns} = 0.8\ \mu\text{s}
$$

**With N wait states:**

$$
T_{\text{OF}}(N) = (4 + N) \times T = (4 + N) \times 200\ \text{ns}
$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Memory Read Machine Cycle

The Memory Read cycle is invoked whenever the EU requires an operand from memory (e.g., `MOV AX, [BX]`).

$$
\boxed{\text{Memory Read:}\ 4T\ \text{states (minimum)}}
$$

**Step-by-step signal evolution:**

**During T₁:**
The 20-bit physical address of the operand is placed on the bus. **ALE** is pulsed HIGH. **S₂S₁S₀ = 100** (memory read). **IO/M̄ = 0**, **DT/R̄ = 1** (CPU receives data from memory).

**During T₂:**
The address bus is floated. **RD̄** is asserted LOW. **DEN̄** is asserted LOW to enable transceivers.

**During T₃:**
The memory device places the operand on AD₀–AD₁₅. At the rising edge of T₃, the 8086 samples the bus, latches the byte (or word, for even-aligned word reads), and the BIU delivers it to the EU's internal register. If the operand is a 16-bit word at an even address, **BHĒ** is also asserted LOW in T₁ so that the memory places data on D₈–D₁₅ simultaneously. **READY** is checked; if LOW, **T_WAIT** states are inserted.

**During T₄:**
The bus cycle ends. RD̄, DEN̄ are deasserted.

**Calculation (no wait states):**

$$
T_{\text{MR}} = 4T = 4 \times 200\ \text{ns} = 800\ \text{ns}
$$

## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation: Memory Write Machine Cycle

The Memory Write cycle stores the result of an EU operation to a memory location (e.g., `MOV [BX], AX`).

$$
\boxed{\text{Memory Write:}\ 4T\ \text{states (minimum)}}
$$

**Step-by-step signal evolution:**

**During T₁:**
The destination physical address is placed on the bus. **ALE** is pulsed HIGH. **S₂S₁S₀ = 000** (memory write). **IO/M̄ = 0**, **DT/R̄ = 0** (CPU transmits data to memory).

**During T₂:**
Crucially, **the data to be written is placed on AD₀–AD₁₅ during T₂ itself** (not T₃ as in a read cycle). **WR̄** is asserted LOW. **DEN̄** is asserted LOW to enable the transceivers in the transmit direction.

**During T₃:**
The data is held on the bus, and **WR̄ remains LOW** to give the memory time to complete the write operation. The memory latches the data on the rising edge of **WR̄** (i.e., at the end of T₃). **READY** is checked.

**During T₄:**
WR̄, DEN̄ are deasserted. The bus cycle ends.

**Calculation (no wait states):**

$$
T_{\text{MW}} = 4T = 800\ \text{ns}
$$

## 3.5 Exhaustive Derivation: I/O Read and I/O Write Machine Cycles

These cycles are analogous to memory read/write, except the **IO/M̄** signal is HIGH (i.e., **S₂ = 1**) to indicate the address is an I/O port address (8-bit, addressed on the lower 16-bit address bus).

$$
\boxed{\text{I/O Read/Write:}\ 4T\ \text{states (minimum)}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{IOR}} = T_{\text{IOW}} = 4T = 800\ \text{ns}
$$

Status encoding: **S₂S₁S₀ = 101** (I/O read) or **001** (I/O write).

## 3.6 Exhaustive Derivation: Interrupt Acknowledge Machine Cycle

When the 8086 receives an interrupt request via the **INTR** pin (with interrupts enabled via IF=1), it completes the current instruction, then performs a special **Interrupt Acknowledge** bus cycle.

$$
\boxed{\text{Interrupt Acknowledge (INTA):}\ 6T\ \text{states (minimum)}}
$$

**Step-by-step signal evolution:**

**During T₁–T₂:**
Two consecutive **INTA** pulses are generated. The 8086 floats the local bus (address and data) to allow the interrupting device to place an instruction (typically a CALL) on the data bus. The status is **S₂S₁S₀ = 011** with **IO/M̄ = 1**.

**During T₃–T₄:**
The first INTA pulse has ended; the second INTA pulse is generated. The interrupting device places a CALL instruction opcode (or interrupt vector) on the data bus, which the 8086 reads.

**Calculation:**

$$
T_{\text{INTA}} = 6T = 6 \times 200\ \text{ns} = 1200\ \text{ns} = 1.2\ \mu\text{s}
$$

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Time for a Complete Instruction

Consider the instruction `MOV AX, [1234H]` (assume DS = 2000H). This instruction requires:

- **1 Opcode Fetch** cycle (4T)
- **1 Memory Read** cycle (4T, to fetch 2 bytes of data from DS:1234H and DS:1235H — actually done in one 4T cycle for a word at even address)

**Total machine cycles:** 2
**Total T-states (no wait):** $4T + 4T = 8T$

**Total execution time:**

$$
T_{\text{exec}} = 8 \times 200\ \text{ns} = 1600\ \text{ns} = 1.6\ \mu\text{s}
$$

Now consider a more complex instruction: `MUL BX` (unsigned multiply 16-bit registers).

**Total machine cycles:** ≈ 70 to 80 (varies; multiply internally takes many cycles for partial-product accumulation in the 8086).

$$
T_{\text{exec}} = 80 \times 200\ \text{ns} = 16\ \mu\text{s}\ (\text{approximate})
$$

This explains why **multiplication and division are slow** on the 8086, motivating the use of an 8087 math coprocessor — a classic KTU question scenario.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: The 8086 Fetch-Execute Pipeline (BIU–EU Decoupling)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MEM["MEMORY SPACE (CS:IP)"]
        M1["Opcode Byte 1"]
        M2["Opcode Byte 2"]
        M3["Operand LSB"]
        M4["Operand MSB"]
    end

    subgraph BIU["BUS INTERFACE UNIT (BIU)"]
        ADDGEN["20-bit Address Generator: (Seg x 16) + Offset"]
        QUEUE["6-Byte Instruction Queue (FIFO)"]
        BUSCTL["Bus Control Logic: ALE, RDbar, WRbar, DENbar, DT/Rbar"]
    end

    subgraph EU["EXECUTION UNIT (EU)"]
        DECODER["Instruction Decoder"]
        ALU["Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)"]
        REGS["General Purpose Registers: AX, BX, CX, DX"]
        FLAGS["Flag Register: CF, ZF, SF, OF, PF, AF"]
    end

    M1 -->|Opcode Fetch Cycle 4T| BUSCTL
    M2 -->|Opcode Fetch Cycle 4T| BUSCTL
    M3 -->|Memory Read Cycle 4T| BUSCTL
    M4 -->|Memory Read Cycle 4T| BUSCTL

    ADDGEN -->|20-bit Address| BUSCTL
    BUSCTL -->|Address and Data| QUEUE
    QUEUE -->|Opcode Byte FIFO Stream| DECODER
    DECODER -->|Control Signals| ALU
    ALU <-->|Operands| REGS
    ALU -->|Result Flags| FLAGS
    ALU -->|Result to Store| BUSCTL
    BUSCTL -.->|Memory Write Cycle 4T| MEM
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Generic Instruction Cycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> FetchOpcode
    FetchOpcode --> Decode : Opcode Latched in Queue
    Decode --> OperandFetch : Operand Needed
    Decode --> Execute : Register Operand
    OperandFetch --> Execute : Operand Latched
    Execute --> Store : Result to Memory or I/O
    Execute --> NextInstruction : Register Destination
    Store --> NextInstruction : Write Complete
    NextInstruction --> FetchOpcode : IP Incremented

    note right of FetchOpcode : BIU performs 4T bus cycle
    note right of OperandFetch : BIU performs Memory or I/O Read 4T
    note right of Execute : EU ALU operation
    note right of Store : BIU performs Memory or I/O Write 4T
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Timing Diagram Representation (Opcode Fetch, 4T, No Wait States)

```mermaid
gantt
    title 8086 Opcode Fetch Bus Cycle - 4 T-States @ 5 MHz
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Address Bus AD0-AD15
    Address Valid (T1)         :a1, 0, 200ns
    High Impedance (T2-T4)     :a2, after a1, 600ns
    section Status Bus S3-S6
    Address A16-A19 (T1)       :s1, 0, 200ns
    Status S3-S6 (T2-T4)       :s2, after s1, 600ns
    section Control Signal ALE
    ALE HIGH (T1)              :ale1, 0, 200ns
    ALE LOW (T2-T4)            :ale2, after ale1, 600ns
    section Control Signal RDbar
    RDbar HIGH (T1)            :rd1, 0, 200ns
    RDbar LOW (T2-T3)          :rd2, after rd1, 400ns
    RDbar HIGH (T4)            :rd3, after rd2, 200ns
    section Data Bus AD0-AD15
    High Impedance (T1)        :d1, 0, 200ns
    Data from Memory (T3-T4)   :d2, after d1, 400ns
    section DENbar Transceiver
    DENbar HIGH (T1)           :de1, 0, 200ns
    DENbar LOW (T2-T4)         :de2, after de1, 600ns
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: 8086 Bus Cycle State Transitions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1["T1: Address placed on bus, ALE HIGH, status S2S1S0 asserted"] -->|After T1 duration 200ns| T2["T2: Address floated, RDbar asserted LOW, DENbar LOW, data bus floated for read"]
    T2 -->|Clock edge sample READY| TW{"READY is HIGH?"}
    TW -->|NO - insert wait| TWAIT["T_WAIT: Idle T-states, bus signals held stable"]
    TWAIT -->|Re-check READY| TW
    TW -->|YES| T3["T3: Data sampled on rising edge of T3 (for read), or WRbar asserted (for write)"]
    T3 -->|After T3 duration 200ns| T4["T4: Bus cycle termination, all control signals deasserted"]
    T4 -->|Next machine cycle| T1
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5: Prefetch Queue Flow (Pipelined Fetch-Execute)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BIU_FETCH["BIU: Fetch Byte 1 from CS:IP"] --> Q1["Queue Byte 1"]
    BIU_FETCH2["BIU: Fetch Byte 2 from CS:IP+1"] --> Q2["Queue Byte 2"]
    BIU_FETCH3["BIU: Fetch Byte 3 from CS:IP+2"] --> Q3["Queue Byte 3"]
    Q1 --> EU_DEC["EU: Decode Instruction 1 (Opcode + Operand)"]
    Q2 --> EU_DEC
    EU_DEC --> EU_EXE["EU: Execute Instruction 1 (ALU)"]
    Q3 --> EU_DEC2["EU: Decode Instruction 2 (next instruction)"]
    EU_EXE --> EU_STORE["EU: Store Result via BIU Write Cycle"]
    EU_DEC2 --> EU_EXE2["EU: Execute Instruction 2"]
```
