# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question A.1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Differentiate between the BIU and the EU of the 8086 microprocessor. Why is this separation advantageous for instruction execution? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Bus Interface Unit (BIU)** is responsible for *all external bus activities* — fetching opcodes from memory, reading/writing data, and managing the 6-byte instruction queue. It contains the Segment Registers (CS, DS, SS, ES), the Instruction Pointer (IP), the address generation adder, and the bus control logic.

The **Execution Unit (EU)** is responsible for *decoding and executing* instructions. It contains the ALU, General Purpose Registers (AX, BX, CX, DX), Pointer and Index Registers (SP, BP, SI, DI), the Flag Register, and the Instruction Decoder.

**Advantage:** The 6-byte instruction queue allows the BIU to **prefetch** subsequent instructions *in parallel* with the EU executing the current instruction. This **overlapped fetch-execute pipelining** significantly reduces the average instruction cycle time compared to non-pipelined designs (like the 8085), where the CPU is idle during operand fetches. **(3 Marks)**

---

### **Question A.2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Define a T-state and a machine cycle. For the 8086 operating at 5 MHz internal clock, calculate the duration of one machine cycle (assume 4 T-states per machine cycle). (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **T-state** is the smallest unit of time recognized by the microprocessor, equal to one period of the internal system clock. A **machine cycle** is the basic bus operation (e.g., opcode fetch, memory read, memory write, I/O read, I/O write) required to access memory or I/O. Each machine cycle consists of multiple T-states.

$$
T = \frac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Hz}} = 0.2\ \mu\text{s} = 200\ \text{ns}
$$

$$
T_{\text{MC}} = 4T = 4 \times 200\ \text{ns} = 800\ \text{ns} = 0.8\ \mu\text{s}
$$

**The duration of one machine cycle is 800 ns (0.8 µs).** **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question B-A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Answer the following:**

**(a)** Explain the **Fetch phase** of the 8086 microprocessor in detail. With a neat signal-timing description, illustrate the four T-states of the opcode fetch machine cycle. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A system uses the 8086 with a 10 MHz crystal. Calculate: (i) the internal clock frequency, (ii) the T-state duration, (iii) the opcode fetch cycle duration, and (iv) the total time to execute the instruction `MOV AX, [2000H]` (assume DS = 3000H and one wait state per memory cycle). **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

The **Fetch phase** is the BIU's responsibility and consists of the following operational steps:

**Step 1 (T₁ state — Address Phase):**
- The BIU places the 20-bit physical address (computed as `CS × 16 + IP`) on the address bus. The lower 16 bits go on the multiplexed **AD₀–AD₁₅** lines, and the upper 4 bits on **A₁₆/S₃–A₁₉/S₆**.
- **ALE** is pulsed HIGH to latch the address externally.
- Status signals **IO/M̄ = 0**, **DT/R̄ = 1**, and **S₂S₁S₀ = 110** identify the cycle as an Opcode Fetch.
- **BHĒ/S₇** is asserted LOW to enable the upper data bus byte.

**Step 2 (T₂ state — Read Activation):**
- The BIU floats the AD bus (releases it for data transfer).
- **RD̄** is asserted LOW, instructing memory to drive the data bus.
- **DEN̄** is asserted LOW to enable external transceivers.
- The status lines A₁₆/S₃–A₁₉/S₆ now reflect segment-register status information (S₃–S₆).

**Step 3 (T₃ state — Data Sampling):**
- The memory places the opcode byte on AD₀–AD₁₅.
- The 8086 samples the data bus at the **rising edge of T₃** and latches the byte into the instruction queue.
- The Instruction Pointer (IP) is incremented by 1.
- If **READY = 0**, **T_WAIT** states are inserted before T₄.

**Step 4 (T₄ state — Cycle Termination):**
- All control signals (RD̄, DEN̄, ALE) are deasserted HIGH.
- The bus cycle terminates. The BIU proceeds to the next cycle or enters an idle T-state (T_I) if the queue is full.

**Timing Summary (4T = 800 ns @ 5 MHz):**

$$
\boxed{T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_3 \rightarrow T_4 = 200 + 200 + 200 + 200 = 800\ \text{ns}}
$$

**(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $f_{\text{XTAL}} = 10\ \text{MHz}$, 1 wait state per memory cycle.

**(i) Internal clock frequency:**

$$
f_{\text{clk}} = \frac{f_{\text{XTAL}}}{2} = \frac{10\ \text{MHz}}{2} = 5\ \text{MHz}
$$

**(ii) T-state duration:**

$$
T = \frac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{6}} = 200\ \text{ns} = 0.2\ \mu\text{s}
$$

**(iii) Opcode fetch cycle duration (no wait states for opcode fetch — only memory cycles have wait states here):**

$$
T_{\text{OF}} = 4T = 4 \times 200\ \text{ns} = 800\ \text{ns}
$$

**(iv) Total time for `MOV AX, [2000H]`:**

This instruction requires:
- **1 Opcode Fetch cycle** = 4T
- **1 Memory Read cycle** (to fetch 2 bytes of operand at DS:2000H and DS:2001H) = (4 + 1)T = 5T  *(1 wait state)*

Total T-states:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 4T + 5T = 9T
$$

Total time:

$$
T_{\text{exec}} = 9 \times 200\ \text{ns} = 1800\ \text{ns} = 1.8\ \mu\text{s}
$$

**The instruction takes 1.8 µs to execute.** **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Question B-B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Answer the following:**

**(a)** Describe the **Execute phase** of the 8086 microprocessor. How does the EU interact with the BIU during execution? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A particular 8086 instruction requires 1 opcode fetch machine cycle and 2 memory read machine cycles. If the system clock is 8 MHz, calculate: (i) the T-state duration, (ii) the duration of each machine cycle (no wait states), and (iii) the total instruction execution time. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

The **Execute phase** is the responsibility of the **Execution Unit (EU)** and consists of the following steps:

**Step 1 — Opcode Withdrawal:** The EU reads the oldest byte from the 6-byte instruction queue (FIFO discipline). Since the BIU prefetches in advance, the EU does not need to wait for the bus in most cases.

**Step 2 — Decoding:** The EU's instruction decoder interprets the opcode and, for multi-byte opcodes, withdraws additional bytes from the queue.

**Step 3 — Operand Resolution:** The EU identifies the addressing mode and computes the **Effective Address (EA)** using the displacement, base register (BX or BP), and index register (SI or DI) as dictated by the addressing mode.

**Step 4 — Operand Fetch (if required):** If the operand is in memory or an I/O port, the EU signals the BIU to perform a dedicated **Memory Read** or **I/O Read** machine cycle. The data is delivered to the EU's internal registers.

**Step 5 — ALU Operation:** The ALU performs the specified arithmetic (e.g., ADD, SUB, INC, MUL) or logic (e.g., AND, OR, XOR, NOT) operation. The result is held in a temporary register.

**Step 6 — Flag Update:** The Status Flags (CF, PF, AF, ZF, SF, OF) are updated to reflect the result of the ALU operation (only for instructions that affect flags).

**Step 7 — Result Storage:** If the destination is a memory location or I/O port, the EU signals the BIU to perform a **Memory Write** or **I/O Write** machine cycle to store the result.

**BIU–EU Interaction during Execute:**

The BIU and EU operate **asynchronously but cooperatively**:
- The EU sends *requests* to the BIU whenever it needs an external bus operation (operand fetch or result store).
- The BIU *interleaves* these requests with its ongoing prefetch activity, prioritizing EU requests.
- The 6-byte queue absorbs the timing mismatch — if the EU is busy executing a slow operation (e.g., MUL), the BIU continues to prefetch until the queue is full, then idles.

This decoupled architecture is the principal reason the 8086 is significantly faster than its predecessor, the 8085. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $f_{\text{clk}} = 8\ \text{MHz}$, 1 opcode fetch + 2 memory reads, no wait states.

**(i) T-state duration:**

$$
T = \frac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}} = \frac{1}{8 \times 10^{6}} = 0.125 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s} = 125\ \text{ns}
$$

**(ii) Duration of each machine cycle (no wait states):**

$$
T_{\text{MC}} = 4T = 4 \times 125\ \text{ns} = 500\ \text{ns}
$$

This applies to both the opcode fetch and the memory read cycles.

**(iii) Total instruction execution time:**

Total machine cycles:

$$
n_{\text{MC}} = 1 + 2 = 3\ \text{machine cycles}
$$

Total T-states:

$$
N_{\text{T}} = 3 \times 4T = 12T
$$

Total execution time:

$$
T_{\text{exec}} = 12T = 12 \times 125\ \text{ns} = 1500\ \text{ns} = 1.5\ \mu\text{s}
$$

**The total instruction execution time is 1.5 µs.** **(7 Marks)**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost KTU Students Valuable Marks:**
> 1. **Crystal vs. Internal Clock Confusion:** The most frequent error is using the crystal frequency (e.g., 10 MHz) directly as the CPU clock. The 8086 **divides the crystal frequency by 2 internally**. Always compute $f_{\text{clk}} = f_{\text{XTAL}} / 2$ first.
> 2. **Forgetting Wait States:** When a problem mentions *slow memory* or *I/O devices*, students often forget to add T_WAIT states. The corrected formula is $T_{\text{MC}} = (4 + N)T$, not just $4T$.
> 3. **Opcode Fetch ≠ Instruction Execution:** A single instruction may require **multiple** machine cycles. The opcode fetch is only *one* of them; operand reads and result writes are separate machine cycles.
> 4. **Forgetting to Increment IP:** A common theoretical mistake is to say the BIU fetches the opcode "and then the EU executes it." The BIU must also increment the Instruction Pointer (IP) so that the *next* fetch obtains the next sequential byte.
> 5. **Misidentifying the Interrupt Acknowledge Cycle:** Some students treat INTA as 4T. The correct value is **6T (1200 ns @ 5 MHz)**, with **two consecutive INTA pulses** during T₂–T₃ and T₃–T₄.
> 6. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Mixing nanoseconds (ns), microseconds (µs), and milliseconds (ms) without explicit conversion is a guaranteed mark deduction. KTU examiners specifically check unit consistency.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Fetch and Execute Cycles**

- ☐ **BIU vs EU:** BIU fetches; EU executes. They are decoupled by the **6-byte instruction queue**.
- ☐ **Three-tier hierarchy:** Instruction Cycle ⊃ Machine Cycle ⊃ T-State.
- ☐ **T-State duration:** $T = 1 / f_{\text{clk}}$. With 10 MHz crystal, $f_{\text{clk}} = 5\ \text{MHz}$ and $T = 200\ \text{ns}$.
- ☐ **Standard machine cycle = 4T = 800 ns** (at 5 MHz), but Interrupt Acknowledge = **6T = 1200 ns**.
- ☐ **Status encoding S₂S₁S₀:** 110 (Opcode Fetch), 100 (Memory Read), 000 (Memory Write), 101 (I/O Read), 001 (I/O Write), 011 (INTA).
- ☐ **Wait state formula:** $T_{\text{MC}}(N) = (4 + N)T$, where N is the number of wait states.
- ☐ **ALE** is HIGH only during T₁ to latch the address.
- ☐ **RD̄** goes LOW in T₂ for read cycles; **WR̄** goes LOW in T₂ for write cycles.
- ☐ **DEN̄** goes LOW in T₂ to enable external data transceivers.
- ☐ **DT/R̄ = 1** for reads (CPU receives); **DT/R̄ = 0** for writes (CPU transmits).
- ☐ **Physical Address = (Segment Register × 16) + Offset**, producing a 20-bit address.
- ☐ **6-byte queue throughput** decouples fetch and execute — a 1-byte queue is a hard bottleneck; a 6-byte queue absorbs most timing mismatches.
- ☐ **IP is auto-incremented** by 1 after every successful opcode byte fetch.
- ☐ **Multiplication and division** instructions consume 70–80+ T-states — a frequent KTU numerical and design question.
- ☐ **READY = 0** inserts wait states; **HOLD** requests the BIU to release the bus; **HLDA** acknowledges the release.
---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Timing Diagram for Instructions - Delay Subroutines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEET504 - MICROPROCESSORS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Timing Diagram for Instructions - Delay Subroutines # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Timing Diagram — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Timing Diagram:** A timing diagram is a graphical representation that depicts the precise sequence of operations performed by the microprocessor (8085) by plotting the logic levels of the **Clock (CLK)**, **Address Bus**, **Data Bus**, **Status signals (IO/M, S1, S0)**, and **Control signals (RD, WR, ALE)** as functions of time during the execution of each instruction. Every operation inside the 8085 is synchronised with the **system clock**. The clock is internally divided by 2, so even if a crystal of 6 MHz is used, the **internal clock frequency is 3 MHz**, giving an internal clock period of **T = 1/3 MHz ≈ 0.333 μs**. ## 1.2 The Three Fundamental Time Units of the 8085 | Term | Definition | Duration (typical) | |------|-----------|-------------------| | **T-State** | One internal clock period; the smallest unit of time | **0.333 μs** (at 6 MHz crystal) | | **Machine Cycle** | The basic operation (Fetch, Read, Write, I/O) needed to communicate with memory or I/O | 3–6 T-states | | **Instruction Cycle** | Total time required to completely fetch, decode and execute an instruction | Sum of all machine cycles | > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic:** *T → Machine → Instruction* (T-state is the smallest, Instruction cycle is the largest) ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Cooking Timer" 🕒 Imagine you are cooking a recipe: - **Clock pulse** = the ticks of your wall clock. - **T-State** = a single clock tick (≈ 0.333 μs — too tiny to perceive). - **Machine Cycle** = one complete cooking step such as *"chopping vegetables"* or *"boiling water"* (always takes a known fixed number of ticks). - **Instruction Cycle** = the entire recipe from start to finish. - **Timing Diagram** = a *Gantt chart* of your kitchen showing when the stove, mixer and oven are switched on or off relative to each clock tick. A **Delay Subroutine** is analogous to *"letting the dough rest for 10 minutes"* — the CPU executes a controlled loop whose total T-state count produces a precise, predictable time delay. ## 1.4 Delay Subroutine — Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Delay Subroutine:** A small program loop (most often using `DCR`/`INX` combined with a conditional jump like `JNZ`/`JNC`) whose total T-state count is mathematically pre-computed to generate a deterministic time delay before the CPU proceeds to the next task. The fundamental governing equation is: $$ T_{delay} = N \times T_{clk} $$ where **N** = total T-states consumed by the loop body and **T_clk** = internal clock period of the 8085. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The 8085 Status & Control Signals The 8085 communicates its *intent* (what it is doing right now) using **three status lines** at the beginning of every machine cycle. | IO/M | S1 | S0 | Machine Cycle Type | Control Signal Activated | |:----:|:--:|:--:|:-------------------|:--------------------------| | **0** | **0** | **0** | Halt / Bus Idle | None | | **0** | **0** | **1** | Memory Write | WR = 0 | | **0** | **1** | **0** | Memory Read | RD = 0 | | **0** | **1** | **1** | **Opcode Fetch** | RD = 0 | | **1** | **0** | **1** | I/O Write | WR = 0 | | **1** | **1** | **0** | I/O Read | RD = 0 | > [!NOTE] > These signals are valid only during the **second half of the first T-state** of every machine cycle. They remain stable until the cycle ends. ## 2.2 Standard Machine Cycle Durations | Machine Cycle | T-States | |:--------------|:--------:| | Opcode Fetch (OF) | **4** | | Memory Read (MR) / Memory Write (MW) | **3** | | I/O Read (IOR) / I/O Write (IOW) | **3** | | Bus Idle / Halt | Variable (≥ 2) | ## 2.3 T-States of Commonly Used 8085 Instructions (Exam Critical) | Instruction | Opcode Bytes | Machine Cycles | **Total T-States** | |:------------|:------------:|:--------------:|:-----------------:| | `MOV r1, r2` | 1 | OF | **4** | | `MVI r, data8` | 2 | OF + MR | **7** | | `LXI rp, data16` | 3 | OF + MR + MR | **10** | | `ADD r` / `SUB r` | 1 | OF | **4** | | `ADI data8` | 2 | OF + MR | **7** | | `DCR r` | 1 | OF | **4** | | `DCR M` | 1 | OF + MR + MW | **10** | | `INR r` | 1 | OF | **4** | | `DCX rp` | 1 | OF | **6** | | `INX rp` | 1 | OF | **6** | | `JMP addr` | 3 | OF + MR + MR | **10** | | `J addr` (taken) | 3 | OF + MR + MR | **10** | | `J addr` (not taken) | 3 | OF | **7** | | `CALL addr` | 3 | OF + MR + MR + MW + MW | **18** | | `RET` | 1 | OF + MR + MR | **10** | | `NOP` | 1 | OF | **4** | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Delay Calculation) > [!IMPORTANT] > The single most important formula for this module: $$ \boxed{\,T_{delay} = T_{clk} \times \big[\,T_{initial} + N_{loop} \times T_{loop} - T_{excess} + T_{final}\,\big]\,} $$ Where: - **T_clk** = ½ × Crystal period (because 8085 divides crystal frequency by 2 internally). - **T_initial** = T-states of setup instructions (e.g., `MVI r, count` = 7). - **N_loop** = number of times the loop body executes (= the loaded count value). - **T_loop** = T-states per loop iteration when jump is *taken* (e.g., `DCR` + `JNZ` taken = 4 + 10 = 14). - **T_excess** = T-states of jump when not taken (10 → 7 ⇒ subtract 3) — subtracted once on the final iteration. - **T_final** = T-states of exit (e.g., `RET` = 10). $$ \boxed{\,T_{delay} = T_{clk} \times (\,14N + 14\,)\,} \quad \text{(for }MVI\,D,N \;|\; DCR\,D \;|\; JNZ \;|\; RET\text{ loop)} $$ ### Real-World Utility - Generating **accurate baud-rate delays** for serial communication. - Producing **debounce delays** for mechanical switches. - **Traffic light sequencing** with fixed ON/OFF durations. - **Sensor sampling intervals** in embedded data loggers. - **LCD character display hold-times** in human-machine interfaces. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Single Register Delay Loop ### Program ```asm DELAY: MVI D, N ; Load 8-bit count into register D LOOP: DCR D ; Decrement D JNZ LOOP ; If D ≠ 0, jump back to LOOP RET ; Return to caller ``` ### T-State Inventory | Instruction | T-States | Times Executed | Subtotal | |:------------|:--------:|:--------------:|:--------:| | `MVI D, N` | 7 | 1 | **7** | | `DCR D` (loop body) | 4 | N | **4N** | | `JNZ LOOP` *(taken)* | 10 | (N − 1) | **10(N − 1)** | | `JNZ LOOP` *(not taken)* | 7 | 1 | **7** | | `RET` | 10 | 1 | **10** | ### Exhaustive Algebraic Derivation $$ \begin{aligned} T_{total} &= T_{MVI} + N \cdot T_{DCR} + (N-1)\cdot T_{JNZ,taken} + 1\cdot T_{JNZ,not\,taken} + T_{RET} \\[4pt] &= 7 + 4N + 10(N-1) + 7 + 10 \\[4pt] &= 7 + 4N + 10N - 10 + 7 + 10 \\[4pt] &= (4N + 10N) + (7 - 10 + 7 + 10) \\[4pt] &= 14N + 14 \end{aligned} $$ Hence: $$ \boxed{\,T_{delay} = (14N + 14) \times T_{clk}\,} $$ ### Worked Numerical Example — Crystal = 2 MHz, N = C8H (= 200) Given: - Crystal frequency = **2 MHz** ⇒ Internal clock = 2 / 2 = **1 MHz** ⇒ T_clk = **1 μs** - N = C8H = **200** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{delay} &= (14 \times 200 + 14) \times 1\,\mu s \\[4pt] &= (2800 + 14)\,\mu s \\[4pt] &= 2814\,\mu s \\[4pt] &\approx 2.814\,\text{ms} \end{aligned} $$ ### Worked Numerical Example — Crystal = 6 MHz, N = FFH (= 255) - Internal clock = 6 / 2 = **3 MHz** ⇒ T_clk = **1/3 μs ≈ 0.333 μs** - N = 255 $$ \begin{aligned} T_{delay} &= (14 \times 255 + 14) \times \tfrac{1}{3}\,\mu s \\[4pt] &= (3570 + 14) \times \tfrac{1}{3}\,\mu s \\[4pt] &= 3584 \times \tfrac{1}{3}\,\mu s \\[4pt] &= 1194.67\,\mu s \approx 1.195\,\text{ms} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Nested (Two-Level) Loop for Long Delays A single 8-bit loop cannot exceed 256 × 14 ≈ 3600 T-states (~3.6 ms at 2 MHz). For *longer* delays (LED blink, key debounce), **nested loops** are used. ### Program ```asm DELAY: MVI B, M ; Outer count LOOP2: MVI C, N ; Inner count (executed M times) LOOP1: DCR C ; 4T JNZ LOOP1 ; 10T taken / 7T not taken DCR B ; 4T JNZ LOOP2 ; 10T taken / 7T not taken RET ; 10T ``` ### Inner-Loop Closure Inner loop body = `MVI C, N` + (N − 1) × (DCR + JNZ taken) + (DCR + JNZ not taken): $$ \begin{aligned} T_{inner} &= 7 + (N-1)(4+10) + (4+7) \\[4pt] &= 7 + 14N - 14 + 11 \\[4pt] &= 14N + 4 \end{aligned} $$ ### Outer-Loop Closure Total = `MVI B` + (M − 1) × (T_inner + DCR + JNZ_taken) + (T_inner + DCR + JNZ_not_taken) + RET $$ \begin{aligned} T_{total} &= 7 + (M-1)\big[(14N+4) + 14\big] + \big[(14N+4) + 11\big] + 10 \\[4pt] &= 7 + (M-1)(14N + 18) + 14N + 15 + 10 \\[4pt] &= 7 + 14MN + 18M - 14N - 18 + 14N + 15 + 10 \\[4pt] &= 14MN + 18M + 14 \end{aligned} $$ Final compact formula: $$ \boxed{\,T_{delay} = (\,14MN + 18M + 14\,)\times T_{clk}\,} $$ ### Worked Numerical — M = N = FFH = 255, Crystal = 2 MHz - T_clk = 1 μs - 14 × 255 × 255 = 910,350 - 18 × 255 = 4,590 - T_states = 910,350 + 4,590 + 14 = **914,954** $$ T_{delay} = 914\,954 \times 1\,\mu s = 914.954\,\text{ms} \approx 0.915\,\text{s} $$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — How to Design a Delay | Required Delay | Approach | Typical Configuration | |:---------------|:---------|:----------------------| | < 100 μs | Direct NOP padding | `NOP × k` (each = 4 T-states) | | 100 μs – 5 ms | Single 8-bit loop | `MVI D, N` + `DCR`/`JNZ` | | 5 ms – 500 ms | Nested (B, C) loop | `MVI B, M` over `MVI C, N` inner loop | | > 500 ms | Triple-nested (H, D, E) loop | `MVI H` ⇒ `MVI D` ⇒ `MVI E` inner | | Very precise | Hardware timer (8253/8254) | Programmable interval timer mode | > [!TIP] > **Resolution of the delay value:** 1 increment of N adds **14 T-states**. With a 2 MHz internal clock (T_clk = 1 μs), the smallest *adjustable* delay step is therefore **14 μs**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Flowchart — Single-Register Delay Subroutine ```mermaid flowchart TD A([Start: CALL DELAY]) --> B[MVI D, N
7 T-states] B --> C[/"DCR D - 4 T-states"/] C --> D{D = 0 ?} D -- No / JNZ taken - 10T --> C D -- Yes / JNZ not-taken - 7T --> E[/"RET - 10 T-states"/] E --> F([Return to caller]) style A fill:#ffd54f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Flowchart — Nested Two-Level Delay Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD A([Start]) --> B["MVI B, M outer count - 7T"] B --> C["MVI C, N inner count - 7T"] C --> D[/"DCR C - 4T"/] D --> E{C = 0 ?} E -- No, JNZ taken - 10T --> D E -- Yes, JNZ not-taken - 7T --> F[/"DCR B - 4T"/] F --> G{B = 0 ?} G -- No, JNZ taken - 10T --> C G -- Yes, JNZ not-taken - 7T --> H[/"RET - 10T"/] H --> I([Return]) style A fill:#ffd54f,stroke:#333 style I fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#333 style E fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#333 style G fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Opcode Fetch Machine Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR CLK["System Clock
Crystal divided by 2"] --> T1["T1 - Status Signals
IO/M = 0, S1 = 1, S0 = 1"] T1 --> T2["T2 - ALE High
Address Latched
PC content -> Address Bus"] T2 --> T3["T3 - RD = 0
Memory drives Data Bus
Opcode read"] T3 --> T4["T4 - Opcode loaded
into Instruction Register
Decode internally"] T4 --> NXT[/"Next Machine Cycle OR
Next T-state"/] style CLK fill:#ffe082 style T1 fill:#ffccbc style T2 fill:#c5e1a5 style T3 fill:#81d4fa style T4 fill:#ce93d8 ``` ## 4.4 Schematic Timing Diagram — Opcode Fetch of 8085 (4 T-States) ``` Signal |---T1---|---T2---|---T3---|---T4---| CLK |__|‾|__|‾|__|‾|__|‾|__|‾|__|‾|__|‾|__ ↑ clock edges trigger operations IO/M ‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾\__________/‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾ (0 = Memory) S1 ‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾\______________________/‾‾ (1) S0 ‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾\______________________/‾‾ (1) ALE ‾‾‾\__/‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾ (high only during T1) A15-A8 ‾‾‾‾‾[Higher-order Address]‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾ (stable through T1-T4) AD7-AD0 [Lower Addr]‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾[Opcode from Memory] RD ‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾\____/‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾ (active LOW during T2-T3) ``` ## 4.5 Combined Block Diagram — T-state → Machine Cycle → Instruction Cycle Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Instruction Cycle
e.g. MVI D, data8"] --> B1["Machine Cycle 1
Opcode Fetch (4T)"] A --> B2["Machine Cycle 2
Memory Read (3T)"] B1 --> C1["T1, T2, T3, T4"] B2 --> C2["T5, T6, T7"] C1 --> D1["Each T = 0.333 μs
at 6 MHz crystal"] C2 --> D1 D1 --> E["Total = 7 T-states
= 2.333 μs"] style A fill:#fff59d style B1 fill:#ffab91 style B2 fill:#ffab91 style E fill:#a5d6a7 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]** Differentiate between a **T-state**, a **machine cycle**, and an **instruction cycle** in the 8085 microprocessor. **Model Answer:** | Term | Definition | Example | |:-----|:-----------|:--------| | **T-state** | One internal clock period (smallest time unit). | One tick of the system clock ≈ 0.333 μs. | | **Machine cycle** | The basic operation needed to communicate with memory/I/O (Fetch, Read, Write). | Opcode Fetch = 4 T-states. | | **Instruction cycle** | Total time to completely fetch, decode and execute one full instruction. | `MVI D, data` = 7 T-states = 1 OF + 1 MR. | A T-state is to a machine cycle as a *second* is to a *minute*; an instruction cycle contains one or more machine cycles. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** List the **status signals** used by the 8085 to identify its current machine cycle. What combination of IO/M, S1 and S0 indicates an **opcode fetch**? **Model Answer:** The three status signals are: **IO/M (active low for memory), S1 and S0**. | IO/M | S1 | S0 | Operation | |:----:|:--:|:--:|:----------| | **0** | **1** | **1** | **Opcode Fetch** | During T1, the 8085 places IO/M = 0, S1 = 1, S0 = 1 on the bus; control signal **RD** is asserted to read the opcode from memory at the address given by the **Program Counter (PC)**. --- ## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### ▶ Question A — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style, 14 Marks]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat timing diagram, explain the **opcode fetch machine cycle** of the 8085 microprocessor. Mention the status of each signal in every T-state. **(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the time delay generated by the following 8085 subroutine when the crystal frequency is **2 MHz**. Assume the count loaded in register D is **C8H**. ```asm DELAY: MVI D, C8H ; 7T LOOP: DCR D ; 4T JNZ LOOP ; 10T taken / 7T not taken RET ; 10T ``` #### Model Solution **(a) Opcode Fetch Timing Diagram** | T-State | IO/M | S1 | S0 | ALE | A15-A8 | AD7-AD0 | RD | Operation | |:-------:|:----:|:--:|:--:|:---:|:------:|:-------:|:--:|:----------| | T1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 → 0 | PC(high) | PC(low) | 1 | PC placed on bus; ALE latches lower address | | T2 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | PC(high) | X | 0 | Memory drives data bus with opcode byte | | T3 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | PC(high) | Opcode | 0 | 8085 reads opcode byte | | T4 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | PC(high) | Opcode | 1 | Opcode decoded and executed internally | Total = **4 T-states**. **(b) Delay Calculation** - Crystal = 2 MHz → Internal clock = 1 MHz → T_clk = **1 μs** - N = C8H = 200 Using the single-register delay formula: $$ T_{delay} = (14N + 14) \times T_{clk} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} T_{delay} &= (14 \times 200 + 14) \times 1\,\mu s \\[4pt] &= (2800 + 14)\,\mu s \\[4pt] &= 2814\,\mu s \\[4pt] &= 2.814\,\text{milliseconds} \end{aligned} $$ **Answer: Delay ≈ 2.814 ms** --- ### ▶ Question B — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style, 14 Marks]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of a **delay subroutine** in 8085. Design a delay subroutine using a single register to generate a delay of **1 ms** with a **2 MHz** crystal. **(b) [7 Marks]** Design a **two-level nested delay loop** to produce a delay of approximately **1 second** using an 8085 with a 2 MHz crystal. Show all calculations. #### Model Solution **(a) Delay Subroutine — Concept & 1 ms Design** A delay subroutine is a small loop that consumes a pre-determined number of clock cycles, producing a fixed time delay. The total delay = (sum of T-states) × clock period. For a 2 MHz crystal, internal clock = 1 MHz ⇒ T_clk = 1 μs. Using the generic subroutine: ```asm DELAY: MVI D, N ; 7T LOOP: DCR D ; 4T JNZ LOOP ; 10T taken / 7T not taken RET ; 10T ``` Formula: T_delay = (14N + 14) × 1 μs Setting T_delay = 1000 μs: $$ \begin{aligned} 1000 &= 14N + 14 \\[4pt] 14N &= 986 \\[4pt] N &= 70.43 \\[4pt] N &\approx 70 = 46\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ Load D with 46H to get a delay ≈ 994 μs (close to 1 ms). **(b) 1-Second Delay Using Nested Loops** Formula for nested loop: $$ T_{delay} = (14MN + 18M + 14) \times T_{clk} $$ For 2 MHz crystal, T_clk = 1 μs, target T_delay = 1 000 000 μs: $$ 14MN + 18M + 14 = 1\,000\,000 $$ Choosing **M = N = FFH = 255**: $$ \begin{aligned} 14 \times 255 \times 255 + 18 \times 255 + 14 &= 910\,350 + 4\,590 + 14 \\[4pt] &= 914\,954\,\mu s \\[4pt] &\approx 0.915\,\text{seconds} \end{aligned} $$ To achieve a full **1 second**, we add a small outer NOP-padding loop, or use a **three-level nested loop** (H, D, E). For M = N = 255 in two levels: ```asm DELAY: MVI B, FFH ; Outer count = 255 LOOP2: MVI C, FFH ; Inner count = 255 LOOP1: DCR C JNZ LOOP1 DCR B JNZ LOOP2 RET ``` Approximate delay produced = **914.954 ms ≈ 0.915 s**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks!** > 1. **Forgetting the ÷2 internal divider** → Use 1 μs for 2 MHz crystal, NOT 0.5 μs. > 2. **Counting T-states incorrectly for JNZ taken vs not-taken** → 10T taken, 7T not taken. > 3. **Including RET T-states wrongly** → Always include the exit overhead (RET = 10T). > 4. **Using `DCX B` instead of `DCR B` for 8-bit count** → DCX B takes 6T-states, NOT 4T; the formula differs. > 5. **Confusing `DCR M` (10T) with `DCR r` (4T)** → Lose marks if memory operand used but T-states listed as 4. > 6. **Not subtracting the JNZ-taken to JNZ-not-taken transition** (subtract 3 once at the end). --- ## 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **T-State** = 1 internal clock pulse; **Clock period** = ½ × Crystal period. - **Opcode Fetch** is always **4 T-states** (status IO/M=0, S1=1, S0=1). - **Memory/IO Read/Write** is always **3 T-states**. - **`DCR r` = 4T**, **`DCR M` = 10T**, **`DCX rp` = 6T**, **`INR r` = 4T**, **`MVI r,data` = 7T**, **`LXI rp,data16` = 10T**. - **`J` taken = 10T, not-taken = 7T**, **`CALL` = 18T**, **`RET` = 10T**, **`NOP` = 4T**. - **Single-loop delay formula:** `T_delay = (14N + 14) × T_clk`. - **Nested-loop delay formula:** `T_delay = (14MN + 18M + 14) × T_clk`. - **Resolution of single-loop delay** = 14 × T_clk (smallest adjustable step). - **Maximum 8-bit delay** = 14 × 256 + 14 = 3598 T-states. - **For precision > 1 ms**, prefer **hardware timers (8255 PPI + counter)** over software loops. - **Always account for CALL overhead (18T) and the stack push/pop** if the delay is itself called from another subroutine. - **Common application areas:** LED blink, key debounce, baud-rate generation, ADC hold time, LCD enable pulse width.

topics.title: Interrupts - Interrupt Service Routines - Interfacing ADC and DAC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET504 - MICROPROCESSORS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Interrupts - Interrupt Service Routines - Interfacing ADC and DAC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Interrupts – The Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> An **Interrupt** is a hardware- or software-generated asynchronous signal/event that temporarily halts the main program execution of the microprocessor, forcing it to switch context and execute a special routine called the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**. Once the ISR completes, the processor resumes the original task from the exact point where it was interrupted.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy: "The Doorbell"

Imagine you are deeply focused on solving a mathematics problem at your study desk. Suddenly, the **doorbell rings**. You don't throw your notebook in the dustbin — instead, you:
1. **Bookmark** the page (save the current state).
2. **Walk to the door** (branch to the ISR).
3. **Receive the delivery** (execute the service).
4. **Return to your chair** (restore state).
5. **Continue solving** (resume main program).

The **doorbell** is the *interrupt request (INTR)*, the act of answering the door is *context switching*, and "receiving the package" is the *ISR*. This saves you from constantly checking the door (which is called **polling** — an inefficient method).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Interrupts Matter in KTU Context:**
> - **CPU efficiency**: CPU doesn't waste cycles "waiting".
> - **Real-time response**: Critical events (e.g., sensor overflow, emergency shutdown) get immediate attention.
> - **Multi-device management**: A single CPU can serve many peripherals (keyboard, ADC, timers) seamlessly.

---

## 1.2 Types of Interrupts (8086 Microprocessor Focus)

The **8086 microprocessor** supports **256 interrupts** numbered from `00H to FFH`.

| Type | Source | Trigger |
|------|--------|---------|
| **Hardware Interrupts** | External pins (NMI, INTR) | Electrical signal on pin |
| **Software Interrupts** | Instructions (INT n) | Execution of `INT n` opcode |
| **Internal Interrupts** | CPU-generated (Divide by 0, Single Step, Overflow) | Error condition during execution |

### Hardware Interrupts in 8086

| Pin | Type | Trigger | Vector Address |
|-----|------|---------|----------------|
| **NMI** (Non-Maskable Interrupt) | Edge-triggered | Rising edge (Type **2**) | `0000:0008H` |
| **INTR** (Interrupt Request) | Level-triggered | High level (Type 0–255) | From 8259 PIC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Maskable vs Non-Maskable:**
> - **INTR** is *maskable* (can be disabled using the `IF` flag in the Status Register).
> - **NMI** is *non-maskable* — it cannot be ignored; the CPU **must** respond. Used for critical events like power failure (via NMI from PGM/Power Good Monitor).

---

## 1.3 Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> An **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** — also called **Interrupt Handler** — is a specialized subroutine whose address is stored in the **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)**. When an interrupt fires, the CPU automatically fetches the CS:IP of the corresponding ISR and executes it. The ISR ends with an `IRET` (Interrupt Return) instruction, which atomically pops **IP, CS, and Flags** from the stack.

### Key Properties of an ISR
- **Atomicity:** Should be short and fast; must not call blocking OS functions.
- **Reentrancy:** Must be designed so multiple interrupts can nest safely.
- **Stack-safe:** Always preserve registers (using `PUSH` at entry, `POP` at exit).

### ISR Execution Sequence (KTU High-Yield Sequence)

1. **Interrupt detected** by CPU (INTR high, NMI edge, or INT n decoded).
2. **Finish current instruction** (no mid-instruction interruption).
3. **Push Flags, CS, IP** onto the stack (in that order).
4. **Clear IF and TF** flags (disables further maskable interrupts).
5. **Fetch new IP and CS** from the IVT (4-byte vector: IP-low, IP-high, CS-low, CS-high).
6. **Execute the ISR** body.
7. **Execute IRET** → pops IP, CS, Flags in reverse order.

---

## 1.4 Interfacing ADC and DAC — The I/O Backbone

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter):** Converts real-world analog signals (temperature, pressure, voice) into binary data the CPU can process.
> **DAC (Digital-to-Analog Converter):** Converts binary data from the CPU back into analog signals (audio output, motor control voltage, waveform generation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU EEE/Electrical Students:**
> - Microprocessors live in the **digital world** (0s and 1s).
> - The physical world (sensors, actuators) is **analog**.
> - ADC and DAC are the **bridges** between the two domains — without them, embedded systems are useless in real applications.

### Common Interfacing ICs (KTU High-Yield)

| IC | Function | Key Specs |
|----|----------|-----------|
| **ADC 0808 / 0809** | 8-bit ADC | 8 channels, 100 µs conversion, successive approximation |
| **DAC 0808** | 8-bit DAC | Settling time ~150 ns, current output |
| **ADC 0804** | 8-bit ADC | Single channel, single supply 5V |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deep Dive: The 8086 Interrupt Architecture

### 2.1.1 Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)

The IVT resides in the **first 1 KB of memory** (`00000H` to `003FFH`). It contains **256 entries** of 4 bytes each (2 bytes IP + 2 bytes CS), giving the CS:IP of the corresponding ISR.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Layout of IVT:**
> - For interrupt type `N`, the vector is at physical address: **`N × 4`**.
> - Example: INT 21H → `(21H × 4) = 84H`. So bytes at `00084H–00087H` contain IP (low, high) and CS (low, high).

### 2.1.2 Classification of 8086 Interrupts (Detailed)

| Class | Examples | Vector | Triggered By |
|-------|----------|--------|--------------|
| **Dedicated/Internal** | Type 0: Divide Error | `00H` | Division by zero |
| | Type 1: Single Step | `04H` | TF = 1 (trap flag) |
| | Type 2: NMI | `08H` | Hardware NMI pin |
| | Type 3: Breakpoint | `0CH` | `INT 3` instruction |
| | Type 4: Overflow | `10H` | `INTO` when OF = 1 |
| **User-Defined Software** | `INT n` | `n × 4` | Program instruction |
| **Hardware (Maskable)** | Type 5–255 | `n × 4` | INTR via 8259A PIC |

### 2.1.3 The 8259A Programmable Interrupt Controller (PIC)

The 8086 has only **1 INTR pin**, but real systems need to handle **8 or more** interrupt sources. The **8259A** expands this:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **8259A Key Facts (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - Handles **8 interrupt inputs** (IR0–IR7) with **prioritization**.
> - Cascadable: **up to 64 interrupts** using 8 × 8259A masters/slaves.
> - Communicates with 8086 via **INTR** and receives acknowledgment via **INTA** (Interrupt Acknowledge).
> - Two ICWs (Initialization Command Words) + three OCWs (Operation Command Words) for configuration.

### Priority Modes of 8259A

- **Fully Nested Mode (default):** IR0 highest, IR7 lowest.
- **Special Fully Nested Mode:** Same as above but allows higher-priority interrupts to interrupt lower-priority ISRs.
- **Rotating Priority (Automatic / Specific):** After servicing, the priority "rotates" to the next input — prevents starvation.

---

## 2.2 Deep Dive: ADC Interfacing (ADC 0808 / 0809)

### Architecture Overview

The **ADC 0808** is a **successive approximation** ADC with:
- **8 analog input channels** (IN0–IN7) selected by 3 address lines (ADD-A, ADD-B, ADD-C).
- **8-bit digital output** (D0–D7).
- **Resolution:** $\frac{V_{REF+}-V_{REF-}}{2^8}$ = $\frac{V_{REF+}-V_{REF-}}{256}$ per LSB.

### Working Cycle (KTU High-Yield)

1. **Select channel** via address lines ADD-C, ADD-B, ADD-A.
2. **Assert ALE** (Address Latch Enable) → latches the channel.
3. **Assert START** (a pulse) → begins conversion.
4. **Wait for EOC** (End of Conversion) → goes HIGH when done.
5. **Assert OE** (Output Enable) → output data appears on D0–D7.
6. **Read data** via IN instruction.

### The Key ADC Formula (KTU Board Essential)

$$V_{in} = \frac{D}{2^n} \times V_{REF}$$

Where:
- $D$ = Digital output (decimal, 0 to 255 for 8-bit)
- $n$ = Number of bits (8 here)
- $V_{REF}$ = Reference voltage (typically +5V)
- $V_{in}$ = Equivalent analog input

**Resolution (Step Size):**
$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} = \frac{5V}{256} \approx 19.53\ \text{mV/LSB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In a temperature sensor circuit where the sensor outputs 0–5V for 0–100°C, a 10-bit ADC would give resolution of $5V/1024 \approx 4.88$ mV, i.e., $\approx 0.1°C$. Higher $n$ → finer measurement.

---

## 2.3 Deep Dive: DAC Interfacing (DAC 0808)

### Architecture Overview

The **DAC 0808** is an **8-bit current-output DAC** with:
- Settling time: **~150 ns**.
- Output current: $I_{out} = I_{ref} \times \frac{D}{256}$ (approx).
- Needs external **op-amp** to convert current to voltage (I-to-V converter).

### The Key DAC Formula (KTU Board Essential)

The output voltage of the op-amp summing junction is:

$$V_{out} = -I_{out} \times R_f = -\left(\frac{V_{ref}}{R_{ref}} \times \frac{D}{256}\right) \times R_f$$

Simplified for a typical $R_f = R_{ref}$ design:

$$V_{out} = -\frac{V_{ref} \times D}{256}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative sign indicates **inverting amplifier** configuration. To get a positive output, we can use a **second inverting stage** (gain = –1) or **rearrange reference polarity**.

---

## 2.4 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | **IVT Vector Address** | $\text{Addr} = N \times 4$ | $N$ = interrupt type (0–255) |
| 2 | **ADC Step Size (Resolution)** | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n}$ | $n$ = bits; V/LSB |
| 3 | **ADC Input Voltage from Digital** | $V_{in} = D \times \Delta V$ | $D$ = decimal output |
| 4 | **ADC Percentage Resolution** | $\%R = \dfrac{1}{2^n} \times 100$ | Higher $n$ → better |
| 5 | **DAC Output Voltage** | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{V_{ref} \times D}{2^n}$ | For inverting config |
| 6 | **DAC Full-Scale Voltage** | $V_{FS} = \dfrac{V_{ref} \times (2^n - 1)}{2^n}$ | Approx. $V_{ref}$ |
| 7 | **Conversion Time (Successive Approx.)** | $T_{conv} = n \times T_{clk}$ | Per bit, $n$ cycles |
| 8 | **Max Sampling Rate** | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_{conv}}$ | Hz |
| 9 | **8259A Max IRQs (cascaded)** | $8 \times m$ where $m$ ≤ 9 | 1 master + 8 slaves = 64 |
| 10 | **SNR (Quantization Noise)** | $SNR \approx 6.02n + 1.76$ dB | For ideal n-bit ADC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Board:** "**VIVT**" — **V**ector, **I**VTR address, **V**oltage = **T**imes.
> - Address = N × 4
> - V_in = D × (V_ref / 2^n)

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ADC 0808** is widely used in **digital voltmeters, digital thermometers, data loggers, and process control** systems.
- **DAC 0808** is found in **function generators, audio amplifiers with digital input, motor speed controllers, and waveform synthesizers**.
- **8259A PIC** is the heart of **PC AT architecture** (modern systems use IO-APIC and LAPIC, but the principle is identical).
- **ISR-based ADC reading** is the foundation of **digital oscilloscopes, ECG machines, and IoT sensor nodes**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1: Calculate the IVT Vector Address

**Problem:** Find the physical address of the Interrupt Vector Table entry for `INT 50H` in an 8086 system. Also find the CS:IP values if memory at `0000:0190H` contains `86 30 A0 20`.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the IVT offset.**
Each vector is 4 bytes, starting at `00000H`. For type `N`:
$$\text{IVT\_Address} = 4 \times N$$
$$\text{IVT\_Address} = 4 \times 50H = 140H$$

**Step 2 — Convert to physical address.**
The IVT lives in segment `0000H`, so physical address = offset.
$$\text{Physical Address} = (0000H \times 10H) + 140H = 00140H$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the 4 bytes as IP and CS.**
The 8086 stores the vector as `[IP-low][IP-high][CS-low][CS-high]`.

- Byte at `00140H` = `86H` → IP low
- Byte at `00141H` = `30H` → IP high
- Byte at `00142H` = `A0H` → CS low
- Byte at `00143H` = `20H` → CS high

**Step 4 — Form the 16-bit registers.**
$$IP = (30H \times 100H) + 86H = 3086H$$
$$CS = (20H \times 100H) + A0H = 20A0H$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** The ISR for `INT 50H` starts at logical address `20A0:3086H` (physical address `20A00H + 3086H = 23A86H`).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2: ADC Resolution and Digital Output

**Problem:** An **8-bit ADC** has a reference voltage of $V_{REF} = +5.0V$. Calculate:
(a) The step size (resolution).
(b) The digital output for an analog input of $V_{in} = 2.5V$.
(c) The percentage resolution.
(d) The quantization noise SNR.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the step size (LSB weight).**
$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} = \frac{5.0}{2^8} = \frac{5.0}{256}$$
$$\Delta V = 0.01953125\ \text{V} \approx \mathbf{19.53\ mV/LSB}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the digital output for 2.5V.**
$$D = \frac{V_{in}}{\Delta V} = \frac{2.5}{0.01953125}$$
$$D = 128 \quad (\text{decimal}) = 10000000_2 = \mathbf{80H}$$

**Verification using direct formula:**
$$D = \frac{V_{in} \times 2^n}{V_{REF}} = \frac{2.5 \times 256}{5.0} = 128 \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Compute percentage resolution.**
$$\%R = \frac{1}{2^n} \times 100 = \frac{1}{256} \times 100$$
$$\%R = 0.3906\%$$

**Step 4 — Compute the theoretical SNR.**
$$SNR = 6.02n + 1.76 = (6.02 \times 8) + 1.76$$
$$SNR = 48.16 + 1.76 = \mathbf{49.92\ dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Every doubling of $n$ adds **6 dB** of SNR — a key Board trick for KTU valuation. So a 16-bit ADC would have ~98 dB SNR (CD quality audio).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3: DAC Output Calculation

**Problem:** A **DAC 0808** is configured with $V_{ref} = +5.0V$, $R_{ref} = R_f = 5\ \text{k}\Omega$ in inverting mode. The digital input byte is `B3H` (= 179 decimal). Find the output voltage.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert the digital input to decimal.**
$$D = B3H = (11 \times 16) + 3 = 176 + 3 = 179$$

**Step 2 — Apply the DAC output formula.**
$$V_{out} = -\frac{V_{ref} \times D}{2^n} = -\frac{5.0 \times 179}{256}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the magnitude.**
$$V_{out} = -\frac{895}{256} = -3.496\ \text{V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $V_{out} \approx \mathbf{-3.50\ V}$ (inverted; for non-inverting, use a second stage or swap op-amp inputs).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Problem 4: 8259A Cascading for Max Interrupts

**Problem:** An industrial control system uses 1 master 8259A and 4 slave 8259As. How many IRQ lines are available? How many ICWs are needed in total?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute total IRQ capacity.**
Each 8259A has 8 IR lines. The master uses IR0–IR2 (or any 2 or 3) to cascade to slaves.

Using the standard arrangement (master IR2 = slave cascade):
- Master: 8 IR lines, but 2 used for cascade → **6 usable + cascade pin = 8 total devices**
- Each slave adds 8 new lines.

For 4 slaves: master contributes (8 – 2) = 6 free lines, plus 4 × 8 = 32 from slaves:
$$N_{IRQ} = 6 + 4 \times 8 = 38\ \text{IRQ lines}$$

If we use a more efficient arrangement with 1 dedicated cascade line:
$$N_{IRQ} = 7 + 4 \times 8 = \mathbf{39\ IRQ\ lines}$$

**Step 2 — Count the ICWs (Initialization Command Words).**
- Each 8259A needs **2 ICWs** (ICW1, ICW2, and optionally ICW3/ICW4).
- Total for master + 4 slaves = **5 ICW1 + 5 ICW2 + 5 ICW3 (with cascade) = 15 ICWs**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative: Maximum Cascade Configuration:** 1 master + 8 slaves = $7 + (8 \times 8) = 71$ IRQs (theoretical max, but standard says 64).

---

| Step Size (5V ref) | 19.53 mV | 4.88 mV | 1.22 mV | 0.076 mV |
| % Resolution | 0.39% | 0.098% | 0.024% | 0.0015% |
| SNR (dB) | 49.92 | 61.96 | 74.00 | 98.08 |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Interrupt Handling Flowchart (8086)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU executing main program] --> B{Interrupt detected? INTR high, NMI edge, INT n decoded}
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C[Complete current instruction]
    C --> D[Push FLAGS onto stack]
    D --> E[Push CS onto stack]
    E --> F[Push IP onto stack]
    F --> G[Clear IF and TF flags]
    G --> H[Fetch CS:IP from IVT at address N x 4]
    H --> I[Execute ISR]
    I --> J{Is this IRET instruction?}
    J -- No --> I
    J -- Yes --> K[Pop IP from stack]
    K --> L[Pop CS from stack]
    L --> M[Pop FLAGS from stack]
    M --> N[Resume main program from saved IP]
    N --> A
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define an interrupt. List the various types of interrupts supported by the 8086 microprocessor with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **interrupt** is an external or internal event that causes the microprocessor to temporarily suspend the execution of the current program, execute a special subroutine called the Interrupt Service Routine (ISR), and then resume the original program.

**Types of interrupts in 8086:**

1. **Hardware Interrupts** (External): NMI (Type 2) and INTR (Type 0–255 via 8259A).
2. **Software Interrupts**: `INT n` instructions (e.g., `INT 21H` for DOS services).
3. **Internal/Processor Interrupts**: Generated by the CPU itself, e.g., Type 0 (Divide by Zero), Type 4 (Overflow via `INTO`).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)? Explain the function of the `IRET` instruction in 8086.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **ISR (Interrupt Service Routine)** is a subroutine that is automatically executed in response to a specific interrupt. The starting address of each ISR is stored in the Interrupt Vector Table (IVT) at location `N × 4`.

**`IRET` (Interrupt Return):**
- It is a 1-byte instruction (opcode `CFH`).
- Performs a **reverse sequence** of operations done at interrupt entry.
- **Pops IP**, then **CS**, and finally **Flags Register** from the stack.
- Returns execution control to the exact instruction in the main program that was interrupted.

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Explain the Interrupt Vector Table (IVT) of 8086 in detail. How is the vector address for a given interrupt computed? (7 marks)**

**(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the interfacing of **ADC 0808** with the 8086 microprocessor. Mention the function of EOC, ALE, START, and OE signals. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — IVT (7 marks)

**Definition:** The **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** is a 1 KB block of memory located at the very beginning of the 8086's address space, from `00000H` to `003FFH`. It contains the **CS:IP (4 bytes)** of the Interrupt Service Routine for each of the 256 possible interrupt types.

**Structure:**
- 256 entries × 4 bytes = 1024 bytes (1 KB).
- For interrupt type `N`, the IVT entry is at physical address: **`N × 4`**.
- Memory order: `IP-low`, `IP-high`, `CS-low`, `CS-high` (little-endian).

**Key reserved vectors:**
- `00000H` – INT 00H: Divide Error
- `00004H` – INT 01H: Single Step (TF = 1)
- `00008H` – INT 02H: NMI
- `0000CH` – INT 03H: Breakpoint
- `00010H` – INT 04H: Overflow (`INTO`)

**Example:** Vector address for `INT 50H` is `50H × 4 = 140H`. If memory at `00140H–00143H` contains `86 30 A0 20`, then:
- IP = `3086H`, CS = `20A0H` → ISR starts at `20A0:3086H`.

**Steps to compute vector address:**

1. Identify the interrupt type `N` (0 to 255).
2. Multiply `N` by 4 → gives the 16-bit offset within segment `0000H`.
3. Read 4 bytes from this offset:
   - Bytes 0, 1 → IP (low, high).
   - Bytes 2, 3 → CS (low, high).
4. Form CS:IP — this is the ISR's starting logical address.

### Part (b) — ADC 0808 Interfacing (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.3).** Key connections:

- **8086 lower data bus (D0–D7)** → **ADC 0808 D0–D7** (bidirectional).
- **8086 address lines A0–A2** (demultiplexed) → **ADC ADD-A, ADD-B, ADD-C** (channel select, 3 bits → 8 channels).
- **8086 address line A3** (or decoded) → **ADC ALE** (Address Latch Enable) and **START**.
- **ADC EOC** → connected to **8086 INTR** (interrupt-driven) or polled via a status port.
- **8086 control line** (decoded) → **ADC OE** (Output Enable).

**Function of key signals:**

- **ALE (Address Latch Enable):** A HIGH pulse latches the channel address (ADD-A, B, C) into the ADC's internal decoder. Without this, the channel selection is unstable.
- **START:** A HIGH pulse initiates the A/D conversion process. The ADC begins the successive approximation on the **rising edge** of START.
- **EOC (End of Conversion):** Goes LOW during conversion and HIGH when complete. Connected to the 8086's INTR to trigger an ISR that reads the digital data.
- **OE (Output Enable):** When HIGH, the tri-state output buffers (D0–D7) are enabled, allowing the 8086 to read the conversion result.

**Working sequence:**

1. CPU sends channel number on A0–A2.
2. CPU generates ALE pulse → channel latched.
3. CPU generates START pulse → conversion begins.
4. ADC runs successive approximation (takes ~100 µs).
5. EOC goes HIGH → interrupts CPU (or sets a polled flag).
6. CPU generates OE = HIGH → reads D0–D7 via `IN` instruction.
7. CPU computes analog equivalent: $V_{in} = D \times (V_{REF}/256)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — ADC Interfacing:**
> Students often confuse **ALE** and **START** — both are pulses, but ALE **latches the address** (channel) while START **begins the conversion**. If you write them as equivalent, you lose 1 mark. Also, students forget that **EOC must be polled or interrupt-driven** — the CPU cannot just wait without a mechanism; this is a frequent 2-mark deduction.
> **Also:** EOC goes **LOW** during conversion and **HIGH** when done — not the other way around. Mistake costs 1 mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Sessional Model, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the architecture and operation of the 8259A Programmable Interrupt Controller. Mention the function of ICW1, ICW2, and OCW1. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the interfacing of **DAC 0808** with the 8086 microprocessor. Derive the expression for the output voltage in terms of the digital input. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — 8259A PIC (7 marks)

**Architecture (refer to Section 4.4):**

The **8259A** is an 8-channel programmable interrupt controller that manages 8 IRQ inputs. Internally it has:

- **IRR (Interrupt Request Register, 8-bit):** Stores pending interrupt requests.
- **PR (Priority Resolver):** Determines which pending IRQ has the highest priority.
- **ISR (In-Service Register, 8-bit):** Tracks which IRQs are currently being serviced.
- **Control Logic:** Generates `INTR` to 8086 and processes `INTA` from 8086.
- **Data Bus Buffer:** 8-bit bidirectional interface to system data bus.
- **Cascade Buffer/Comparator:** Used when multiple 8259As are cascaded.

**Operation:**

1. Peripheral raises one of `IR0–IR7`.
2. IRR sets the corresponding bit.
3. Priority Resolver picks the highest-priority active bit.
4. 8259A asserts `INTR` to 8086.
5. 8086 responds with `INTA` pulses.
6. On the **first INTA pulse**, the highest-priority bit is moved from IRR to ISR.
7. On the **second INTA pulse**, 8259A places an **8-bit interrupt type** (0–255) on the data bus.
8. 8086 reads the type, computes the IVT address, fetches the ISR, and executes it.
9. At EOI (End of Interrupt), 8086 issues OCW2 to clear the ISR bit.

**Function of command words:**

- **ICW1 (Initialization Command Word 1):** Initiates 8259A initialization. Configures whether 8086 mode is used, cascaded or single, and whether ICW4 is needed.
- **ICW2 (ICW 2):** Sets the **upper 5 bits of the interrupt type** (the lower 3 bits come from the IR number). For example, ICW2 = `40H` means IR0 → Type `40H`, IR1 → Type `41H`, etc.
- **OCW1 (Operation Command Word 1):** Used to **mask/unmask individual IR inputs** by writing to the IMR (Interrupt Mask Register). Writing `1` to a bit masks that IR line.

### Part (b) — DAC 0808 Interfacing (7 marks)

**Block diagram (refer to Section 4.5).** Key connections:

- **8086 data bus D0–D7** → **DAC 0808 digital inputs A1–A8** (8 bits, MSB to LSB).
- **Vref** (+5V or +10V reference) applied via resistor `Rref` to DAC pin 14.
- **DAC Iout** (current output, pin 4) → **Op-Amp inverting input**.
- **Op-Amp feedback resistor `Rf`** between output and inverting input.
- **Non-inverting input of op-amp** → Ground.

**Derivation of output voltage:**

The DAC 0808 produces a current output proportional to the digital input. The reference current is:
$$I_{ref} = \frac{V_{ref}}{R_{ref}}$$

The output current of the DAC:
$$I_{out} = I_{ref} \times \frac{D}{2^n} = \frac{V_{ref}}{R_{ref}} \times \frac{D}{2^n}$$

The op-amp acts as a current-to-voltage converter (transimpedance amplifier). The inverting input is at virtual ground, so all $I_{out}$ flows through $R_f$. The output voltage is:
$$V_{out} = -I_{out} \times R_f = -\frac{V_{ref}}{R_{ref}} \times \frac{D}{2^n} \times R_f$$

For a **standard configuration** with $R_f = R_{ref}$ (the typical design choice):
$$V_{out} = -\frac{V_{ref} \times D}{2^n} = -\frac{V_{ref} \times D}{256}$$

**Sign convention:** The output is **negative** because the op-amp is in inverting configuration. To obtain a positive output, one of the following is used:
- An additional **inverting amplifier** (gain = –1) at the output.
- Reverse the polarity of $V_{ref}$.

**Example:** If $V_{ref} = 5V$, $D = 128$ (`80H`):
$$V_{out} = -\frac{5 \times 128}{256} = -2.5\ \text{V}$$

**For a unipolar 0 to +5V output** (most common), use a **second inverting stage** or choose $V_{ref} = -5V$ at the reference pin of the DAC.

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — DAC Interfacing:**
> Two common mistakes cost students marks:
> 1. **Forgetting the op-amp** — The DAC 0808 produces **current**, not voltage. You **must** show the op-amp I-to-V stage. Drawing DAC output directly to a "Vout" pin costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong sign convention** — The output is **negative** (inverting). If a problem asks for "0 to +5V output" and you write $V_{out} = +V_{ref} \times D/256$ without justification, you lose 1–2 marks. State the assumption: "Assuming $V_{ref} = -5V$" or "with a second inverting stage".
> 3. **Not specifying n=8** — for ADC 0808 / DAC 0808 specifically. Generic $2^n$ is acceptable but writing 256 explicitly shows mastery.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap: Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### ⚡ Interrupts Core

- [x] 8086 supports **256 interrupts** (Type `00H–FFH`).
- [x] **IVT** size = 1 KB, located at `00000H–003FFH`.
- [x] **IVT address for type N = N × 4**.
- [x] **5 dedicated interrupts**: Types 0, 1, 2, 3, 4.
- [x] **NMI** is non-maskable (Type 2, vector at `00008H`).
- [x] **INTR** is maskable (controlled by `IF` flag); serviced via 8259A.
- [x] **TF** (Trap Flag) enables single-stepping (Type 1).
- [x] **INT 3** (Type 3) is a 1-byte breakpoint instruction.
- [x] **INTO** triggers Type 4 only if `OF = 1`.
- [x] **IRET** pops IP, CS, Flags (in that order).
- [x] During ISR entry, **IF and TF are cleared** automatically.

### ⚡ 8259A PIC Core

- [x] Manages **8 IRQs** per chip; cascadable up to **64 IRQs**.
- [x] 2 ICWs + 3 OCWs.
- [x] **ICW1** = initialization trigger; **ICW2** = base vector; **ICW3** = cascade arrangement.
- [x] **OCW1** = IMR (mask); **OCW2** = EOI command; **OCW3** = read IRR/ISR.
- [x] **EOI** (End of Interrupt) sent via OCW2 to clear ISR bit.
- [x] **INTA** sequence requires 2 pulses — first latches priority, second places type on data bus.

### ⚡ ADC Interfacing Core

- [x] **ADC 0808**: 8 channels, 8-bit, successive approximation, ~100 µs conversion.
- [x] **Step size** $\Delta V = V_{REF}/2^n$.
- [x] **Digital output** $D = V_{in} \times 2^n / V_{REF}$.
- [x] **EOC** → connected to INTR for interrupt-driven I/O.
- [x] **ALE latches channel**; **START begins conversion**; **OE enables output**.
- [x] **Higher n → better resolution**; SNR ≈ $6.02n + 1.76$ dB.

### ⚡ DAC Interfacing Core

- [x] **DAC 0808**: 8-bit, current output, needs op-amp I-to-V stage.
- [x] Output voltage $V_{out} = -V_{ref} \times D / 2^n$ (inverting).
- [x] Settling time ~150 ns.
- [x] **Settling time** is the most critical speed parameter.
- [x] For **0 to +5V unipolar output**, use a second inverting op-amp stage.

### ⚡ Numerical Quick-Reference

| Quantity | 8-bit | 10-bit | 12-bit |
|----------|-------|--------|--------|
| Step size (5V ref) | 19.53 mV | 4.88 mV | 1.22 mV |
| % Resolution | 0.39% | 0.098% | 0.024% |
| SNR (dB) | 49.92 | 61.96 | 74.00 |
| Conversion time (1 µs/bit) | 8 µs | 10 µs | 12 µs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 Final KTU Board Tip:** When a question asks "explain ADC interfacing", always include:
> 1. A **block diagram** (3 marks).
> 2. The **timing/control signals** (ALE, START, EOC, OE) with their functions (2 marks).
> 3. The **digital-to-analog conversion formula** with a numerical example (2 marks).
>
> This 3-step structure mirrors the KTU 2024 marking scheme and guarantees full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Embedded Systems - Application Domain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET504 - MICROPROCESSORS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Embedded Systems - Application Domain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

An **Embedded System** is a specialized, domain-specific computing subsystem designed to perform a dedicated function or a small set of related functions, *embedded* as an integral part of a larger electro-mechanical or electronic device. It is a combination of **hardware (microprocessor/microcontroller + peripherals)** and **software (firmware/RTOS)** engineered to meet real-time, performance, power, size, and cost constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Standard):** *"An embedded system is any computing system that uses a computer (microprocessor or microcontroller) as a component, but whose presence is not explicitly recognised by the user as being a computer."* — This is the most accepted framing for university examinations.

### The Three Pillars of an Embedded System

1. **Hardware** — Processor (Microprocessor/Microcontroller/DSP/ASIC/SoC/FPGA), Memory (RAM/ROM/Flash), I/O Devices, Sensors & Actuators, Power Supply, Communication Interfaces.
2. **Software** — Application Firmware, Device Drivers, Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) — *only in advanced systems*.
3. **Mechanical/Physical Component** — The housing, chassis, or the larger system into which the computing element is integrated (e.g., the car body in an ECU).

---

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Specialist Worker" Model

Imagine a **general-purpose computer (your laptop)** as a *versatile graduate* who can cook, drive, teach, paint, and do accounting — but none of these tasks to a professional standard. Now imagine an **embedded system** as a *specialist chef* who only cooks, but does it faster, cheaper, more reliably, and with far less energy than the generalist could ever achieve.

| Aspect | General-Purpose Computer (Laptop) | Embedded System (e.g., Washing Machine) |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Multi-purpose | Single, dedicated task |
| User awareness of OS | Always visible | Usually invisible |
| Power consumption | 60–250 W | 0.1 mW – 5 W |
| Cost | ₹30,000+ | ₹20 – ₹200 (controller IC) |
| Real-time guarantee | Best-effort | **Hard real-time** often required |
| Upgradability | Frequent | Rare / none |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-time** means correctness depends not only on *what* the output is, but also on *when* it is produced. A delayed airbag deployment is mathematically "correct" but practically fatal.

---

### Why Study "Application Domain"?

KTU 2024 Module 3 explicitly highlights the **Application Domain** because the *requirements, constraints, and architecture* of an embedded system are dictated entirely by **where it is deployed**. A medical pacemaker demands ultra-low power and 100% reliability; a fighter-jet radar demands teraflop-scale processing under vibration; a TV remote demands 3 months of battery life on a coin cell. Same conceptual definition — radically different engineering.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Content Sheet

### Core Characteristics of Embedded Systems (High-Yield)

These seven characteristics appear in almost every KTU question paper. Memorize as a framework.

- **Single-Functioned / Domain-Specific:** Performs one (or a tightly related set of) function(s). Cannot run MS Word or browse the web.
- **Tightly Constrained:** Bound by design limits — power, performance, memory, cost, size, weight, heat dissipation.
- **Reactive & Real-Time:** Continuously interacts with the environment; must respond within strict timing deadlines.
- **Hardware-Software Co-Design:** Optimal solution requires *joint* design of ASIC/microcontroller selection and firmware architecture.
- **High Reliability & Safety-Critical:** Failure may be catastrophic (airbags, pacemakers, anti-lock braking).
- **Minimal User Interface:** Often a few LEDs, buttons, or a small LCD — not a full GUI.
- **Firmware in ROM/Flash:** Code is burned into non-volatile memory; no secondary storage like HDD/SSD in most cases.

### KTU Formula / Metrics Cheat Sheet

Although this is conceptual, KTU examiners expect students to quote industry-standard metrics. Use the table below as a rapid-reference.

> **Table 1: Industry-Standard Metrics for Embedded System Classification**

| Metric | Symbol/Unit | Typical Value / Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Processing Power | **MIPS / DMIPS** | Dhrystone MIPS — measures integer CPU throughput |
| Clock Frequency | **f\_clk** in **MHz / GHz** | Tick rate of the system clock |
| Power Dissipation | **P** in **mW / W** | Critical for battery-operated devices |
| Energy per Operation | **E** in **nJ/op** | E = P / (instructions per second) |
| Response Time / Latency | **t\_r** in **µs / ms** | Time from stimulus to system response |
| Jitter | **Δt** in **µs** | Variation in latency over many cycles |
| Memory Footprint | **M** in **KB / MB** | Program + data RAM/ROM required |
| Mean Time Between Failures | **MTBF** in **hours** | Reliability indicator (10⁵ – 10⁶ hours typical) |
| Operating Temperature | **T** in **°C** | Industrial: −40 °C to +85 °C; Military: −55 °C to +125 °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always write units using **International System (SI)** notation in KTU exams. Spelling out "MegaHertz" loses marks; use **MHz**.

### Classification of Embedded Systems by Scale (KTU Favourite)

KTU repeatedly tests this 3-tier classification:

- **Small-Scale Embedded Systems** — 8-bit/16-bit microcontroller (8051, PIC16F, ATmega328), no OS, simple control loop, C/assembly firmware. *Example:* Digital thermometer, TV remote, calculator.
- **Medium-Scale Embedded Systems** — 32-bit microcontroller (ARM Cortex-M0/M3/M4), RTOS (FreeRTOS, RTX), complex algorithms. *Example:* Digital camera, GPS unit, IoT sensor node, washing machine.
- **Large-Scale / Sophisticated Embedded Systems** — High-performance SoC (ARM Cortex-A, multi-core), full OS (Embedded Linux, Android, VxWorks), Gbps networking, hardware accelerators (GPU/DSP/NPU). *Example:* Smartphone, ADAS in cars, avionics, medical imaging systems.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the application domain enables a designer to answer the four cardinal engineering questions:

1. **What processor?** — 8-bit for a remote, 64-bit SoC for an infotainment cluster.
2. **What OS (if any)?** — Bare-metal for a toaster, RTOS for a drone, Embedded Linux for a smart TV.
3. **What power budget?** — Coin cell (3 V, 220 mAh) for IoT, vehicle battery (12 V, 50 Ah) for ECU.
4. **What reliability class?** — Consumer (acceptable failure rate 10⁻³), Industrial (10⁻⁵), Automotive (10⁻⁷), Medical/Aerospace (10⁻⁹).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Application Domain Deep Dive

The Application Domain of embedded systems spans virtually every sector of modern engineering. Below is the **exhaustive analytical breakdown** of each domain as per KTU Module 3 expectations, including examples, key constraints, representative processor choices, and design challenges.

### Analytical Matrix: Application Domain → Engineering Characteristics

> **Table 2: Domain-wise Analytical Breakdown (KTU Exam Reference)**

| # | Application Domain | Representative Products | Key Embedded Constraints | Typical Processor/SoC | Design Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Consumer Electronics** | Smart TV, Set-Top Box, Smart Speaker (Alexa), Game Console, Digital Camera | High multimedia throughput, low cost, sleek form factor | ARM Cortex-A53/A55, MediaTek, Qualcomm Snapdragon | Performance-per-Watt, UI fluidity |
| 2 | **Home Appliances** | Washing Machine, Refrigerator, Microwave Oven, AC, Vacuum Robot (Roomba), Dishwasher | Ultra-low cost (₹20–100 controller), sensor integration, motor control | 8051, PIC16F877A, STM8, ATmega328 | Cost, EMI/EMC robustness |
| 3 | **Automotive / Vehicular** | ECU (Engine Control Unit), ABS, Airbag System, ADAS, Infotainment, TPMS, EV Battery Management | ISO 26262 functional safety, −40 °C to +125 °C, vibration tolerance | Infineon Aurix (TC3xx), NXP S32K, Renesas RH850 | Safety, reliability (10⁻⁷ FIT) |
| 4 | **Industrial Automation** | PLC, SCADA RTUs, CNC Machine, Robotic Arms, Conveyor Controllers, Smart Meters | Determinism, long-term stability (10+ years), fieldbus/industrial Ethernet | TI Sitara, STM32F4/F7, Cortex-R5 | Determinism, ruggedness |
| 5 | **Medical / Healthcare** | Pacemaker, Infusion Pump, MRI/CT Scanner, Blood Glucose Monitor, Ventilator, Patient Monitor | IEC 62304 compliance, fault tolerance, biocompatibility | MSP430 (ultra-low-power), ARM Cortex-M4, DSPs | Reliability, ultra-low power |
| 6 | **Aerospace & Defense** | Flight Control Computer, UAV/Drone, GPS, Radar, Missile Guidance, Satellite Payload | Radiation hardening (RAD-hard), vibration, MIL-STD-810 | RAD-hardened FPGAs (Xilinx Virtex-QV), PowerPC, Leon SPARC | Fault tolerance, real-time |
| 7 | **Telecommunications** | Routers, Modems, 5G Base Stations, Optical Switches, VoIP Gateways | Gbps packet processing, encryption, QoS | Network processors (Cavium/Marvell), SoCs with hardware crypto | Throughput, security |
| 8 | **IoT & Smart Devices** | Smart Bulb (Philips Hue), Smart Lock, Wearables (Smartwatch), Environmental Sensors, Asset Trackers | Coin-cell battery life (years), wireless (BLE/LoRa/Zigbee), cloud integration | ESP32, Nordic nRF52, STM32WB | Power, connectivity |
| 9 | **Office Automation** | Laser Printer, Photocopier, Fax, Barcode Scanner, POS Terminal | Mechanical-electrical co-design, motor control, image processing | ARM Cortex-M4/M7, dedicated ASICs | Throughput, image quality |
| 10 | **Banking & Finance** | Smart Cards (Credit/Debit), ATMs, POS, Crypto Wallets, NFC Payment | Tamper resistance, cryptographic security (FIPS 140-2), ultra-low power | Secure Element ICs (NXP SmartMX), ARM SecurCore | Security, certification |
| 11 | **Robotics** | Industrial Robots (KUKA, FANUC), AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles), Surgical Robots (Da Vinci) | Real-time motion control, sensor fusion, deterministic network (EtherCAT) | ARM Cortex-R/M, DSPs, FPGAs | Real-time determinism |
| 12 | **Security & Surveillance** | CCTV/IP Cameras, Biometric Scanners, Intrusion Alarms, Drones for patrolling | Image processing (H.264/H.265), edge AI inference, network security | HiSilicon, Ambarella SoCs, edge-AI chips (Google Edge TPU) | AI inference, bandwidth |
| 13 | **Smart Grid & Energy** | Smart Meters, Solar Inverters, Wind Turbine Controllers, Battery Management Systems | Long-life (15+ years), wireless mesh (Zigbee/Wi-SUN), EMI immunity | TI CC13xx/CC26xx, STM32 | Power, longevity |
| 14 | **Agriculture / Smart Farming** | Drone-based Crop Monitoring, Automatic Irrigation, Livestock Tracking, Soil Sensors | Solar-powered, outdoor (IP67), long-range wireless (LoRaWAN) | LoRa + Cortex-M0, ESP32-S3 | Power, weatherproofing |

---

### Step-by-Step Engineering Decision Walkthrough (Worked Analytical Example)

> **Problem Scenario (KTU-style):** *Design constraints for a digital wristwatch vs. a fighter-jet radar.*

This is a classic "compare-and-justify" KTU 14-mark question. The exhaustive step-by-step analysis is shown below using formal aligned equations where quantitative metrics are relevant.

**Step 1 — Identify the function.**

- Wristwatch: Display time, date, alarm, optionally heart rate and Bluetooth notifications.
- Radar: Detect, track, and identify aerial targets at ranges of 100+ km, in real-time.

**Step 2 — Quantify the timing deadline.**

For both, latency $t\_r$ and jitter $\Delta t$ are critical. We can model deadline margin:

$$
t\_r \;\leq\; t\_\text{deadline} - \Delta t_\text{scheduling}
$$

For the wristwatch, $t\_\text{deadline} \approx 1 \text{ s}$ (refresh LCD).

$$
t\_{r,\text{watch}} \;\leq\; 1000 \text{ ms} - 10 \text{ ms} \;\approx\; 990 \text{ ms}
$$

For the radar, $t\_\text{deadline} \approx 10 \text{ ms}$ (track refresh).

$$
t\_{r,\text{radar}} \;\leq\; 10 \text{ ms} - 0.05 \text{ ms} \;\approx\; 9.95 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Quantify the power budget.**

Energy per operation for a battery-powered node of capacity $C$ in mAh and required lifetime $L$ in hours:

$$
P\_\text{avg} \;\leq\; \frac{V \cdot C}{L}
$$

Wristwatch (CR2032 coin cell: $V = 3 \text{ V}$, $C = 220 \text{ mAh}$, $L = 12 \text{ months} = 8760 \text{ h}$):

$$
P\_{\text{watch}} \;\leq\; \frac{3 \times 220 \times 10^{-3}}{8760} \;\approx\; 7.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W} \;=\; 75 \,\mu\text{W}
$$

Radar (powered by aircraft generator, no real constraint):

$$
P\_{\text{radar}} \;\leq\; 5000 \text{ W} \quad (\text{typical AESA radar})
$$

**Step 4 — Compute required throughput in MIPS.**

For an algorithm requiring $N$ clock cycles per output update, running at frequency $f\_{\text{clk}}$:

$$
\text{Throughput} \;=\; \frac{f\_{\text{clk}}}{N} \quad \text{(outputs/second)}
$$

- Wristwatch: $f\_{\text{clk}} = 32.768 \text{ kHz}$ (low-power crystal), $N \approx 100$ → Throughput $\approx 327$ updates/s. ✅
- Radar: $f\_{\text{clk}} \approx 1 \text{ GHz}$, $N \approx 10^{4}$ → Throughput $\approx 10^{5}$ updates/s. ✅

**Step 5 — Map to processor class.**

- Wristwatch → 8-bit / 32-bit ultra-low-power microcontroller (e.g., MSP430, STM32L0, Ambiq Apollo3 at sub-µA/MHz).
- Radar → Multi-core DSP + FPGA + GPU array (e.g., TI C6678 DSP + Xilinx RFSoC).

**Step 6 — Reliability class.**

Failure rates $\lambda$ are specified in **FITs (Failures In Time)**, where 1 FIT = 1 failure per $10^9$ device-hours.

$$
\text{MTBF} \;=\; \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

- Wristwatch: $\lambda \approx 10^3$ FIT → MTBF $\approx 10^6$ hours (≈ 114 years).
- Radar / Avionics: $\lambda \leq 1$ FIT → MTBF $\geq 10^9$ hours.

> [!NOTE]
> This single worked example teaches the student the **full analytical chain** — from function → timing → power → throughput → processor → reliability. KTU 14-mark questions in Module 3 often follow exactly this six-step template.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Generic Block Architecture of an Embedded System

This diagram captures the universal hardware architecture that reappears (in variations) across all application domains.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sensors\nTemperature, Pressure,\nLight, Motion] --> B[Signal Conditioning\nAmplifier, ADC, Filter]
    B --> C[Microcontroller SoC\nCPU + RAM + ROM + Peripherals]
    D[User Interface\nButtons, LCD, Touch] --> C
    E[Communication\nUART, SPI, I2C, BLE, CAN] --> C
    C --> F[Actuators\nMotors, Relays, Valves,\nLEDs, Buzzers]
    C --> G[Power Supply\nBattery, Regulator, PoE]

    style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0
    style B fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0
    style C fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A
    style E fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style F fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828
    style G fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F9A825
```

### Diagram 2: Application Domain Taxonomy

```mermaid

    style ROOT fill:#FFE082,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:3px

    style IOT fill:#FFCCBC
    style OFF fill:#DCEDC8
    style FIN fill:#C5CAE9
    style ROB fill:#B0BEC5
    style SEC fill:#FFAB91
```
