# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank, Module 3]**

### **Q1. Differentiate between a microprocessor and a microcontroller. Give one example of each. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Microprocessor (MPU)** | **Microcontroller (MCU)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | CPU-only chip | CPU + memory + I/O on one chip |
| **Memory** | External RAM and ROM | Internal Flash, SRAM, EEPROM |
| **Peripherals** | External I/O controllers | Built-in timers, ADC, UART, I²C, SPI |
| **Cost** | High (>$10 typically) | Low (<$5) |
| **Power** | High (multi-Watt) | Low (mW to µA sleep) |
| **Application** | PC, laptop, server | Washing machine, IoT, Arduino |
| **Example** | Intel Core i7, AMD Ryzen | ATmega328P, PIC16F877A, MSP430 |

A **microprocessor** is suited for general-purpose, computation-heavy applications requiring a complex OS, while a **microcontroller** is designed for dedicated, real-time, embedded control tasks.

---

### **Q2. List any three key features of the ATmega328P microcontroller used in Arduino UNO. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **8-bit AVR RISC architecture** with **131 instructions**, most executing in a **single clock cycle**, delivering up to **16 MIPS at 16 MHz**.
2. **In-system programmable Flash memory of 32 KB**, **2 KB SRAM**, and **1 KB EEPROM** for non-volatile data storage.
3. **Rich on-chip peripherals:** 3 timers (2×8-bit + 1×16-bit), 6-channel 10-bit ADC, 1 UART, 1 I²C, 1 SPI, Watchdog Timer, and 23 programmable I/O lines.

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Bank, Module 3]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper, Module 3]**

**Q (a).** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of the ATmega328P microcontroller. List the functions of any four on-chip peripherals. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The ATmega328P is an **8-bit CMOS microcontroller** based on the **AVR enhanced RISC architecture**. It integrates a CPU, memory, and I/O peripherals on a single chip.

**Block Diagram (textual representation):**

```
                 +----------------------------------+
                 |           ATmega328P             |
                 |                                  |
   External ---> |  16 MHz  -->  CPU  -->  ALU      | ---> I/O Ports
   Crystal       |    |          |          |        |       (B, C, D)
                 |    v          v          v        |
                 |  Flash    Register   Control      |
                 |  32 KB    File 32x8  Unit         |
                 |    |          |          |        |
                 |  SRAM     EEPROM      Peripheral  |
                 |  2 KB     1 KB        Bus         |
                 +----------------------------------+
                  |    |    |    |    |    |    |
                  v    v    v    v    v    v    v
                 T0   T1   T2  ADC UART I2C SPI WDT
```

**Functions of Four Peripherals:**

1. **10-bit ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter):** Converts analog voltages (0–5 V) on pins A0–A5 into 10-bit digital values (0–1023) using successive approximation. Used for reading sensors (temperature, light, potentiometer).

2. **16-bit Timer/Counter1:** Used for precise timing, event counting, and PWM generation. Can operate in Normal, CTC, Fast PWM, and Phase Correct PWM modes. Top value up to 65535.

3. **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter):** Provides asynchronous serial communication on digital pins D0 (RX) and D1 (TX). Configurable baud rates up to 1 Mbps (with 2x mode). Used for PC-Arduino communication.

4. **Watchdog Timer (WDT):** Independent hardware timer with its own **128 kHz internal oscillator**. Resets the MCU if the main program hangs or fails to clear the WDT within the timeout (configurable 16 ms to 8 s).

> **Key Architectural Features:**
> - **Harvard architecture** (separate program + data buses) → single-cycle instruction execution.
> - **32 × 8-bit general purpose registers** mapped into SRAM (0x0000–0x001F) for fast ALU access.
> - **6 sleep modes** for power saving: Idle, Power-down, Power-save, Standby, Extended Standby, ADC Noise Reduction.

---

**Q (b).** Compare Von Neumann and Harvard architectures. Explain why ATmega328P uses a modified Harvard architecture. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| **Feature** | **Von Neumann** | **Harvard** |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory** | Single memory for program + data | Separate program and data memories |
| **Bus** | Single shared bus | Two independent buses |
| **Simultaneous access** | **Not possible** (bottleneck) | **Possible** (parallel fetch + access) |
| **Speed** | Slower (fetch and access serial) | Faster (single-cycle) |
| **Hardware complexity** | Simpler | More complex (extra bus) |
| **Example** | Intel x86, 8051 (modified) | AVR ATmega328P, PIC, ARM Cortex-M |

**Why ATmega328P uses Modified Harvard:**

The ATmega328P employs a **modified Harvard architecture** because:

1. **Single-cycle execution:** Separate instruction and data buses allow the CPU to **fetch the next instruction** from Flash **simultaneously** with **reading/writing data** in SRAM. This achieves the **1 MIPS/MHz** throughput.

2. **Deterministic timing:** The pipeline does not stall due to bus conflicts — critical for real-time control loops (e.g., motor control at fixed 1 kHz).

3. **Bootloader support:** The architecture allows self-programming of Flash via a resident bootloader (stored in the boot section), using the SPM (Store Program Memory) instruction — this is a *modification* over the pure Harvard model, which strictly forbids program memory writes.

4. **Reduced CPI (Cycles Per Instruction):** Most AVR instructions complete in **1 clock cycle**, vs. 4–12 cycles for typical Von Neumann CISC designs (e.g., 8051: 12 cycles per machine cycle).

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, Module 3]**

**Q (a).** Explain the power supply, clock, and reset subsystems of the Arduino UNO board. Mention the role of the ATmega16U2. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Power Supply Subsystem:**

The Arduino UNO can be powered from **three sources**, selected automatically:

- **USB Type-B connector:** Provides **5 V** directly to the 5 V rail (after a PTC resettable fuse for overcurrent protection).
- **DC Barrel Jack:** Accepts **7–12 V DC** (recommended). A **NCP1117 or AMS1117-5.0 LDO regulator** drops this to **5 V** for the digital rail and to **3.3 V** (via LP2985) for the 3V3 pin.
- **VIN pin:** External supply can be applied directly to the VIN pin (bypasses the barrel jack but goes through the LDO).

**Power Tree:**

```
DC Jack (7-12V) ---> LDO Regulator ---> 5V Rail
USB (5V)          -----------------------> 5V Rail
VIN (7-12V)       ---> LDO Regulator ---> 5V Rail
5V Rail           ---> LP2985 LDO  ---> 3.3V Rail (50 mA max)
```

**2. Clock Subsystem:**

A **16 MHz ceramic resonator** is connected between pins 9 and 10 (XTAL1, XTAL2) of the ATmega328P. This provides the master clock for the CPU and peripherals. The **ATmega16U2** has its own **16 MHz crystal** for USB timing accuracy.

**3. Reset Subsystem:**

- **Reset pin (active LOW)** is held high via a **10 kΩ pull-up resistor** to 5 V.
- A **RESET push button** pulls the pin to ground when pressed.
- The **DTR (Data Terminal Ready)** pin of the ATmega16U2 is connected to the 328P's RESET via a **100 nF capacitor**. When the Arduino IDE opens the serial port, DTR pulses low, which — through the capacitor — momentarily pulls RESET low, triggering an **auto-reset** for uploading sketches.

**4. Role of ATmega16U2:**

The ATmega16U2 is a second microcontroller on the UNO board that acts as the **USB-to-Serial converter** (replacing the older FTDI FT232R chip). It:

- Implements the **USB CDC ACM** class, making the UNO appear as a **virtual COM port** on the host PC.
- Performs **auto-reset** sequencing via the DTR capacitor circuit.
- Has its own **ICSP header** (2×3 pins) on the board for firmware updates (e.g., to make UNO act as a keyboard, mouse, or MIDI device).

---

**Q (b).** An ATmega328P operates at 16 MHz with a 10-bit ADC. Calculate the ADC step resolution for a 5 V reference and the digital output for an input of 1.25 V. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $f_{clk} = 16 \text{ MHz}$
- $n = 10$ bits
- $V_{ref} = 5 \text{ V}$
- $V_{in} = 1.25 \text{ V}$

**Step 1 — Number of Quantization Levels:**

$$N_{levels} = 2^n - 1 = 2^{10} - 1 = 1023$$

**Step 2 — Step Resolution (LSB Voltage):**

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{N_{levels}} = \frac{5}{1023}$$

$$\Delta V = 0.0048876 \text{ V} \approx 4.888 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 3 — Digital Output Code:**

$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{\Delta V} \right\rceil = \left\lfloor \frac{1.25}{0.0048876} \right\rceil$$

$$D = \left\lfloor 255.75 \right\rceil = 256$$

**Step 4 — Verification:**

The reconstructed input voltage from digital code 256 is:

$$V_{reconstructed} = 256 \times 0.0048876 = 1.2512 \text{ V}$$

The quantization error magnitude is:

$$\vert \epsilon_q \vert = \vert 1.25 - 1.2512 \vert = 0.0012 \text{ V} = 1.2 \text{ mV}$$

Since $\vert \epsilon_q \vert < \Delta V = 4.888 \text{ mV}$, the conversion is **valid**.

**Result Summary:**
- **Step Resolution:** $\Delta V = 4.888 \text{ mV}$
- **Digital Output Code:** $D = 256$ (binary: `0b0100000000`)

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Module 3:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $2^n$ with $2^n - 1$:** A 10-bit ADC has **1024 codes (0 to 1023)**, but only **1023 intervals**. Using $2^n = 1024$ in the denominator gives an incorrect (slightly larger) step size. Always use $2^n - 1$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the bootloader's 0.5 KB Flash reservation:** The 32 KB Flash is **not fully available** to user code. Usable sketch space is **~31.5 KB** (32256 bytes).
>
> 3. **Wrong ATMega clock assumption:** UNO runs at **16 MHz** (not 20 MHz). 20 MHz is the **maximum rated** clock of the ATmega328P, not the UNO's operating frequency.
>
> 4. **UBRR rounding direction:** Always round the UBRR value to the **nearest integer**, then check if the error is **within ±2.5%**. If not, try the **U2X (double-speed) mode** for better accuracy at non-standard baud rates.
>
> 5. **Mixing up Harvard with Von Neumann:** The ATmega328P uses a **modified Harvard**, not pure Harvard (because the bootloader can rewrite Flash). Use the correct term in the exam.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 (Arduino UNO + ATmega328P)**

- ✅ **Microprocessor** = CPU only (needs external memory/I/O). **Microcontroller** = CPU + memory + I/O (single chip).
- ✅ **Arduino UNO** uses the **ATmega328P** (8-bit AVR RISC, **16 MHz**, **32 KB Flash**, **2 KB SRAM**, **1 KB EEPROM**).
- ✅ **ATmega16U2** is the **USB-to-Serial converter** on the UNO R3 (replaces FTDI FT232R).
- ✅ **14 digital I/O pins** (6 PWM), **6 analog input pins** (10-bit ADC).
- ✅ **ADC Resolution:** $\Delta V = V_{ref} / (2^n - 1)$ → for 5 V, 10-bit ADC, $\Delta V = 4.888 \text{ mV}$.
- ✅ **ADC Output:** $D = \text{round}(V_{in} / \Delta V)$ → range 0 to 1023.
- ✅ **PWM Duty Cycle:** $D_{\%} = (T_{on} / T_{period}) \times 100$ → $V_{avg} = V_{cc} \times D_{\%}$.
- ✅ **UART Baud Rate:** $UBRR = (f_{clk} / (16 \times f_{baud})) - 1$ → for 9600 baud @ 16 MHz, $UBRR = 103$.
- ✅ **Timer Overflow:** $f_{ovf} = f_{clk} / (N \times (TOP + 1))$ → prescaler $N \in \{1, 8, 64, 256, 1024\}$.
- ✅ **ATmega328P uses Modified Harvard architecture** (separate buses, but Flash is self-programmable).
- ✅ **6 sleep modes** for power saving; Power-Down mode current is **~1 µA**.
- ✅ **6 PWM channels** (8-bit) from Timer0 (D5, D6), Timer1 (D9, D10), Timer2 (D3, D11).
- ✅ **Flash endurance:** 10,000 write cycles. **EEPROM endurance:** 100,000 write cycles.
- ✅ **Watchdog Timer** has its own 128 kHz internal oscillator (independent of system clock).
- ✅ **Arduino UNO R3** is the most widely produced version; **Arduino UNO R4** (released 2023) uses a **Renesas RA4M1 32-bit ARM Cortex-M4** (major architecture change).

> **Final Tip for KTU 2024 Exam:** Always draw **neat block diagrams** in Part B answers (7-mark sub-questions). Examiners award **1–2 marks specifically for diagrams**. Use the diagrams from Section 4 of these notes as templates.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pin Diagram and Functions of Pins - Overview of Main Features such as I/O Ports
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEET504 - MICROPROCESSORS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Pin Diagram and Functions of Pins - Overview of Main Features such as I/O Ports # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **8051 Microcontroller** is an **8-bit** single-chip microcontroller manufactured originally by Intel, packaged in a **40-pin Dual In-line Package (DIP)**. The pin diagram defines the physical interface of the IC to the external world. The **40 pins** are categorized into: - **Four I/O Ports** (P0, P1, P2, P3) → **32 I/O lines** - **Two Power Pins** (VCC = **+5V**, VSS = **GND**) - **Two Clock Pins** (XTAL1, XTAL2) - **One Reset Pin** (RST, Pin 9) - **Three Control Pins** (ALE, PSEN, EA) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The 8051 architecture is the *intellectual backbone* of this module. Pin-level understanding is mandatory because every peripheral (Timer, UART, Interrupts) is mapped to a physical pin. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: "The City with Four Gates" Imagine the **8051 IC** as a **fortified walled city** with **four main gates** (P0, P1, P2, P3). Each gate has **8 turnstiles** (8 bits per port). - **Port 0 (Gate 0)** → The "**Goods-Only Gate**" — it has no internal gate-keeper (no pull-up), so external policemen (pull-up resistors) must be hired. It is also a "**shared gate**" because it helps the city address other towns during bus travel (multiplexed address/data bus). - **Port 1 (Gate 1)** → The "**Plain Gate**" — no special alternate function, just a normal 8-bit entry/exit for citizens. - **Port 2 (Gate 2)** → The "**High-Address Gate**" — used for the upper 8 bits of memory addresses when traveling to far cities. - **Port 3 (Gate 3)** → The "**Multi-Purpose Gate**" — each turnstile has a *secret secondary job* (UART, Interrupts, Timers, RD/WR). ## 1.3 Overview of Main Features | Feature | Specification | Physical Pin Range | |---|---|---| | **CPU** | 8-bit | Internal | | **Operating Frequency** | **12 MHz** (typical) | XTAL1, XTAL2 | | **I/O Ports** | 4 × 8-bit = **32 lines** | Pins 1–8, 10–17, 21–28, 32–39 | | **ROM (On-chip)** | 4 KB | Internal | | **RAM (On-chip)** | 128 Bytes | Internal | | **Timers/Counters** | 2 × 16-bit | P3.4 (T0), P3.5 (T1) | | **Serial Port (UART)** | 1 × Full-duplex | P3.0 (RxD), P3.1 (TxD) | | **Interrupts** | 5 Sources | P3.2 (INT0), P3.3 (INT1) | > [!NOTE] > A single pin can act as either a **General Purpose I/O (GPIO)** OR as a **dedicated alternate function pin**, but **NOT both at the same time**. Selection is done via the **SFR (Special Function Register)**. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Pin Architecture The 8051 has **40 pins**, numbered **1 to 40**, with **Pin 1** being on the left side of the notch (counter-clockwise convention). The IC has a **U-shaped notch** at one end and a **dot** at Pin 1 for orientation. ### 2.1.1 Port 0 (P0) — Pins 32 to 39 - **Type:** 8-bit, **Bidirectional**, **Open-Drain** (no internal pull-up) - **Alternate Function:** **Multiplexed Lower-order Address/Data bus (AD0–AD7)** - **SFR Address:** `0x80` (Bit-addressable) - **Key Reason for External Pull-up:** Each line of P0 has an **open-drain FET** to ground only. To output a logic HIGH, the drain must be pulled up externally through a **10 kΩ resistor** to VCC. > [!IMPORTANT] > During external memory access, P0 automatically functions as the **Address/Data bus**. Hence, it is "shared" — it cannot be used as a normal I/O port while accessing external memory. ### 2.1.2 Port 1 (P1) — Pins 1 to 8 - **Type:** 8-bit, **Bidirectional**, **Internal Pull-up** (quasi-bidirectional) - **Alternate Function:** **None (General Purpose Only)** - **SFR Address:** `0x90` (Bit-addressable) - **Internal Structure:** Each pin has an internal pull-up FET, latch, and output driver. Writing **1** turns the output FET OFF (high-Z pulled up). Writing **0** drives the pin LOW. ### 2.1.3 Port 2 (P2) — Pins 21 to 28 - **Type:** 8-bit, **Bidirectional**, **Internal Pull-up** - **Alternate Function:** **Upper-order Address bus (A8–A15)** during external memory access - **SFR Address:** `0xA0` (Bit-addressable) - **Usage:** When the program counter goes beyond the on-chip ROM (4 KB), P2 emits the **high byte of the 16-bit address**. ### 2.1.4 Port 3 (P3) — Pins 10 to 17 - **Type:** 8-bit, **Bidirectional**, **Internal Pull-up** - **Alternate Function:** **Peripheral I/O** (every pin has a dedicated second job) - **SFR Address:** `0xB0` (Bit-addressable) | Port 3 Pin | Alternate Function | Description | |---|---|---| | P3.0 (Pin 10) | **RxD** | Serial Data Receive | | P3.1 (Pin 11) | **TxD** | Serial Data Transmit | | P3.2 (Pin 12) | **INT0** | External Interrupt 0 | | P3.3 (Pin 13) | **INT1** | External Interrupt 1 | | P3.4 (Pin 14) | **T0** | Timer 0 External Input | | P3.5 (Pin 15) | **T1** | Timer 1 External Input | | P3.6 (Pin 16) | **WR** | External Memory Write Strobe | | P3.7 (Pin 17) | **RD** | External Memory Read Strobe | ### 2.1.5 Control & Miscellaneous Pins | Pin | Name | Type | Function | |---|---|---|---| | 9 | **RST** | Input | Active-HIGH reset. Holds HIGH for **≥ 2 machine cycles** to reset. | | 18 | **XTAL2** | Output | Crystal oscillator output | | 19 | **XTAL1** | Input | Crystal oscillator input | | 20 | **VSS** | Power | **Ground (0V)** | | 29 | **PSEN** | Output | Program Store Enable (active LOW). Enables external code memory. | | 30 | **ALE** | Output | Address Latch Enable. Latches low-byte address from P0. | | 31 | **EA/VPP** | Input | External Access. **LOW = external code memory**; **HIGH = internal code memory**. Also used for **flash programming voltage (VPP = 12V)**. | | 40 | **VCC** | Power | **+5V Supply** | ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---| | Machine Cycle Duration | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ | seconds | Determines instruction timing | | For 11.0592 MHz crystal | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \approx 1.085 \, \mu s$ | μs | Generates exact 9600 baud rate | | For 12 MHz crystal | $T_{MC} = 1 \, \mu s$ | μs | Standard KTU classroom default | | Reset Pulse Width (min) | $T_{RST} \geq 2 \times T_{MC}$ | seconds | Pin 9 high-time requirement | | ALE Pulse Frequency | $f_{ALE} = \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{6}$ | Hz | Used to latch address bytes | | Oscillator Frequency Range | $1 \, \text{MHz} \leq f_{OSC} \leq 24 \, \text{MHz}$ | Hz | Hardware design constraint | | Total I/O Lines | $4 \text{ ports} \times 8 \text{ bits} = 32$ | lines | GPIO count | | Address Bus Width (External) | $16 \text{ bits} \rightarrow 2^{16} = 65,536 = 64 \, \text{KB}$ | bytes | Max external memory | ## 2.3 Why This Matters in Engineering The 8051 pin configuration forms the **Hardware-Software Bridge** of all real-time embedded systems — from **washing machines** and **microwave ovens** to **automotive ECUs** and **industrial PLCs**. Understanding the pinout enables: - **PCB Layout Design** — proper placement of decoupling capacitors, crystal loading capacitors (typically **22 pF** to **33 pF**), and reset networks. - **Memory Interfacing** — correct use of **74LS373 latch** with ALE for demultiplexing. - **Peripheral Interfacing** — connecting LCDs, keypads, ADC0804, motors through ports. > [!NOTE] > The quasi-bidirectional I/O port of 8051 is unique — it can drive a load when LOW (sinks current) but only weakly sources current when HIGH. This is why a **current-limiting resistor** of **330 Ω to 10 kΩ** is mandatory when driving LEDs. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Machine Cycle Frequency and ALE Pulse Timing **Given:** Crystal oscillator frequency $f_{OSC} = 12 \, \text{MHz}$. **Step 1 — Define Oscillation Period:** $$T_{OSC} = \dfrac{1}{f_{OSC}} = \dfrac{1}{12 \times 10^6} \, \text{seconds}$$ **Step 2 — Define Machine Cycle:** The 8051 divides the oscillator frequency by **12** to generate one machine cycle. $$T_{MC} = 12 \times T_{OSC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the Value:** $$T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{seconds} = 1 \, \mu s$$ **Step 4 — Compute ALE Frequency:** ALE signal toggles once per machine cycle (S1 to S2 transition, then latches in S3). $$f_{ALE} = \dfrac{1}{2 \times T_{MC}} = \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{12 \times 2} = \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{6}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute $f_{OSC} = 12 \, \text{MHz}$:** $$f_{ALE} = \dfrac{12 \times 10^6}{6} = 2 \, \text{MHz}$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** ALE is a 2 MHz signal at 12 MHz crystal. The 74LS373 latch uses this falling edge to freeze the lower address byte from P0. ## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum External Memory Addressable **Step 1 — Total Address Lines:** P0 provides **8 lines (A0–A7)**, P2 provides **8 lines (A8–A15)**. $$A_{total} = 8 + 8 = 16 \, \text{lines}$$ **Step 2 — Maximum Memory Space:** $$M_{max} = 2^{16} = 65,536 \, \text{bytes} = 64 \, \text{KB}$$ **Step 3 — Code Memory Space:** $$\text{External Code Memory} = 64 \, \text{KB (if EA is LOW)}$$ **Step 4 — Data Memory Space:** $$\text{External Data Memory} = 64 \, \text{KB (accessed via MOVX instruction)}$$ **Step 5 — Total Addressable Memory:** $$M_{total} = 64 \, \text{KB (Code)} + 64 \, \text{KB (Data)} = 128 \, \text{KB}$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Port Comparison Table | Parameter | Port 0 | Port 1 | Port 2 | Port 3 | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Pin Numbers** | 32–39 | 1–8 | 21–28 | 10–17 | | **SFR Address** | `0x80` | `0x90` | `0xA0` | `0xB0` | | **Bit-Addressable?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | **Direction** | Bidirectional | Bidirectional | Bidirectional | Bidirectional | | **Internal Pull-up** | ❌ No (Open-drain) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | **External Pull-up Needed?** | ✅ Yes (10 kΩ) | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | | **Alternate Function** | AD0–AD7 | None | A8–A15 | RxD, TxD, INT0/1, T0/1, RD, WR | | **Sink Current (per pin)** | ~10 mA | ~10 mA | ~10 mA | ~10 mA | | **Use in 8031 (No ROM)** | Address/Data bus | General I/O | High Address | General I/O | | **Use in 8051 (Internal ROM)** | General I/O* | General I/O | General I/O* | General I/O* | *Only when external memory access is disabled / not required. ## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Reset Circuit Analysis **Step 1 — Reset Pin (Pin 9) Requirements:** The RST pin must be held HIGH for at least **2 machine cycles** to guarantee a valid reset. $$T_{RST(min)} = 2 \times T_{MC} = 2 \times 1 \, \mu s = 2 \, \mu s \quad \text{(at 12 MHz)}$$ **Step 2 — Standard Reset Circuit:** A typical reset network has: - Capacitor $C = 10 \, \mu F$ (from VCC to RST) - Resistor $R = 8.2 \, k\Omega$ (from RST to GND) - Schmitt trigger inverter (74LS14) for clean pulse **Step 3 — Time Constant (RC):** $$\tau = R \times C = 8.2 \times 10^3 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} = 0.082 \, \text{seconds} = 82 \, \text{ms}$$ **Step 4 — Voltage at RST pin during power-up:** The capacitor charges exponentially: $$V_{RST}(t) = V_{CC} \times \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$ At $t = \tau$: $$V_{RST}(\tau) = 5 \times (1 - e^{-1}) = 5 \times 0.632 = 3.16 \, \text{V}$$ This is well above the Schmitt trigger HIGH threshold (~2.0 V for 74LS14), ensuring a **clean 82 ms reset pulse** — vastly more than the **2 μs minimum**, providing a safe margin. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 8051 Pin Diagram (40-Pin DIP — Top View) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph LEFT["Pins 1-20 (Left Side)"] direction TB L1["Pin 1: P1.0"] L2["Pin 2: P1.1"] L3["Pin 3: P1.2"] L4["Pin 4: P1.3"] L5["Pin 5: P1.4"] L6["Pin 6: P1.5"] L7["Pin 7: P1.6"] L8["Pin 8: P1.7"] L9["Pin 9: RST"] L10["Pin 10: P3.0 / RxD"] L11["Pin 11: P3.1 / TxD"] L12["Pin 12: P3.2 / INT0"] L13["Pin 13: P3.3 / INT1"] L14["Pin 14: P3.4 / T0"] L15["Pin 15: P3.5 / T1"] L16["Pin 16: P3.6 / WR"] L17["Pin 17: P3.7 / RD"] L18["Pin 18: XTAL2"] L19["Pin 19: XTAL1"] L20["Pin 20: VSS (GND)"] end subgraph RIGHT["Pins 21-40 (Right Side)"] direction TB R1["Pin 21: P2.0 / A8"] R2["Pin 22: P2.1 / A9"] R3["Pin 23: P2.2 / A10"] R4["Pin 24: P2.3 / A11"] R5["Pin 25: P2.4 / A12"] R6["Pin 26: P2.5 / A13"] R7["Pin 27: P2.6 / A14"] R8["Pin 28: P2.7 / A15"] R9["Pin 29: PSEN"] R10["Pin 30: ALE"] R11["Pin 31: EA / VPP"] R12["Pin 32: P0.7 / AD7"] R13["Pin 33: P0.6 / AD6"] R14["Pin 34: P0.5 / AD5"] R15["Pin 35: P0.4 / AD4"] R16["Pin 36: P0.3 / AD3"] R17["Pin 37: P0.2 / AD2"] R18["Pin 38: P0.1 / AD1"] R19["Pin 39: P0.0 / AD0"] R20["Pin 40: VCC (5V)"] end LEFT <--> RIGHT ``` ## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture of an 8051 Port Pin ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CPU["CPU Bus"] DATABUS["Internal Data Bus"] end subgraph PORTPIN["Single Port Pin (e.g., P1.0)"] direction TB LATCH["D-Latch
SFR Bit"] READBUF["Read Buffer"] DRIVER["Output Driver FET"] PULLUP["Internal Pull-up FET"] PINPAD["Physical Pin"] end DATABUS --> LATCH LATCH --> DRIVER DRIVER --> PINPAD PULLUP --> PINPAD PINPAD --> READBUF READBUF --> DATABUS ``` ## 4.3 Port 3 Alternate Function Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TB P3["Port 3 SFR (0xB0)
8-bit Latch"] P3 --> P30["P3.0
RxD"] P3 --> P31["P3.1
TxD"] P3 --> P32["P3.2
INT0"] P3 --> P33["P3.3
INT1"] P3 --> P34["P3.4
T0"] P3 --> P35["P3.5
T1"] P3 --> P36["P3.6
WR"] P3 --> P37["P3.7
RD"] P30 --> UART["UART
Serial Port"] P31 --> UART P32 --> IE0["Ext. Interrupt 0"] P33 --> IE1["Ext. Interrupt 1"] P34 --> TMR0["Timer 0 Input"] P35 --> TMR1["Timer 1 Input"] P36 --> XMEMW["External Memory Write"] P37 --> XMEMR["External Memory Read"] ``` > [!NOTE] > The alternate function is **selected automatically by the hardware** when the corresponding SFR is configured. For instance, configuring **SCON** for UART automatically routes P3.0 and P3.1 to the Serial Port engine. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **List the four I/O ports of 8051 with their SFR addresses.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The 8051 microcontroller has four 8-bit I/O ports: 1. **Port 0 (P0)** — SFR Address: `0x80` — Pins 32–39 2. **Port 1 (P1)** — SFR Address: `0x90` — Pins 1–8 3. **Port 2 (P2)** — SFR Address: `0xA0` — Pins 21–28 4. **Port 3 (P3)** — SFR Address: `0xB0` — Pins 10–17 All four ports are **bit-addressable**, providing 32 individual I/O lines. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Why does Port 0 require external pull-up resistors while Port 1 does not?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Port 0 has an **open-drain** output structure — it contains only an **N-channel FET to ground** and no internal pull-up transistor. To generate a logic HIGH, the drain must be pulled up externally using a resistor (typically **10 kΩ**) connected to VCC. In contrast, **Port 1, Port 2, and Port 3** are **quasi-bidirectional** ports with **internal pull-up FETs** built into each pin. Hence, they can drive a HIGH output without external resistors. (Any valid circuit-level explanation with reference to open-drain vs quasi-bidirectional architecture is acceptable.) --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1: [KTU University Exam — July 2024] **a) Draw the pin diagram of the 8051 microcontroller. Explain the functions of the following pins: (7 marks)** - (i) RST (Pin 9) - (ii) EA / VPP (Pin 31) - (iii) PSEN (Pin 29) - (iv) ALE (Pin 30) **b) Explain the structure and functions of Port 3. List all its alternate functions. (7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Pin Diagram & Key Pin Functions (7 marks):** **Pin Diagram (1 mark):** The 40-pin DIP layout as shown in Section 4.1 (top view with U-notch at top). **Pin Functions (6 marks — 1.5 each):** **(i) RST (Pin 9):** - **Function:** Active-HIGH reset input. - **Operation:** When a HIGH pulse (≥ 2 machine cycles) is applied, all SFRs are reset to default values, the Program Counter (PC) is loaded with `0x0000`, and execution begins from the reset vector. - **Pin Type:** Input with Schmitt trigger. - **Voltage Threshold:** HIGH ≥ **3.0V**, LOW ≤ **1.5V** (for standard 8051 at 5V VCC). **(ii) EA / VPP (Pin 31):** - **Dual-function pin.** - **EA (External Access):** Logic **HIGH** → executes from **internal ROM (4 KB)**. Logic **LOW** → fetches code only from **external ROM** (used in 8031 system). - **VPP:** During Flash programming, this pin receives the **+12V programming voltage**. **(iii) PSEN (Pin 29):** - **Program Store Enable** — active-LOW output. - Activated only when fetching code from **external ROM**. - Connects to the **OE (Output Enable)** pin of external EPROM/ROM chip. - Remains HIGH during internal ROM execution or data memory access. **(iv) ALE (Pin 30):** - **Address Latch Enable** — output. - Generates a pulse to **latch the lower 8-bit address (from P0) into an external latch (74LS373)** during external memory access. - Frequency = $f_{OSC} / 6$ (e.g., **2 MHz at 12 MHz crystal**). - Also emits pulses even without external memory (used for system diagnostics). --- **Part (b) — Port 3 Structure and Alternate Functions (7 marks):** **Structure (3 marks):** - 8-bit bidirectional quasi-bidirectional port with **internal pull-up FETs**. - SFR Address: `0xB0` (bit-addressable). - Pins 10 to 17 of the 40-pin DIP. - Each pin has a **D-latch** in SFR, a **read buffer**, an **output driver**, and an **internal pull-up transistor**. - Unlike Port 0, no external pull-up resistors required. **Alternate Functions Table (4 marks):** | Port Pin | Pin # | Alternate Function | Description | |---|---|---|---| | P3.0 | 10 | **RxD** | Serial data input (UART receive) | | P3.1 | 11 | **TxD** | Serial data output (UART transmit) | | P3.2 | 12 | **INT0** | External hardware interrupt 0 (active LOW) | | P3.3 | 13 | **INT1** | External hardware interrupt 1 (active LOW) | | P3.4 | 14 | **T0** | Timer 0 external clock/count input | | P3.5 | 15 | **T1** | Timer 1 external clock/count input | | P3.6 | 16 | **WR** | External data memory write strobe (active LOW) | | P3.7 | 17 | **RD** | External data memory read strobe (active LOW) | **Engineering Note:** The alternate function is enabled only when the corresponding **SFR** (e.g., SCON for UART, TCON for INT0/1, TMOD for T0/T1) is configured. Until then, the pin behaves as a **general-purpose I/O**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **a) Explain the dual role of Port 0 in the 8051 microcontroller. Why is it called a multiplexed address/data bus? (7 marks)** **b) Differentiate between the four I/O ports of 8051 with respect to internal pull-up, alternate function, and SFR address. (7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Dual Role of Port 0 (7 marks):** Port 0 serves **two distinct roles** depending on the operational mode: **Role 1: General Purpose I/O (GPIO) — When NOT accessing external memory (3 marks):** - Acts as a simple 8-bit bidirectional I/O port. - Each pin can be independently set HIGH or LOW. - **Limitation:** It is **open-drain** — needs an **external 10 kΩ pull-up resistor** on each pin to output a HIGH. - Writing `1` to a P0 bit makes the output FET OFF (high-impedance); writing `0` turns the FET ON (pin pulled to GND). **Role 2: Multiplexed Address/Data Bus — During external memory access (4 marks):** - When the 8051 executes `MOVX` (external data memory) or fetches code beyond 4 KB, P0 is **time-multiplexed**: - **First half of the machine cycle (S1–S2):** Emits the **lower-order address A0–A7** on P0. - **Second half (S3–S4):** Same lines carry the **data D0–D7** (read or write). - The **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** signal tells the external **74LS373 latch** to capture the address byte before it is overwritten by data. **Why "Multiplexed"?** Because the **same 8 physical lines** carry both **address (A0–A7)** and **data (D0–D7)** information at **different time instants**, the bus is said to be **time-multiplexed**. This design saves pins (no need for 16 separate address lines for an 8-bit bus) but requires an external latch. --- **Part (b) — Comparison of the Four I/O Ports (7 marks):** | Feature | **Port 0** | **Port 1** | **Port 2** | **Port 3** | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Pin Numbers** | 32–39 | 1–8 | 21–28 | 10–17 | | **SFR Address** | `0x80` | `0x90` | `0xA0` | `0xB0` | | **Internal Pull-up** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | **External Pull-up** | ✅ Required (10 kΩ) | ❌ Not needed | ❌ Not needed | ❌ Not needed | | **Alternate Function** | AD0–AD7 | None | A8–A15 | RxD, TxD, INT0/1, T0/1, RD, WR | | **Used as Bus?** | Address + Data | No | Upper Address | Control signals | | **Bit-Addressable** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | **Direction** | Bidirectional | Bidirectional | Bidirectional | Bidirectional | **Conclusion:** Among the four ports, **P1 is the most "well-behaved"** for general I/O because it has internal pull-up and no alternate function. **P0 and P2 are "system bus" ports** that are released for I/O only when external memory is not used. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting to mention the SFR address** — Examiners specifically expect `0x80, 0x90, 0xA0, 0xB0` for P0, P1, P2, P3 respectively. Skipping these costs **1 mark per port**. > > 2. **Confusing open-drain (Port 0) with quasi-bidirectional (P1/P2/P3)** — A frequent error. Port 0 **requires** external pull-up; P1, P2, P3 do not. > > 3. **Writing "Port 3 has RxD and TxD only"** — The alternate functions are **8 separate jobs** (RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD). Forgetting the last two (`WR`, `RD`) results in **−0.5 mark**. > > 4. **Incorrect pin numbering** — Pin 1 is **P1.0**, not P0.0. P0 starts at **Pin 32**. Mixing this up is a classic mistake. > > 5. **Saying "EA pin selects RAM or ROM"** — Wrong. EA selects **internal vs external code memory** only, never data memory. > > 6. **Forgetting PSEN vs RD/ WR distinction** — PSEN enables **code memory** (ROM), while RD/WR strobes enable **data memory** (RAM). This is a 14-mark question differentiator. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ⭐ The 8051 has **40 pins** in a **DIP package**, with **32 I/O lines** spread across **4 ports**. - ⭐ **Port 0** → Pins 32–39, SFR `0x80`, **open-drain**, multiplexed **AD0–AD7**, needs **10 kΩ pull-up**. - ⭐ **Port 1** → Pins 1–8, SFR `0x90`, **general purpose only**, no alternate function, internal pull-up. - ⭐ **Port 2** → Pins 21–28, SFR `0xA0`, internal pull-up, carries **A8–A15** during external memory access. - ⭐ **Port 3** → Pins 10–17, SFR `0xB0`, internal pull-up, **8 alternate functions** (RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD). - ⭐ **Pin 9 (RST)** → Active-HIGH reset, must be HIGH for **≥ 2 machine cycles**. - ⭐ **Pin 18, 19 (XTAL2, XTAL1)** → Crystal oscillator connections; typical crystal is **11.0592 MHz** (for UART) or **12 MHz** (standard). - ⭐ **Pin 20 (VSS)** → **Ground (0V)**. **Pin 40 (VCC)** → **+5V**. - ⭐ **Pin 29 (PSEN)** → Program Store Enable, active-LOW, used to enable **external code memory**. - ⭐ **Pin 30 (ALE)** → Address Latch Enable, frequency = $f_{OSC}/6$. - ⭐ **Pin 31 (EA/VPP)** → External Access: HIGH = internal ROM, LOW = external ROM; VPP = **+12V** for flash programming. - ⭐ **One Machine Cycle = 12 oscillator periods** → $T_{MC} = 12 / f_{OSC}$. - ⭐ **Maximum external memory** = **64 KB code + 64 KB data = 128 KB** total addressable. - ⭐ All four ports are **bit-addressable** (SFR addresses end in `0` or `0`). - ⭐ **Open-drain vs Quasi-bidirectional** is the single most important port architecture distinction in this module. - ⭐ The **8051 port pin can sink more current than it can source** — this is the design reason LEDs are connected with cathode to the port pin and anode to VCC through a resistor. --- > **End of Module 3 Notes — Pin Diagram and Functions of Pins — Overview of Main Features such as I/O Ports** > *(Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education Framework — CO3 Mapped)*

topics.title: Timers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET504 - MICROPROCESSORS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Timers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⏱️ Module 3: Timers in 8051 Microcontroller

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Academic Definition

A **Timer** is a specialized hardware peripheral inside the **8051 Microcontroller** that operates as a binary up-counter, incrementing on every machine cycle (or on an external trigger when configured as a counter). The 8051 contains **two 16-bit Timer/Counters** — **Timer 0** and **Timer 1** — which can be used independently for precise time-delay generation, event counting, or baud-rate generation for the on-chip serial port.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PBEET504 - Module 3):**
> The 8051 has **two 16-bit timers/counters** accessed via four 8-bit registers: **TH0, TL0, TH1, TL1**. Each timer is controlled by the **TMOD** (Timer Mode) register and the **TCON** (Timer Control) register.

---

### 🍳 Conceptual Analogy: The Stopwatch Inside the Chip

Imagine you are baking a cake and need to time exactly **500 milliseconds** for the dough to rise. Instead of staring at a wall clock, you use a **digital stopwatch** that ticks every millisecond.

Now think of the 8051's Timer as a **miniature stopwatch embedded inside the chip**:

| Component | Stopwatch (Real World) | 8051 Timer (Embedded World) |
|---|---|---|
| Start button | The "GO" key | Setting the **TR0 / TR1** bit in TCON |
| Counting ticks | Seconds ticking | Machine cycles (1 / 12 × oscillator frequency) |
| Maximum count | 60 seconds (or 9999) | **65536** counts (for 16-bit mode) |
| Overflow indication | Beep at end | **TF0 / TF1** flag set in TCON |
| Reset to a specific time | Setting initial value | Loading **THx / TLx** registers |

The key insight: a hardware timer **runs in parallel with the CPU** without consuming software cycles. It simply counts clock pulses, and when it overflows (reaches its maximum), it raises a flag that the CPU can detect (polling) or be interrupted by.

---

### 🔢 Timer vs. Counter — The Critical Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> A **Timer** increments on the **internal oscillator** (machine cycles), while a **Counter** increments on an **external event** applied to pin **T0 (P3.4)** or **T1 (P3.5)**. The only difference between the two is the **clock source**, selected by the **C/T̄** bit in TMOD.

### 📐 Standard 8051 Timing Metrics

- **Machine Cycle** = **12 oscillator periods** = **12 / f<sub>osc</sub>**
- **One Timer Increment** = **1.085 µs** (when f<sub>osc</sub> = **11.0592 MHz**)
- **Maximum Count (16-bit)** = **2<sup>16</sup> = 65536**
- **Maximum Delay (Mode 1)** = **65536 × 1.085 µs ≈ 71.1 ms**
- **Total Registers Used** = **4** (TH0, TL0, TH1, TL1)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🧩 2.1 The TMOD Register (Timer Mode Register) — Address 89H

TMOD is an **8-bit non-bit-addressable** register used to configure the **mode** and **operation** of both timers. The upper nibble (**4 bits**) controls **Timer 1**, and the lower nibble controls **Timer 0**.

### Bit Layout of TMOD

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | **GATE1** | **C/T̄1** | **M11** | **M01** | **GATE0** | **C/T̄0** | **M10** | **M00** |
| Timer | \multicolumn{4}{\vert c \vert}{Timer 1} | \multicolumn{4}{\vert c \vert}{Timer 0} |

### Function of Each Bit

- **GATE (Gate Control):** When set to **1**, the timer is enabled only when the **INTx pin (P3.2 / P3.3) is HIGH** AND the **TRx bit is set**. When **0**, the timer is enabled solely by the **TRx bit** (used for software control).
- **C/T̄ (Counter / Timer Select):** **1** = Counter mode (external pulses on T0/T1 pin). **0** = Timer mode (internal oscillator).
- **M1 and M0 (Mode Bits):** Select one of the **four operating modes** described below.

---

## 🧩 2.2 The TCON Register (Timer Control Register) — Address 88H

TCON is **8-bit and bit-addressable**, allowing individual bit manipulation. It contains both **timer control flags** and **external interrupt flags**.

| Bit | TF1 | TR1 | TF0 | TR0 | IE1 | IT1 | IE0 | IT0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Addr | 8FH | 8EH | 8DH | 8CH | 8BH | 8AH | 89H | 88H |

- **TRx (Timer Run Control Bit):** **1** = Start the timer. **0** = Stop the timer.
- **TFx (Timer Overflow Flag):** **Set to 1** automatically when the timer overflows (rolls over from FFFFH to 0000H). Must be **cleared by software** in polling mode.

---

## ⚙️ 2.3 Timer Operating Modes

### Mode 0 — 13-Bit Timer (Legacy 8048 Mode)

The timer uses **8 bits of TLx** and **5 bits of THx** (with the upper 3 bits of THx unused). Total count = **8192** (2<sup>13</sup>). Rarely used in modern designs.

### Mode 1 — 16-Bit Timer (Most Common for Delay Generation)

- Uses all **8 bits of TLx** AND all **8 bits of THx**.
- Counts from the loaded value up to **FFFFH**.
- Upon overflow (FFFFH → 0000H), the **TFx flag is set**.
- This is the **default mode chosen by most KTU exam problems** because it gives the largest variable range.

### Mode 2 — 8-Bit Auto-Reload Timer

- **TLx** acts as the 8-bit counter.
- **THx** holds the **reload value**.
- When TLx overflows from **FFH to 00H**, the value in THx is **automatically copied into TLx** (no software reload needed).
- Commonly used for **baud-rate generation** in serial communication.

### Mode 3 — Split Timer Mode (Timer 0 Only)

- Splits **Timer 0** into **two independent 8-bit timers**: **TL0** and **TH0**.
- Timer 1 is **stopped** (its SFRs can still be used as simple registers).
- Used when the application needs **three simultaneous timers**.

---

## 📊 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Formulas — They Appear in Almost Every KTU Numerical!**

| \# | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Machine Cycle Period | T<sub>mc</sub> = 12 / f<sub>osc</sub> | seconds; f<sub>osc</sub> in Hz |
| 2 | Timer Tick Duration | T<sub>tick</sub> = 12 / f<sub>osc</sub> | identical to T<sub>mc</sub> |
| 3 | Counts Needed for Delay | N = Desired\_Delay / T<sub>tick</sub> | dimensionless integer |
| 4 | Initial Value (16-bit, Mode 1) | Initial\_Value = 65536 − N | decimal; split into THx and TLx |
| 5 | Initial Value (13-bit, Mode 0) | Initial\_Value = 8192 − N | decimal; load only lower 5 bits of THx |
| 6 | Initial Value (8-bit, Mode 2) | Initial\_Value = 256 − N | direct load into THx (auto-reload) |
| 7 | Maximum Delay (Mode 1, 11.0592 MHz) | D<sub>max</sub> = 65536 × 12 / 11.0592×10<sup>6</sup> ≈ 71.106 ms | ms |
| 8 | Overflow Threshold (Mode 1) | FFFFH = 65535<sub>10</sub> | next clock pulse overflows |

### 🏗️ Real-World Engineering Utility

Timers are the **backbone of every embedded system**:
- **Automotive ECUs:** Triggering fuel injection pulses every few milliseconds.
- **Industrial PLCs:** Generating PWM signals to control motor speed.
- **IoT Devices:** Periodic sensor sampling every 100 ms to conserve power.
- **Medical Devices:** Heart-rate monitoring and defibrillator pulse timing.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Generating accurate baud rates for UART communication in modems and GPS modules.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🧮 3.1 The Master Derivation: Delay Calculation for Mode 1 (16-Bit Timer)

This derivation is **the single most important calculation** in KTU exams on this topic. We derive the value to be loaded into **THx:TLx** to produce a specific delay.

---

### 📐 Given Inputs

Let:
- f<sub>osc</sub> = **Crystal oscillator frequency** (commonly **12 MHz**, **11.0592 MHz**, or **6 MHz**).
- Desired delay = D seconds.
- We are using **Timer 0 in Mode 1** (16-bit).

### 🔢 Step 1: Compute the Machine Cycle Period

The 8051 divides the oscillator frequency by **12** to produce one machine cycle. Therefore:

$$
T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}
$$

This is the time taken for **one timer increment**.

### 🔢 Step 2: Compute the Number of Timer Counts Required

To obtain a delay of D seconds, the timer must count exactly:

$$
N = \frac{D}{T_{mc}} = \frac{D \times f_{osc}}{12}
$$

If N exceeds 65536 (Mode 1 maximum), you must **use a software loop** to repeat the timer overflow.

### 🔢 Step 3: Compute the Initial Value to Load

The timer counts **up** from the loaded value. It overflows when it reaches **65536** total counts (i.e., when the register transitions from **FFFFH** to **0000H**).

So the loaded value must be:

$$
\text{Initial\_Value} = 65536 - N
$$

### 🔢 Step 4: Split the Initial Value into THx and TLx

The high byte is loaded into **THx**, the low byte into **TLx**:

$$
\text{THx} = \frac{\text{Initial\_Value}}{256} \quad \text{(integer division)}
$$

$$
\text{TLx} = \text{Initial\_Value} \mod 256
$$

---

### 📝 Worked Example (Highly Probable KTU Question Type)

**Problem:** Find the value to be loaded into TH0 and TL0 to generate a delay of **5 ms** using Timer 0 in **Mode 1**, with f<sub>osc</sub> = **11.0592 MHz**.

**Step 1 — Machine Cycle:**

$$
T_{mc} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Number of Counts:**

$$
N = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 4608 \text{ counts}
$$

**Step 3 — Initial Value:**

$$
\text{Initial\_Value} = 65536 - 4608 = 60928
$$

Converting 60928<sub>10</sub> to hex:

$$
60928 \div 16 = 3808 \quad \text{remainder} \, 0
$$
$$
3808 \div 16 = 238 \quad \text{remainder} \, 0
$$
$$
238 \div 16 = 14 \quad \text{remainder} \, 14 = E
$$
$$
14 \div 16 = 0 \quad \text{remainder} \, 14 = E
$$

Reading bottom-up: **EE00H**

**Step 4 — Register Loading:**

$$
\text{TH0} = \text{EEH}, \quad \text{TL0} = \text{00H}
$$

**Verification:**
Delay = (65536 − 60928) × 1.085 µs = 4608 × 1.085 µs = **5.00 ms** ✓

---

## 🧩 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of All Four Modes

| Feature | Mode 0 | Mode 1 | Mode 2 | Mode 3 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Resolution | 13-bit | **16-bit** | 8-bit | 8-bit × 2 |
| Counter Range | 0 to 8191 | 0 to 65535 | 0 to 255 | 0 to 255 (each) |
| Register Usage | TLx (8) + THx (5) | TLx (8) + THx (8) | TLx (8) only | TL0 and TH0 separately |
| Auto-Reload? | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ **Yes** | ❌ No |
| Software Reload Needed? | Yes | Yes | **No** | Yes |
| Typical Application | Legacy compatibility | **General delays** | **Baud rate generation** | Three-timer systems |
| KTU Exam Frequency | Low | **Very High** | Medium | Low |
| Maximum Delay @ 11.0592 MHz | ≈ 8.88 ms | ≈ 71.11 ms | ≈ 0.277 ms | ≈ 0.277 ms each |

---

## 🛠️ 3.3 Standard Timer Programming Procedure (Mode 1, Polling)

This is the **canonical 4-step initialization** every 8051 programmer follows:

**Step 1 — Choose Timer and Mode (load TMOD):**

$$
\text{TMOD} = 0x01 \quad \text{(Timer 0, Mode 1, software control, timer mode)}
$$

**Step 2 — Load Initial Value into TH0 and TL0:**

$$
\text{TH0} = \text{HighByte}; \quad \text{TL0} = \text{LowByte};
$$

**Step 3 — Start the Timer (set TR0 bit):**

$$
\text{TR0} = 1;
$$

**Step 4 — Wait for Overflow (poll TF0):**

$$
\text{while (TF0 == 0);} \quad \text{/* wait here */}
$$

**Step 5 — Stop Timer and Clear Flag:**

$$
\text{TR0} = 0; \quad \text{TF0} = 0;
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does TF0 need to be cleared by software?**
> Unlike some microcontrollers where the overflow flag clears automatically, the 8051's TFx flag **remains set until software clears it**. If you forget this step, the next time you test TF0 it will appear "already overflowed," causing incorrect timing behavior.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 🔧 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of 8051 Timer/Counter Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OSC["OSCILLATOR f osc DIVIDED BY 12 MACHINE CYCLE CLOCK"]
    EXT["EXTERNAL PIN T0 P3 4 or T1 P3 5"]
    SEL["MUX SELECTED BY C / T BAR BIT IN TMOD"]
    GATE["GATE CONTROL BIT IN TMOD"]
    INTX["EXTERNAL INTERRUPT PIN INT0 P3 2 or INT1 P3 3"]
    TR["TR0 or TR1 BIT IN TCON"]
    ENBL["AND GATE COMBINES GATE INTx AND TRx"]
    CT16["16 BIT UP COUNTER THx and TLx REGISTER PAIR"]
    OVFL["OVERFLOW DETECTOR MONITORS FFFFH TO 0000H TRANSITION"]
    TF["TF0 or TF1 OVERFLOW FLAG IN TCON"]
    CPU["CPU READS THx TLx OR DETECTS TFx"]

    OSC --> SEL
    EXT --> SEL
    SEL --> ENBL
    GATE --> ENBL
    INTX --> ENBL
    TR --> ENBL
    ENBL --> CT16
    CT16 --> OVFL
    OVFL --> TF
    OVFL --> CPU
    CT16 --> CPU
```

---

## 🔄 4.2 Timer Programming and Overflow Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START(["START PROGRAM"])
    LOADTMOD["LOAD TMOD REGISTER SELECT TIMER MODE 0 1 2 3"]
    LOADVAL["LOAD INITIAL VALUE INTO THx AND TLx"]
    SETTR["SET TRx BIT TO 1 START TIMER"]
    POLLTF["POLL TFx IS IT 1"]
    WAIT["KEEP WAITING TIMER STILL COUNTING"]
    STOPTR["CLEAR TRx BIT STOP TIMER"]
    CLRTF["CLEAR TFx FLAG MANUALLY"]
    REEXEC["RESTART FROM LOADVAL FOR REPEATED DELAY"]
    END(["END OF DELAY ROUTINE"])

    START --> LOADTMOD --> LOADVAL --> SETTR --> POLLTF
    POLLTF -->|NO| WAIT --> POLLTF
    POLLTF -->|YES| STOPTR --> CLRTF --> REEXEC
    REEXEC --> LOADVAL
    REEXEC --> END
```

---

## 🗂️ 4.3 TMOD Register Bit-Assignment Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TMOD_BYTE["TMOD REGISTER ADDRESS 89H NOT BIT ADDRESSABLE"]
        direction LR
        B7["BIT 7 GATE1"]
        B6["BIT 6 C T BAR 1"]
        B5["BIT 5 M11"]
        B4["BIT 4 M01"]
        B3["BIT 3 GATE0"]
        B2["BIT 2 C T BAR 0"]
        B1["BIT 1 M10"]
        B0["BIT 0 M00"]
    end

    B7 --> G1["TIMER 1 CONTROL"]
    B6 --> G1
    B5 --> G1
    B4 --> G1
    B3 --> G2["TIMER 0 CONTROL"]
    B2 --> G2
    B1 --> G2
    B0 --> G2

    G1 --> M1["MODE SELECT 00 01 10 11"]
    G2 --> M1
```

---

## 🧠 4.4 Conceptual Diagram: Timer as a Count-Up "Bucket"

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EMPTY["EMPTY BUCKET THx TLx LOADED WITH INITIAL VALUE e.g. FC18H"]
    FILL1["AFTER 1 TICK FFFFH - 1"]
    FILL2["AFTER N TICKS FFFFH - N"]
    FULL["BUCKET FULL FFFFH"]
    SPILL["OVERFLOW SPILL TFx FLAG SET TO 1"]
    EMPTYAGAIN["BUCKET EMPTIES TO 0000H AUTOMATICALLY"]

    EMPTY --> FILL1 --> FILL2 --> FULL --> SPILL --> EMPTYAGAIN
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Think of the 16-bit register pair as a bucket that holds water. Water drops (timer ticks) fill it one by one. When the bucket is full (FFFFH), the very next drop causes it to overflow — that overflow is the **TFx flag**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** 
*Explain the function of the TMOD register in the 8051 microcontroller. List the four timer modes with their corresponding M1 and M0 values.*

**Model Answer:**

The **TMOD (Timer Mode) register** is an 8-bit register located at SFR address **89H**. It is **not bit-addressable**, so the entire byte must be loaded as a hex value. It configures the operating mode of **Timer 0** (lower nibble) and **Timer 1** (upper nibble).

| Bit | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| GATE | Gate Control | When 1, timer is hardware-controlled by INTx pin |
| C/T̄ | Counter/Timer Select | 1 = Counter (external), 0 = Timer (internal) |
| M1 | Mode Bit 1 | Selects mode (high bit) |
| M0 | Mode Bit 0 | Selects mode (low bit) |

**Four Timer Modes:**

| M1 | M0 | Mode | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | Mode 0 | 13-bit timer (legacy) |
| 0 | 1 | Mode 1 | **16-bit timer** (most common) |
| 1 | 0 | Mode 2 | **8-bit auto-reload** |
| 1 | 1 | Mode 3 | Split timer (Timer 0 only) |

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Differentiate between Timer mode and Counter mode in the 8051. Mention the relevant pins and control bits.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Timer Mode | Counter Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Clock Source | **Internal oscillator** (machine cycles) | **External pulse** on T0 / T1 pin |
| Trigger Signal | Each machine cycle (every 12 oscillator cycles) | Each HIGH-to-LOW transition on T0 (P3.4) or T1 (P3.5) |
| Control Bit | **C/T̄ = 0** in TMOD | **C/T̄ = 1** in TMOD |
| Typical Use | Delay generation, baud-rate generation | Counting external events (objects on conveyor, pulses) |
| Frequency | Fixed by crystal oscillator | Limited by microcontroller's sampling rate (max ≈ f<sub>osc</sub>/24) |

The hardware counter mechanism is **identical** in both modes — only the clock source differs, selected by the **C/T̄** bit in the TMOD register.

---

## 📚 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### ✏️ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q3(a). With a neat diagram, explain the TMOD and TCON registers of the 8051 microcontroller. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**TMOD Register (Address 89H):** Refer to the diagram in Section 4.3 above. TMOD is not bit-addressable and must be loaded byte-wise.

**Bit-by-bit explanation:**

- **GATE (Gate Bit):** When GATE = 1, hardware control is enabled. Timer runs **only** when the corresponding **INTx pin is HIGH** AND the **TRx bit is set**. This is used for **pulse-width measurement**. When GATE = 0, only the TRx bit controls the timer (software-only control).
- **C/T̄ (Counter/Timer):** Selects the clock source. 0 = internal timer, 1 = external counter.
- **M1, M0 (Mode Bits):** Select one of four modes as listed in the Mode Table.

**Example:** `MOV TMOD, #01H` → Timer 0 in Mode 1, software control, timer mode.

**TCON Register (Address 88H):** Bit-addressable. Lower 4 bits are timer-related; upper 4 bits are interrupt-related.

| Bit | Symbol | Address | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | TF1 | 8FH | Timer 1 overflow flag |
| 6 | TR1 | 8EH | Timer 1 run control bit |
| 5 | TF0 | 8DH | Timer 0 overflow flag |
| 4 | TR0 | 8CH | Timer 0 run control bit |
| 3 | IE1 | 8BH | External interrupt 1 edge flag |
| 2 | IT1 | 8AH | External interrupt 1 type (edge/level) |
| 1 | IE0 | 89H | External interrupt 0 edge flag |
| 0 | IT0 | 88H | External interrupt 0 type (edge/level) |

---

**Q3(b). Calculate the values to be loaded into TH0 and TL0 to generate a delay of **10 ms** using Timer 0 in Mode 1. Assume f<sub>osc</sub> = **12 MHz**. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Machine Cycle Period:**

$$
T_{mc} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Required Number of Counts:**

$$
N = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 10000
$$

**Step 3 — Initial Value:**

$$
\text{Initial\_Value} = 65536 - 10000 = 55536
$$

**Step 4 — Convert 55536 to Hexadecimal:**

$$
55536 \div 16 = 3471 \text{ rem } 0
$$
$$
3471 \div 16 = 216 \text{ rem } 15 = F
$$
$$
216 \div 16 = 13 \text{ rem } 8
$$
$$
13 \div 16 = 0 \text{ rem } 13 = D
$$

Reading bottom-up: **D8F0H**

**Step 5 — Register Values:**

$$
\text{TH0} = \text{D8H}, \quad \text{TL0} = \text{F0H}
$$

**Verification:**

$$
\text{Delay} = (65536 - 55536) \times 1 \,\mu s = 10000 \times 1 \,\mu s = 10 \,ms \quad \checkmark
$$

---

### ✏️ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q4(a). Explain the four operating modes of the 8051 timer in detail. Mention at least one application of each mode. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Mode 0 — 13-Bit Mode:**
- Uses TLx (8 bits) and lower 5 bits of THx (3 upper bits unused).
- Total counts = **8192**. Overflow from 1FFFH to 0000H sets TFx.
- **Application:** Compatibility with the older Intel 8048 microcontroller. Rarely used in new designs.

**Mode 1 — 16-Bit Mode:**
- Uses all 8 bits of TLx and THx.
- Total counts = **65536**. Maximum delay ≈ 71.1 ms at 11.0592 MHz.
- **Application:** Most widely used for **precise hardware delay generation** in applications such as keypad debouncing, LCD timing, and stepper motor step pulses.

**Mode 2 — 8-Bit Auto-Reload Mode:**
- TLx is the 8-bit counter; THx holds the reload value.
- When TLx overflows from FFH to 00H, **THx value is automatically copied into TLx** in the next cycle — no software intervention required.
- **Application:** **Generating accurate baud rates** for serial communication (e.g., Timer 1 in Mode 2 generates standard baud rates like 9600 in the 8051).

**Mode 3 — Split Timer Mode:**
- Available **only for Timer 0**. Splits Timer 0 into two independent 8-bit timers: **TL0** (controlled by TR0) and **TH0** (controlled by TR1, since TR0 is reused).
- Timer 1 cannot be used for its own purpose in this mode but its registers remain accessible.
- **Application:** Scenarios needing **three concurrent timing channels** (e.g., simultaneous baud-rate generation + two independent delays).

---

**Q4(b). Write an 8051 assembly language program to toggle all bits of Port P1 continuously every **500 µs** using Timer 0 in Mode 2. Assume f<sub>osc</sub> = **12 MHz**. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Machine Cycle:**

$$
T_{mc} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Counts Needed:**

$$
N = \frac{500 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 500
$$

**Step 3 — Initial Value (Mode 2 is 8-bit, max 256):**

$$
N = 500 > 256
$$

Since 500 exceeds the maximum count of 256, **a single timer overflow is insufficient**. We must use a **software loop counter**. To minimize delay per iteration:

$$
\text{Loop Iterations} = \lceil 500 / 256 \rceil = 2
$$

$$
\text{Effective counts per iteration} = 256 \quad \text{(full overflow)}
$$

$$
\text{Delay per iteration} = 256 \times 1 \,\mu s = 256 \,\mu s
$$

$$
\text{Loop Iterations} = \lceil 500 / 256 \rceil = 2 \text{ iterations}
$$

**Step 4 — Initial Value for Mode 2:**

$$
\text{Initial Value} = 256 - 256 = 0 \Rightarrow \text{TH0} = 00\text{H}
$$

**Step 5 — Assembly Program:**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        MOV TMOD, #02H      ; Timer 0, Mode 2, software control
        MOV TH0, #00H       ; Reload value = 00H (256 counts)
        SETB TR0            ; Start Timer 0

LOOP:   MOV R2, #02         ; Software loop counter (2 iterations)
WAIT:   JNB TF0, WAIT       ; Poll until overflow
        CLR TR0             ; Stop timer
        CLR TF0             ; Clear overflow flag
        SETB TR0            ; Restart timer
        DJNZ R2, WAIT       ; Decrement loop, repeat if not zero
        CPL P1              ; Toggle all bits of Port 1
        SJMP LOOP           ; Repeat forever
        END
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to clear TF0 after detection.** Since 8051 does not auto-clear the flag, forgetting this step causes the next iteration to immediately exit the wait loop, producing the wrong delay. **Lose 2 marks.**
> 2. **Calculating Initial Value for Mode 2 as `256 - N` instead of using THx as reload.** Many students write to TLx first then THx — incorrect; in Mode 2, **only THx is loaded** with the reload value.
> 3. **Setting TMOD = 01H (Mode 1) when asked for Mode 2.** Always re-read the question carefully — Mode number is explicit.
> 4. **Forgetting the software loop** when the required delay exceeds 71.1 ms (Mode 1) or 256 µs (Mode 2).
> 5. **Incorrect hex-decimal conversion.** Always show your division steps to claim full marks.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Your Exam!**

- ⏱️ **8051 has TWO 16-bit timers:** Timer 0 and Timer 1, accessed via TH0/TL0 and TH1/TL1.
- 📋 **TMOD (89H):** Not bit-addressable; sets MODE and CONTROL of both timers (upper nibble = T1, lower nibble = T0).
- 🚦 **TCON (88H):** Bit-addressable; contains **TRx** (run control) and **TFx** (overflow flag).
- 🔀 **C/T̄ bit in TMOD:** 0 = Timer (internal), 1 = Counter (external T0/T1 pins).
- 🔓 **GATE bit in TMOD:** 1 = hardware gating via INTx pin, 0 = software-only via TRx.
- 🎯 **Mode 1 (16-bit)** is the most common for delay generation; **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)** is used for baud-rate generation.
- 🔄 **Mode 3** is available only for Timer 0 and creates two separate 8-bit timers.
- 📐 **Master formula:** `Initial_Value = Max_Count − (Delay_Time / Timer_Tick_Duration)`
- 💎 **Tick duration** = 12 / f<sub>osc</sub>. At **11.0592 MHz** it is exactly **1.085 µs**; at **12 MHz** it is exactly **1 µs**.
- 🧮 **Max Delay (Mode 1):** 65536 × 12 / f<sub>osc</sub>. At 11.0592 MHz → **≈ 71.1 ms**.
- ✏️ **Splitting hex values:** THx = high byte, TLx = low byte. Example: FC18H → TH0 = FCH, TL0 = 18H.
- 🚫 **TFx is NOT auto-cleared** in the 8051 — software must clear it; otherwise the next overflow check will erroneously succeed.
- 🔁 **For delays longer than max count**, use a software loop (a register like R2 holding the iteration count) around the timer wait.
- 🎛️ **TMOD example values:** `01H` = T0 Mode 1; `02H` = T0 Mode 2; `10H` = T1 Mode 1; `20H` = T1 Mode 2; `12H` = T0 Mode 1 + T1 Mode 2 (typical for serial communication setup).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interrupts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET504 - MICROPROCESSORS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Interrupts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Interrupt** is a hardware- or software-initiated asynchronous event that temporarily suspends the normal sequential execution of the microprocessor, transferring control to a dedicated routine called the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** or **Interrupt Handler**. Once the ISR completes its execution, the processor resumes the original task from the point where it was interrupted, restoring all critical register states.

In the context of the **Intel 8086 microprocessor**, the interrupt system is a sophisticated mechanism comprising **256 interrupt types**, each identified by a unique **8-bit interrupt type number (0–255)**. Every interrupt points to a **4-byte entry in the Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)**, which is stored at the absolute memory address starting at `00000H` and occupies the first **1024 bytes (00000H – 003FFH)** of the system memory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 — Interrupts):**
> As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PBEET504, the *Interrupts* topic mandates the study of the 8086 interrupt structure, the Interrupt Vector Table, types of interrupts (hardware & software), the Interrupt Service Routine, the Interrupt Enable / Disable flag mechanism, and the priority resolution scheme used by the 8086 to arbitrate simultaneous interrupt requests.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Doctor's Doorbell"

Imagine a busy doctor (the **CPU**) is deeply engaged attending to a patient (executing the **main program**). Suddenly, the doorbell rings (an **interrupt signal** arrives). The doctor does not ignore it, nor does he abandon his current patient. Instead, he:

1. **Pauses** the current work (saves the **context** — patient's chart on the desk).
2. **Opens the door** to check who is calling (executes the **ISR**).
3. If it is urgent (a **high-priority interrupt**), he attends immediately.
4. If the doorbell is disconnected (the **Interrupt Flag** is cleared), he ignores it (interrupt disabled / **masked**).
5. After handling, he **returns** to the original patient exactly where he left off (**returns from interrupt**).

This doorbell mechanism is precisely how the **8086 interrupt architecture** works.

## 1.3 Why Interrupts Matter — Engineering Perspective

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Reason Interrupts Exist:**
> Without interrupts, the CPU would have to continuously *poll* (check) every peripheral device to know if it needs service. This wastes enormous CPU cycles. Interrupts enable **event-driven programming**, allowing the CPU to perform useful background work and respond to peripherals **only when required**, achieving superior real-time responsiveness — the very foundation of **embedded systems design**.

## 1.4 Interrupt Classification (8086 Perspective)

| Class | Sub-Types | Examples in 8086 |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardware Interrupts** | Maskable / Non-Maskable | `INTR`, `NMI` |
| **Software Interrupts** | Predefined / User-defined | `INT 0`–`INT 255`, `INT 3`, `INTO` |
| **Internal Interrupts** | Generated by CPU itself | Divide error (Type 0), Single-step (Type 1), Overflow (Type 4) |
| **External Interrupts** | Generated by external hardware | `INTR` (maskable), `NMI` (non-maskable) |

## 1.5 Key Terminology at a Glance

- **ISR (Interrupt Service Routine):** The dedicated subroutine that services a particular interrupt.
- **IVT (Interrupt Vector Table):** A 256-entry × 4-byte table storing the CS:IP of every ISR, located at `00000H`.
- **Type Number:** An 8-bit code (0–255) that uniquely identifies an interrupt source.
- **Maskable Interrupt:** Can be disabled by clearing the **Interrupt Flag (IF)** in the **FLAGS register**.
- **Non-Maskable Interrupt (NMI):** Cannot be disabled — the CPU *must* service it (Type 2).
- **Vectored Interrupt:** The interrupting device supplies the interrupt type number directly (e.g., 8259A PIC).
- **Non-Vectored Interrupt:** The CPU must poll devices to identify the source.

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8086 Interrupt Vector Table (IVT) — The Heart of Interrupt Handling

The 8086 microprocessor reserves the **first 1 KB of physical memory** (physical addresses `00000H` to `003FFH`) for the **Interrupt Vector Table**. Each entry occupies **4 bytes**:

- **Lower 2 bytes → IP (Instruction Pointer)** of the ISR
- **Higher 2 bytes → CS (Code Segment)** of the ISR

The **physical address of the vector for interrupt type N** is computed using the canonical 8086 memory addressing formula:

$$\text{Vector Address} = 4 \times N$$

where **N** is the interrupt type number. This 20-bit physical address is then split into CS and IP, where:

- `IP` is loaded from the lower 2 bytes at address `4N`.
- `CS` is loaded from the higher 2 bytes at address `4N + 2`.

## 2.2 Detailed Interrupt Types in the 8086

### 2.2.1 Hardware Interrupts

| Pin | Type | Maskable? | Trigger | Vector Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `NMI` | Type 2 | **No (Non-Maskable)** | Edge-triggered (rising) | Fixed at IVT entry `00008H` |
| `INTR` | Variable | **Yes (Maskable via IF)** | Level-sensitive | External controller (e.g., 8259A) provides type |

> [!NOTE]
> **NMI (Type 2):** Reserved for catastrophic events such as **power failure** or **memory parity errors**. The 8086 saves the FLAGS, CS, and IP on the stack, clears **IF** and **TF**, and jumps to the ISR located at `00008H`–`0000BH` in the IVT.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **INTR vs NMI Memory Trick:**
> - **NMI** → *Non-Maskable Interrupt* → Type 2 → Fixed vector at `00008H`.
> - **INTR** → *Interrupt Request* → Maskable → Type supplied by 8259A PIC.

### 2.2.2 Software Interrupts (Predefined)

| Interrupt | Type | Trigger Condition |
|---|---|---|
| `INT 0` | 0 | Division by zero (or quotient overflow in `DIV`/`IDIV`) |
| `INT 1` | 1 | Single-step (TF = 1) — used by debuggers |
| `INT 2` | 2 | NMI pin activation |
| `INT 3` | 3 | One-byte breakpoint instruction |
| `INT 4` | 4 | `INTO` instruction — executed only if **OF = 1** (overflow flag) |

### 2.2.3 User-defined Software Interrupts (`INT n`)

The `INT n` instruction can generate any interrupt from `INT 0` to `INT 255`. Even when a "user" interrupt is invoked, the CPU reads the corresponding IVT entry to obtain the ISR address.

## 2.3 The Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Interrupt Request Arrival:** An external device asserts the `INTR` pin (for hardware) OR software executes `INT n`.
2. **IF Check (Maskable only):** If `IF = 1`, the CPU acknowledges. If `IF = 0`, the request is ignored (masked).
3. **INTA Cycle:** The 8086 performs an **Interrupt Acknowledge (INTA)** bus cycle. An external controller (e.g., **8259A PIC**) places the interrupt type number on the data bus.
4. **Context Save (Hardware-Pushed):** The CPU automatically pushes, in order: **FLAGS → CS → IP** onto the stack. The current **IP** is saved at the next lower memory location.
5. **IF and TF Cleared:** The CPU clears the **Interrupt Flag (IF)** and **Trap Flag (TF)** to prevent nested-interrupt chaos during ISR execution.
6. **Vector Fetch:** The CPU reads the IVT entry at `4 × N`, loads `IP` and `CS`, and transfers execution to the ISR.
7. **ISR Execution:** The user's ISR runs. Standard practice is to push additional registers (`AX`, `BX`, `CX`, `DX`, `SI`, `DI`, `BP`) used within the ISR.
8. **EOI (End of Interrupt):** For hardware interrupts via 8259A, a specific command word must be issued to the PIC.
9. **IRET Execution:** The `IRET` instruction pops **IP → CS → FLAGS** from the stack, restoring the previous execution state and re-enabling interrupts.

## 2.4 Stack Operations During an Interrupt

When an interrupt is accepted, the 8086 performs the following **pushing sequence** (highest address first on the stack):

$$\text{Stack} \leftarrow \text{FLAGS}, \quad \text{Stack} \leftarrow \text{CS}, \quad \text{Stack} \leftarrow \text{IP}$$

Each push decrements the **Stack Pointer (SP)** by 2. After this, **SP = SP – 6**.

## 2.5 Interrupt Priority — Daisy-Chained 8086 Architecture

When multiple interrupts occur simultaneously, the 8086 follows this fixed priority (highest → lowest):

| Priority Rank | Interrupt Source |
|---|---|
| 1 (Highest) | **Internal Interrupts** (NMI, Divide Error, INT n, INT 3, INTO) — and software `INT` instructions |
| 2 | **NMI (Type 2)** — non-maskable, edge-triggered |
| 3 | **INTR** — maskable hardware interrupt |
| 4 (Lowest) | **Single-Step (Type 1)** — only active if TF = 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** All *software* interrupts (including `INT n` and the predefined ones) take precedence over *external* hardware interrupts, because internally generated events are checked **first** during the instruction fetch-decode-execute cycle.

## 2.6 The Interrupt Flag (IF) — Masking Mechanism

The **IF (Interrupt Flag)** is bit 9 of the 8086 **FLAGS register**:

- **`IF = 1` (Set):** The `INTR` pin is **enabled**. The CPU will respond to maskable interrupts.
- **`IF = 0` (Reset):** The `INTR` pin is **disabled** (masked). The CPU ignores maskable hardware requests.

The IF can be controlled via two privileged instructions:

- `STI` (Set Interrupt Flag) → Sets IF = 1
- `CLI` (Clear Interrupt Flag) → Clears IF = 0

> [!NOTE]
> The IF does **not** affect `NMI` or software interrupts — these always execute.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| IVT Starting Address | `00000H` | Hex Bytes | Hardwired in 8086 |
| IVT Ending Address | `003FFH` | Hex Bytes | 1 KB total |
| Vector Size per Entry | 4 bytes | Bytes | 2 bytes IP + 2 bytes CS |
| Total Number of Interrupts | 256 | — | Type 0 to 255 |
| Vector Address for Type N | $4 \times N$ | Hex | Example: Type 80H → `200H` |
| Stack Pushes per Interrupt | 3 | Words | FLAGS, CS, IP (each 16-bit) |
| SP Change per Interrupt | $\text{SP} - 6$ | Bytes | 3 × 2 bytes |
| SP Change on IRET | $\text{SP} + 6$ | Bytes | Restores all three |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS):** Interrupts are the *backbone* of preemptive task scheduling.
- **Embedded Sensors:** Microcontrollers (ARM, AVR, PIC) use interrupts to wake from sleep on a sensor event.
- **Industrial Control:** Motor control systems rely on interrupts for **precise timing** via hardware timers.
- **Communication Protocols:** UART, SPI, I²C handlers are almost always ISR-driven to avoid missing bytes.
- **Fault Handling:** NMI is universally used for **power-fail detection** and **memory parity errors** in safety-critical systems.
