# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 — Computing the IVT Address for a Given Interrupt Type

**Question:** Find the **physical address** in the IVT for the interrupt type number `80H` (used as the DOS print-screen service in legacy systems). State the exact bytes and their meaning at that location.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the type number N.**

$$N = 80\text{H} = 128_{10}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the IVT address formula.**

$$\text{Vector Address} = 4 \times N$$

$$\text{Vector Address} = 4 \times 80\text{H} = 200\text{H}$$

**Step 3 — Express the result.**

$$\text{Vector Address} = 00200\text{H}$$

**Step 4 — State the contents at that address.**

| Address | Contents (2 bytes) | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `00200H` – `00201H` | Low byte of IP, High byte of IP | **IP** of ISR |
| `00202H` – `00203H` | Low byte of CS, High byte of CS | **CS** of ISR |

When the CPU receives `INT 80H`, it fetches:
- `IP ← MEM[00200H : 00201H]`
- `CS ← MEM[00202H : 00203H]`

**Final Answer:** The interrupt vector for `INT 80H` is stored at physical address **`00200H`**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — Stack Memory Analysis After Interrupt Acceptance

**Question:** Suppose `SP = 1000H` before an `INT 21H` is executed. Compute the **new value of SP** and the **addresses where FLAGS, CS, and IP are stored**, assuming the stack segment is `SS = 2000H`. (Assume the ISR is currently at `CS:IP = 3000H:0500H`.)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the automatic push order.**

The 8086 pushes, in this order: **FLAGS → CS → IP**. (Highest-address first in conceptual sense; SP decreases each time.)

**Step 2 — Calculate new SP after 3 pushes.**

$$\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 6$$

$$\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = 1000\text{H} - 6 = 0\text{FFAH}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the physical addresses where each register is stored.**

Using the formula:
$$\text{Physical Address} = \text{SS} \times 16 + \text{SP}_{\text{after push}}$$

**Push 1 — FLAGS** (pushed first; SP becomes `0FFEH`):

$$\text{Addr}_{\text{FLAGS}} = 2000\text{H} \times 10\text{H} + 0\text{FFEH} = 20\text{FFEH}$$

**Push 2 — CS** (SP becomes `0FFCH`):

$$\text{Addr}_{\text{CS}} = 2000\text{H} \times 10\text{H} + 0\text{FFCH} = 20\text{FFCH}$$

**Push 3 — IP** (SP becomes `0FFAH`):

$$\text{Addr}_{\text{IP}} = 2000\text{H} \times 10\text{H} + 0\text{FFAH} = 20\text{FFAH}$$

**Step 4 — Summary Table.**

| Push Order | Register | Physical Address (SS:SP after push) |
|---|---|---|
| 1st (top of stack conceptually) | FLAGS | `20FFEH : 20FFFH` |
| 2nd | CS | `20FFCH : 20FFDH` |
| 3rd (bottom — SP points here) | IP | `20FFAH : 20FFBH` |

**Step 5 — On IRET, the order is reversed: IP → CS → FLAGS are popped.**

$$\text{SP}_{\text{after IRET}} = 0\text{FFAH} + 6 = 1000\text{H} \quad \text{(restored!)}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3 — Interrupt Priority Resolution Scenario

**Question:** Consider the following simultaneous events in an 8086 system:

1. A `DIV` instruction causes a divide-by-zero error.
2. The `NMI` line is asserted.
3. The `INTR` line is asserted by an 8259A PIC with type `40H`.
4. The Trap Flag (`TF`) is set.

**Which interrupt is serviced first, and in what order are the remaining ones handled?**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the 8086 interrupt priority order.**

| Rank | Source | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Internal/Software Interrupts** | Includes `INT n`, `INT 0`, `INT 1`, `INT 3`, `INTO`, and any instruction fault |
| 2 | **NMI (Type 2)** | Edge-triggered, non-maskable |
| 3 | **INTR** | Maskable |
| 4 | **Single-Step (Type 1)** | Requires TF = 1 |

**Step 2 — Classify each event.**

- **Event 1 (Divide by zero):** Internal — Type 0 software interrupt. **Rank 1.**
- **Event 2 (NMI):** Rank 2.
- **Event 3 (INTR, type 40H):** Rank 3.
- **Event 4 (TF = 1):** Rank 4 (only if active and no higher-priority interrupt is taken).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Rule:** The 8086 checks for **pending interrupts and traps at the END of every instruction**. If multiple are pending, the *highest priority* is serviced first, and others are re-evaluated after `IRET` (or during the same flow if nested).

**Step 3 — Final priority resolution order.**

$$\text{Service Order: Divide-by-Zero (Type 0)} \rightarrow \text{NMI (Type 2)} \rightarrow \text{INTR (Type 40H)} \rightarrow \text{Single-Step (Type 1)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Subtle Point:** Single-step (Type 1) is the *lowest* priority because it is checked only after each instruction AND only when no other interrupt is pending. In practice, after the ISR for divide-by-zero completes (via IRET), the next instruction boundary is reached, and NMI is serviced, and so on.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Problem 4 — Designing a Custom ISR for `INT 60H`

**Question:** Write the 8086 assembly code to install an ISR at `INT 60H`. The ISR should perform these tasks:
- Save all general-purpose registers.
- Read the keyboard status port at address `60H` (assume standard PC hardware).
- Store the value in memory location `BUFFER` (in the data segment).
- Restore all registers and return using `IRET`.

**Assume:** ISR is at `1000:0200H`, and `BUFFER` is at offset `0300H` in the current data segment.

### Step-by-Step Assembly Solution

**Step 1 — Store the ISR address in the IVT at `4 × 60H = 180H`.**

**Step 2 — Install the ISR vector in IVT.**

```asm
; Assume DS already points to data segment where vector will be written
; We need to write to absolute address 0000:0180H.
; So we set ES = 0000H, then write to ES:0180H.

      CLI                       ; Disable maskable interrupts during vector setup
      MOV  AX, 0000H
      MOV  ES, AX               ; ES = 0 (IVT segment)
      MOV  DI, 0180H            ; Offset = 4 × 60H = 180H
      MOV  AX, 0200H            ; Offset of ISR within its code segment
      MOV  ES:[DI], AX          ; Store low word (IP)
      MOV  AX, 1000H            ; Code segment of ISR
      MOV  ES:[DI+2], AX        ; Store high word (CS)
      STI                       ; Re-enable interrupts
```

**Step 3 — The ISR body.**

```asm
; ----- ISR for INT 60H begins here at 1000:0200H -----
MY_ISR PROC FAR
      PUSH AX
      PUSH BX
      PUSH CX
      PUSH DX
      PUSH SI
      PUSH DI
      PUSH BP

      ; Read keyboard status port
      IN   AL, 60H              ; Read byte from I/O port 60H

      ; Store in BUFFER (DS:0300H)
      MOV  BX, DS               ; Copy DS
      MOV  [BX + 0300H], AL     ; Store AL into BUFFER

      ; Restore registers in reverse order
      POP  BP
      POP  DI
      POP  SI
      POP  DX
      POP  CX
      POP  BX
      POP  AX
      IRET                      ; Pops IP, CS, FLAGS — return from interrupt
MY_ISR ENDP
```

**Step 4 — Verification.**

- The IVT entry at `0000:0180H` now contains the ISR pointer.
- When `INT 60H` is executed, the CPU reads `IP = 0200H`, `CS = 1000H`, and jumps to the ISR.
- After execution, `IRET` restores the original state.

---

## 3.5 Summary Table — Interrupt Vector Table Entries for Common 8086 Interrupts

| Type | Interrupt | IVT Address (Hex) | CS:IP Content Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Divide Error | `00000H` | Internal CPU logic |
| 1 | Single Step | `00004H` | Internal (TF-driven) |
| 2 | NMI | `00008H` | Edge on NMI pin |
| 3 | Breakpoint | `0000CH` | INT 3 opcode |
| 4 | Overflow | `00010H` | INTO + OF = 1 |
| 5–31 | Reserved (Intel) | `00014H`–`0007FH` | Intel reserved |
| 32–255 | User / DOS / BIOS | `00080H`–`003FFH` | System / user-defined |

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 8086 Interrupt Handling Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram captures the full functional flow of an 8086 interrupt cycle — from request to return.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Instruction Cycle] --> B{Is there a pending interrupt?}
    B -- No --> Z[Continue normal execution]
    B -- Yes --> C[Identify interrupt type and source]
    C --> D{Is it a software INT instruction?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Read type N directly from opcode]
    D -- No --> F{Is it NMI Type 2?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Vector fixed at 00008H]
    F -- No --> H{Is it INTR with IF=1?}
    H -- No --> I[Ignore request, continue execution]
    H -- Yes --> J[Issue INTA bus cycle]
    J --> K[8259A PIC places type N on data bus]
    K --> L[Push FLAGS onto stack]
    L --> M[Push CS onto stack]
    M --> N[Push IP onto stack]
    N --> O[Clear IF and TF flags]
    O --> P[Compute vector address as 4 times N]
    P --> Q[Fetch IP from 4N and CS from 4N plus 2]
    Q --> R[Transfer control to ISR]
    R --> S[Execute Interrupt Service Routine]
    S --> T[Push any general purpose registers used]
    T --> U[Perform required device service]
    U --> V[Send EOI to 8259A if applicable]
    V --> W[Pop saved registers in reverse order]
    W --> X[Execute IRET instruction]
    X --> Y[Pop IP, CS, and FLAGS from stack]
    Y --> Z[Resume original program execution]
```

## 4.2 8086 Interrupt Vector Table Layout — Memory Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Address 00000H] --> B[Type 0: 4 bytes IP plus CS for DIVERR]
    B --> C[Type 1: 4 bytes IP plus CS for Single Step]
    C --> D[Type 2: 4 bytes IP plus CS for NMI]
    D --> E[Type 3: 4 bytes IP plus CS for Breakpoint]
    E --> F[Type 4: 4 bytes IP plus CS for INTO]
    F --> G[Types 5 to 31: Intel Reserved]
    G --> H[Types 32 to 255: User and DOS and BIOS]
    H --> I[Physical Address 003FFH end of IVT]
```

## 4.3 8086 Interrupt Priority Resolution — Daisy-Chain Order

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Highest Priority: Internal Software Interrupts - INT 0, INT 1, INT 3, INT n, INTO] --> P2
    P2[Second Priority: NMI - Type 2, edge triggered] --> P3
    P3[Third Priority: INTR - maskable via IF flag] --> P4
    P4[Lowest Priority: Single Step - Type 1, only if TF equals 1]
```

## 4.4 ISR Stack Frame — Visual Memory Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H[Higher Memory Address] --> F1[FLAGS at SS:SP plus 4]
    F1 --> F2[CS at SS:SP plus 2]
    F2 --> F3[IP at SS:SP - this is the new SP after interrupt]
    F3 --> L[Lower Memory Address]
    L --> S[Stack grows downward into lower addresses]
```

## 4.5 Interrupt Acknowledge Bus Cycle — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[8086 CPU issues INTA pulse 1] --> B[8259A PIC freezes current priority and arbitration]
    B --> C[8086 CPU issues INTA pulse 2]
    C --> D[8259A PIC places interrupt type N on lower data bus D0 to D7]
    D --> E[8086 CPU reads type N from data bus]
    E --> F[CPU multiplies N by 4 to get IVT address]
    F --> G[CPU pushes FLAGS, CS, IP onto stack]
    G --> H[CPU clears IF and TF flags]
    H --> I[CPU fetches CS and IP from IVT and jumps to ISR]
```

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

### Question 1: What is an interrupt? Differentiate between hardware and software interrupts in 8086.

**Model Answer:**

An **interrupt** is an external or internal event that temporarily halts the microprocessor's main execution, causing it to service a dedicated routine (ISR), and then resume the original program.

| Parameter | Hardware Interrupt | Software Interrupt |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | External hardware device | Program instruction (`INT n`) |
| **Trigger** | Voltage on `INTR` / `NMI` pin | Execution of `INT n` opcode |
| **Vector Source** | Supplied by external PIC (e.g., 8259A) | Embedded in the opcode |
| **Maskable?** | INTR yes; NMI no | No — always executed |
| **Examples** | Keyboard, Timer, I/O device ready | `INT 21H` (DOS), `INT 10H` (BIOS video) |

*(3 marks — Definition 1 mark, Tabular comparison 2 marks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

### Question 2: What is the Interrupt Vector Table (IVT) in 8086? State its size, location, and entry size.

**Model Answer:**

The **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** is a lookup table in the 8086's memory that stores the **CS:IP (starting address)** of every Interrupt Service Routine.

- **Location:** Starting at physical address `00000H` (the very first byte of memory).
- **Size:** **1 KB (1024 bytes)** — covers `00000H` to `003FFH`.
- **Entry Size:** Each entry is **4 bytes** (2 bytes IP + 2 bytes CS).
- **Number of Entries:** **256** (Type 0 to Type 255).
- **Formula:** Address of vector for type N = `4 × N`.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for each: location, size, entry size)*

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Format)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3 Pattern]**

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**Q. A (a)** Explain the **8086 interrupt structure** in detail with a neat diagram of the **Interrupt Vector Table**. List the **dedicated interrupts** of 8086 with their type numbers. (7 marks)

**Q. A (b)** With a suitable example, explain how the 8086 services an **INTR** interrupt via the **8259A Programmable Interrupt Controller**. (7 marks)

---

### ✅ Model Solution to Question A (a)

**Step 1 — The 8086 Interrupt Structure:**

The 8086 supports **256 interrupt types**, each identified by an **8-bit type number (0 to 255)**. The structure is centered around the **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)**, which is a 1 KB block at the start of physical memory.

**Step 2 — IVT Layout (with diagram):**

```
00000H  +---------------+  <-- Type 0:  Divide Error
        |     IP        |
00002H  +---------------+
        |     CS        |
00004H  +---------------+  <-- Type 1:  Single Step (Trap)
        |     IP        |
00006H  +---------------+
        |     CS        |
00008H  +---------------+  <-- Type 2:  NMI (Non-Maskable)
        |     IP        |
0000AH  +---------------+
        |     CS        |
0000CH  +---------------+  <-- Type 3:  Breakpoint (INT 3)
        |     IP        |
0000EH  +---------------+
        |     CS        |
00010H  +---------------+  <-- Type 4:  Overflow (INTO)
        |     IP        |
00012H  +---------------+
        |     CS        |
        |   . . . . .   |
        | (Reserved &   |
        |  User-defined)|
        |   . . . . .   |
003FFH  +---------------+  <-- End of IVT
```

**Step 3 — Dedicated Interrupts of 8086:**

| Type | Name | Cause |
|---|---|---|
| **0** | Divide Error | Quotient overflow in `DIV` / `IDIV` |
| **1** | Single Step | Trap Flag (TF) = 1 — executes ISR after each instruction |
| **2** | NMI | Edge on the NMI pin (e.g., memory parity, power fail) |
| **3** | Breakpoint | One-byte `INT 3` opcode used by debuggers |
| **4** | Overflow | `INTO` instruction executed when OF = 1 |

*(7 marks: 2 marks IVT explanation, 2 marks diagram, 3 marks dedicated interrupts list)*

---

### ✅ Model Solution to Question A (b)

**Step 1 — Role of the 8259A PIC:**

The 8086 has only **one INTR pin**, but in real systems, multiple devices (keyboard, timer, disk, etc.) must share this single pin. The **8259A Programmable Interrupt Controller (PIC)** acts as a **multi-line interrupt manager** — it accepts interrupt requests from up to **8 external devices**, prioritizes them, and presents a single request to the 8086's `INTR` pin.

**Step 2 — The INTA Cycle (Two Pulses):**

1. **INTA Pulse 1 (First):** The 8086 sends a pulse to the 8259A, telling it to *freeze* its current priority state and prepare to send the type number.
2. **INTA Pulse 2 (Second):** The 8086 reads the **interrupt type number** placed by the 8259A on the **lower data bus (D0–D7)**.

**Step 3 — Example: Keyboard Generates Interrupt via 8259A**

- Keyboard (IRQ1 of 8259A) asserts its request line.
- 8259A sets INTR high → 8086 sees `INTR = 1` AND `IF = 1`.
- 8086 sends **INTA1** → 8259A freezes priority and selects the highest-priority request (keyboard = IRQ1).
- 8086 sends **INTA2** → 8259A places the **type number** (e.g., `09H` for IRQ1) on D0–D7.
- 8086 reads type = `09H`, computes vector address = `4 × 09H = 24H`.
- 8086 pushes **FLAGS, CS, IP** onto the stack.
- Clears **IF** and **TF**.
- Fetches `CS` from `00026H:00027H` and `IP` from `00024H:00025H`.
- Transfers control to the keyboard ISR.
- The ISR handles the keystroke, sends **EOI (End of Interrupt)** to 8259A, and executes **IRET**.

**Step 4 — End of Interrupt (EOI) Command:**

The ISR must send a specific **Operation Command Word (OCW2)** to the 8259A to inform it that the interrupt has been serviced. Without EOI, the 8259A will block further interrupts at the same or lower priority.

*(7 marks: 2 marks role of 8259A, 3 marks INTA cycle explanation, 2 marks example + EOI)*

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q. B (a)** Discuss the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** for the 8086 in detail. Explain the role of the **`STI`, `CLI`, and `IRET`** instructions. (7 marks)

**Q. B (b)** Compute the **physical address of the interrupt vector** for `INT 8AH` and `INT F0H`. If the SP register initially holds the value `0400H`, compute the new SP value after the interrupt is accepted. Assume the stack segment is `SS = 3000H`. (7 marks)

---

### ✅ Model Solution to Question B (a)

**Step 1 — Definition of ISR:**

An **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** is a specialized subroutine that the CPU executes in response to a specific interrupt. It typically:
- **Saves** the CPU's current context (registers used).
- **Services** the interrupting device.
- **Restores** the context.
- **Returns** control using `IRET`.

**Step 2 — Role of `STI`, `CLI`, and `IRET`:**

| Instruction | Function | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| `STI` | **Set Interrupt Flag** — sets `IF = 1`, enabling maskable interrupts | Re-enable interrupts after critical code |
| `CLI` | **Clear Interrupt Flag** — sets `IF = 0`, disabling maskable interrupts | Protect critical sections / vector setup |
| `IRET` | **Interrupt Return** — pops `IP`, `CS`, and `FLAGS` from the stack | The mandatory final instruction of every ISR |

**Step 3 — Sample ISR Skeleton:**

```asm
MY_ISR PROC FAR
      STI                       ; Allow nested interrupts (optional)
      PUSH AX
      PUSH BX
      PUSH CX
      ; ... service code here ...
      POP  CX
      POP  BX
      POP  AX
      IRET
MY_ISR ENDP
```

**Step 4 — Difference between `IRET` and `RET`:**

- `RET` pops only `IP` (and `CS` for far return) — does **not** restore FLAGS.
- `IRET` pops **IP, CS, and FLAGS** in that order — restoring the complete pre-interrupt state.

*(7 marks: 2 marks ISR definition, 3 marks instruction roles, 2 marks sample code)*

---

### ✅ Model Solution to Question B (b)

**Step 1 — Vector Address for `INT 8AH`:**

$$\text{Addr}_{8\text{AH}} = 4 \times 8\text{AH} = 4 \times 138_{10} = 552_{10} = 228\text{H}$$

$$\text{Vector Address for INT 8AH} = \mathbf{00228\text{H}}$$

**Step 2 — Vector Address for `INT F0H`:**

$$\text{Addr}_{\text{F0H}} = 4 \times \text{F0H} = 4 \times 240_{10} = 960_{10} = 3\text{C0H}$$

$$\text{Vector Address for INT F0H} = \mathbf{003\text{C0H}}$$

**Step 3 — New SP after interrupt acceptance:**

Each interrupt causes 3 pushes: FLAGS, CS, IP — each 2 bytes = **6 bytes total**.

$$\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = 0400\text{H} - 6 = \mathbf{03\text{FAH}}$$

**Step 4 — Physical address where IP is stored (the last push):**

$$\text{Physical}_{\text{IP}} = \text{SS} \times 16 + \text{SP}_{\text{new}}$$

$$\text{Physical}_{\text{IP}} = 3000\text{H} \times 10\text{H} + 03\text{FAH} = 30\text{3FAH}$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks for each vector address, 2 marks for SP calculation, 1 mark for physical address of IP)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing the formula:** Some students write `N × 4` as `4N` but get the byte order wrong. Remember: at `4N` you store **IP**, at `4N+2` you store **CS** (low-to-high in memory).
> 2. **Forgetting the IF flag:** Many answers describe `INTR` but forget to mention that **`IF = 1` is required** for the CPU to accept maskable hardware interrupts.
> 3. **SP change:** Some students incorrectly say "SP decreases by 3" — it decreases by **6 bytes** (3 word-pushes).
> 4. **NMI vs INTR:** Examiners deduct heavily when students swap the properties of NMI and INTR. Memorize: **NMI → non-maskable, Type 2, edge-triggered; INTR → maskable, level-sensitive**.
> 5. **EOI command:** In 8259A-based questions, students often forget the **EOI (End of Interrupt)** command. The 8259A will not accept further interrupts without it.
> 6. **`IRET` vs `RET`:** A very common mistake is ending an ISR with `RET` instead of `IRET`. This will leave the FLAGS register unrestored.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Interrupts in 8086:**

- ⭐ **Interrupt** = event that suspends normal CPU execution and transfers control to an ISR.
- ⭐ The 8086 supports **256 interrupt types** (Type 0 to 255), each with a unique 8-bit type number.
- ⭐ The **IVT** occupies memory `00000H` to `003FFH` (1 KB), with each entry being **4 bytes**.
- ⭐ The vector address for type N is computed as **`4 × N`**.
- ⭐ Lower 2 bytes = **IP**, upper 2 bytes = **CS**.
- ⭐ **Hardware interrupts:** `INTR` (maskable, type from 8259A) and `NMI` (non-maskable, Type 2).
- ⭐ **Software interrupts:** `INT 0` (divide error), `INT 1` (single step), `INT 2` (NMI), `INT 3` (breakpoint), `INT 4` (`INTO` if OF=1), and `INT n` (any user interrupt).
- ⭐ The **Interrupt Flag (IF)** in FLAGS register controls maskable interrupts — `STI` sets it, `CLI` clears it.
- ⭐ On interrupt, the CPU automatically pushes **FLAGS → CS → IP** in that order, decreasing **SP by 6 bytes**.
- ⭐ The CPU automatically clears **IF and TF** during interrupt to prevent nested chaos.
- ⭐ **`IRET`** (not `RET`) is used to return — it pops **IP → CS → FLAGS**, increasing **SP by 6 bytes**.
- ⭐ **Priority order (high → low):** Software → NMI → INTR → Single-step.
- ⭐ The **8259A PIC** arbitrates multiple device requests, sending a type number to 8086 via two `INTA` pulses.
- ⭐ **EOI (End of Interrupt)** command must be sent to 8259A at the end of a hardware ISR.
- ⭐ The NMI's vector is **hardwired at `00008H`**, while INTR's vector is **dynamic** (supplied by the 8259A).

---

> *End of Notes — Topic: Interrupts | PBEET504 Module 3 | KTU 2024 Scheme*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ADC (Introduction Only) - Introduction to Arduino IDE - Arduino Libraries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET504 - MICROPROCESSORS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** ADC (Introduction Only) - Introduction to Arduino IDE - Arduino Libraries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) — Formal Definition

An **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** is an electronic integrated subsystem that transforms a continuously varying analog input signal (typically a voltage level) into a discrete, multi-level binary numerical representation suitable for processing by a digital microcontroller. The conversion process involves two fundamental operations: **discretization in time (sampling)** and **discretization in amplitude (quantization)**, followed by binary encoding.

In the context of the ATmega328P microcontroller (the brain of the Arduino UNO), the ADC is a **Successive Approximation Register (SAR)** type, **10-bit** resolution, with **6 multiplexed input channels** accessible via analog pins **A0–A5**, accepting analog voltages in the range **0 V to 5 V** (default) or **0 V to 1.1 V** (internal reference).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 — ADC Introduction):**
> Only the conceptual understanding of ADC is mandated at this stage — students must understand the **block-level working**, the **resolution formula**, and the role of ADC as a bridge between the **analog physical world** and the **digital processing domain**.

### 1.2 ADC — Real-World Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **digital weighing scale** that can only display whole numbers (1 kg, 2 kg, 3 kg…). If you place a 2.7 kg apple, the scale rounds it to **3 kg** because it lacks fine resolution. The "fineness" of the scale (whether it shows 1 kg steps or 0.1 kg steps) is its **resolution**. Similarly:

- The **analog voltage** is the actual weight (continuous, infinite values).
- The **ADC** is the scale that "rounds" the voltage into discrete integer steps.
- The **number of steps** the scale can distinguish = **2ⁿ**, where **n** is the number of bits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Quantization Step Size (Resolution):**
> 
> $$\text{Step Size } Q = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$
> 
> For Arduino UNO: $Q = \frac{5\,\text{V}}{2^{10}} = \frac{5}{1024} \approx \mathbf{4.88\,mV}$ per step.

### 1.3 Introduction to Arduino IDE — Formal Definition

The **Arduino Integrated Development Environment (Arduino IDE)** is a cross-platform, Java-based desktop application that provides a unified graphical interface to **write, compile, verify, and upload** firmware (called **sketches**) onto Arduino-compatible microcontroller boards. The IDE bundles a **text editor**, a **compiler toolchain (avr-gcc for AVR boards)**, an **uploader (avrdude)**, and a **serial monitor** for real-time debugging.

### 1.4 Arduino IDE — Real-World Intuitive Analogy

Think of the Arduino IDE as a **digital kitchen with a recipe book**:
- The **editor** is your writing pad where you write the recipe (the code).
- The **compiler** is the head chef who translates your recipe into precise kitchen commands the chef (microcontroller) understands.
- The **uploader** is the waiter who delivers the dish (compiled hex file) to the customer (the board).
- The **Serial Monitor** is your tasting window where you can check the result of cooking in real time.

### 1.5 Arduino Libraries — Formal Definition

An **Arduino Library** is a collection of pre-compiled, reusable C/C++ functions, classes, and constant definitions bundled together that abstracts low-level hardware register manipulation into high-level, user-friendly function calls. Libraries drastically reduce code complexity, promote modularity, and shield the user from directly configuring complex peripherals (timers, ADC, I²C, SPI, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Categories of Arduino Libraries:**
> 
> 1. **Built-in (Standard) Libraries** — Ship with the IDE (e.g., `Wire.h`, `SPI.h`, `Servo.h`, `EEPROM.h`).
> 2. **Library Manager Libraries** — Installable via *Sketch → Include Library → Manage Libraries* (e.g., `Adafruit_Sensor`, `DHT sensor library`).
> 3. **User-Defined (Custom) Libraries** — Created by the user for project-specific reuse; saved in the `libraries/` folder inside the sketchbook.

### 1.6 Libraries — Real-World Intuitive Analogy

A **library is like a toolbelt of pre-built tools**. Instead of building a hammer from scratch every time, you grab a ready-made hammer from the toolbelt. In Arduino, when you write `#include <Servo.h>`, you are essentially "opening the toolbelt" that contains the ready-made `Servo` class — and you can directly use functions like `myservo.write(90)` without knowing the internal timer register configurations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 ADC — Operational Concept Breakdown (Step-by-Step Logic)

The ADC conversion process is governed by **four sequential stages**. Understanding these stages is essential because each introduces a specific type of error or design consideration.

#### Stage 1 — Anti-Aliasing (Pre-Filtering)
- **Why:** Real-world analog signals contain high-frequency noise that would otherwise fold back into the sampled signal (aliasing).
- **How:** A low-pass **RC filter** at the ADC input attenuates frequencies above half the sampling rate.
- **Engineering Rule (Nyquist Theorem):** The sampling frequency $f_s$ must be **at least twice** the highest frequency $f_{max}$ present in the input signal.

$$f_s \geq 2 \times f_{max}$$

#### Stage 2 — Sampling and Hold (S/H)
- **Why:** During conversion, the analog signal must be held **frozen** at a stable voltage, or the digital output would be meaningless.
- **How:** A sample-and-hold capacitor charges to the input voltage, and a switch isolates it from the input during quantization.

#### Stage 3 — Quantization
- **Why:** A continuous range (0 V to $V_{ref}$) must be mapped to a finite set of integer codes.
- **How:** The full-scale range is divided into $2^n$ equal intervals, each of size $Q$ (the step size).

$$Q = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} \quad \text{(Volts per LSB)}$$

#### Stage 4 — Encoding
- The discrete quantized level is assigned a unique **binary code** of $n$ bits.
- For Arduino UNO's **10-bit ADC**, the output ranges from `0` (for 0 V input) to `1023` (for $V_{ref}$ input).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Digital Output Formula (the most-tested KTU formula):**
> 
> $$D_{out} = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 2^n\right)$$
> 
> The corresponding **reconstructed analog voltage** is:
> 
> $$V_{in} = \frac{D_{out} \times V_{ref}}{2^n} = D_{out} \times Q$$

### 2.2 Key ADC Performance Metrics

- **Resolution ($n$):** Number of bits in the digital output.
- **Step Size ($Q$):** Smallest detectable voltage change.
- **Sampling Rate ($f_s$):** Samples per second (for Arduino UNO ≈ **15 kSPS** at full resolution).
- **Quantization Error:** Inherent ±½ LSB uncertainty.
- **Full-Scale Range (FSR):** $V_{ref, max} - V_{ref, min}$.

### 2.3 Arduino IDE — Architecture Breakdown

The Arduino IDE is structured around a **simplified C/C++ dialect** that hides `main()` from the user. The IDE's build pipeline performs:

| IDE Component | Underlying Tool | Engineering Role |
|---|---|---|
| Text Editor | Custom Java Editor | User code authoring |
| Verifier (Verify) | `avr-gcc` compiler | Source → Object translation |
| Uploader (Upload) | `avrdude` | Object → Flash memory transfer |
| Serial Monitor | Virtual COM port bridge | Real-time `Serial.print()` display |
| Library Manager | ZIP/JSON resolver | Dependency installation |

### 2.4 Arduino Sketch — Anatomy

Every Arduino sketch has a **mandatory two-function structure**:

| Function | Purpose | Execution |
|---|---|---|
| `void setup()` | Initialization (pin modes, Serial begin) | Runs **once** at power-up/reset |
| `void loop()` | Main logic (reads, writes, computes) | Runs **infinitely** in a tight cycle |

> [!NOTE]
> Hidden by the IDE: The IDE auto-injects a `main()` function that internally calls `init()`, `setup()`, and an infinite `while(1) { loop(); }` loop. This abstraction is what makes Arduino beginner-friendly.

### 2.5 Arduino Library — Internal Architecture

A library typically consists of **two files** placed in a folder named after the library:

| File | Extension | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Header File | `.h` | Class declarations, `#define`, prototypes |
| Source File | `.cpp` | Function definitions, hardware logic |
| Keywords File | `keywords.txt` | Syntax highlighting for the IDE |
| Examples Folder | `examples/` | Sample sketches for users |

**Library Inclusion Methods:**

| Method | Syntax | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Include with `< >` | `#include <Wire.h>` | Standard/built-in libraries |
| Include with `" "` | `#include "MyLib.h"` | Custom/user libraries (local) |
| Add to Sketch | Sketch → Include Library → Add .ZIP | External downloaded libraries |

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — All KTU-Tested Formulas for ADC + Arduino IDE + Libraries**

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ADC Step Size | $Q = V_{ref} / 2^n$ | Volts per LSB |
| 2 | Digital Code | $D_{out} = (V_{in} / V_{ref}) \cdot 2^n$ | Integer (0 to $2^n - 1$) |
| 3 | Reconstructed Voltage | $V_{in} = D_{out} \cdot Q$ | Volts |
| 4 | Nyquist Sampling | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Hz |
| 5 | Quantization Error | $e_q \leq \pm Q/2$ | Volts |
| 6 | Arduino UNO ADC | $Q = 5/1024 = 4.88$ mV | $n = 10$, $V_{ref} = 5$ V |
| 7 | ADC Channels (UNO) | 6 channels | A0 – A5 |
| 8 | Sketch Structure | `setup()` + `loop()` | Mandatory functions |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | ADC + Arduino Application |
|---|---|
| IoT Weather Stations | TMP36 temperature sensor → ADC → cloud upload |
| Biomedical Instrumentation | ECG/EMG signal digitization (12-bit external ADCs) |
| Industrial Automation | Pressure/flow sensor monitoring with Arduino |
| Robotics | IR/ultrasonic sensor data acquisition |
| Smart Agriculture | Soil moisture (analog) → ADC → irrigation control |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of ADC Quantization Step Size ($Q$)

**Goal:** Derive the smallest voltage change that an $n$-bit ADC can resolve.

**Step 1 — Define the Full-Scale Range.**
The ADC accepts an input voltage ranging from $V_{ref,min}$ to $V_{ref,max}$. For the Arduino UNO (default configuration), this range is $0$ V to $5$ V.

$$\text{Full-Scale Range (FSR)} = V_{ref,max} - V_{ref,min}$$

**Step 2 — Count the Number of Discrete Output Codes.**
An $n$-bit ADC produces $2^n$ unique binary codes, ranging from $0$ (lowest) to $2^n - 1$ (highest).

$$\text{Number of codes} = 2^n$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Step Size.**
By definition, the step size $Q$ is the FSR divided by the number of distinct codes. Each LSB (Least Significant Bit) represents one step.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{\text{FSR}}{2^n} \\
  &= \frac{V_{ref,max} - V_{ref,min}}{2^n} \\
  &= \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} \quad \text{(for unipolar } 0 \text{ to } V_{ref} \text{ ADC)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Arduino UNO Values.**
With $V_{ref} = 5$ V and $n = 10$ bits:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{5}{2^{10}} \\
  &= \frac{5}{1024} \\
  &= 0.0048828125 \text{ V} \\
  &\approx 4.88 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The Arduino UNO can detect voltage changes as small as **4.88 mV** with its 10-bit ADC.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Digital Output Code ($D_{out}$)

**Goal:** Express the digital output as a function of input voltage.

**Step 1 — Recognize Linearity.**
A well-designed ADC exhibits a **linear transfer characteristic**, meaning the digital output is directly proportional to the input voltage within the FSR.

**Step 2 — Use the Proportionality Constant.**
The proportionality constant is $2^n / V_{ref}$ (i.e., codes per volt).

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{out} &= \frac{V_{in}}{Q} \\
        &= \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}/2^n} \\
        &= \frac{V_{in} \cdot 2^n}{V_{ref}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Handle Quantization (Rounding).**
Since $D_{out}$ must be an integer, the actual ADC output is the nearest integer to the proportional value.

$$D_{out} = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot 2^n\right)$$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example.**
For Arduino UNO, $V_{in} = 2.5$ V, $V_{ref} = 5$ V, $n = 10$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{out} &= \frac{2.5}{5} \times 2^{10} \\
        &= 0.5 \times 1024 \\
        &= 512
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification using inverse formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{in,reconstructed} &= D_{out} \times Q \\
                      &= 512 \times 4.88 \text{ mV} \\
                      &= 2498.56 \text{ mV} \\
                      &\approx 2.50 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The minor discrepancy (~1.44 mV) is the inherent **quantization error**, which is always $\leq \pm Q/2 \approx \pm 2.44$ mV for the Arduino UNO.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Maximum Quantization Error

**Goal:** Find the worst-case error introduced by quantization.

**Step 1 — Define Error.**
Quantization error is the difference between the actual analog input and its reconstructed digital equivalent.

$$e_q = V_{in} - V_{in,reconstructed}$$

**Step 2 — Establish Bounds.**
By definition of rounding, the error lies within half a step on either side.

$$-\frac{Q}{2} \leq e_q \leq +\frac{Q}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Express in Volts (Arduino UNO).**

$$e_{q,\max} = \pm \frac{Q}{2} = \pm \frac{4.88\,\text{mV}}{2} = \pm 2.44\,\text{mV}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — ADC vs. Arduino-IDE-vs. Libraries

| Analytical Aspect | ADC | Arduino IDE | Arduino Libraries |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Core Function** | Convert analog → digital | Author/compile/upload code | Abstract hardware complexity |
| **Primary User Benefit** | Sense real-world signals | Single-click upload workflow | Reuse, modularity, simplicity |
| **Key Configuration** | Reference voltage, prescaler, channel | Board, port selection | `#include` directive |
| **KTU-Mandated Formula** | $Q = V_{ref} / 2^n$ | `setup()` / `loop()` | Header + Source files |
| **Common Pitfall** | Forgetting to set `analogReference()` | Wrong COM port selection | Library version mismatch |
| **Error Source** | Quantization error (±½ LSB) | Compilation errors | Missing dependencies |

---

### 3.5 Worked Example — End-to-End ADC Reading on Arduino UNO

**Problem:** An LM35 temperature sensor outputs 0.250 V at 25 °C (10 mV/°C). What digital value will the Arduino UNO's 10-bit ADC return? What temperature does it represent?

**Given:** $V_{in} = 0.250$ V, $V_{ref} = 5$ V, $n = 10$.

**Step 1 — Compute Digital Code:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{out} &= \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 2^n \\
        &= \frac{0.250}{5} \times 1024 \\
        &= 0.05 \times 1024 \\
        &= 51.2 \\
        &\approx 51 \quad \text{(after rounding)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Reconstruct Voltage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{in,reconstructed} &= 51 \times 4.88 \text{ mV} \\
                      &= 248.88 \text{ mV} \\
                      &\approx 0.249 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Temperature (LM35 Scale = 10 mV/°C):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{V_{in}}{10\,\text{mV/°C}} \\
  &= \frac{249\,\text{mV}}{10\,\text{mV/°C}} \\
  &= 24.9\,°\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The Arduino UNO reports digital value **51**, corresponding to a temperature of **≈ 24.9 °C** (close to the true 25 °C, within the ±2.44 mV quantization tolerance).

---

### 3.6 Library Inclusion — Step-by-Step Logical Flow

**Step 1 — Search:** Open *Sketch → Include Library → Manage Libraries*.
**Step 2 — Install:** Search library name, click *Install*.
**Step 3 — Include:** Library is now usable via `#include <LibraryName.h>`.
**Step 4 — Instantiate:** Create an object (e.g., `Servo myservo;`).
**Step 5 — Use Methods:** Call library functions (e.g., `myservo.attach(9);`).

```
Sketch Lifecycle:
  #include <Library.h>   →  Pre-processor inserts library header
       ↓
  object_name.method()    →  Linker resolves to library object code
       ↓
  Verify (Compile)        →  avr-gcc compiles + links
       ↓
  Upload (avrdude)        →  Flash memory programmed
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ADC — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Input Signal Vin] --> B[Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter]
    B --> C[Sample and Hold Circuit]
    C --> D[Quantizer n-bit Resolution]
    D --> E[Binary Encoder]
    E --> F[Digital Output D0 to D9]
    G[Vref Reference Voltage] --> D
    H[Clock Source] --> C
    H --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **SAR (Successive Approximation Register) ADC** in the ATmega328P uses a binary search algorithm internally: starting from the MSB, it tests each bit by comparing $V_{in}$ with an internal DAC output. This takes exactly **n clock cycles** for an n-bit conversion.

---

### 4.2 Arduino IDE — Workflow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Write Sketch in Editor] --> P2[Step 2: Select Board and COM Port]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Click Verify to Compile]
    P3 --> P4{Compilation Successful?}
    P4 -- No --> P5[Fix Errors in Editor]
    P5 --> P3
    P4 -- Yes --> P6[Step 4: Click Upload]
    P6 --> P7[avrdude Transfers Hex to Flash]
    P7 --> P8[Sketch Runs on Microcontroller]
    P8 --> P9[Use Serial Monitor for Debugging]
    P9 --> P1
```

---

### 4.3 Arduino Sketch — Execution Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Board Power On or Reset]) --> init[Hardware Initialization init]
    init --> setup[Call User setup Function]
    setup --> loopStart{loop Start}
    loopStart --> loopBody[Execute User loop Function]
    loopBody --> loopEnd{Return to loopStart}
    loopEnd --> loopStart
```

---

### 4.4 Arduino Library — Internal File Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Header File MyLib.h] --> C[Source File MyLib.cpp]
    C --> OBJ[Object Code MyLib.o]
    OBJ --> L[Linked with User Sketch]
    K[Keywords.txt] --> IDE[IDE Syntax Highlighting]
    E[Examples Folder] --> USER[User Learning Reference]
    H --> L
```

**Typical Library Folder Layout:**

```
libraries/
└── MySensorLib/
    ├── MySensorLib.h       (class declaration)
    ├── MySensorLib.cpp     (function definitions)
    ├── keywords.txt        (syntax color codes)
    └── examples/
        ├── BasicRead/
        │   └── BasicRead.ino
        └── AdvancedCalib/
            └── AdvancedCalib.ino
```

---

### 4.5 End-to-End System — Sensor + ADC + Arduino IDE + Library

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENS[Physical Sensor LM35 Temperature] --> AIN[Analog Voltage 0 to 5V]
    AIN --> ARDADC[Arduino UNO 10-bit ADC]
    ARDADC --> DIG[Digital Code 0 to 1023]
    DIG --> SKETCH[Arduino Sketch Code]
    SKETCH --> LIB[Library Function Call e.g. map]
    LIB --> OUT[Output Serial Monitor or Actuator]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This end-to-end flow is the **central theme of Module 3** — bridging **physical-world analog signals** with the **digital programmable world** of microcontrollers, using **libraries** to simplify complex processing.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define ADC. What is the resolution of the 10-bit ADC used in Arduino UNO? Calculate the step size if the reference voltage is 5 V.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** is an electronic circuit that converts a continuous analog signal into a discrete digital binary code for processing by a digital system.

The Arduino UNO (ATmega328P) has a **10-bit** ADC, so:

$$Q = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5}{2^{10}} = \frac{5}{1024} \approx 4.88 \text{ mV}$$

The ADC produces digital outputs from **0** (at 0 V) to **1023** (at 5 V).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is an Arduino library? Mention any two built-in libraries and state their functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Arduino library** is a collection of pre-written C/C++ functions that abstract hardware register-level operations into simple, reusable function calls.

| Library | Function |
|---|---|
| `Wire.h` | Implements **I²C** (Two-Wire Interface) communication. |
| `Servo.h` | Controls **servo motors** using PWM signals on digital pins. |
| `SPI.h` | Implements **Serial Peripheral Interface** for fast chip-to-chip communication. |
| `EEPROM.h` | Provides read/write access to **non-volatile internal EEPROM** memory. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often confuse **built-in libraries** (ship with IDE) with **Library Manager libraries** (must be installed). Always specify the inclusion method — `#include <Wire.h>` (with `< >`) for built-in.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q.A (a)** Explain the block diagram of an ADC and describe the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**Q.A (b)** With a 10-bit ADC and a reference voltage of 5 V, calculate:
  (i) The step size
  (ii) The digital output for an analog input of 3.2 V
  (iii) The reconstructed input voltage if the digital output is 750
  (iv) The maximum quantization error
**(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Q.A (a) [7 marks]**

**Block Diagram Description:**

1. **Analog Input ($V_{in}$):** Continuous voltage signal from the sensor (e.g., LM35, potentiometer).
2. **Anti-Aliasing Filter (Low-Pass RC):** Removes high-frequency noise components that would otherwise be misrepresented due to the Nyquist sampling theorem.
3. **Sample and Hold (S/H) Circuit:** Captures the instantaneous input voltage and holds it constant during the conversion process. Uses a hold capacitor and analog switch.
4. **Quantizer:** Divides the full-scale voltage range ($0$ to $V_{ref}$) into $2^n$ discrete levels.
5. **Encoder:** Converts the quantized level into a unique binary code of $n$ bits.
6. **Digital Output ($D_{out}$):** Binary representation delivered to the microcontroller (e.g., 10-bit value `0` to `1023` for Arduino UNO).

**Supporting Formula:**

$$D_{out} = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 2^n$$

**Conclusion:** The ADC thus serves as a critical interface between real-world analog phenomena and digital embedded processing.

---

### **Model Solution — Q.A (b) [7 marks]**

**Given:** $V_{ref} = 5$ V, $n = 10$ bits.

**(i) Step Size:**

$$
Q = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5}{1024} = 4.88 \text{ mV/LSB}
$$

**(ii) Digital Output for $V_{in} = 3.2$ V:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{out} &= \frac{3.2}{5} \times 1024 \\
        &= 0.64 \times 1024 \\
        &= 655.36 \\
        &\approx 655 \quad \text{(rounded to nearest integer)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Reconstructed Voltage for $D_{out} = 750$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{in,reconstructed} &= D_{out} \times Q \\
                      &= 750 \times 4.88 \text{ mV} \\
                      &= 3660 \text{ mV} \\
                      &= 3.66 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Maximum Quantization Error:**

$$
e_{q,\max} = \pm \frac{Q}{2} = \pm 2.44 \text{ mV}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q.B (a)** What is the Arduino IDE? Explain the typical workflow used to write, compile, and upload a sketch to an Arduino board. Mention the role of `setup()` and `loop()` functions. **(7 marks)**

**Q.B (b)** Define Arduino libraries. Explain the internal structure of a typical user-defined library with a neat diagram. List the steps to install and use an external library in an Arduino project. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Q.B (a) [7 marks]**

**Arduino IDE Definition:**
The **Arduino Integrated Development Environment (IDE)** is a cross-platform Java-based application that provides tools to author, compile, verify, and upload code (sketches) to Arduino microcontroller boards.

**Workflow Steps:**

1. **Write:** Open the IDE, create a new sketch, and write code in the editor.
2. **Select Board and Port:** Use *Tools → Board* (e.g., Arduino UNO) and *Tools → Port* (e.g., COM3).
3. **Verify (Compile):** Click the ✓ button. The IDE invokes `avr-gcc` to compile and link the sketch with the Arduino core library.
4. **Upload:** Click the → button. The IDE invokes `avrdude` to transfer the compiled `.hex` file to the board's flash memory via USB.
5. **Monitor:** Open *Tools → Serial Monitor* to view debug messages via `Serial.print()`.

**`setup()` and `loop()` Functions:**

| Function | Role | Execution |
|---|---|---|
| `void setup()` | Initializes pins, peripherals, `Serial` | Runs **once** at startup |
| `void loop()` | Contains the main repeated logic | Runs **continuously** in an infinite cycle |

**Hidden Main:** The IDE auto-generates a `main()` function that calls `init()`, `setup()`, then runs `while(1) { loop(); }`.

---

### **Model Solution — Q.B (b) [7 marks]**

**Library Definition:**
A library is a **reusable collection of C/C++ code** that encapsulates complex hardware functions behind a simple API.

**Internal File Structure (Diagram):**

```
MyLib/
├── MyLib.h          (class declaration, prototypes)
├── MyLib.cpp        (function definitions, logic)
├── keywords.txt     (syntax highlighting config)
└── examples/        (sample sketches)
```

**Steps to Install an External Library:**

1. **Method 1 — Library Manager:** Open *Sketch → Include Library → Manage Libraries*, search for the library name, and click *Install*.
2. **Method 2 — Add .ZIP:** Download the library `.zip` file, then go to *Sketch → Include Library → Add .ZIP Library…*, and select the file.
3. **Method 3 — Manual:** Extract the `.zip` into the `Arduino/libraries/` folder inside your sketchbook directory, then restart the IDE.

**Usage in Code:**

```c
#include <MyLib.h>      // Include the library
MyLib sensor;           // Instantiate an object
sensor.begin();         // Call an initialization method
int val = sensor.read(); // Use library functions
```

**Categories:**

| Type | Example | Storage |
|---|---|---|
| Standard | `Wire.h`, `SPI.h` | Inside IDE installation |
| Library Manager | `Adafruit_Sensor.h` | User's `libraries/` folder |
| Custom | `MyProjectLib.h` | User's `libraries/` folder |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Arduino IDE/Library Section:**
> 
> 1. **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Forgetting to specify the **correct COM port** — students leave it at default and wonder why upload fails.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2 (–2 marks):** Mixing up `#include < >` (system/standard) with `#include " "` (local/user) — both work, but examiners expect awareness.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Confusing **void setup()** (one-time) with **void loop()** (infinite) — a classic 14-mark question pitfall.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4 (–2 marks):** Not mentioning the **IDE auto-generates `main()`** — this is a frequently-tested conceptual question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — ADC + Arduino IDE + Libraries**

#### ADC Essentials
- ADC = **Analog-to-Digital Converter**; converts continuous voltage into discrete binary codes.
- Arduino UNO's ATmega328P uses a **10-bit SAR (Successive Approximation Register) ADC**.
- **6 channels** available: `A0` to `A5`.
- Default reference voltage: **5 V**; alternatives: **1.1 V** (internal) or external.
- **Step size formula:** $Q = V_{ref} / 2^n = 4.88$ mV (for UNO).
- **Digital output formula:** $D_{out} = (V_{in}/V_{ref}) \cdot 2^n$.
- **Reconstruction formula:** $V_{in} = D_{out} \cdot Q$.
- **Quantization error bound:** $\pm Q/2 = \pm 2.44$ mV (UNO).
- **Nyquist criterion:** $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$.
- **Sampling rate:** Arduino UNO default ≈ **15 kSPS** (prescaler = 128).
- Function call: `analogRead(pin)` returns value 0–1023.

#### Arduino IDE Essentials
- IDE = **Integrated Development Environment** (Java-based, cross-platform).
- Compilation tool: `avr-gcc`. Uploader: `avrdude`.
- Every sketch **must** contain `setup()` and `loop()`.
- `setup()` runs **once**; `loop()` runs **forever**.
- IDE **auto-generates** a `main()` that calls `init()`, `setup()`, then enters an infinite loop calling `loop()`.
- Workflow: **Write → Verify (Compile) → Upload (avrdude) → Monitor (Serial Monitor)**.

#### Arduino Libraries Essentials
- Library = **reusable C/C++ code bundle** that abstracts hardware.
- Two core files: `.h` (declaration) and `.cpp` (definition).
- Inclusion methods: `#include <X.h>` (standard), `#include "X.h"` (local).
- Categories: **Built-in / Library Manager / User-defined**.
- Installation: **Library Manager** or **Add .ZIP Library** or **Manual copy** to `libraries/` folder.
- Examples: `Wire.h` (I²C), `SPI.h` (SPI), `Servo.h` (servos), `EEPROM.h` (non-volatile storage).

#### Quick-Recall Numerical Anchor
- $2^{10} = 1024$ → Arduino UNO ADC max code = **1023**.
- $5\,\text{V} / 1024 \approx 4.88$ mV/LSB.

#### Common Exam Traps
- Do not confuse **bit count** with **code count** (1024 codes for 10 bits, not 1024 levels on the y-axis of the transfer curve — it's 1024 distinct code values).
- Do not say "10-bit ADC has 10 levels" — it has **$2^{10} = 1024$ levels**.
- Always state $V_{ref}$ in calculations — examiners deduct marks if omitted.
- For libraries, always mention the **file extension** (`.h` and `.cpp`) and the **folder name** matching the library name.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steps for Creating an Arduino Program - Arduino Sketch Structure and Flow - Setup and Loop Functions - Programming in Embedded C - Data Types - Operators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET504 - MICROPROCESSORS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Steps for Creating an Arduino Program - Arduino Sketch Structure and Flow - Setup and Loop Functions - Programming in Embedded C - Data Types - Operators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Arduino Program (Sketch)?

An **Arduino Program**, officially called a **Sketch**, is a set of instructions written in a simplified version of **C/C++** that is compiled by the **Arduino IDE** and uploaded to the microcontroller board to perform a specific embedded task. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, an Arduino sketch follows a strict two-function architecture (`setup()` and `loop()`) that maps directly onto the **bare-metal embedded execution model** of AVR/ARM microcontrollers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** A sketch is a source-code file (`.ino` extension) containing at least two mandatory functions — `setup()` (initialization) and `loop()` (infinite execution) — which the Arduino build system converts into a hex file and burns into the non-volatile flash memory of the target MCU.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

Think of an Arduino program like a **restaurant kitchen during service hours**:
- The **`setup()` function** is what happens **once before doors open** — the chef turns on the gas, sharpens the knives, arranges the ingredients, and preheats the oven. This is done exactly **one time**.
- The **`loop()` function** is the **continuous service cycle** — the chef keeps taking orders, cooking, plating, and serving **forever** until the restaurant closes (or the power is cut).
- **Variables** are the ingredients stored on the counter; **operators** are the cooking actions (chop, mix, heat); **data types** are the containers (small bowl vs. large pot vs. measuring cup).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Architectural Insight:** Unlike a desktop program (which starts at `main()` and exits), an embedded Arduino program **never returns from `loop()`**. This is because the underlying `main()` in `main.cpp` (inside the Arduino core) internally calls `setup()` once and then executes `loop()` inside an infinite `for(;;)` loop. The user never writes `main()` — it is auto-generated by the IDE.

## 1.2 Steps for Creating an Arduino Program — High-Level Workflow

The complete workflow from idea to deployed firmware involves **6 canonical steps**:

| Step | Action | Tool / Outcome |
|------|--------|----------------|
| 1 | Define the Problem & Hardware | Pen-and-paper, datasheet |
| 2 | Write the Sketch in the Arduino IDE | `.ino` file |
| 3 | Verify / Compile the Code | IDE converts to `.hex` |
| 4 | Connect the Board via USB | Driver handshake, port detection |
| 5 | Upload the Code to the MCU | Flash memory burning |
| 6 | Verify Operation (Serial Monitor / LEDs) | Functional testing |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** If a question asks *"List the steps to create and upload an Arduino program"*, always present these 6 steps in order. Examiners reward clear sequential logic and tool-naming (e.g., specifying **avrdude** as the upload tool under the hood).

---

## 1.3 Arduino Sketch — Top-Level File Structure

Every `.ino` sketch file internally contains **three logical blocks**:

1. **Header / Comment Block** — A multi-line comment at the top describing author, date, and purpose (begins with `/* … */` or `//`).
2. **Global Declarations Section** — `#include` directives, `#define` macros, global variable declarations, and custom function prototypes. These live **outside** any function and are visible to *all* functions in the file.
3. **Function Definitions Section** — The two mandatory functions (`setup()` and `loop()`) plus any user-defined helper functions (e.g., `readSensor()`, `blinkLED()`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PBEET504 Module 3):** Students must clearly understand that the Arduino build process **auto-generates function prototypes** for any function defined *after* its first use in the file. This is why you can place `loop()` before a custom function without a forward declaration.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The `setup()` Function — Initialization Phase

The `setup()` function is the **initialization routine** that executes **exactly once** immediately after the MCU resets or powers up. Its primary job is to **configure hardware peripherals** and **initialize global state**.

### Operational Logic — Step by Step

1. **Power-On / Reset Event** — The MCU begins execution from the reset vector at address `0x0000` (for AVR) or the boot ROM.
2. **Auto-generated `main()` runs** — The Arduino core's `main.cpp` (hidden from user) sets up timers, ADC, and watchdog.
3. **`setup()` is invoked once** — Control transfers to the user's initialization code.
4. **Pin modes are configured** — Each digital pin is set as `INPUT`, `OUTPUT`, or `INPUT_PULLUP` via `pinMode()`.
5. **Communication interfaces are started** — `Serial.begin(9600)`, `Wire.begin()`, `SPI.begin()`, etc.
6. **Initial sensor calibration** — Any sensor-specific setup like `dht.begin()` is performed.
7. **Control returns to the hidden `main()`** — Execution falls into the `for(;;)` loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Footprint:** `setup()` code resides in flash and consumes SRAM only for local variables. Since it runs once, the stack frame is reclaimed immediately after return.

### Real-World Utility

In industrial automation, `setup()` initializes the **MODBUS slave address**, configures the **HMI display**, and calibrates the **load cell** before the production line starts moving. Any error here halts the system — hence `setup()` is the **safety-critical** phase.

---

## 2.2 The `loop()` Function — The Infinite Heartbeat

The `loop()` function is the **infinite control loop** that the hidden `main()` calls repeatedly. It is where the **reactive embedded logic** lives — reading sensors, making decisions, and actuating outputs.

### Operational Logic — Step by Step

1. **`loop()` is invoked** by the hidden `for(;;) { loop(); }` structure.
2. **Inputs are read** — `digitalRead()`, `analogRead()`, `Serial.read()`, etc.
3. **Computation / Decision** — Conditional logic (`if`, `switch`) processes the data.
4. **Actuation** — `digitalWrite()`, `analogWrite()`, `Serial.print()` triggers output.
5. **Timing / Delay** — `delay()` blocks execution; `millis()` / `micros()` allows non-blocking timing.
6. **Function returns** — Control goes back to the hidden loop, which immediately calls `loop()` again.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Writing `delay(1000)` inside `loop()` is **acceptable** for simple demos but **blocks** the MCU from doing anything else. In real embedded systems, the preferred pattern is the **"Blink Without Delay"** technique using `millis()`.

### Sketch Execution Timeline

$$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{setup}} + \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} T_{\text{loop}_i}$$

Where $T_{\text{loop}_i}$ is the time of the $i$-th iteration. Since the loop never terminates, $T_{\text{total}} \to \infty$ as long as power is supplied.

---

## 2.3 Embedded C Data Types in Arduino

Arduino's data types are inherited from **C/C++** but constrained by the **AVR 8-bit** or **ARM 32-bit** architecture. Choosing the right type is **critical for memory efficiency** on resource-constrained MCUs.

### KTU Formula Sheet — Data Types Reference

| Data Type | Size (Bytes) | Range / Precision | Use Case |
|-----------|:------------:|-------------------|----------|
| `void` | 0 | No value | Function returns |
| `boolean` / `bool` | 1 | `true` or `false` (0/1) | Flags, state |
| `char` | 1 | $-128$ to $127$ (signed), $0$ to $255$ (unsigned) | ASCII characters |
| `unsigned char` / `byte` | 1 | $0$ to $255$ | Raw byte, RGB pixel |
| `int` | 2 (AVR) / 4 (ARM) | $-32{,}768$ to $32{,}767$ (AVR) | General integer |
| `unsigned int` | 2 (AVR) / 4 (ARM) | $0$ to $65{,}535$ | Positive counts |
| `word` | 2 | $0$ to $65{,}535$ | Alias for `unsigned int` |
| `long` | 4 | $-2{,}147{,}483{,}648$ to $2{,}147{,}483{,}647$ | Time (`millis()`) |
| `unsigned long` | 4 | $0$ to $4{,}294{,}967{,}295$ | Large counters |
| `short` | 2 | $-32{,}768$ to $32{,}767$ | Compact integer |
| `float` | 4 | $\pm 3.4028235 \times 10^{38}$, ~6–7 decimal digits | Sensor data, PID |
| `double` | 4 (AVR) / 8 (ARM) | Same as float on AVR | High precision |
| `String` (object) | Dynamic | Heap-allocated | Text manipulation |
| `char[]` (array) | Variable | Heap-allocated | Fixed text |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Engineering Note:** On an **Arduino Uno (ATmega328P)**, you have only **2 KB of SRAM**. Using `float` or `String` carelessly can cause **heap fragmentation** and **stack overflow** — a classic KTU viva question.

---

## 2.4 Operators in Arduino C

Operators are the **verbs** of the language — they perform actions on operands. KTU Module 3 expects you to identify and apply all major categories.

### KTU Formula Sheet — Operator Categories

| Category | Operators | Example | Notes |
|----------|-----------|---------|-------|
| **Arithmetic** | $+ \quad - \quad \ast \quad / \quad \%$ | `a + b`, `a % b` | `%` is modulo |
| **Assignment** | $= \quad += \quad -= \quad \ast= \quad /= \quad \%=$ | `x += 5` | Shorthand |
| **Comparison** | $== \quad != \quad \textless \quad \textgreater \quad \textless= \quad \textgreater=$ | `if (x == 5)` | Returns `bool` |
| **Logical** | `&&` (AND), `$\vert\vert$` (OR), `!` (NOT) | `if (a>0 && b<10)` | Boolean logic |
| **Bitwise** | `&` `$\vert$` `^` `~` `<<` `>>` | `PORTB $\vert$= 0x01;` | Hardware registers |
| **Increment / Decrement** | `++` `--` | `i++`, `--j` | Prefix vs postfix |
| **Pointer / Address** | `*` (dereference), `&` (address-of) | `int* p = &x;` | Direct memory access |
| **Ternary** | `? :` | `max = (a>b) ? a : b;` | Inline conditional |
| **Comma** | `,` | `int x=1, y=2;` | Sequential evaluation |
| **sizeof** | `sizeof(type)` | `sizeof(int)` returns 2 | Compile-time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Differentiate between `=` and `==`"*. Always answer: **`=` is assignment** (stores value), **`==` is comparison** (returns boolean). Confusing them is the #1 bug in beginner sketches.

### Operator Precedence Cheat Sheet (Highest → Lowest)

1. Parentheses `()` and array subscript `[]`
2. Unary `++ -- ! ~ * & (type)`
3. Multiplicative `* / %`
4. Additive `+ -`
5. Shift `<< >>`
6. Relational `< <= > >=`
7. Equality `== !=`
8. Bitwise AND `&`
9. Bitwise XOR `^`
10. Bitwise OR `|`
11. Logical AND `&&`
12. Logical OR `||`
13. Ternary `?:`
14. Assignment `= += -= …`
15. Comma `,`

> [!TIP]
> When in doubt, use **parentheses** — the compiler respects them, and the examiner awards clarity points.

---

## 2.5 Arduino Compilation & Build Pipeline (Hidden from User)

The IDE performs these steps transparently:

1. **Preprocessing** — `#include` directives merge header files; `#define` macros substitute.
2. **Prototype Generation** — Function prototypes are auto-generated for user-defined functions.
3. **Compilation** — `avr-gcc` (for AVR boards) converts `.ino` to `.elf`.
4. **Assembly** — Converts to assembly.
5. **Linking** — Links against `core.a` (Arduino core library).
6. **Hex Generation** — Produces `.hex` file.
7. **Upload** — `avrdude` (or `bossac` for ARM) burns the hex into flash via the bootloader.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Viva Insight:** Asking about this pipeline is a classic way examiners test if you understand the **separation between user code and core firmware**. The hidden `main()` is in `…/arduino/hardware/arduino/avr/cores/arduino/main.cpp`.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation — Sketch Execution Time Calculation

### Problem Statement
A sketch reads an analog sensor and blinks an LED. The `loop()` function contains:
- `analogRead()` taking $t_1 = 100\,\mu s$
- A floating-point computation taking $t_2 = 250\,\mu s$
- `digitalWrite()` taking $t_3 = 5\,\mu s$
- `delay(50)` taking $t_4 = 50{,}000\,\mu s$

Derive the **total execution time over 1 hour** of continuous operation.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify components in `loop()`:**

$$T_{\text{loop}} = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_4$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$T_{\text{loop}} = 100\,\mu s + 250\,\mu s + 5\,\mu s + 50{,}000\,\mu s$$

**Step 3 — Sum the active (non-delay) work:**

$$T_{\text{active}} = 100 + 250 + 5 = 355\,\mu s$$

**Step 4 — Add the blocking delay:**

$$T_{\text{loop}} = 355\,\mu s + 50{,}000\,\mu s = 50{,}355\,\mu s$$

**Step 5 — Convert to milliseconds:**

$$T_{\text{loop}} = 50.355\,ms$$

**Step 6 — Compute number of loop iterations in 1 hour:**

$$N = \frac{T_{\text{hour}}}{T_{\text{loop}}} = \frac{3600 \times 1000\,ms}{50.355\,ms}$$

$$N = \frac{3{,}600{,}000}{50.355} \approx 71{,}494.79 \text{ iterations}$$

**Step 7 — Total active CPU time over 1 hour:**

$$T_{\text{CPU,total}} = N \times T_{\text{active}}$$

$$T_{\text{CPU,total}} \approx 71{,}495 \times 355\,\mu s$$

$$T_{\text{CPU,total}} \approx 25{,}380{,}725\,\mu s \approx 25.38 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 8 — CPU utilization percentage:**

$$U_{\text{CPU}} = \frac{T_{\text{CPU,total}}}{T_{\text{hour}}} \times 100\%$$

$$U_{\text{CPU}} = \frac{25.38}{3600} \times 100\% \approx 0.705\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Despite running "constantly", the MCU is **idle 99.295% of the time** during the `delay()`. This is why production firmware avoids `delay()` and uses **state machines with `millis()`** to achieve true multitasking on a single core.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — SRAM Memory Budget

### Problem Statement
An Arduino Uno (ATmega328P) has **2 KB = 2048 bytes of SRAM**. The sketch declares:
- `char msg[100]` (string buffer)
- `int sensorValues[200]` (sensor array)
- `float calibration[50]` (calibration table)
- 10 `int` local variables in functions

Verify if this fits in SRAM.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Static global allocations:**

$$\text{msg: } 100 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2 — Sensor array (int = 2 bytes on AVR):**

$$200 \times 2 = 400 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 3 — Calibration table (float = 4 bytes):**

$$50 \times 4 = 200 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 4 — Local variables (worst case, 10 ints on stack):**

$$10 \times 2 = 20 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 5 — Total estimated SRAM usage:**

$$S_{\text{total}} = 100 + 400 + 200 + 20 = 720 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 6 — Compare to available SRAM:**

$$S_{\text{available}} = 2048 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 7 — Utilization percentage:**

$$U_{\text{SRAM}} = \frac{720}{2048} \times 100\% \approx 35.16\%$$

**Step 8 — Verdict:** ✅ Fits comfortably. However, this excludes the **heap** used by `malloc()` and the **Arduino core overhead** (~150–200 bytes for `Serial`, timers, etc.), so true headroom is:

$$S_{\text{headroom}} = 2048 - 720 - 200 \approx 1128 \text{ bytes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Takeaway:** Always compute SRAM budget before declaring large arrays. Boards like the **Arduino Uno** have severe constraints; the **Arduino Mega** (8 KB SRAM) or **ESP32** (520 KB SRAM) are chosen for larger applications.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Setup vs Loop Comparison

| Parameter | `setup()` | `loop()` |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| **Execution Count** | Once (at boot/reset) | Infinite |
| **Purpose** | Configuration & initialization | Reactive logic |
| **Typical Calls** | `pinMode()`, `Serial.begin()` | `digitalRead()`, `analogWrite()` |
| **Blocking OK?** | Yes (one-time setup) | Avoid `delay()` in production |
| **Returns To** | Hidden `for(;;)` in `main()` | Same — re-invoked immediately |
| **Memory Impact** | Local stack reclaimed after return | Persistent (loop reuses) |
| **Failure Effect** | System never starts correctly | System runs but misbehaves |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Arduino Sketch Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power On or Reset MCU] --> B[Hidden main in core.cpp starts]
    B --> C[Init Timers and Watchdog]
    C --> D[Call user setup function]
    D --> E[setup function runs once]
    E --> F[Configure PinModes]
    F --> G[Begin Serial Wire SPI]
    G --> H[Init Sensors and Globals]
    H --> I[Return from setup]
    I --> J[Enter infinite for loop]
    J --> K[Call user loop function]
    K --> L[Read Inputs]
    L --> M[Process Logic]
    M --> N[Write Outputs]
    N --> O[Return from loop]
    O --> J
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The hidden `main()` is a `for(;;) { loop(); }` in the Arduino core. This is why `loop()` never returns control to your code — the hidden loop re-invokes it instantly.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Arduino Sketch File Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph sketchFile[Arduino Sketch .ino File]
        A1[Header Comment Block] --> A2[Include Directives]
        A2 --> A3[Define Macros]
        A3 --> A4[Global Variable Declarations]
        A4 --> A5[Function Prototypes optional]
        A5 --> A6[setup function definition]
        A5 --> A7[loop function definition]
        A5 --> A8[Custom user functions]
    end
    A6 --> A9[Mandatory runs once]
    A7 --> A10[Mandatory runs forever]
    A8 --> A11[Helper called by setup or loop]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Arduino IDE Build Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sketch .ino source] --> P1[Preprocessor expands macros and includes]
    P1 --> P2[Function prototypes auto-generated]
    P2 --> C1[avr-gcc compilation to .elf]
    C1 --> C2[Linking with core.a]
    C2 --> C3[Object copy to .hex file]
    C3 --> U1[avrdude upload tool]
    U1 --> M1[Arduino board flash memory]
    M1 --> V1[Verify and execute on reset]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Data Type Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is the data nature?}
    Q1 -->|Boolean flag| R1[Use bool or boolean]
    Q1 -->|Whole number| Q2{Signed or unsigned?}
    Q1 -->|Decimal number| R2[Use float]
    Q1 -->|Text| Q3{Fixed or dynamic length?}
    Q2 -->|Signed| Q4{Range less than 32768?}
    Q2 -->|Unsigned| R3[Use unsigned int or word]
    Q4 -->|Yes| R4[Use int]
    Q4 -->|No| R5[Use long or unsigned long]
    Q3 -->|Fixed| R6[Use char array]
    Q3 -->|Dynamic| R7[Use String object]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**Q: Define an Arduino Sketch. Explain the role of the `setup()` function.**

**Model Answer:**

An **Arduino Sketch** is a program written in the Arduino IDE (file extension `.ino`) using a simplified C/C++ dialect. The IDE compiles it and uploads the resulting hex code to the microcontroller's flash memory.

The **`setup()` function** is a user-defined function that executes **exactly once** when the board is powered on or reset. Its primary role is **hardware initialization**:
- Configuring pin directions using `pinMode()`.
- Starting communication protocols like `Serial.begin(9600)`.
- Initializing sensors and global variables.

After `setup()` returns, the hidden `main()` function in the Arduino core enters an infinite loop that repeatedly calls the `loop()` function.

> **Keywords for full marks:** *initialization, runs once, pinMode, Serial.begin, boot, reset.*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

**Q: List any four data types in Arduino C and state their memory size on an Arduino Uno.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Data Type | Size (bytes) |
|---|-----------|:------------:|
| 1 | `boolean` / `bool` | 1 |
| 2 | `char` | 1 |
| 3 | `int` | 2 |
| 4 | `float` | 4 |
| 5 | `long` | 4 |
| 6 | `double` | 4 (on AVR Uno) |

On an Arduino Uno (ATmega328P, 8-bit AVR architecture), the sizes are fixed as shown above. Note that `int` is **16 bits** (not 32) on AVR, unlike desktop systems.

> **Examiner's Note:** Award full marks only if the candidate mentions that `int` is **2 bytes on Uno** — confusing it with desktop `int` (4 bytes) is a common error.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure to create, compile, and upload an Arduino program to a board. List the tools used at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the structure of an Arduino sketch file. Differentiate between `setup()` and `loop()` functions in detail. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**The 6 canonical steps to create and upload an Arduino program:**

1. **Define the Problem and Select Hardware** — Identify inputs (sensors, switches), outputs (LEDs, motors), and the appropriate Arduino board (Uno, Mega, Nano). Refer to datasheets for pin ratings. *(1 mark)*

2. **Write the Sketch in Arduino IDE** — Open the IDE, write code in the editor using two mandatory functions: `setup()` and `loop()`. Save with `.ino` extension. *(1 mark)*

3. **Select Board and Port** — From the **Tools** menu, choose the correct board (e.g., "Arduino Uno") and the COM/serial port where the board is connected. *(1 mark)*

4. **Verify / Compile the Code** — Click the **Verify** (✓) button. The IDE invokes the preprocessor, `avr-gcc` compiler, and linker. Output: a `.hex` file in a temporary build folder. Errors are shown in the console. *(1 mark)*

5. **Upload the Code** — Click the **Upload** (→) button. The IDE calls **`avrdude`** (a command-line tool) which communicates with the **on-board bootloader** (e.g., Optiboot) to write the hex file to flash memory via the serial/USB interface. *(2 marks)*

6. **Verify and Test** — Open the **Serial Monitor** (Tools → Serial Monitor) at the correct baud rate to observe debug output. Visually confirm LED behavior, sensor readings, etc. *(1 mark)*

> **Tools used at each stage:**
> - Editor: Arduino IDE
> - Compiler: `avr-gcc`
> - Linker: `avr-gcc` (with `core.a`)
> - Uploader: `avrdude`
> - Hardware: USB-to-Serial converter (often CH340 or ATmega16U2)

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Structure of an Arduino Sketch File:**

An Arduino sketch (`.ino` file) is logically divided into **three blocks**:

1. **Header / Comment Block** — At the top, a multi-line comment `/* … */` or `//` lines describing the project, author, date, and version. *(1 mark)*

2. **Global Declarations** — Outside all functions, you can write:
   - `#include <Library.h>` — to add libraries (e.g., `Servo.h`).
   - `#define PIN_LED 13` — preprocessor macros.
   - `int sensorValue = 0;` — global variables.
   - Function prototypes (often auto-generated by the IDE). *(2 marks)*

3. **Function Definitions** —
   - `void setup() { … }` — runs once.
   - `void loop() { … }` — runs forever.
   - User-defined helper functions like `void blinkLED(int pin) { … }`. *(2 marks)*

**Comparison Table — `setup()` vs `loop()`:**

| Feature | `setup()` | `loop()` |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| Runs | Once at boot | Infinitely |
| Purpose | Initialize hardware | Reactive control logic |
| Typical API | `pinMode()`, `Serial.begin()` | `digitalRead()`, `analogWrite()` |
| Blocking calls | Acceptable | Discouraged in production |
| Return target | Hidden `main()` infinite loop | Same — re-invoked by hidden loop | *(2 marks)*

> **Neat diagram** (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram in these notes for the flow representation).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the various **data types** available in Arduino programming. Mention the size and range of each on an Arduino Uno. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the different **categories of operators** in Arduino C with two examples each. Differentiate between the assignment operator `=` and the comparison operator `==` with a code example. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Arduino Data Types — Detailed Description:**

Arduino supports the following data types, sized for the **ATmega328P (Arduino Uno)** architecture:

1. **`void`** — Indicates no value. Used in function declarations like `void setup()`. *(0.5 mark)*

2. **`boolean`** — Stores logical `true` (1) or `false` (0). Occupies 1 byte. Use for flags and conditional states. *(0.5 mark)*

3. **`char`** — Stores an 8-bit character. Range: $-128$ to $127$ (signed) or $0$ to $255$ (unsigned). Used to hold ASCII characters like `'A'`. *(1 mark)*

4. **`byte` (unsigned char)** — Same as unsigned char; range $0$ to $255$. Used for raw byte data (e.g., RGB pixel values). *(0.5 mark)*

5. **`int`** — 16-bit signed integer on AVR. Range: $-32{,}768$ to $32{,}767$. Most common for general counting. *(1 mark)*

6. **`unsigned int`** — 16-bit unsigned. Range: $0$ to $65{,}535$. Used for positive-only counters. *(0.5 mark)*

7. **`word`** — Alias for `unsigned int`. Range: $0$ to $65{,}535$. *(0.5 mark)*

8. **`long`** — 32-bit signed. Range: $-2{,}147{,}483{,}648$ to $2{,}147{,}483{,}647$. Used for `millis()` return values. *(0.5 mark)*

9. **`unsigned long`** — 32-bit unsigned. Range: $0$ to $4{,}294{,}967{,}295$. Crucial for timing without overflow. *(0.5 mark)*

10. **`float`** — 32-bit IEEE 754. Range: $\pm 3.4028235 \times 10^{38}$ with ~6–7 decimal digits. Used for sensor data and PID calculations. *(1 mark)*

11. **`double`** — On AVR Uno, it is **4 bytes** (same as float). On ARM boards (Due, Zero), it is **8 bytes** for true double precision. *(0.5 mark)*

12. **`String`** (capital S) — A class wrapping dynamic char arrays. Stored on the heap. Use cautiously due to fragmentation. *(0.5 mark)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The choice of data type directly impacts **SRAM consumption** and **execution speed**. Floating-point operations on AVR are **~10× slower** than integer operations because they are emulated in software (no FPU on ATmega328P).

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Categories of Operators in Arduino C:**

1. **Arithmetic Operators** *(1 mark)*
   - `+` (Addition): `int sum = a + b;`
   - `-` (Subtraction): `int diff = a - b;`
   - `*` (Multiplication): `int prod = a * b;`
   - `/` (Division): `int q = a / b;`
   - `%` (Modulo): `int r = a % b;`

2. **Assignment / Compound Assignment** *(1 mark)*
   - `=` : `x = 10;`
   - `+=` : `x += 5;` (equivalent to `x = x + 5;`)
   - `-=`, `*=`, `/=`, `%=` follow the same pattern.

3. **Comparison / Relational Operators** *(1 mark)*
   - `==` (equal to), `!=` (not equal to)
   - `<` (less than), `>` (greater than), `<=` (less than or equal), `>=` (greater than or equal)
   - Example: `if (temperature > 50) { fanOn(); }`

4. **Logical / Boolean Operators** *(1 mark)*
   - `&&` (logical AND): `if (a > 0 && b < 10)`
   - `||` (logical OR): `if (mode == 1 || mode == 3)`
   - `!` (logical NOT): `if (!buttonPressed)`

5. **Bitwise Operators** *(1 mark)*
   - `&` AND, `|` OR, `^` XOR, `~` NOT, `<<` left shift, `>>` right shift
   - Example: `PORTB |= 0x01;` sets bit 0 of PORTB (turns on Arduino pin 8).

6. **Increment / Decrement** *(0.5 mark)*
   - `i++` (post-increment), `++i` (pre-increment), `i--`, `--i`

7. **Ternary Operator** `(?:)` *(0.5 mark)*
