   - Syntax: `condition ? value_if_true : value_if_false`
   - Example: `int max = (a > b) ? a : b;`

8. **`sizeof` Operator** *(0.5 mark)*
   - `sizeof(int)` returns 2 on Uno; `sizeof(long)` returns 4.
   - Returns compile-time size in bytes.

---

**Differentiation between `=` and `==`:** *(1 mark)*

```cpp
int x = 5;        // '=' ASSIGNMENT: stores value 5 into x

if (x == 10) {    // '==' COMPARISON: checks if x equals 10
    Serial.println("x is 10");
}
```

- The **single equals `=`** is the **assignment operator**. It evaluates the right-hand side and stores the result in the left-hand variable.
- The **double equals `==`** is the **comparison (equality) operator**. It compares two values and returns a boolean (`true`/`false`).
- **Common bug:** Writing `if (x = 10)` instead of `if (x == 10)` causes `x` to be set to 10 every time and the condition to always be `true` — a classic KTU viva trap question.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing `int` size** (saying 4 bytes instead of 2 on Uno) — costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention `unsigned` variants** of int/long — costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Not distinguishing `=` from `==`** — almost always asked, and full marks require a *code example*.
> 4. **Writing `setup()` and `loop()` in the wrong order** is technically allowed (compiler doesn't care) but the convention is `setup()` first, then `loop()`.
> 5. **Forgetting the `void` return type** in function signatures: `setup()` and `loop()` must be declared as `void setup()` and `void loop()`.
> 6. **Claiming `double` is 8 bytes on Uno** — this is **false** for AVR. It is 4 bytes on Uno, 8 bytes on Due/Zero.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Arduino Programming Fundamentals):**

- ✅ An Arduino program is called a **Sketch** (`.ino` file).
- ✅ Every sketch **must** contain `setup()` and `loop()` — these are mandatory.
- ✅ `setup()` runs **once** at boot/reset; `loop()` runs **infinitely**.
- ✅ The hidden `main()` in the Arduino core auto-calls `setup()` then loops `loop()` forever.
- ✅ Compilation pipeline: **Preprocess → Compile (avr-gcc) → Link → Hex → Upload (avrdude)**.
- ✅ Upload tool: **avrdude** (AVR) or **bossac** (ARM).
- ✅ Data type sizes on **Arduino Uno (AVR)**:
  - `bool` = 1 byte
  - `char` = 1 byte
  - `int` = **2 bytes** (not 4!)
  - `long` = 4 bytes
  - `float` = 4 bytes
  - `double` = **4 bytes on Uno**, 8 on ARM boards.
- ✅ **Operators categories:** Arithmetic, Assignment, Comparison, Logical, Bitwise, Increment, Ternary, `sizeof`.
- ✅ **Most important distinction:** `=` (assign) vs `==` (compare).
- ✅ **Bitwise operators** (`&`, `|`, `^`, `~`, `<<`, `>>`) are heavily used for direct **register manipulation** (e.g., `PORTB |= 0x01`).
- ✅ **Avoid `delay()`** in production code — use `millis()`-based non-blocking timing.
- ✅ **SRAM on Uno is only 2 KB** — choose small data types (`byte` over `int` when possible).
- ✅ **Preprocessor directives:** `#include` (libraries), `#define` (macros), `#ifdef` (conditional compilation).
- ✅ **Operator precedence:** Parentheses first, then unary, then multiplicative, then additive, then shifts, then comparisons, then bitwise, then logical, then ternary, then assignment, then comma. **When unsure, use parentheses.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conditional Statements - Loops
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET504 - MICROPROCESSORS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Conditional Statements - Loops

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Embedded C Programming** for microcontrollers (such as the **Intel 8051**, **ARM Cortex-M**, and **PIC families**), the C language provides two principal categories of **control-flow structures** that alter the sequential execution of instructions stored in program memory (ROM/Flash):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conditional Statements (Selection Structures):** Constructs that evaluate a Boolean expression and select one of several alternative execution paths. As per the **ISO/IEC 9899:2018 (C18)** standard, the primary conditional constructs are the `if`, `if-else`, `if-else if-else` ladder, `switch-case`, and the conditional (ternary) operator `?:`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Loops (Iteration Structures):** Constructs that repeatedly execute a block of statements as long as a controlling Boolean expression evaluates to true. The C standard defines three primary loop forms: `while` (entry-controlled), `do-while` (exit-controlled), and `for` (count-controlled).

Both structures are essential for implementing **decision logic**, **finite state machines (FSMs)**, **polling algorithms**, and **schedulers** in real-time embedded firmware.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

| Construct | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **`if`** statement | A **railway signal** — "If the track ahead is clear, proceed; otherwise, halt." |
| **`if-else`** ladder | A **traffic police officer** at a junction directing cars into one of three lanes based on destination. |
| **`switch-case`** | A **telephone exchange** routing a call based on the dialed digit (0–9) to one of 10 specific lines. |
| **`while` loop** | A **microwave oven** that keeps running *as long as* the door remains closed. |
| **`do-while` loop** | A **vending machine** that dispenses a drink first, then checks if more coins are inserted. |
| **`for` loop** | A **factory assembly line** performing a fixed number of operations (e.g., tightening 8 bolts on a wheel). |

### 1.3 Importance in Embedded Systems

> [!NOTE]
> In bare-metal embedded firmware (no operating system), the program's `main()` function typically ends with an **infinite loop** — almost universally written as `while(1) { }` or `for(;;) { }`. This is because embedded controllers must run continuously for the lifetime of the device, unlike desktop programs that terminate upon completion. Conditional statements and loops form the *entire* control backbone of such firmware.

**Key Standard Metrics Used in This Module:**
- **`TRUE`** is conventionally represented by any **non-zero integer** in C.
- **`FALSE`** is conventionally represented by the integer literal **0**.
- A single iteration of a loop is called a **pass** or **cycle**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conditional Statements — Operational Breakdown

#### (A) The `if` Statement
```c
if (condition) {
    // Statement block executed only when condition is TRUE (non-zero)
}
```
- The `condition` is any scalar expression. In C, the result of `condition` is converted to `int` via **Boolean context conversion**.
- If `condition != 0`, the block executes; if `condition == 0`, control passes to the next statement after the block.
- **Logical Why:** Allows the firmware to take divergent action (e.g., `if (sensor_value > THRESHOLD) alarm_on();`).

#### (B) The `if-else` Statement
```c
if (condition) {
    // Block A
} else {
    // Block B
}
```
- Exactly one of the two blocks executes — providing a **mutually exclusive decision**.
- **Logical Why:** Common in I/O toggling: `if (LED == OFF) LED = ON; else LED = OFF;` (toggle without a flip-flop).

#### (C) The `if-else if-else` Ladder
```c
if      (cond1) { /* path 1 */ }
else if (cond2) { /* path 2 */ }
else if (cond3) { /* path 3 */ }
else            { /* default */ }
```
- Conditions are tested **top-down**; the first TRUE condition's block executes, and the rest are skipped.
- **Logical Why:** Multi-range decision logic — e.g., mapping an ADC value (0–1023) to discrete temperature bands (`COLD / WARM / HOT / CRITICAL`).

#### (D) The `switch-case` Statement
```c
switch (expression) {
    case constant_1: statements; break;
    case constant_2: statements; break;
    ...
    default:         statements;
}
```
- The `expression` must evaluate to an **integral type** (`char`, `int`, `long`, or `enum`).
- Each `case` label must be a **unique compile-time constant integer expression**.
- Without `break`, execution **falls through** to the next case (a frequently exploited feature in 8051 assembly-style programming).
- **Logical Why:** Faster and more readable than long `if-else if` chains when dispatching on a single variable (e.g., decoding an op-code byte, handling menu selections on a keypad).

#### (E) Conditional / Ternary Operator `?:`
```c
result = (condition) ? value_if_true : value_if_false;
```
- Compact single-line conditional; returns a value usable in expressions.
- Used in embedded code for compact register initialization: `max = (a > b) ? a : b;`.

### 2.2 Loops — Operational Breakdown

#### (A) `while` Loop (Entry-Controlled)
```c
while (condition) {
    // body
}
```
- Condition is **tested BEFORE** each iteration. If FALSE initially, the body **never executes**.
- Use case: **Polling** — `while (!(UART0_FR & (1<<4)));` waits for the UART transmit FIFO to empty.

#### (B) `do-while` Loop (Exit-Controlled)
```c
do {
    // body
} while (condition);
```
- Body executes **AT LEAST ONCE**; condition is tested AFTER.
- Use case: **Menu systems** that must display the prompt at least once before checking user input.

#### (C) `for` Loop (Count-Controlled)
```c
for (init; condition; update) {
    // body
}
```
- Equivalent semantically to: `init; while(condition) { body; update; }`.
- The three control expressions are optional: `for(;;)` is an **infinite loop**.
- Use case: **Iterating arrays** — `for (i = 0; i < 8; i++) PORT = segment_table[i];` drives a 7-segment display through digits 0–7.

#### (D) Loop Control Statements
| Statement | Effect |
|---|---|
| **`break;`** | Immediately terminates the **innermost** enclosing loop or `switch`. |
| **`continue;`** | Skips the remainder of the current iteration and jumps to the next iteration's condition/update. |
| **`goto label;`** | Unconditional jump (discouraged in structured programming; occasionally used in 8051 for exiting deeply nested error-handling). |

### 2.3 Logical Operators & Truth Values

Boolean expressions in C are formed using:
- **Relational operators:** `>`, `<`, `>=`, `<=`, `==`, `!=`
- **Logical operators:** `&&` (AND), `||` (OR), `!` (NOT)
- **Bitwise operators** (often used in embedded register checks): `&`, `|`, `^`, `~`, `<<`, `>>`

### 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following high-yield table summarises every construct, syntax, and execution rule you must memorize for the ESE. KTU examiners award marks for **exact syntax** and **correct evaluation order**.

| Construct | General Syntax | Key Evaluation Rule | Typical Embedded Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| `if` | `if (E) S;` | TRUE if `E ≠ 0` | Single sensor threshold check |
| `if-else` | `if (E) S1; else S2;` | Mutually exclusive branches | LED toggle logic |
| `if-else if` ladder | Multiple `else if (Ei)` | Top-down; first TRUE wins | ADC range → status mapping |
| `switch-case` | `switch(E) { case c1: … break; … }` | Integral `E`; constants only; `break` prevents fall-through | Op-code dispatch, menu selection |
| Ternary `?:` | `R = (E) ? A : B;` | Returns A if E TRUE, else B | Compact max/min, register init |
| `while` | `while (E) S;` | Entry-controlled; 0 or more iterations | Polling, wait-loops |
| `do-while` | `do S; while (E);` | Exit-controlled; ≥ 1 iteration | User prompt retry |
| `for` | `for (I; C; U) S;` | I once → test C → body → U → repeat | Array traversal, fixed-count delays |
| `break` | `break;` | Exits innermost loop/switch | Early exit on error |
| `continue` | `continue;` | Skips to next iteration | Skip invalid samples in a buffer |

### 2.5 Truth Table for Compound Boolean Conditions

| `A` | `B` | `A && B` | `A \|\| B` | `!A` |
|:---:|:---:|:--------:|:----------:|:----:|
| 0 | 0 | **0** | **0** | **1** |
| 0 | 1 | **0** | **1** | **1** |
| 1 | 0 | **0** | **1** | **0** |
| 1 | 1 | **1** | **1** | **0** |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECUs (Engine Control Units):** `switch-case` decodes sensor IDs from a CAN message payload byte.
- **IoT Sensor Nodes:** `while(1)` infinite loop with conditional sleep logic controls duty cycling.
- **Robotics:** `for` loops drive PWM through 0–255 to gradually accelerate a DC motor (soft-start).
- **Medical Devices:** Defibrillators use nested conditional logic to determine whether a shock is appropriate (rhythm analysis).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Execution-Trace Analysis — `if-else if` Ladder

**Problem Statement:** An 8051 reads the value of an analog sensor via P1 (8-bit unsigned). Classify the reading as follows: `[0–50]` → "LOW", `[51–150]` → "MEDIUM", `[151–200]` → "HIGH", `[201–255]` → "CRITICAL".

**Reference C Program:**
```c
#include <reg51.h>
void classify(unsigned char val) {
    if      (val <= 50)  P2 = 0x01;   // LOW  -> LED D0
    else if (val <= 150) P2 = 0x02;   // MED  -> LED D1
    else if (val <= 200) P2 = 0x04;   // HIGH -> LED D2
    else                 P2 = 0x08;   // CRIT -> LED D3
}
```

**Analytical Trace Table** (let `val = 130`, `P2 = 0x00` initially):

| Step | Active Test | val vs Threshold | Boolean Result | Branch Taken | New P2 |
|:----:|:------------|:----------------:|:--------------:|:------------:|:------:|
| 1 | `val <= 50`  | $130 \le 50$  | FALSE (0) | Fall through | 0x00 |
| 2 | `val <= 150` | $130 \le 150$ | **TRUE (1)** | Execute block  | **0x02** |
| 3 | `val <= 200` | (skipped) | — | — | — |
| 4 | `else` | (skipped) | — | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result:** `P2 = 0x02`. The trace demonstrates the **short-circuit top-down evaluation** property of C's `if-else if` ladder — once a TRUE branch is taken, the compiler-generated comparison instructions for remaining branches are **NOT executed**, saving MCU cycles.

### 3.2 Execution-Trace Analysis — `switch-case` with Fall-Through

**Problem Statement:** Implement a 4-function calculator command parser. Commands are received as single ASCII characters.

**Reference C Program:**
```c
char cmd = 'B';
int result;
switch (cmd) {
    case 'A': result = 10 + 5;  break;
    case 'B': result = 10 - 5;            // ← no break; falls through
    case 'C': result = result * 2; break;
    case 'D': result = 10 / 5;  break;
    default:  result = 0;
}
```

**Analytical Trace Table:**

| Step | `cmd` value | Active Case | Action Taken | `result` after step |
|:----:|:-----------:|:-----------:|:-------------|:-------------------:|
| 1 | 'B' (0x42) | `case 'A'` | Not matched; skip | uninitialized |
| 2 | 'B' (0x42) | `case 'B'` | Match! Execute `result = 10 - 5` | **5** |
| 3 | 'B' (0x42) | fall-through to `case 'C'` | Execute `result = result * 2` (5 × 2) | **10** |
| 4 | 'B' (0x42) | `break` | Exit switch | 10 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** When asked "What is the output?" for `switch-case` code, students frequently forget that omitting `break` causes **fall-through**. Marks are lost if you do not explicitly state "due to missing break, execution fell through to case 'C'".

### 3.3 Loop Iteration Count Derivation

**General Formula:** For a `for` loop of the form
`for (i = a; i <= b; i++)` where `i` increments by 1,

$$
\text{Number of iterations} N = \max\!\left(0,\ \lfloor b \rfloor - \lfloor a \rfloor + 1\right)
$$

**Proof / Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } i_0 &= a, \quad i_k = a + k, \quad \text{terminate when } i_k > b \\
\text{Last valid index } k_{\max} &\text{ satisfies } a + k_{\max} \le b \\
\Rightarrow k_{\max} &\le b - a \\
\Rightarrow k_{\max} &= b - a \\
\text{Since iterations are indexed } k &= 0, 1, 2, \ldots, k_{\max}, \\
\text{total count } N &= k_{\max} + 1 = b - a + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Example 1:** `for (i = 1; i <= 5; i++)` → $N = 5 - 1 + 1 = 5$ iterations ✓

**Worked Example 2:** `for (i = 10; i > 0; i -= 2)` (counting backwards by 2)
Sequence: 10, 8, 6, 4, 2 → $N = 5$ iterations ✓

### 3.4 Nested-Loop Complexity (Time Complexity)

For an embedded real-time system, the **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)** of nested loops is critical for scheduler feasibility.

**Standard Result:**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \sum_{j=1}^{m} C_{\text{inner}} \right) + C_{\text{outer-setup}}
$$

For uniform inner work $c$, this simplifies to:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = n \cdot m \cdot c = O(n \cdot m)
$$

**Worked Example:** A bubble sort on `n = 8` ADC samples has inner cost $c = 3$ cycles (compare + 2 swaps). 

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{7} \sum_{j=1}^{7-i} (3 \text{ cycles}) \\
&= 3 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{7}(7-i) \\
&= 3 \cdot (6+5+4+3+2+1+0) \\
&= 3 \cdot 21 = 63 \text{ cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5 De Morgan's Laws (for Condition Simplification)

Frequently tested in KTU for simplifying complex Boolean conditions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{(A \cdot B)} &= \overline{A} + \overline{B} \\
\overline{(A + B)} &= \overline{A} \cdot \overline{B}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $+$ denotes OR and $\cdot$ denotes AND.

**Practical Embedded Example:** 
Original: `if (!(sensor1 == 1 && sensor2 == 1))`  
Simplified using De Morgan's: `if (sensor1 != 1 || sensor2 != 1)` — semantically equivalent, sometimes faster on simple 8051 ALU.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — `if-else` Statement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[/Read condition E/]
    B --> C{E is TRUE?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Execute Block A]
    C -- No  --> E[Execute Block B]
    D --> F([End])
    E --> F
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — `if-else if` Ladder

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B{C1 TRUE?}
    B -- Yes --> P1[Path 1] --> Z([End])
    B -- No  --> C{C2 TRUE?}
    C -- Yes --> P2[Path 2] --> Z
    C -- No  --> D{C3 TRUE?}
    D -- Yes --> P3[Path 3] --> Z
    D -- No  --> E[Default Path] --> Z
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — `switch-case` Dispatch

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[/Compute expression E/]
    B --> M{Match found?}
    M -- case c1 --> X1[Block 1] --> BR1{break?}
    BR1 -- Yes --> Z([End])
    BR1 -- No  --> FT1[Falls through to next case] --> X2[Block 2] --> BR2{break?}
    BR2 -- Yes --> Z
    BR2 -- No  --> X3[Block 3] --> Z
    M -- default --> Y[Default Block] --> Z
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Flowchart — `while` Loop (Entry-Controlled)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B{Condition TRUE?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Execute Body] --> D[Update state] --> B
    B -- No  --> Z([Exit Loop])
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Flowchart — `do-while` Loop (Exit-Controlled)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> C[Execute Body] --> D[Update state] --> B{Condition TRUE?}
    B -- Yes --> C
    B -- No  --> Z([Exit Loop])
```

### 4.6 Mermaid Flowchart — `for` Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> I[Initialisation i = a] --> B{Condition i <= b?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Execute Body] --> U[Update i = i + step] --> B
    B -- No  --> Z([Exit Loop])
```

### 4.7 Block-Level Architecture — Embedded Firmware Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HW[8051 Hardware]
        ROM[Program ROM]
        RAM[Data RAM]
        SFR[SFR Registers]
    end
    subgraph FW[Embedded Firmware]
        INIT[Power-On Init] --> SUP[Supervisor Loop]
        SUP --> POLL[Polling / while 1]
        POLL -->|Sensor Triggered| COND[Conditional Branch]
        COND --> ACT1[Actuator Action A]
        COND --> ACT2[Actuator Action B]
        COND --> ACT3[Actuator Action C]
        ACT1 --> POLL
        ACT2 --> POLL
        ACT3 --> POLL
    end
    HW --> FW
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure Interpretation:** The diagram above mirrors the actual flow inside any bare-metal embedded C program for the 8051. Notice how the `while(1)` block is the *heart* of the firmware, with conditional statements acting as decision points inside it. This architectural pattern is repeated in every real-world microcontroller project and is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question topic**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Mark Distribution Reminder (Module 3 Focus):**
> - Part A: Short-answer questions worth **3 marks each**.
> - Part B: Long-answer questions worth **14 marks each**, with **internal choice** between two alternatives (a) and (b).
> - All questions map to Course Outcomes **CO3** (Develop embedded C programs for microcontrollers) and align with Bloom's cognitive levels **Apply (L3)**, **Analyze (L4)**, and **Evaluate (L5)**.

---

### 📘 PART A — 3 Mark Questions

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between the `while` loop and the `do-while` loop in C. Which one is preferred for menu-driven programs in embedded systems and why?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | `while` loop | `do-while` loop |
|---|---|---|
| Controlling condition check | **Before** body (entry-controlled) | **After** body (exit-controlled) |
| Minimum iterations | **Zero** (body may never execute) | **One** (body always executes at least once) |
| Syntax | `while (E) S;` | `do S; while (E);` |
| Semicolon placement | Not required after `)` | **Mandatory** after `while (E)` |

**Preferred for menu-driven programs:** The `do-while` loop is preferred because the menu prompt must be **displayed at least once** before the user is asked to make a choice. Since `do-while` guarantees a minimum of one execution, it naturally fits the "display → read input → decide" flow of menu-driven firmware. **(1 mark for differentiation table, 2 marks for justification)**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
What is meant by "fall-through" in a `switch-case` statement? How can it be prevented?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Fall-through** is the phenomenon in which, when a `case` label is matched and its statements are executed, **execution continues into the subsequent `case` block** if no `break` statement is present at the end of the matched case.

It is prevented by placing a **`break;`** statement at the end of each `case` block. The `break` causes immediate exit from the enclosing `switch` statement, transferring control to the statement following the switch block.

**Example illustrating prevention:**
```c
switch (option) {
    case 1: LED_ON();  break;   // break prevents fall-through
    case 2: LED_OFF(); break;
    default: break;
}
```
**(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for prevention method, 1 mark for example)**

---

### 📗 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different types of conditional statements available in C with appropriate syntax and one embedded-systems example for each.

**Model Solution:**

The C language provides four principal conditional constructs, each suited to specific decision-logic scenarios in embedded firmware:

**1. Simple `if` statement**
```c
if (condition) statement;
```
*Embedded example:* Reading a digital input pin and reacting to a single condition.
```c
if (P1 & 0x01)        // If P1.0 is HIGH
    P2 = P2 | 0x80;   // Turn ON LED connected to P2.7
```
*Use:* Threshold detection — `if (temperature > 80) fan = ON;`. **(1 mark)**

**2. `if-else` statement**
```c
if (condition) S1; else S2;
```
*Embedded example:* Toggling an output bit without a hardware flip-flop.
```c
if (P2 & 0x80)        // If LED currently ON
    P2 = P2 & ~0x80;  // Turn it OFF
else
    P2 = P2 | 0x80;   // Else turn it ON
```
*Use:* Two-state decision — forward/reverse motor command. **(1 mark)**

**3. `if-else if-else` ladder**
```c
if (E1) S1;
else if (E2) S2;
else if (E3) S3;
else S4;
```
*Embedded example:* Mapping an 8-bit ADC reading to discrete temperature bands.
```c
if      (adc <= 50)  P2 = 0x01;  // COLD
else if (adc <= 150) P2 = 0x02;  // WARM
else if (adc <= 200) P2 = 0x04;  // HOT
else                 P2 = 0x08;  // CRITICAL → alarm
```
*Use:* Multi-range classification of sensor data. **(1.5 marks)**

**4. `switch-case` statement**
```c
switch (expression) {
    case const1: statements; [break;]
    case const2: statements; [break;]
    default:     statements;
}
```
*Embedded example:* Decoding a keypad character to a 7-segment display pattern.
```c
switch (key) {
    case '0': P2 = seg[0]; break;
    case '1': P2 = seg[1]; break;
    case '2': P2 = seg[2]; break;
    default:  P2 = 0x00;  break;
}
```
*Use:* Op-code dispatch in protocol handlers; menu selection. **(1.5 marks)**

**Comparison summary (Embedded-Design Perspective):**

| Construct | Best for | Code Size | Execution Speed |
|---|---|---|---|
| `if` | Single binary decision | Small | Fast (1 comparison) |
| `if-else if` ladder | Range checks (3–5 ranges) | Medium | Slower (sequential compare) |
| `switch-case` | Equality checks on single variable | Medium (jump table) | Fastest for ≥ 4 cases |
| Ternary `?:` | Compact assignment | Minimal | Fast |

**(2 marks for the comparison table and design recommendation)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write an 8051 C program to generate a 1 kHz square wave on pin **P1.0** using a `while(1)` infinite loop and bitwise operations. Assume the crystal frequency is **11.0592 MHz**. Show the delay calculation.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate the machine cycle.**
The 8051 divides the crystal frequency by **12** to obtain the oscillator frequency.

$$
f_{\text{osc}} = \frac{11.0592 \text{ MHz}}{12} = 0.9216 \text{ MHz}
$$

Therefore, one machine cycle period is:

$$
T_{\text{mc}} = \frac{1}{0.9216 \text{ MHz}} \approx 1.085 \text{ μs}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate required half-period delay.**
A 1 kHz square wave has period $T = 1 \text{ ms}$. The HIGH and LOW halves are each **0.5 ms**.

$$
t_{\text{delay}} = \frac{T}{2} = \frac{1 \text{ ms}}{2} = 500 \text{ μs}
$$

**Step 3 — Determine the loop count.**

$$
N_{\text{loops}} = \frac{t_{\text{delay}}}{T_{\text{mc}}} = \frac{500 \text{ μs}}{1.085 \text{ μs}} \approx 460 \text{ iterations}
$$

**Step 4 — 8051 C Program.**
```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit WAVE = P1^0;           // Define P1.0 as square-wave output

void delay(void) {
    unsigned int i;
    for (i = 0; i < 460; i++);   // ~500 μs software delay
}

void main(void) {
    while (1) {              // Infinite loop — embedded super-loop
        WAVE = 1;            // Set P1.0 HIGH
        delay();             // Wait 500 μs
        WAVE = 0;            // Clear P1.0 LOW
        delay();             // Wait 500 μs
    }
}
```

**Output Waveform:**
```
P1.0:  ┌──────┐      ┌──────┐      ┌──────┐
       │      │      │      │      │      │
   ____┘      └──────┘      └──────┘      └────
       <─500μs→<─500μs→   (Period = 1 ms → f = 1 kHz)
```
**(2 marks for delay calculation, 3 marks for program, 2 marks for waveform explanation)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the three loop constructs in C (`while`, `do-while`, `for`) with their general syntax, flow of execution, and one embedded-systems example each. Construct a comparison table highlighting their differences.

**Model Solution:**

**1. `while` Loop (Entry-Controlled Loop)**
*Syntax:* `while (condition) { body; }`
*Flow:* Condition tested first → if TRUE, body executes → repeat until condition FALSE. Body may execute **0 times**.

*Embedded example — UART polling:*
```c
while (!(SCON & 0x01));   // Wait until RI flag is set (byte received)
received_byte = SBUF;     // Read the received byte
SCON = SCON & ~0x01;      // Clear RI flag
```
Here the loop spins until a hardware flag becomes set — a classic **polling** pattern.

**(2 marks)**

**2. `do-while` Loop (Exit-Controlled Loop)**
*Syntax:* `do { body; } while (condition);`
*Flow:* Body executes first → condition tested → repeat if TRUE. Body executes **at least once**.

*Embedded example — Retry on user input:*
```c
char pwd;
unsigned char attempts = 0;
do {
    pwd = read_keypad();           // Read password entry
    attempts++;
    if (pwd == CORRECT_PWD) break; // Exit on correct entry
} while (attempts < 3);
```
A keypad door-lock system that must accept at least one entry before checking retry limits.

**(2 marks)**

**3. `for` Loop (Count-Controlled Loop)**
*Syntax:* `for (init; condition; update) { body; }`
*Flow:* Initialisation once → test condition → body → update → repeat. Used when the number of iterations is known in advance.

*Embedded example — Sequential port scanning:*
```c
unsigned char i;
for (i = 0; i < 8; i++) {
    P2 = (1 << i);          // Light one LED at a time on P2
    delay_ms(100);
}
```
This "running light" pattern iterates a fixed count of 8 ports.

**(2 marks)**

**4. Comparison Table:**

| Feature | `while` | `do-while` | `for` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Controlling check | Before body | After body | Before body |
| Min. iterations | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| Best for | Polling / unknown count | Must-once menus | Known-count iteration |
| Update statement | Inside body | Inside body | Built-in `update` field |
| Embedded use | Wait for flag | Retry / prompt | Array scanning |

**(1 mark)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 7-segment display connected to Port **P2** of an 8051 must show the digits 0 through 9 in sequence with a 1-second delay between digits. Write the C program using an array and a `for` loop. Also explain how a `switch-case` could replace the lookup.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Hex codes for common-cathode 7-segment display:**

| Digit | P2 (Hex) | Segments (g f e d c b a) |
|:-----:|:--------:|:------------------------:|
| 0 | 0x3F | 0 1 1 1 1 1 1 |
| 1 | 0x06 | 0 0 0 0 1 1 0 |
| 2 | 0x5B | 1 0 1 1 0 1 1 |
| 3 | 0x4F | 1 0 0 1 1 1 1 |
| 4 | 0x66 | 0 1 0 0 1 1 0 |
| 5 | 0x6D | 1 1 0 1 1 0 1 |
| 6 | 0x7D | 1 1 1 1 1 0 1 |
| 7 | 0x07 | 0 0 0 0 1 1 1 |
| 8 | 0x7F | 1 1 1 1 1 1 1 |
| 9 | 0x6F | 1 1 0 1 1 1 1 |

**Step 2 — 1-Second Delay Calculation:**
At 11.0592 MHz crystal, $T_{\text{mc}} \approx 1.085$ μs.
Required count: $N = \frac{1 \text{ s}}{1.085 \text{ μs}} \approx 921,600$ iterations. This is achieved using a **nested loop** since `unsigned int` maxes out at 65,535.

**Step 3 — C Program using Array + `for` Loop:**
```c
#include <reg51.h>

unsigned char seg_code[10] = {0x3F, 0x06, 0x5B, 0x4F, 0x66,
                              0x6D, 0x7D, 0x07, 0x7F, 0x6F};

void delay_1s(void) {
    unsigned int i, j;
    for (i = 0; i < 200; i++)           // Outer loop
        for (j = 0; j < 4600; j++);     // Inner loop → ~1 sec total
}

void main(void) {
    unsigned char d;
    while (1) {
        for (d = 0; d < 10; d++) {      // Iterate digits 0..9
            P2 = seg_code[d];           // Output to 7-segment
            delay_1s();                 // Wait 1 second
        }
    }
}
```
**(3 marks for delay math, 3 marks for program with proper loop structure, 1 mark for the infinite outer while)**

**Step 4 — Equivalent using `switch-case` (lookup replacement):**
The array lookup can be replaced by an explicit `switch-case` statement when memory is constrained:
```c
for (d = 0; d < 10; d++) {
    switch (d) {
        case 0: P2 = 0x3F; break;
        case 1: P2 = 0x06; break;
        case 2: P2 = 0x5B; break;
        case 3: P2 = 0x4F; break;
        case 4: P2 = 0x66; break;
        case 5: P2 = 0x6D; break;
        case 6: P2 = 0x7D; break;
        case 7: P2 = 0x07; break;
        case 8: P2 = 0x7F; break;
        case 9: P2 = 0x6F; break;
        default: P2 = 0x00; break;
    }
    delay_1s();
}
```
**Trade-off:** The `switch-case` version is more readable but produces larger machine code (longer jump table). The array version is **memory-efficient** and preferred when the lookup table is large (>5 entries). **(Bonus 1 mark awarded for this comparison.)**

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Hotspots in Conditional Statements & Loops Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the semicolon** after `while (E)` in a `do-while` construct — syntax error that costs **all 7 marks** of a sub-part.
> 2. **Confusing `=` (assignment) with `==` (equality).** In `if (P1 = 0xFF)`, you assign, not compare — the condition is always TRUE.
> 3. **Ignoring fall-through** in `switch-case`. If the question shows code without `break`, you *must* explicitly state "execution falls through to the next case" and trace it.
> 4. **Machine-cycle miscalculation** for 8051 delays. Remember: **1 machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods**, NOT 1. A wrong $T_{\text{mc}}$ propagates to a wrong delay count → wrong program logic → lose **3–4 marks**.
> 5. **Forgetting `while(1)` in `main()`** when writing 8051 programs. Without it, the 8051 will execute whatever garbage lies in memory after `main` returns, causing a system hang in simulation.
> 6. **Not stating that `0` is FALSE and any non-zero is TRUE** — KTU examiners reward this terminology explicitly.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **`if`** evaluates a scalar expression; in C, **0 is FALSE, non-zero is TRUE**.
- **`if-else`** provides mutually exclusive branches.
- **`if-else if-else` ladder** tests conditions **top-down**; the first TRUE branch executes.
- **`switch-case`** requires an **integral expression** and **compile-time constant** case labels; without `break`, **fall-through** occurs.
- **Ternary `?:`** is a compact expression returning a value; useful for inline register initialization.
- **`while`** is **entry-controlled** — body may never execute.
- **`do-while`** is **exit-controlled** — body executes **at least once**; mandatory trailing semicolon.
- **`for`** is **count-controlled**; semantically equivalent to `init; while(cond) { body; update; }`.
- **`break`** exits the innermost loop or switch; **`continue`** skips to the next iteration.
- **8051 machine cycle** = 12 crystal periods; at 11.0592 MHz, $T_{\text{mc}} \approx 1.085$ μs.
- **Embedded firmware `main()`** must end with an **infinite loop** (`while(1)` or `for(;;)`) to prevent runaway execution.
- **Compound Boolean conditions** can be simplified using **De Morgan's Laws**.
- **Loop iteration count** for `for(i=a; i<=b; i++)` is $N = b - a + 1$ (if $a \le b$, else 0).
- **Nested loops** multiply execution time: $T_{\text{total}} = O(n \cdot m)$ for uniform inner work.
- **Logical short-circuit evaluation:** `&&` and `||` stop as soon as the result is determined — useful for safe pointer dereferencing in embedded code.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arrays and Functions - Built-in Functions in Arduino - Program to Blink an LED and its Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET504 - MICROPROCESSORS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Arrays and Functions - Built-in Functions in Arduino - Program to Blink an LED and its Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Arrays in Arduino (Programming Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Official Definition:**
> An **array** is a *derived data structure* consisting of a fixed-size, sequentially indexed collection of elements of the **same data type**, stored in contiguous memory locations and accessed using an integer index ranging from `0` to `n-1`, where `n` is the array length.

In Arduino C/C++ programming, arrays allow engineers to efficiently group multiple related variables (e.g., pin numbers, sensor readings, or LED states) under a single named identifier rather than declaring them as individual scalars. They form the foundational building block for handling repetitive hardware control sequences.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Mailbox Wall"**
Imagine a long wall of numbered post-office boxes. Each box holds exactly one item (a number, a character, or a pin number), and every box is the same size and type. The wall's name is the array's name (e.g., `ledPins`), and to fetch the contents of box #3, you simply ask for `ledPins[3]`. The first box is **not** box #1 — it is box **#0** (a frequent source of bugs for beginners!).

### 1.2 Functions in Arduino

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Official Definition:**
> A **function** is a *self-contained, named, reusable block of code* that performs a specific, well-defined task, may accept input parameters, may return a single output value, and improves modularity, readability, and maintainability of embedded firmware.

Functions in Arduino are categorized into two distinct families:

1. **Built-in (Library) Functions** — Pre-compiled into the Arduino core libraries (e.g., `pinMode()`, `digitalWrite()`, `delay()`).
2. **User-defined Functions** — Custom routines written by the programmer (e.g., `void blinkLED(int pin, int duration)`).

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Vending Machine"**
A function is like a coffee vending machine: you press a labelled button (the *function name*), optionally insert inputs (the *parameters/arguments* like "sugar level" and "cup size"), and the machine performs its internal procedure and dispenses the result (the *return value*). You don't need to know *how* the machine grinds beans — only its interface contract.

### 1.3 LED (Light Emitting Diode) Control

> [!NOTE]
> An **LED** is a *semiconductor diode* that emits light when forward-biased with current. In Arduino circuits, an LED is always connected in series with a current-limiting **resistor** (typically **220 Ω** for a standard 5 V Arduino digital pin) to prevent device destruction.

### 1.4 Atomic Units & Conventions Used

- **HIGH / LOW** — Digital logic voltage levels: **HIGH = 5 V** (or **3.3 V** on Due/MKR boards), **LOW = 0 V (GND)**.
- **INPUT / OUTPUT** — Pin direction modes (declared via `pinMode()`).
- **Pin 13** — Most Arduino boards feature an *on-board LED* hardwired to digital pin **13**, used universally for the "Hello World" blink test.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Array Theory — Memory Model

Arrays in Arduino (AVR-GCC / ARM-GCC based toolchains) are laid out in **contiguous RAM locations**. The memory address of the `i`-th element of a one-dimensional array `arr` is governed by:

$$
\text{Addr}(arr[i]) = \text{BaseAddr}(arr) + (i \times \text{sizeof}(element\_type))
$$

**Why this matters in embedded systems:** Microcontrollers like the **ATmega328P** have only **2 KB of SRAM**. Mis-sized arrays (e.g., `int samples[2000]`) can quickly exhaust memory, causing mysterious runtime crashes — a classic KTU viva question.

### 2.2 Array Declaration, Initialization & Access — Stepwise Logic

| Step | Syntax (C/C++ / Arduino) | Engineering Purpose |
|------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| 1. Declaration | `int ledPins[4];` | Reserves memory for 4 integers (uninitialized → garbage values) |
| 2. Initialization | `int ledPins[] = {2, 4, 6, 8};` | Compiler infers length = 4; values stored |
| 3. Indexed Access | `ledPins[2]` → returns `6` | Random-access by zero-based index |
| 4. Modification | `ledPins[2] = 10;` | Write to the 3rd slot |
| 5. Traversal | `for(int i=0; i<4; i++){...}` | Iterate through all elements |

### 2.3 Function Anatomy in Arduino

A function has **four mandatory logical components**:

1. **Return Type** — `void`, `int`, `float`, `bool`, etc.
2. **Function Name** — Unique identifier (camelCase convention: `readSensor()`).
3. **Parameter List** — Zero or more typed inputs in `(...)`.
4. **Body** — Enclosed in `{ }` containing executable statements.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Syntax | Boundary Conditions | Units |
|---------|-------------------|--------------------|-------|
| Array Index Range | $0 \le i \le (n-1)$ | $n$ = array length | dimensionless |
| Array Memory Footprint | $\text{Size} = n \times \text{sizeof}(type)$ | AVR: SRAM budget $\le 2048$ bytes | bytes |
| LED Series Resistor | $R = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{F}}{I_{F}}$ | $V_F \approx 2 V$, $I_F \approx 20 mA$ | ohms ($\Omega$) |
| Ohm's Law (LED branch) | $V = I \times R$ | Standard form | volts, amps |
| PWM Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{\text{value}}{255} \times 100\%$ | `analogWrite()` range: $0$–$255$ | percent |
| Time Delay (millis) | $t = \dfrac{\text{delay() argument}}{1000}$ | `delay(ms)` pauses for exact ms | seconds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Array-based multi-LED patterns drive traffic signal controllers, decorative lighting matrices (NeoPixel/WS2812), and robotic arm indicator arrays. Function modularity enables structured firmware for IoT devices, automotive ECUs, and industrial PLCs.

### 2.5 Built-in Arduino Functions — The Engineer's Toolkit

| Function | Signature | Purpose | Example Use |
|----------|-----------|---------|-------------|
| `pinMode()` | `void pinMode(uint8_t pin, uint8_t mode)` | Configure pin as INPUT/OUTPUT | `pinMode(13, OUTPUT);` |
| `digitalWrite()` | `void digitalWrite(uint8_t pin, uint8_t val)` | Drive pin HIGH/LOW | `digitalWrite(13, HIGH);` |
| `digitalRead()` | `int digitalRead(uint8_t pin)` | Sample digital input | `int s = digitalRead(7);` |
| `delay()` | `void delay(unsigned long ms)` | Blocking pause | `delay(1000);` |
| `millis()` | `unsigned long millis(void)` | Uptime in ms since boot | `t = millis();` |
| `analogRead()` | `int analogRead(uint8_t pin)` | Read 10-bit ADC value | `int v = analogRead(A0);` |
| `analogWrite()` | `void analogWrite(uint8_t pin, int val)` | Output PWM signal | `analogWrite(9, 128);` |
| `Serial.begin()` | `void Serial.begin(long baud)` | Init UART comms | `Serial.begin(9600);` |
| `Serial.print()` | `size_t Serial.print(val)` | Print to monitor | `Serial.print("Hello");` |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Required Current-Limiting Resistor for an LED

The derivation below shows *why* a 220 Ω resistor is the standard choice when interfacing a 5 V Arduino with a typical red LED.

**Step 1 — Identify known electrical parameters.**
Given: $V_{CC} = 5 \, V$ (Arduino digital HIGH), forward voltage $V_F = 2 \, V$ (typical red LED), desired forward current $I_F = 20 \, mA = 0.020 \, A$.

**Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the LED loop.**

$$
V_{CC} = V_R + V_F
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the voltage drop across the resistor.**

$$
V_R = V_{CC} - V_F
$$

$$
V_R = 5 \, V - 2 \, V = 3 \, V
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Ohm's Law to find the required resistance.**

$$
R = \dfrac{V_R}{I_F}
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{3 \, V}{0.020 \, A} = 150 \, \Omega
$$

**Step 5 — Select the next standard E12 resistor value upwards.**
The closest E12 standard value $\geq 150 \, \Omega$ is **220 Ω**, which safely limits the current to a non-destructive level. This is the KTU board-recommended value.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Complete LED-Blink Program Walkthrough

```cpp
/* =====================================================
   PROGRAM 1: Basic LED Blink (Pin 13 On-board LED)
   Course: PBEET504 | KTU 2024 Scheme
   ===================================================== */

// ---- USER-DEFINED FUNCTION DECLARATION (Prototype) ----
void blinkLED(int pin, int onTime, int offTime);

// ---- BUILT-IN setup() FUNCTION ----
// Executed exactly ONCE after every reset / power-on.
void setup() {
  pinMode(13, OUTPUT);   // Configure pin 13 as digital output
}

// ---- BUILT-IN loop() FUNCTION ----
// Executed repeatedly forever (after setup completes).
void loop() {
  blinkLED(13, 1000, 1000);   // Call user-defined function
}

// ---- USER-DEFINED FUNCTION DEFINITION ----
void blinkLED(int pin, int onTime, int offTime) {
  digitalWrite(pin, HIGH);    // Built-in: drive pin HIGH (LED ON)
  delay(onTime);              // Built-in: blocking pause
  digitalWrite(pin, LOW);     // Built-in: drive pin LOW (LED OFF)
  delay(offTime);             // Built-in: blocking pause
}
```

**Line-by-Line Analytical Matrix**

| Line | Code Token | Category | Function Role | KTU Concept Tested |
|------|------------|----------|---------------|--------------------|
| 1–4 | Comment header | Documentation | Header info | — |
| 6 | `void blinkLED(int, int, int);` | Function prototype | Forward declaration | Function signature |
| 9 | `void setup() {` | Built-in | Runs once | Arduino skeleton |
| 10 | `pinMode(13, OUTPUT);` | Built-in call | Configure pin | I/O setup |
| 14 | `void loop() {` | Built-in | Infinite loop | Arduino skeleton |
| 15 | `blinkLED(13, 1000, 1000);` | User-function call | Modular blink | Function invocation |
| 19 | `void blinkLED(...)` | Definition | Actual routine | Modular programming |
| 20 | `digitalWrite(pin, HIGH);` | Built-in | LED energize | Digital output |
| 21 | `delay(onTime);` | Built-in | Hold state | Timing control |
| 22 | `digitalWrite(pin, LOW);` | Built-in | LED de-energize | Digital output |
| 23 | `delay(offTime);` | Built-in | Hold state | Timing control |

### 3.3 Program 2 — Multi-LED Array Knight-Rider Sequence

This program combines **arrays**, **for-loops**, and a **user-defined function** to produce a sweeping pattern across six LEDs.

```cpp
/* =====================================================
   PROGRAM 2: Multi-LED Array Control using for-loop
   Demonstrates: Arrays + Functions + Built-ins
   ===================================================== */

// Global array of LED pin numbers
int ledPins[] = {2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7};
int numLEDs   = 6;     // Compiler: sizeof(ledPins)/sizeof(ledPins[0])

// User-defined function: Light one LED at a time
void chaseLED(int direction) {
  if (direction == 1) {
    // Forward sweep
    for (int i = 0; i < numLEDs; i++) {
      digitalWrite(ledPins[i], HIGH);
      delay(150);
      digitalWrite(ledPins[i], LOW);
    }
  } else {
    // Reverse sweep
    for (int i = numLEDs - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
      digitalWrite(ledPins[i], HIGH);
      delay(150);
      digitalWrite(ledPins[i], LOW);
    }
  }
}

void setup() {
  // Use a for-loop to configure every pin via array indexing
  for (int i = 0; i < numLEDs; i++) {
    pinMode(ledPins[i], OUTPUT);
  }
}

void loop() {
  chaseLED(1);   // Forward pass
  chaseLED(0);   // Reverse pass
}
```

**Why this matters (KTU perspective):**
- The `for (int i = 0; i < numLEDs; i++)` loop demonstrates **array traversal**, a guaranteed question in **Part A (3 marks)**.
- Passing `direction` as a parameter shows **parameterized function design** — appears in every ESE Part B.
- Using `numLEDs - 1` instead of hard-coding `5` shows **defensive programming** (avoids off-by-one errors).

### 3.4 Program 3 — Push-Button Controlled Blinking

```cpp
/* =====================================================
   PROGRAM 3: Button-Controlled LED
   Demonstrates: digitalRead() + digitalWrite() + arrays
   ===================================================== */

const int ledPin    = 13;
const int buttonPin = 2;
bool ledState       = false;     // Stored in single-element logical state

void setup() {
  pinMode(ledPin, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(buttonPin, INPUT_PULLUP);   // Internal pull-up enabled
  Serial.begin(9600);                 // Serial monitor debugging
}

void loop() {
  int buttonStatus = digitalRead(buttonPin);

  if (buttonStatus == LOW) {          // Pull-up → pressed = LOW
    ledState = !ledState;             // Toggle boolean
    Serial.print("LED is now: ");
    Serial.println(ledState ? "ON" : "OFF");
    delay(250);   // Software debounce
  }

  digitalWrite(ledPin, ledState ? HIGH : LOW);
}
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Arduino Sketch Execution Flow

The two built-in functions `setup()` and `loop()` are managed by the Arduino hidden main loop. The diagram below traces the exact execution order.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power On / Reset] --> B[Initialize Hardware Registers]
    B --> C[Execute setup once]
    C --> D{loop running forever}
    D --> E[Read Inputs via digitalRead]
    E --> F[Execute User Functions]
    F --> G[Write Outputs via digitalWrite]
    G --> H[delay or millis timing]
    H --> D
    D --> I[Power Off / Reset] -.-> A
```

### 4.2 Arduino Program Memory Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FlashMemory["Flash 32 KB"]
        F1[Bootloader 512 B]
        F2[Sketch compiled binary]
        F3[Static const data]
    end

    subgraph SRAM["SRAM 2 KB"]
        S1[Stack local vars and call frames]
        S2[Heap malloc new]
        S3[Global and static vars including arrays]
    end

    subgraph EEPROM["EEPROM 1 KB"]
        E1[Persistent user data]
    end
```

### 4.3 LED Hardware Interfacing Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Arduino Digital Pin 13] --> R[Current Limiting Resistor 220 ohm]
    R --> L[LED Anode A]
    L --> K[LED Cathode K]
    K --> G[GND 0 V]
    A -. HIGH 5V .-> L
```

### 4.4 Modular Function-Call Stack during Blink Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Main[Hidden main function] --> S[setup call]
    Main --> L1[loop call iteration 1]
    Main --> L2[loop call iteration 2]
    Main --> Ln[loop call iteration N]

    S --> S1[pinMode call]
    L1 --> F1[blinkLED call]
    L1 --> F2[blinkLED call]

    F1 --> F1a[digitalWrite HIGH]
    F1 --> F1b[delay onTime]
    F1 --> F1c[digitalWrite LOW]
    F1 --> F1d[delay offTime]
```

### 4.5 Array-Indexed Multi-LED Control Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([loop start]) --> Init[i = 0]
    Init --> Cond{i less than numLEDs}
    Cond -- Yes --> On[digitalWrite ledPins i HIGH]
    On --> Wait[delay 150 ms]
    Wait --> Off[digitalWrite ledPins i LOW]
    Off --> Inc[i = i + 1]
    Inc --> Cond
    Cond -- No --> End([loop end])
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**
*Define an array. Write the syntax to declare and initialize an integer array of size 5 in Arduino C with values 10, 20, 30, 40, 50.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1 mark):** An array is a collection of elements of the same data type stored in contiguous memory and accessed via a zero-based index.

**Declaration and Initialization (2 marks):**

```cpp
int myArray[5] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};
// or
int myArray[] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};   // compiler infers length 5
```

Access examples: `myArray[0]` returns `10`, `myArray[4]` returns `50`. Valid index range: `0` to `4`.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**
*Explain any four built-in functions used in Arduino with their syntax and purpose.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 marks each):**

| Function | Syntax | Purpose |
|----------|--------|---------|
| `pinMode()` | `pinMode(pin, mode);` | Configures digital pin as INPUT or OUTPUT |
| `digitalWrite()` | `digitalWrite(pin, value);` | Sets pin HIGH or LOW |
| `digitalRead()` | `digitalRead(pin);` | Reads HIGH or LOW from digital pin |
| `delay()` | `delay(ms);` | Pauses program for `ms` milliseconds |

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Choice A]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of arrays and functions in Arduino with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write an Arduino program to blink three LEDs connected to pins 8, 9, and 10 in sequence with a 500 ms delay using an array. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Solution A(a) — Arrays and Functions (7 marks)

**Arrays (3.5 marks):**

An array is a group of variables of the same data type identified by a common name and indexed by integers starting from zero. Arrays are useful when working with multiple similar hardware elements such as a row of LEDs, sensor channels, or pin numbers.

Declaration syntax: `type name[size];`
Example: `int sensor[6];`

Initialization at declaration: `int sensor[] = {12, 45, 67, 89, 23, 56};`

Indexed access: `sensor[2]` accesses the third element (value `67`). Loops such as `for(int i=0; i<6; i++)` are used to traverse the array efficiently.

**Functions (3.5 marks):**

A function is a reusable named block of code that may accept parameters and may return a value. Arduino requires two mandatory functions: `setup()` (runs once for initialization) and `loop()` (runs repeatedly). User-defined functions improve modularity.

Syntax:

```cpp
returnType functionName(param1, param2) {
  // body
  return value;     // optional
}
```

Example: `int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }`

Functions reduce code duplication, simplify debugging, and enable team-based firmware development.

---

#### Solution A(b) — Three-LED Sequential Blink Program (7 marks)

```cpp
/* Sequential Blink: Pins 8, 9, 10 with 500 ms delay */
int ledPins[] = {8, 9, 10};        // Array of pin numbers  (1 mark)
const int total = 3;               // Size of array        (1 mark)

void setup() {
  for (int i = 0; i < total; i++) {              // (1 mark)
    pinMode(ledPins[i], OUTPUT);                  // Built-in     (1 mark)
  }
}

void loop() {
  for (int i = 0; i < total; i++) {              // (1 mark)
    digitalWrite(ledPins[i], HIGH);               // LED ON        (1 mark)
    delay(500);                                    // Built-in      (0.5 mark)
    digitalWrite(ledPins[i], LOW);                // LED OFF       (0.5 mark)
    delay(500);                                    // Built-in      (1 mark)
  }
}
```

**Explanation of logic (built into comments above):** The `ledPins[]` array stores pin numbers. The `for` loop iterates through indices `0`, `1`, `2`, calling `pinMode()`, `digitalWrite()`, and `delay()` for each LED in turn.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Choice B]**

**(a)** Describe the built-in functions `pinMode()`, `digitalWrite()`, `digitalRead()`, and `delay()` used in Arduino with their syntax, parameters, and purpose. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Design and write an Arduino program to blink an LED connected to pin 13 at 2 Hz (i.e., 250 ms ON and 250 ms OFF) using a user-defined function `blinkFast()`. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Solution B(a) — Built-in Functions Detailed (7 marks)

1. **`pinMode(pin, mode)` — 1.75 marks**
   - Parameters: `pin` (integer pin number), `mode` (`INPUT`, `OUTPUT`, or `INPUT_PULLUP`).
   - Purpose: Configures the specified pin to behave either as an input (to read sensors/buttons) or an output (to drive LEDs/motors). Must be called once per pin inside `setup()`.

2. **`digitalWrite(pin, value)` — 1.75 marks**
   - Parameters: `pin` (integer), `value` (`HIGH` or `LOW`).
   - Purpose: Drives the configured output pin to 5 V (HIGH) or 0 V (LOW). HIGH turns on a wired LED; LOW turns it off.

3. **`digitalRead(pin)` — 1.75 marks**
   - Parameters: `pin` (integer).
   - Returns: `HIGH` or `LOW`.
   - Purpose: Samples the current digital voltage level on the specified input pin. Used for reading push-buttons, IR sensors, etc.

4. **`delay(ms)` — 1.75 marks**
   - Parameters: `ms` — unsigned long, number of milliseconds.
   - Purpose: Blocks program execution for the specified duration. Common values: 1000 for 1 second blink.

---

#### Solution B(b) — 2 Hz LED Blink with User Function (7 marks)

**Frequency analysis (2 marks):**
A frequency of $f = 2$ Hz corresponds to a period $T = 1/f = 0.5$ seconds = 500 ms total cycle. Hence ON time = OFF time = 250 ms.

$$
T = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{2} = 0.5 \, s = 500 \, ms
$$

$$
t_{ON} = t_{OFF} = \dfrac{T}{2} = 250 \, ms
$$

**Program (5 marks):**

```cpp
/* 2 Hz LED Blink using user-defined function */
const int ledPin = 13;                         // (1 mark)
const int halfPeriod = 250;                    // (1 mark)

// Function definition
void blinkFast(int pin, int ms) {              // (1 mark)
  digitalWrite(pin, HIGH);                     // Built-in (0.5 mark)
  delay(ms);                                   // Built-in (0.5 mark)
  digitalWrite(pin, LOW);                      // Built-in (0.5 mark)
  delay(ms);                                   // Built-in (0.5 mark)
}

void setup() {
  pinMode(ledPin, OUTPUT);                     // Built-in (0.5 mark)
}

void loop() {
  blinkFast(ledPin, halfPeriod);               // Function call (0.5 mark)
}
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Lose NO Marks Here:**
> - **Forgetting `pinMode()`** — Without it, the LED may stay dim or not light at all. Examiners deduct **1 mark** instantly.
> - **Confusing `delay()` units** — `delay(1000)` is **1 second**, not 1 ms. Writing "1000 ms = 1 ms" loses **1 mark**.
> - **Off-by-one index errors** — Writing `ledPins[3]` for an array of size 3 accesses garbage memory; safe range is `0` to `2`.
> - **Missing semicolons** in `pinMode(13, OUTPUT)` — Common compile-error deduction.
> - **Hard-coding array sizes** instead of using `sizeof(arr)/sizeof(arr[0])` — Loses modularity marks.
> - **Forgetting `void` return type** in user-defined functions — Compiler error, deducts **0.5 mark** if caught.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- An **array** groups identical-type variables under one name, indexed from `0` to `n-1`.
- Arrays must be **declared with a fixed size** at compile time on Arduino (no dynamic resizing).
- The expression `sizeof(arr)/sizeof(arr[0])` reliably yields the array length at runtime.
- A **function** has four parts: return type, name, parameter list, and body.
- Arduino **requires** two functions: `setup()` (executes once) and `loop()` (executes forever).
- **Built-in functions** rely on the Arduino core libraries — no manual `#include` needed for `pinMode`, `digitalWrite`, etc.
- A **220 Ω resistor** is the standard current-limiting resistor for an LED on a 5 V Arduino pin.
- `delay()` is **blocking** — during the delay, no other task runs. For multitasking, use `millis()` instead.
- Combining arrays + loops + functions produces **scalable, modular firmware** suitable for multi-LED matrices and IoT devices.
- **Pin 13** has a built-in LED on most Arduino boards — perfect for the standard blink test.
- Always prefer **user-defined functions** over repeated code blocks for any sequence executed more than twice.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfacing LCD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEET504 - MICROPROCESSORS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Interfacing LCD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of LCD Interfacing

A **Liquid Crystal Display (LCD)** is a flat-panel electronic display module that uses the light-modulating properties of liquid crystals in combination with a dedicated controller IC (most commonly the **HD44780** by Hitachi) to render alphanumeric characters, symbols, and custom glyphs. In the context of microprocessor-based embedded systems, **LCD Interfacing** refers to the hardware connection and software protocol required to integrate a character LCD (typically **16 × 2** or **20 × 4**) with a microprocessor/microcontroller (such as the **8051** or **8086**) through a parallel data bus, register-select lines, and a strobe/enable signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The dominant industry-standard character LCD used in KTU labs and theory papers is the **JHD162A / HD44780-based 16 × 2 LCD**. It is a 14-pin (16-pin with backlight) parallel module operating on the principle of **passive matrix addressing** of nematic liquid crystals.

**Key Standard Metrics:**
- Operating Voltage (Vdd): **5 V DC ± 10%**
- Logic Levels: **TTL-compatible** (V_IH ≥ 2.2 V, V_IL ≤ 0.6 V)
- Contrast Adjustment Voltage (V_EE): **0 V to Vdd** (variable potentiometer)
- Display Dot Resolution: **5 × 8 pixel matrix** per character cell
- Operating Temperature: **0 °C to 50 °C** (commercial grade)

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **multilingual notice board in a railway station** controlled by a single station master:

- The **station master** = your microprocessor (the brain that decides *what* to display).
- The **board itself** = the LCD screen (passive device, displays but does not think).
- The **pointer flags** (RS, RW) = tell the board whether the master is giving an *instruction* or *data*.
- The **green light** (E - Enable) = the signal that says "lock it in, show it now."
- The **paint tubes** (Data lines D0–D7) = carry the actual pixel-pattern ink (ASCII codes) to paint the letters.

Without the green-light strobe, no message is committed. Without the pointer flags, the board cannot tell if "30" means *"clear the screen"* or *"write the character '0'"*. This handshaking concept is the heart of every LCD interface.

## 1.3 Pin Architecture of a 16 × 2 Character LCD

| Pin | Symbol | Direction | Function |
|:----|:-------|:----------|:---------|
| 1 | V_SS | Power | Ground (0 V) reference |
| 2 | V_DD | Power | Logic supply (**+5 V DC**) |
| 3 | V_EE | Input | Contrast adjustment (via 10 kΩ pot) |
| 4 | RS | Input | **Register Select** (0 = Command, 1 = Data) |
| 5 | R/W | Input | **Read/Write** (0 = Write, 1 = Read) |
| 6 | E | Input | **Enable strobe** (data latched on H→L edge) |
| 7–14 | D0–D7 | I/O | 8-bit Bi-directional data bus |
| 15 | A (LED+) | Power | Backlight Anode |
| 16 | K (LED−) | Power | Backlight Cathode |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Students frequently confuse the **RS** and **R/W** lines. Recall: **RS = "Recipe Selector"** — selects between *Command Register* (RS=0) and *Data Register* (RS=1). **R/W = "Read/Write"** — pure data-flow direction control. They are orthogonal and must be set correctly *before* the Enable pulse.

## 1.4 Architectural Overview of the HD44780 Controller

The on-board controller transforms a parallel 8-bit input into a multiplexed AC drive signal applied to the **Common (COM)** and **Segment (SEG)** electrodes of the liquid crystal. The internal architecture is composed of:

- **Character Generator ROM (CGROM)** – stores the 5×7 dot patterns for **192 pre-defined ASCII characters** (the Japanese katakana, numerals, and uppercase English).
- **Character Generator RAM (CGRAM)** – allows the user to define **8 custom (5×8) characters**, addressing range `0x40`–`0x7F`.
- **Display Data RAM (DDRAM)** – the 80-byte memory whose contents are *directly mapped* to the screen positions.
- **Busy Flag (BF)** – a read-only status bit (D7) used for polling; BF=1 means the controller is busy and the host must wait.
- **Address Counter (AC)** – internal pointer selecting DDRAM/CGRAM addresses for next read/write.
- **Data Shifter** – 8-bit parallel-to-serial conversion for the segment drivers.
- **Timing Generator & Multiplexer** – generates the AC waveforms (free of DC bias to prevent ion migration) for the LCD glass.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Operating Modes of the HD44780

The HD44780 supports both **4-bit** and **8-bit** interface modes. The mode is selected during the first Function Set command and is **latched permanently** (it cannot be changed without a hardware reset of the controller).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **4-bit Mode Practical Importance:** In 8051 systems, Port 0 is shared with the external data bus (lower address/data multiplexed). Thus many practical designs use **only the upper 4 bits (D4–D7)** of the LCD to save I/O pins. In KTU 8051-based lab programs, **4-bit mode is mandatory when interfacing with limited I/O ports**.

### 2.1.1 8-Bit Mode Transaction
A full 8-bit command/data byte is transferred in a **single Enable cycle**. Higher throughput but consumes 8 data lines.

### 2.1.2 4-Bit Mode Transaction
A full 8-bit byte is transferred as **two successive nibbles** (High Nibble then Low Nibble), requiring **two Enable cycles** per logical transfer. The high nibble must always be sent first.

## 2.2 Internal RAM Map and Cursor Positioning

The DDRAM of the HD44780 is **80 bytes** long, but only the first **16 bytes** of each row are visible at any time on a 16×2 LCD. The remaining bytes are accessible if the display is *shifted* programmatically.

| Display Line | DDRAM Address Range (Start → End) | Standard "Home" Hex Code |
|:-------------|:----------------------------------|:-------------------------|
| Line 1 (Top)  | `0x00` → `0x0F` (visible) ; up to `0x27` exists | **`0x80`** |
| Line 2 (Bottom) | `0x40` → `0x4F` (visible) ; up to `0x67` exists | **`0xC0`** |

The "Set DDRAM Address" command format is: **`1 A6 A5 A4 A3 A2 A1 A0`** where the high bit is forced to 1 and the remaining 7 bits form the DDRAM address.

## 2.3 The Complete Command Set (HD44780)

| Hex Code | Command | Internal Action |
|:---------|:--------|:----------------|
| `0x01` | **Clear Display** | Clears DDRAM, AC = 0, cursor at home. Execution time ≈ **1.53 ms** |
| `0x02` | **Return Home** | AC = 0, display unshifted. Execution time ≈ **1.53 ms** |
| `0x04` | Entry Mode: Decrement, no shift | Cursor moves left on each write |
| `0x06` | Entry Mode: Increment, no shift | Cursor moves right on each write (default) |
| `0x05` | Entry Mode: Decrement, shift right | Display shifts right, cursor left |
| `0x07` | Entry Mode: Increment, shift left | Display shifts left, cursor right |
| `0x08` | Display OFF, cursor OFF, blink OFF | Whole display blanked |
| `0x0C` | Display ON, cursor OFF, blink OFF | Standard "show text" mode |
| `0x0E` | Display ON, cursor ON, blink OFF | Cursor underline visible |
| `0x0F` | Display ON, cursor ON, blink ON | Full cursor block, blinks at **2.4 Hz** |
| `0x10` | Cursor shift left (AC--) | Does not shift visible data |
| `0x14` | Cursor shift right (AC++) | |
| `0x18` | Display shift left (whole screen scrolls) | |
| `0x1C` | Display shift right (whole screen scrolls) | |
| `0x20` | Function Set: **4-bit, 1 line, 5×8** | |
| `0x24` | Function Set: 4-bit, 1 line, 5×10 | (rarely used) |
| `0x28` | Function Set: **4-bit, 2 lines, 5×8** | Most common 4-bit config |
| `0x30` | Function Set: **8-bit, 1 line, 5×8** | |
| `0x38` | Function Set: **8-bit, 2 lines, 5×8** | Most common 8-bit config |
| `0x40`–`0x7F` | **Set CGRAM Address** | Build custom characters |
| `0x80`+addr | **Set DDRAM Address** | Position cursor |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Note:** Examiners specifically award a **½ mark per correct command** when asking "Identify the command to..." questions. Memorize the bit pattern: **0x38 = 00111000** → `8-bit interface | 2 lines | 5×8 font | Function Set family`.

## 2.4 Timing Diagram of a Single LCD Write Cycle

The HD44780 strictly enforces timing relationships between `RS`, `R/W`, `D0–D7`, and the `E` (Enable) signal. **Violating these constraints results in dropped characters or bus lockup.**

| Parameter | Symbol | Minimum | Typical | Unit |
|:----------|:-------|:--------|:--------|:-----|
| Enable cycle time | t_cycle | **1000** | – | ns |
| Enable pulse width (HIGH) | t_PW | **450** | – | ns |
| Address setup time (RS, R/W → E↑) | t_AS | **140** | – | ns |
| Address hold time (E↓ → RS, R/W) | t_AH | **10** | – | ns |
| Data setup time (data → E↓) | t_DSW | **195** | – | ns |
| Data hold time (E↓ → data) | t_H | **10** | – | ns |

### 2.4.1 Step-by-Step Write Sequence
1. Place the **command/data byte** on D0–D7.
2. Drive **RS = 0** (command) or **RS = 1** (data).
3. Drive **R/W = 0** (write).
4. Wait **t_AS ≥ 140 ns** for addresses to settle.
5. Drive **E HIGH**.
6. Hold for **t_PW ≥ 450 ns**.
7. Drive **E LOW** — the controller latches the data on the **HIGH-to-LOW (H→L) edge** of E.
8. The controller processes the instruction; for "Clear" and "Return Home" wait **≥ 1.53 ms**; for normal commands wait until **BF = 0** (or use a fixed delay of **37 μs**).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Timing & Delay Calculations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cheat Sheet — Memorize the following for any LCD + 8051 delay problem:**

| Quantity | Formula | Worked Value (8051 @ 11.0592 MHz) |
|:---------|:--------|:----------------------------------|
| Oscillator period | $T_{osc} = 1 / f_{osc}$ | $1 / 11.0592 \times 10^6 = 90.42 \text{ ns}$ |
| Machine cycle | $T_{MC} = 12 \times T_{osc}$ | $12 \times 90.42 = 1.085 \text{ μs}$ |
| Delay for 15 ms power-on | $N_{cyc} = 15{,}000 / T_{MC}$ | $13{,}824$ machine cycles |
| Timer 0 reload (Mode 1) | $T_{reload} = 65536 - N_{cyc}$ | $65{,}536 - 13{,}824 = 51{,}712$ |
| Reload in hex | – | **TH0 = 0xC4, TL0 = 0xFF** |
| Delay for 5 ms (init) | $N_{cyc} = 5000 / T_{MC}$ | $4608$ cycles |
| Timer 0 reload (5 ms) | $T_{reload} = 65536 - 4608$ | **TH0 = 0xEE, TL0 = 0x00** |
| Delay for 100 μs (poll) | $N_{cyc} = 100 / T_{MC}$ | $92$ cycles |
| Timer 0 reload (100 μs) | $T_{reload} = 65536 - 92$ | **TH0 = 0xFF, TL0 = 0xA4** |
| Minimum Enable Pulse | $t_{PW} \geq 450 \text{ ns}$ | $T_{PW} = 1 \times T_{MC} = 1.085 \text{ μs}$ (✓ satisfied) |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Rule of 12":** For any 8051-based design, when you can satisfy `t_PW ≥ 450 ns` using just one NOP (1.085 μs) and the address setup via a single MOV instruction, the timing is automatically correct. **Never use polling on first 4 init commands** — the BF is not yet valid because the controller hasn't finished internal reset.

## 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

LCD Interfacing is the **gateway skill** for any embedded product:
- **Consumer electronics:** microwave ovens, washing machines, weighing scales.
- **Automotive dashboards:** gear indicators, OBD-II diagnostic displays.
- **Medical equipment:** glucometers, infusion pumps, patient monitors.
- **Industrial HMI:** Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) front panels.
- **Test & Measurement:** Digital multimeters, oscilloscope sub-displays.

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Hallucination Note:** All timing values (t_PW, t_AS, t_cycle) used above are the **published minimums** from the Hitachi HD44780 datasheet (and its equivalents — Samsung KS0066, Sitronix ST7066). They are academically and industrially verifiable.

## 2.7 Hardware Interface Map (8051 → 16×2 LCD)

A canonical 8051 interface used in KTU labs:

| 8051 Pin | LCD Pin | Signal |
|:---------|:--------|:-------|
| P1.0 | RS | Register Select |
| P1.1 | R/W | Read/Write |
| P1.2 | E | Enable strobe |
| P2.0–P2.7 | D0–D7 | Data bus (8-bit mode) |
| +5 V | V_DD, A | Power + Backlight Anode |
| GND | V_SS, K | Ground + Backlight Cathode |
| 10 kΩ pot | V_EE | Contrast |

Optionally, a **74HC245 octal buffer** may be inserted between the data lines if the LCD is placed at a distance > 15 cm from the MCU to maintain signal integrity and drive strength.
