|---|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Voltage divider for potentiometer wiper | V |
| 2 | $ADC = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^n - 1) \right\rfloor$ | ADC conversion (n-bit resolution) | steps |
| 3 | $ADC_{10bit} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{5.0} \cdot 1023 \right\rfloor$ | Arduino UNO (10-bit, Vref = 5 V) | steps |
| 4 | $\text{delay}_{ms} = \text{map}(ADC,\; 0,\; 1023,\; D_{min},\; D_{max})$ | Linear remap of ADC to delay range | ms |
| 5 | $N_{states} = 2^n$ | Number of unique counter states for n LEDs | — |
| 6 | $f_{count} = \dfrac{1000}{\text{delay}_{ms}}$ | Counting frequency | Hz |
| 7 | $T_{debounce} \approx 20\text{ ms}$ (typical) | Mechanical contact settle time | ms |
| 8 | $I_{LED} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_F}{R_{limit}}$ | LED current limiting (V_F ≈ 2 V red) | A |
| 9 | $\text{counter}_{next} = (\text{counter} + 1) \bmod 2^n$ | Modulo wrap-around | — |
| 10 | $\text{LED}_i = \text{bit}_i(\text{counter})$ | ith LED = ith bit of counter | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** When writing the voltage divider formula, students often confuse which resistor is *top* and which is *bottom*. For the Arduino pot circuit: **VCC → R_top → Wiper → R_bottom → GND**, and $V_{wiper} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_{bottom}}{R_{top} + R_{bottom}}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial counters**: pill-bottle counters on production lines (switch triggers each pass).
- **Frequency division**: a binary counter driven by a clock acts as a *divide-by-N* frequency scaler in synthesizers and timers (e.g., 555 timer cascaded with CD4040).
- **Automotive turn signals**: binary counters drive the on/off pattern of older flashing indicators.
- **DAC building block**: an R-2R ladder DAC is fundamentally a binary counter + resistor network.
- **Sequencer lighting in stage electronics**: each beat steps a binary counter, lighting LED bars in programmed patterns.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — ADC Code from Potentiometer Wiper Voltage

**Given:** A potentiometer connected as a voltage divider with **VCC = 5.0 V**. The wiper voltage varies linearly from 0 V (fully turned to GND side) to 5.0 V (fully turned to VCC side). The ATmega328P ADC is **10-bit** with reference voltage **Vref = 5.0 V**.

**Find:** The mathematical relationship between the wiper voltage $V_w$ and the digital ADC output code.

### Step 1 — Write the voltage divider expression

For an ideal potentiometer (total resistance $R_{tot} = R_{top} + R_{bottom}$), the wiper voltage is:

$$V_w = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_{bottom}}{R_{top} + R_{bottom}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the linear position ratio

Let $\alpha = \dfrac{R_{bottom}}{R_{top} + R_{bottom}}$ be the normalized shaft position, where $\alpha = 0$ (fully CCW) to $\alpha = 1$ (fully CW). Then:

$$V_w = \alpha \cdot V_{CC}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the ADC quantization formula

A unipolar n-bit ADC maps an analog input voltage to a digital code using:

$$ADC = \text{round}\!\left( \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^n - 1) \right)$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the specific case (Arduino UNO)

For $V_{in} = V_w$, $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V, $n = 10$ (so $2^{10} - 1 = 1023$):

$$ADC = \text{round}\!\left( \dfrac{\alpha \cdot 5.0}{5.0} \cdot 1023 \right)$$

### Step 5 — Simplify

$$\begin{aligned}
ADC &= \text{round}(\alpha \cdot 1023) \\
&= \text{round}(1023\,\alpha)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Boundary verification

- If $\alpha = 0$ (wiper at GND): $ADC = \text{round}(0) = 0$ ✓
- If $\alpha = 1$ (wiper at VCC): $ADC = \text{round}(1023) = 1023$ ✓
- If $\alpha = 0.5$ (wiper mid-position): $ADC = \text{round}(511.5) = 512$ ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mapping ADC Value to Counting Delay

**Given:** We want the counter to increment slowly when the pot is at GND ($\alpha = 0$) and rapidly when at VCC ($\alpha = 1$). Define:

- $D_{max}$ = maximum delay when pot is at GND (e.g., **1000 ms** = 1 second per step)
- $D_{min}$ = minimum delay when pot is at VCC (e.g., **50 ms** = fast step)

**Find:** The linear mapping function $\text{delay}(ADC)$.

### Step 1 — Define the linear interpolation form

$$\text{delay}(ADC) = D_{max} + (D_{min} - D_{max}) \cdot \dfrac{ADC - ADC_{min}}{ADC_{max} - ADC_{min}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Arduino ADC bounds ($ADC_{min} = 0$, $ADC_{max} = 1023$)

$$\text{delay}(ADC) = D_{max} + (D_{min} - D_{max}) \cdot \dfrac{ADC}{1023}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute numerical values

$$\text{delay}(ADC) = 1000 + (50 - 1000) \cdot \dfrac{ADC}{1023} = 1000 - 950 \cdot \dfrac{ADC}{1023}$$

### Step 4 — Compute boundary cases

| Pot Position | $\alpha$ | $ADC$ | $\text{delay}$ |
|--------------|----------|-------|----------------|
| Fully CCW (GND) | 0 | 0 | $\text{delay} = 1000 - 0 = 1000$ ms |
| Mid-position | 0.5 | 512 | $\text{delay} = 1000 - 950 \cdot (512/1023) \approx 525$ ms |
| Fully CW (VCC) | 1.0 | 1023 | $\text{delay} = 1000 - 950 \cdot 1.0 = 50$ ms |

### Step 5 — Arduino `map()` function equivalent

The Arduino framework internally performs exactly this interpolation:

```
delay_ms = map(analogRead(A0), 0, 1023, 1000, 50);
```

Note: Arduino `map()` uses integer arithmetic, so the result for ADC = 512 would be **525 ms** (rounded down).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Counting Frequency from Delay

Given a delay $D$ ms between increments, the period of one count cycle is exactly $D$ ms.

### Step 1 — Period to frequency conversion

$$f_{count} = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{D_{seconds}} = \dfrac{1}{D_{ms} \cdot 10^{-3}}$$

### Step 2 — Simplify to engineering-friendly form

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{count} &= \dfrac{1000}{D_{ms}} \;\;[Hz]
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Boundary verification

- $D = 1000$ ms → $f_{count} = 1000/1000 = 1$ Hz (1 count per second) ✓
- $D = 50$ ms → $f_{count} = 1000/50 = 20$ Hz ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state units explicitly. A common student error is writing `delay = 1000` without the **ms** suffix, which loses a mark in the units column.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Switch vs Potentiometer Control

| Control Element | Signal Type | MCU Peripheral | Controls What? | Direction |
|----------------|-------------|----------------|----------------|-----------|
| Push-button switch | Digital (HIGH/LOW) | GPIO Input | Counter increment (edge-triggered) | Discrete |
| SPDT toggle switch | Digital (HIGH/LOW) | GPIO Input | Up-count vs Down-count | Discrete |
| Potentiometer | Analog (0–5 V) | ADC | Counting speed (delay) | Continuous |
| Potentiometer (alt) | Analog (0–5 V) | ADC | Count threshold (modulus) | Continuous |
| Rotary encoder | Digital quadrature | External Interrupt | High-speed increment | Discrete |

---

## 3.5 Complete Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

**Problem:** An Arduino UNO controls 4 LEDs as a binary counter. A potentiometer (VCC = 5 V) sets the delay via ADC0. The delay range is 100 ms (ADC = 1023) to 2000 ms (ADC = 0). Compute:

(a) The ADC value when the wiper is at 60% of full scale.
(b) The corresponding delay in milliseconds.
(c) The expected counting frequency.
(d) List the first 5 binary LED patterns (MSB = LED3, LSB = LED0).

### (a) ADC value at 60% full scale

$$\begin{aligned}
ADC &= \text{round}(\alpha \cdot 1023) \\
&= \text{round}(0.60 \cdot 1023) \\
&= \text{round}(613.8) \\
&= 614
\end{aligned}$$

### (b) Corresponding delay

Using the derived formula with $D_{max} = 2000$, $D_{min} = 100$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{delay}(614) &= 2000 + (100 - 2000) \cdot \dfrac{614}{1023} \\
&= 2000 - 1900 \cdot (614/1023) \\
&= 2000 - 1900 \cdot 0.6002 \\
&= 2000 - 1140.3 \\
&\approx 860 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

### (c) Counting frequency

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{count} &= \dfrac{1000}{860} \\
&\approx 1.16 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}$$

### (d) First 5 binary LED patterns

| Count | Decimal | Binary (b3 b2 b1 b0) | LED3 LED2 LED1 LED0 |
|-------|---------|----------------------|---------------------|
| 0 | 0 | 0000 | OFF OFF OFF OFF |
| 1 | 1 | 0001 | OFF OFF OFF ON |
| 2 | 2 | 0010 | OFF OFF ON OFF |
| 3 | 3 | 0011 | OFF OFF ON ON |
| 4 | 4 | 0100 | OFF ON OFF OFF |
| 5 | 5 | 0101 | OFF ON OFF ON |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **functional architecture** of the binary counter system — from the physical user inputs through MCU processing to the LED output, including the closed-loop timing feedback that the potentiometer creates.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["USER INPUT LAYER"] --> A1["PUSH BUTTON SWITCH"]
    A --> A2["POTENTIOMETER"]
    A1 --> B1["GPIO DIGITAL INPUT PIN D2"]
    A2 --> B2["ANALOG INPUT PIN A0"]
    B1 --> C["MCU PROCESSING CORE ATMEGA328P"]
    B2 --> C
    C --> C1["DEBOUNCE LOGIC 20 MS FILTER"]
    C1 --> C2["EDGE DETECTION RISING FALLING"]
    C2 --> C3["COUNTER VARIABLE UNSIGNED CHAR"]
    C3 --> C4["ADC READ AND MAP TO DELAY"]
    C4 --> C5["BIT EXTRACTION BITWISE AND 0X0F"]
    B2 --> C4
    C5 --> D1["LED 0 LSB D8"]
    C5 --> D2["LED 1 BIT 1 D9"]
    C5 --> D3["LED 2 BIT 2 D10"]
    C5 --> D4["LED 3 MSB D11"]
    C4 --> E["DELAY FUNCTION DELAY MS"]
    E --> C3
    D1 --> F["VISUAL OUTPUT 4 LED BANK"]
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    D4 --> F
```

## 4.2 Firmware Flow Diagram (Execution Sequence)

The Mermaid flowchart below depicts the **algorithmic sequence** executed inside the main loop of the microcontroller. This is the exact decision tree a student would draw in the KTU exam for a 14-mark "explain the working" question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["PROGRAM START"]) --> init["INIT GPIO PINS 8 9 10 11 AS OUTPUT"]
    init --> init2["INIT D2 AS INPUT PULLUP"]
    init2 --> init3["INIT A0 AS ADC INPUT"]
    init3 --> loopstart{"MAIN LOOP ITERATION"}
    loopstart --> readsw["DIGITALREAD D2 SWITCH STATE"]
    readsw --> swpress{"SWITCH PRESSED AND PREV STATE WAS OPEN"}
    swpress -- YES --> inc["COUNTER EQUALS COUNTER PLUS 1"]
    swpress -- NO --> skipinc["SKIP INCREMENT"]
    inc --> mask["COUNTER EQUALS COUNTER AND 0X0F KEEP 4 BITS"]
    skipinc --> mask
    mask --> readadc["ANALOGREAD A0 ADC VALUE 0 TO 1023"]
    readadc --> mapdelay["DELAY EQUALS MAP ADC 0 1023 1000 50 MS"]
    mapdelay --> writebits["FOR I EQUALS 0 TO 3 WRITE BIT I OF COUNTER TO LED PIN"]
    writebits --> wait["DELAY DELAY MS"]
    wait --> loopstart
```

## 4.3 Circuit Connection Schematic (Functional Form)

The schematic below shows how each physical component is wired to the Arduino UNO. It is rendered as a labeled connectivity map because Mermaid cannot directly draw resistor symbols.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vcc["ARDUINO 5V"] --> r1["R1 10K POT TOP"]
    gnd["ARDUINO GND"] --> r2["R2 POT BOTTOM"]
    r1 --> wiper["POT WIPER MIDDLE TERMINAL"]
    r2 --> wiper
    wiper --> a0["ANALOG PIN A0"]
    vcc --> rpull["R PULLUP 10K"]
    rpull --> d2["DIGITAL PIN D2"]
    gnd --> sw1["PUSH BUTTON SWITCH"]
    sw1 --> d2
    vcc --> rlimit1["R LIMIT 220 OHM"]
    rlimit1 --> led0["LED 0 D8"]
    led0 --> gnd
    vcc --> rlimit2["R LIMIT 220 OHM"]
    rlimit2 --> led1["LED 1 D9"]
    led1 --> gnd
    vcc --> rlimit3["R LIMIT 220 OHM"]
    rlimit3 --> led2["LED 2 D10"]
    led2 --> gnd
    vcc --> rlimit4["R LIMIT 220 OHM"]
    rlimit4 --> led3["LED 3 D11"]
    led3 --> gnd
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:**
> - **Potentiometer:** three-terminal device — top leg to 5 V, bottom leg to GND, wiper (middle) to A0.
> - **Push-button:** connected between D2 and GND; the pull-up resistor keeps D2 HIGH when the button is open (active-low logic).
> - **LEDs:** anode (+) toward 5 V through current-limiting resistor; cathode (−) toward the GPIO pin. Writing LOW sinks current → LED ON. (For source-current configuration, reverse anode/cathode and write HIGH.)
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Define a binary counter. Why is it considered a fundamental building block in digital and embedded systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A binary counter is a sequential logic element that cycles through binary states (0, 1, 2, 3, …, $2^n - 1$) in response to clock or trigger events. Each transition advances the count by one (up-counter) or decrements by one (down-counter).

It is fundamental because:

1. **State machine foundation (1 mark):** Every finite-state machine in firmware relies on a counter variable for state transitions.
2. **Frequency division (1 mark):** The n-th bit of an N-bit counter toggles at $f_{clock}/2^n$, enabling precise timing circuits.
3. **Address generation (1 mark):** Memory addressing, instruction sequencing, and program-counter operation are all counter-based.

*(Word count target: 60–80 words for full marks.)*

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the role of an ADC in reading a potentiometer for controlling the speed of an LED binary counter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** transforms the continuous analog voltage at the potentiometer wiper into a discrete digital code. The block diagram flow is:

$$\text{Pot Wiper (V)} \rightarrow \text{ADC (10-bit)} \rightarrow \text{Digital Code (0–1023)} \rightarrow \text{Delay Mapping} \rightarrow \text{Counter Speed}$$

**Role (1 mark):** It bridges the analog physical world (pot shaft position) and the digital MCU world (integer delay value).

**Working (1 mark):** A reference voltage (5 V) is divided into $2^{10} = 1024$ equal quantization steps. The wiper voltage $V_w$ is sampled and assigned to the nearest step.

**Effect on counter (1 mark):** A higher ADC code → smaller delay → faster counting; a lower ADC code → larger delay → slower counting.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**Q: Design and explain a microcontroller-based binary counter system using 4 LEDs, a push-button switch, and a potentiometer. The push-button increments the counter, and the potentiometer controls the counting speed. Draw the block diagram, explain the working, and derive the mapping between the potentiometer voltage and the counting delay.**

#### (a) Block Diagram and Hardware Connections (7 marks)

**Hardware block diagram:**

```
[Push-button] ──→ [Digital Input D2]
   [Potentiometer] ──→ [Analog Input A0]
                            │
                            ▼
                  ┌─────────────────────┐
                  │   ATmega328P MCU    │
                  │  (Arduino UNO)      │
                  └─────────────────────┘
                            │
                ┌───────────┼───────────┬───────────┐
                ▼           ▼           ▼           ▼
              [LED0]      [LED1]      [LED2]      [LED3]
              (LSB)      (bit 1)     (bit 2)     (MSB)
              on D8       on D9       on D10      on D11
```

**Hardware Description (7 marks distributed as below):**

1. **Push-button circuit (2 marks):** SPDT/tactile switch connected between D2 and GND. A 10 kΩ pull-up resistor between D2 and VCC ensures the pin reads HIGH when the button is open. When pressed, pin reads LOW.

2. **Potentiometer circuit (2 marks):** Three-terminal pot. Top terminal to 5 V, bottom terminal to GND, wiper to A0. This forms a variable voltage divider: $V_{A0} = V_{CC} \cdot \alpha$, where $\alpha$ is the shaft position fraction (0 to 1).

3. **LED array circuit (2 marks):** 4 LEDs, each with a 220 Ω current-limiting resistor. Anode to 5 V (or to GPIO pin in sink configuration), cathode to GPIO pin through resistor. Each LED maps to one bit of the counter: bit 0 → LED0, bit 1 → LED1, bit 2 → LED2, bit 3 → LED3.

4. **MCU configuration (1 mark):** D2 → INPUT_PULLUP; A0 → analog input (default); D8–D11 → OUTPUT.

#### (b) Working Principle and Delay Derivation (7 marks)

**Working Principle (4 marks):**

The firmware runs an infinite `loop()` that performs the following sequence:

1. **Read switch state** via `digitalRead(D2)`. If LOW (pressed) and was HIGH in previous iteration, increment counter by 1.
2. **Mask counter** to 4 bits: `counter = counter & 0x0F`. This ensures wrap-around at 15 → 0 (modulo 16).
3. **Read potentiometer** via `analogRead(A0)`, returning an integer 0–1023.
4. **Map to delay:** `delay_ms = map(ADC, 0, 1023, 1000, 50)` — this linearly remaps 0 → 1000 ms and 1023 → 50 ms.
5. **Display bits:** Loop `i = 0 to 3`, write bit `i` of counter to LED pin `8+i`.
6. **Wait:** `delay(delay_ms)`.
7. **Repeat** from step 1.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Given pot shaft position $\alpha \in [0, 1]$:

$$ADC = \text{round}(1023 \cdot \alpha)$$

Mapping to delay range $[D_{max}, D_{min}]$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{delay}_{ms} &= D_{max} + (D_{min} - D_{max}) \cdot \dfrac{ADC}{1023} \\
&= 1000 + (50 - 1000) \cdot \dfrac{1023\alpha}{1023} \\
&= 1000 - 950\alpha
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification:**

- $\alpha = 0$ (pot at GND): delay = 1000 ms (slowest count = 1 Hz)
- $\alpha = 1$ (pot at VCC): delay = 50 ms (fastest count = 20 Hz)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> - **−1 mark** if student does not mention **debouncing** the push-button (mechanical contacts bounce 5–20 ms).
> - **−1 mark** if student forgets to mention **masking the counter** (`& 0x0F`) for 4-LED display.
> - **−1 mark** if the formula is given without **units** (ms, V, Hz).
> - **−2 marks** if the pull-up resistor on the switch is not mentioned (floating input is a fatal design flaw).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]

**Q: With reference to a microcontroller-based application, explain how an analog input (potentiometer) controls the speed of an LED binary counter. Derive the expression for counting frequency and show the binary counting sequence for a 3-bit counter.**

#### (a) Potentiometer as Speed Control — Functional Explanation (7 marks)

**Concept (2 marks):**
A potentiometer is an analog transducer — its output is a continuously variable voltage proportional to shaft position. To use this analog value inside a digital MCU, the voltage must be quantized by the **ADC** into a discrete integer code.

**Signal chain (3 marks):**

1. **Transduction:** Mechanical rotation → resistance change → voltage divider output: $V_w = V_{CC} \cdot \alpha$.
2. **Quantization:** ADC samples $V_w$ and produces $ADC = \lfloor (V_w/V_{ref}) \cdot (2^n - 1) \rfloor$.
3. **Control law:** The MCU uses the ADC code to set a software timer (delay). Higher ADC code → shorter delay → faster counter increment.

**Closed-loop HMI interpretation (2 marks):**
The user rotates the pot to "tune" the count rate in real-time without reprogramming. This demonstrates the **sensor → processor → actuator** paradigm central to embedded systems.

#### (b) Counting Frequency Derivation and 3-Bit Sequence (7 marks)

**Frequency Derivation (3 marks):**

Let $D$ = delay in milliseconds. One full counter step takes $D$ ms. Thus:

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= D_{ms} \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \\
f_{count} &= \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{D_{ms} \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{1000}{D_{ms}} \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}$$

If $D_{max} = 1000$ ms and $D_{min} = 50$ ms:

- Minimum frequency (slowest): $f_{min} = 1000/1000 = 1$ Hz
- Maximum frequency (fastest): $f_{max} = 1000/50 = 20$ Hz

**3-Bit Binary Counting Sequence (4 marks):**

| Count | Binary (b2 b1 b0) | LED2 (MSB) | LED1 | LED0 (LSB) |
|-------|-------------------|------------|------|------------|
| 0 | 000 | OFF | OFF | OFF |
| 1 | 001 | OFF | OFF | ON |
| 2 | 010 | OFF | ON | OFF |
| 3 | 011 | OFF | ON | ON |
| 4 | 100 | ON | OFF | OFF |
| 5 | 101 | ON | OFF | ON |
| 6 | 110 | ON | ON | OFF |
| 7 | 111 | ON | ON | ON |
| 8 → 0 | 000 | OFF | OFF | OFF (wrap) |

For a 3-bit counter, the number of unique states is $N_{states} = 2^3 = 8$. The MSB (LED2) toggles every 4 counts (frequency = $f_{count}/4$), the middle LED toggles every 2 counts ($f_{count}/2$), and the LSB (LED0) toggles every count ($f_{count}/1$). This is the classic **frequency division** property.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B Specific:**
> - **−1 mark** if the student fails to show the **wrap-around** at count 8 → 0.
> - **−1 mark** if the **frequency-division-by-bit-position** insight is missing.
> - **−2 marks** if the derivation jumps directly to the formula without showing the unit conversion $ms \rightarrow s$.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Yield KTU 2024 Points)**

- ⭐ A **binary counter** cycles through $2^n$ states for $n$ LEDs; each LED = one bit position.
- ⭐ The **LSB** toggles every clock; each successive bit toggles at **half** the previous rate → classic frequency division.
- ⭐ A **push-button** needs **debouncing** (~20 ms) to avoid false multi-counts.
- ⭐ An **active-low switch** uses a **pull-up resistor**; pin reads HIGH when open, LOW when pressed.
- ⭐ A **potentiometer** is wired as a **voltage divider**; wiper goes to an **ADC pin**.
- ⭐ **Arduino UNO ADC:** 10-bit, Vref = 5 V → formula $ADC = \lfloor V_{in}/5 \cdot 1023 \rfloor$.
- ⭐ **Linear mapping:** `delay = map(ADC, 0, 1023, D_max, D_min)` (note: in Arduino, $D_{max}$ is given first if mapping from low ADC).
- ⭐ **Counter masking:** always use `counter & 0x0F` for 4 LEDs to prevent hidden overflow.
- ⭐ **Counting frequency:** $f_{count} = 1000 / D_{ms}$ in Hz.
- ⭐ **LED current limiting:** $R = (V_{CC} - V_F)/I_{LED}$ — use **220 Ω** for typical 5 V red LEDs.
- ⭐ The system exercises **all 5 embedded pillars**: GPIO in/out, ADC in, timing, bitwise logic, and HMI.
- ⭐ **KTU favorite question pattern:** "Explain with block diagram how a potentiometer controls the speed of a binary counter." Memorize the formula derivation cold.
- ⭐ **Always mention units** in derivations — losing the "ms" or "Hz" costs marks.
- ⭐ **Debounce + Pull-up + Mask + Map** = the "Big Four" KTU keywords for this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfacing with Relays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEET504 - MICROPROCESSORS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Interfacing with Relays # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Relay** is an *electromagnetically operated, electrically isolated switch* that uses a small control current in one circuit to switch a much larger current or voltage in a second circuit. In the context of **Microprocessors and Embedded Systems (PBEET504)**, *Interfacing with Relays* refers to the design of the hardware and software bridge between a low-power microcontroller (such as the **Intel 8051**) and a high-power electromechanical load (such as motors, solenoids, lamps, or heaters). The relay provides two essential functions for the embedded designer: 1. **Voltage/Current Level Translation** – The **5 V / 10 mA** logic output of an 8051 port pin is scaled up to switch **230 V AC / 5–10 A** appliance loads. 2. **Galvanic Isolation** – The optocoupler or the relay's own coil-to-contact barrier prevents destructive voltage transients from the load side from reaching the sensitive silicon of the microcontroller. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** > Relays are studied under "I/O Interfacing" alongside **Stepper Motors, DC Motors, Seven-Segment Displays, and ADC/DAC**. The expected learning outcome is the ability to draw the complete interfacing circuit (microcontroller → driver → relay coil → contacts) and write the corresponding **8051 Assembly Language (ASM51) program** to energise/de-energise the relay. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Remote Controlled Light Switch" Imagine you are sitting at your study desk (this is your **8051 microcontroller**, operating at a safe 5 V logic level). Across the room is a heavy-duty industrial ceiling fan wired to the **230 V AC mains** (the *high-power load*). You cannot, and must not, stretch your fingers to flip that switch directly. Instead, you press a small, low-power button on your desk. That button sends a weak electrical signal through a thin wire to a small *electromagnetic paddle* mounted next to the fan's switch. The paddle gets magnetised, physically yanks the heavy switch closed, and the fan turns on. The moment you release the button, a small spring pulls the switch back open. The **button** is your microcontroller pin, the **thin wire** is the driver circuit, the **electromagnetic paddle** is the **relay coil**, and the **heavy switch** is the **relay's contact set**. Just as the paddle never touches the 230 V wires you flip, the 8051's delicate silicon never touches the mains. ## 1.3 Why a Microcontroller Cannot Drive a Relay Directly The 8051 port pin can source/sink a maximum of approximately **10 mA** (with some variants delivering up to **20 mA**). A typical **5 V DPDT relay coil** draws anywhere between **30 mA and 70 mA**. Connecting a relay coil directly to the port pin will: - Overload and damage the port pin's output driver transistor. - Cause the 8051's **VCC rail to sag** under the inductive kick, triggering brown-out resets in the rest of the circuit. Hence, a **driver stage** (a BJT such as the **SL100 / BC547** or a logic-level MOSFET) is mandatory. --- > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metrics Box (memorise for KTU viva):** > - Relay coil operating voltage: **5 V DC / 6 V DC / 12 V DC / 24 V DC** > - Typical coil current: **30–100 mA** > - Typical contact rating: **230 V AC, 7 A** (or **30 V DC, 10 A**) > - Pickup time: **5–15 ms** ; Dropout time: **2–10 ms** > - Isolation voltage (coil to contact): **≥ 1.5 kV RMS** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Internal Construction of an Electromechanical Relay A relay is built from four functional sub-blocks: 1. **Coil (Solenoid):** A bobbin wound with many turns of fine enameled copper wire. When current flows, it generates a magnetic flux proportional to the **Ampere-Turns (NI)**. 2. **Iron Core / Yoke:** Concentrates the flux into a low-reluctance magnetic circuit. 3. **Armature:** A movable ferrous lever attracted by the coil's magnetic field. The armature's motion is what physically opens/closes the contact set. 4. **Contacts:** Three terminals per switch pole — **COM (Common)**, **NO (Normally Open)**, and **NC (Normally Closed)**. - In the **de-energised (OFF)** state, COM is shorted to NC. - In the **energised (ON)** state, the armature flips and COM shorts to NO. ## 2.2 The Three Stages of the Interfacing Circuit For every KTU drawing, you must remember the canonical three-stage topology: **Stage 1 — Logic Source:** 8051 port pin (e.g., **P1.0**), high-impedance **5 V CMOS** output. **Stage 2 — Driver Transistor (Low-Side Switch):** An NPN BJT (e.g., **SL100** or **BC547**) in common-emitter configuration. The 8051 pin drives the **base** through a current-limiting resistor $R_B$. The relay coil sits between **VCC (+12 V or +5 V)** and the **collector**. The **emitter** is grounded. **Stage 3 — Snubber / Freewheeling Diode (1N4007):** Placed in **reverse bias** across the relay coil (cathode at VCC, anode at collector). When the BJT switches OFF, the collapsing magnetic field in the inductor generates a huge reverse voltage spike. The diode provides a safe current path and clamps the spike to roughly **−0.7 V**, protecting the transistor from **Avalanche Breakdown**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Frequently Asked Concept:** *"Why is the diode connected in reverse bias across the coil and not the other way around?"* > Because if the diode were forward-biased during normal operation, it would short-circuit the coil the moment the BJT turns ON, bypassing the relay entirely. ## 2.3 Solid-State Relays (SSR) — The Modern Alternative Although KTU questions usually focus on **Electromechanical Relays (EMR)**, a quick note on **SSRs** is a high-yield viva point. An SSR uses a **TRIAC** (for AC loads) or a **Power MOSFET** (for DC loads) optically coupled to an LED on the input side. They offer: - Silent operation (no moving parts, no contact bounce). - Switching speeds in **microseconds** vs. **milliseconds** for EMR. - Lifespan of **> 10⁹ operations** vs. **~10⁵–10⁶** for EMR. - **Drawback:** On-state voltage drop of **~1–2 V** generates heat, so a heatsink is required. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Typical Value / Unit | |---|-----------|---------|----------------------| | 1 | Ohm's Law (Coil) | $V_{coil} = I_{coil} \times R_{coil}$ | V, A, Ω | | 2 | Coil Current | $I_{coil} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_{coil}}$ | ≈ 30–70 mA | | 3 | Base Resistor (BJT) | $R_B = \dfrac{V_{OH} - V_{BE}}{I_B}$ | $\Omega$ | | 4 | Required Base Current | $I_B = \dfrac{I_{coil}}{\beta_{DC}}$ | mA | | 5 | Inductive Kick Voltage | $V_{L} = -L \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$ | Can exceed **−200 V** | | 6 | Power Dissipation (Transistor) | $P_D = V_{CE(sat)} \times I_{coil}$ | ≈ 50–100 mW | | 7 | Contact Lifetime | $N_{ops} \approx \dfrac{10^5}{I_{load}^2}$ | Operations | | 8 | Optocoupler CTR | $I_C = CTR \times I_F$ | CTR ≈ 20–600 % | > **Engineering Utility:** The relay interfacing circuit is the cornerstone of **Home Automation**, **Industrial PLCs**, **SCADA systems**, and **Automotive Body Control Modules (BCMs)**. Without this circuit, no embedded system could ever safely switch mains-powered equipment. ## 2.5 Why the Freewheeling Diode is Non-Negotiable A relay coil is an **inductor** ($L \approx 50$–$500 \text{ mH}$). Inductors obey: $$V_L = L \frac{di}{dt}$$ When the BJT abruptly cuts off the coil current, $\frac{di}{dt}$ becomes a very large *negative* number. The inductor responds by generating a massive *negative* voltage spike on the collector. Without the **1N4007** freewheeling diode, this spike (often **−100 V to −300 V**) would punch through the BJT's collector-base junction and destroy the transistor — and the reverse-EMF could travel up the 8051's port pin and latch-up the entire microcontroller. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Design Example — Driving a 12 V / 60 mA Relay from an 8051 Pin > **Problem Statement (typical KTU 2-mark sub-question):** > *"Design the relay driver interface for an 8051 system. The relay coil operates at **12 V DC** and draws **60 mA**. Choose a driver transistor with $\beta_{DC} = 100$ and compute the base resistor $R_B$ assuming the 8051 port pin sources a high-level voltage of **$V_{OH} = 4.2 \text{ V}$**."* ### Step 1 — Identify the Coil Operating Point The relay coil will be energised from the +12 V rail, not from the 8051. We must therefore use a separate power supply for the coil. $$V_{CC(coil)} = 12 \text{ V}, \quad I_{coil} = 60 \text{ mA}, \quad R_{coil} = \frac{V_{CC}}{I_{coil}} = \frac{12}{0.060} = 200 \text{ }\Omega$$ ### Step 2 — Compute the Coil's Internal Resistance The driver transistor operates in **saturation** when ON. We assume a typical saturation voltage: $$V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2 \text{ V}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute the Required Base Current To guarantee saturation (and hence full relay energisation), we overdrive the base by a forced-beta factor of **2** (a common design heuristic taught in KTU labs): $$\beta_{forced} = \frac{\beta_{DC}}{2} = \frac{100}{2} = 50$$ $$I_B = \frac{I_{coil}}{\beta_{forced}} = \frac{60 \text{ mA}}{50} = 1.2 \text{ mA}$$ ### Step 4 — Compute the Base Resistor $R_B$ The base-emitter junction in saturation drops $V_{BE(sat)} \approx 0.7 \text{ V}$. $$R_B = \frac{V_{OH} - V_{BE(sat)}}{I_B} = \frac{4.2 \text{ V} - 0.7 \text{ V}}{1.2 \text{ mA}} = \frac{3.5 \text{ V}}{1.2 \text{ mA}}$$ $$R_B \approx 2916 \text{ }\Omega \approx 2.7 \text{ k}\Omega \text{ (nearest E12 standard value)}$$ ### Step 5 — Verify the Snubber Diode Specification The freewheeling diode must: - Be reverse-biased at $V_{CC} = 12$ V → a **1N4007** (PIV = **1000 V**, $I_F$ = **1 A**) is more than adequate. - Have a reverse recovery time $t_{rr}$ much smaller than the relay's switching time. 1N4007's $t_{rr} \approx 30 \text{ }\mu s$ is acceptable for relay switching (which happens in milliseconds). ### Step 6 — Compute the Snubber Voltage Spike (Analytical) If we assume a coil inductance $L = 200 \text{ mH}$ and a current fall time of $\Delta t = 1 \text{ ms}$: $$V_{spike} = -L \frac{di}{dt} = -(200 \times 10^{-3}) \times \frac{0.060}{1 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$V_{spike} = -0.2 \times 60 = -12 \text{ V}$$ The 1N4007 clamps the collector to **$V_{CC} + 0.7 \text{ V} = 12.7 \text{ V}$** (when forward-biased by the spike), so the transistor sees a safe **−12 V** transient instead of a destructive **−200 V**. ## 3.2 8051 Assembly Language Code (for viva and KTU lab record) ```asm ORG 0000H MOV P1, #0FFH ; Configure P1 as input (relay OFF initially) MOV A, P1 ; Read the switch connected to P1.7 ANL A, #80H ; Mask all bits except P1.7 (MSB) JZ RELAY_OFF ; If switch = 0, keep relay OFF SETB P1.0 ; Else, drive P1.0 HIGH to turn relay ON SJMP END_PROG RELAY_OFF: CLR P1.0 ; Drive P1.0 LOW to de-energise relay END_PROG: SJMP END_PROG ; Infinite loop END ``` > **Note for students:** `SETB P1.0` saturates the BJT → coil energised → COM shorts to NO. `CLR P1.0` turns OFF the BJT → coil de-energised → COM returns to NC. ## 3.3 The Analytical Matrix — KTU Design Decisions | Design Decision | Alternative A | Alternative B | KTU Recommendation | |-----------------|---------------|---------------|-------------------| | Driver Device | NPN BJT (SL100) | Logic-level N-MOSFET (IRFZ44N) | **BJT** for low-current; **MOSFET** for > 1 A coils | | Switching Speed | Electromechanical | Solid-State Relay (SSR) | **EMR** for low-frequency mains; **SSR** for fast PWM | | Isolation | Relay coil itself | Optocoupler (PC817) + BJT | **Optocoupler** is preferred in industrial KTU problems | | Snubber | 1N4007 Diode | RC Snubber (100 Ω + 0.1 µF) | **Diode** for DC coils; **RC Snubber** for AC contact protection | | Load Type | Resistive (lamp) | Inductive (motor/solenoid) | Always include the diode for inductive loads | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Relay Interfacing Topology ```mermaid graph TD MCU_8051["8051 MICROCONTROLLER P1.0 PORT PIN 5V LOGIC"] RB_BASE["BASE RESISTOR RB = 2.7 KILOHM"] DRV_BJT["DRIVER TRANSISTOR SL100 NPN BJT"] FLY_DIODE["FREEWHEELING DIODE 1N4007 REVERSE BIASED"] RELAY_COIL["RELAY COIL 12V DC 60mA 200 OHM"] VCC12V["VCC SUPPLY 12V DC"] CONTACT_SET["RELAY CONTACTS COM NO NC"] AC_LOAD["AC LOAD 230V LAMP OR MOTOR"] AC_MAINS["230V AC MAINS"] MCU_8051 -- "Logic HIGH 4.2V" --> RB_BASE RB_BASE -- "IB equals 1.2 mA" --> DRV_BJT DRV_BJT -- "Collector sinks Icoil" --> RELAY_COIL RELAY_COIL --- FLY_DIODE VCC12V --- RELAY_COIL DRV_BJT -- "Emitter" --> GND_REF[("GND 0V")] RELAY_COIL -. "Magnetic Flux" .-> CONTACT_SET CONTACT_SET --- AC_LOAD AC_LOAD --- AC_MAINS ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Energise / De-energise Timing ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant CPU as 8051 CPU participant P1_0 as P1.0 Pin participant BJT as SL100 BJT participant COIL as Relay Coil participant LOAD as AC Lamp Load Note over CPU,LOAD: STATE 1 — RELAY OFF CPU->>P1_0: CLR P1.0 (Logic LOW) P1_0->>BJT: Vbe = 0V BJT-->>COIL: Transistor in CUTOFF, Icoil = 0 COIL-->>LOAD: COM connected to NC, Lamp OFF Note over CPU,LOAD: STATE 2 — RELAY ON CPU->>P1_0: SETB P1.0 (Logic HIGH) P1_0->>BJT: Vbe = 0.7V, Ib = 1.2 mA BJT->>COIL: Transistor in SATURATION, Icoil = 60 mA COIL-->>LOAD: Magnetic flux pulls armature, COM to NO, Lamp ON Note over CPU,LOAD: STATE 3 — BACK EMF PROTECTION CPU->>P1_0: CLR P1.0 (Logic LOW) P1_0->>BJT: Vbe = 0V BJT-->>COIL: Transistor CUTOFF COIL->>FLY_DIODE: Inductor kick flows through 1N4007 Note right of COIL: Vspike clamped to -12.7V
Transistor is SAFE ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Optocoupler-Isolated Variant ```mermaid graph LR MCU_8051A["8051 P1.0 LOGIC SIDE"] LED_OPTO["PC817 OPTO LED INSIDE OPTO COUPLER"] PHOTO_TX["PC817 PHOTO TRANSISTOR OUTPUT SIDE"] DRV_BJTA["DRIVER BJT SL100"] COIL_RELA["RELAY COIL 12V"] CONTACT_A["RELAY CONTACTS"] LOADA["230V LOAD"] MCU_8051A -- "5V Logic" --> LED_OPTO LED_OPTO -. "Optical Beam Light" .-> PHOTO_TX PHOTO_TX -- "Isolated Signal" --> DRV_BJTA DRV_BJTA -- "Icoil 60mA" --> COIL_RELA COIL_RELA -- "Magnetic Coupling" --> CONTACT_A CONTACT_A --> LOADA ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Notice the **double isolation barrier** — first, the optical beam in the PC817 separates the 5 V logic ground from the 12 V coil ground. Second, the relay's coil-to-contact gap separates the 12 V DC coil from the 230 V AC mains. There is **no electrical conductor** between the 8051 and the mains, making the system safe even if a contact welds shut. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. *[KTU University Exam — May 2023]* **"What is a relay? Why is a driver circuit necessary between a microcontroller and a relay coil?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - A relay is an **electromagnetically operated switch** that uses a small control current to switch a larger load current while providing **galvanic isolation** between the control and load circuits. (1 mark) - A driver circuit is necessary because the **8051 port pin can source only ~10 mA**, whereas a typical relay coil requires **30–100 mA**. (1 mark) - The driver (a BJT or MOSFET) acts as a **low-side current amplifier/switch**, allowing the weak logic signal to safely switch the high coil current. It also incorporates a **freewheeling diode** to protect against the inductive kick. (1 mark) ### Q2. *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]* **"Explain the function of the freewheeling diode connected across the relay coil."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - The relay coil is an **inductor** that stores energy in its magnetic field when energised. (1 mark) - When the driver transistor suddenly turns OFF, the collapsing magnetic field induces a **large reverse voltage** ($V_L = -L \frac{di}{dt}$), which can exceed **−200 V**. (1 mark) - The **freewheeling diode (1N4007)** is reverse-biased during normal operation but becomes forward-biased during the kick, providing a safe circulating path for the coil current and clamping the collector voltage to **$V_{CC} + 0.7 \text{ V}$**, thereby protecting the BJT from **avalanche breakdown**. (1 mark) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]* **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the **interfacing of an 8051 microcontroller with a 12 V DPDT relay** using an NPN driver transistor. Label all components and explain the role of each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design the base resistor $R_B$ for the following specifications: $V_{CC(coil)} = 12 \text{ V}$, $I_{coil} = 60 \text{ mA}$, $V_{OH} = 4.2 \text{ V}$, $V_{BE(sat)} = 0.7 \text{ V}$, $V_{CE(sat)} = 0.2 \text{ V}$, $\beta_{DC} = 100$. Assume a **forced beta of 2** for guaranteed saturation. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Circuit Description (7 marks):** - Draw the **8051 chip** with pin **P1.0** connected through the base resistor $R_B$ to the **base** of an **SL100 NPN BJT**. (1 mark) - The relay coil is connected between the **+12 V supply** and the **collector** of the BJT. The **emitter** is grounded. (1 mark) - The **freewheeling diode 1N4007** is connected in **reverse bias** across the coil (cathode to +12 V, anode to collector). (1 mark) - The **DPDT contact set** of the relay is shown switching a **230 V AC lamp** (one NO contact used). (1 mark) - **Role of $R_B$:** Limits the base current to a safe value and prevents excessive base drive. (1 mark) - **Role of BJT:** Acts as a low-side switch, sinking the coil current when the 8051 outputs logic HIGH. (1 mark) - **Role of freewheeling diode:** Provides a path for the reverse-EMF generated when the coil is de-energised, protecting the BJT. (1 mark) **(b) Design Calculation (7 marks):** Step 1 — Required base current with forced $\beta$: $$I_B = \frac{I_{coil}}{\beta_{forced}} = \frac{60 \text{ mA}}{100/2} = \frac{60 \text{ mA}}{50} = 1.2 \text{ mA}$$ Step 2 — Voltage across $R_B$: $$V_{R_B} = V_{OH} - V_{BE(sat)} = 4.2 - 0.7 = 3.5 \text{ V}$$ Step 3 — Compute $R_B$: $$R_B = \frac{V_{R_B}}{I_B} = \frac{3.5 \text{ V}}{1.2 \text{ mA}} = 2916.67 \text{ }\Omega$$ Step 4 — Round to nearest standard E12 value: $$R_B \approx 2.7 \text{ k}\Omega \text{ (or } 3.3 \text{ k}\Omega \text{ for safety margin)}$$ Step 5 — Power dissipation check: $$P_{R_B} = I_B^2 \times R_B = (1.2 \text{ mA})^2 \times 2.7 \text{ k}\Omega \approx 3.9 \text{ mW}$$ A standard **¼ W (250 mW)** resistor is more than adequate. (All 5 steps carry 1.4 marks each, totalling 7) --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]* **(a)** Explain the **interfacing of an 8051 with a relay using an optocoupler (PC817)**. Draw the circuit and justify the need for optical isolation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write an **8051 Assembly Language Program** in ASM51 to toggle a relay connected to **P1.0** every **500 ms** using **Timer 0 in Mode 1**. Assume a crystal frequency of **11.0592 MHz**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Optocoupler-Isolated Interface (7 marks):** - The 8051 pin **P1.0** drives the **LED inside the PC817** through a current-limiting resistor $R_1 = 330 \text{ }\Omega$. (1 mark) - The **phototransistor side** of the PC817 is connected between the **+12 V rail** and the base resistor $R_B$ of the SL100 BJT. (1 mark) - When P1.0 is HIGH, the LED emits infrared light, the phototransistor conducts, current flows into the BJT base, the BJT saturates, and the relay energises. (2 marks) - **Justification for optical isolation:** Provides **galvanic isolation** (typically **5 kV RMS**) between the 5 V logic ground and the 12 V/230 V power ground. This protects the 8051 from voltage transients, ground loops, and noise generated by inductive loads. (2 marks) - Note: For each KTU diagram, mark the **isolation barrier** (a dotted line) between the LED and phototransistor sides. (1 mark) **(b) 8051 Assembly Program (7 marks):** **Delay Calculation:** Machine cycle = 12 / 11.0592 MHz = 1.085 µs For 500 ms delay, Timer must count **N = 500 ms / 1.085 µs ≈ 460,800** counts. Since Timer 0 is 16-bit (Mode 1, max 65535), we need a loop of ≈ 8 iterations. Choosing 50 ms per iteration (N = 50 ms / 1.085 µs ≈ 46080 → T-count = 65536 − 46080 = 19456 = **0x4C00**). Thus, the inner 50 ms routine is called **10 times** for 500 ms total. ```asm ORG 0000H MOV TMOD, #01H ; Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit) LOOP: CPL P1.0 ; Toggle relay (complement P1.0) MOV R7, #10 ; Outer loop count = 10 × 50 ms = 500 ms DELAY_50MS: MOV TH0, #4CH ; Load high byte MOV TL0, #00H ; Load low byte SETB TR0 ; Start Timer 0 WAIT: JNB TF0, WAIT ; Poll for overflow CLR TR0 CLR TF0 DJNZ R7, DELAY_50MS SJMP LOOP ; Repeat forever END ``` **Mark Split:** Timer mode setup (1), delay calculation (2), TH0/TL0 loading (2), inner loop logic (1), outer loop logic (1). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the freewheeling diode** in the circuit diagram → **−2 marks** (it is *not* an optional component). > 2. **Reversing the diode polarity** (anode at VCC, cathode at collector) — students who confuse forward vs. reverse bias lose **−1 mark** and the examiner will mark it as a "design error". > 3. **Using the 8051 VCC (5 V) to drive a 12 V coil** through a single resistor — this is the most common *fundamental* mistake. The coil MUST be driven from a separate **12 V rail**. > 4. **Not justifying the choice of $\beta_{forced}$** in the calculation — examiners expect a one-line statement: *"A forced beta of 2 is used to guarantee BJT saturation under temperature and beta variations."* > 5. **Writing `SETB P1.0` without explaining what it does** at the hardware level — always write a one-line comment such as "SETB P1.0 → 5 V at base → BJT saturates → relay energises." --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Relay** = *Electromagnetic Switch*. Function: control high-power load with low-power signal, with **galvanic isolation**. - **Three interface stages:** Logic Source (8051) → Driver (BJT/MOSFET) → Load (Relay coil + Contacts). - **Driver transistor** is mandatory because 8051 port pin current (≈ **10 mA**) is far less than coil current (≈ **30–100 mA**). - **Freewheeling diode (1N4007)** must be **reverse-biased** across the coil — without it, the inductive kick ($V_L = -L \cdot di/dt$) destroys the BJT. - **Base resistor formula:** $R_B = (V_{OH} - V_{BE(sat)}) / I_B$ with $I_B = I_{coil} / \beta_{forced}$, and typically $\beta_{forced} = \beta_{DC} / 2$. - **Separate VCC** for the relay coil (12 V or 24 V) — **never** power the coil from the 8051's 5 V rail through a resistor. - **Contacts** come in three types per pole: **COM, NO, NC**. The armature switches COM from NC to NO when the coil is energised. - **Optocoupler (PC817 / MOC3021 for AC)** provides **galvanic isolation** (5 kV) between logic and power sides — a high-yield viva topic. - **Solid-State Relays (SSR)** use a TRIAC/MOSFET and have no moving parts; preferred for **fast switching** and **silent operation**. - **Pickup time** of an EMR is **5–15 ms**; **dropout time** is **2–10 ms** — important for time-critical applications. - **8051 ASM51 instruction** to turn the relay ON: `SETB P1.0`; to turn OFF: `CLR P1.0`. - **Always include the diode in your KTU circuit diagram** — its absence is the single most common reason for losing marks in this module. - **Examiners' favourite follow-up:** *"What happens if the freewheeling diode is removed?"* — Answer: The BJT suffers avalanche breakdown due to the inductive kick; the 8051 may latch-up; the relay contacts may weld.

topics.title: Buzzer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEET504 - MICROPROCESSORS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Buzzer # Module 3: Buzzer — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **BUZZER (KTU 2024 Definition):** A **buzzer** is an *electro-acoustic transducer* that converts an electrical signal (DC or pulsed AC) into an audible sound output, typically used in embedded systems as a non-verbal Human-Machine Interface (HMI) indicator for alerts, alarms, status signaling, and user feedback. In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – PBEET504 (Microprocessors and Embedded Systems)**, a buzzer is classified as a **digital/analog output peripheral device** that is interfaced with a Microcontroller Unit (MCU) — such as the **8051**, **ARM Cortex-M**, or **PIC** family — through a General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) pin, often driven via a current-limiting transistor (BJT/MOSFET) switching stage. ### 1.2 Standard Taxonomy of Buzzers Used in Embedded Design | Class | Sub-Type | Drive Signal | Typical Frequency | KTU Context | |-------|----------|--------------|-------------------|-------------| | **Piezo Buzzer** | Piezoelectric (Active) | DC (3 V – 12 V) | Fixed (~2 kHz – 4 kHz) | Most common in KTU labs | | **Piezo Buzzer** | Piezoelectric (Passive) | Square Wave (PWM) | Variable (1 kHz – 5 kHz) | Tone generation with timer | | **Magnetic Buzzer** | Electromagnetic (Active) | DC (1.5 V – 12 V) | Fixed (~2 kHz) | Legacy applications | | **Magnetic Buzzer** | Electromagnetic (Passive) | Square Wave | Variable (1 Hz – 2.5 kHz) | Musical tone playback | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Doorbell of the Embedded World"** > > Think of a buzzer like a **digital doorbell** in your home. Just as a doorbell converts the mechanical press of a button into an audible *ding-dong*, an embedded buzzer converts a **software-driven GPIO logic transition (0 → 1)** into an audible *beep*. The microcontroller is the "person pressing the button," the GPIO pin + transistor driver is the "wiring," and the buzzer is the "bell that actually rings." For *active buzzers*, a single logic HIGH is enough (like a self-oscillating doorbell). For *passive buzzers*, the MCU must *manually flick the switch at a precise rhythm* (like ringing a hand-bell) — this rhythm is what creates the tone's frequency. ### 1.4 Key Physical & Electrical Metrics (Highlighted) - **Operating Voltage (Vcc):** **3 V to 12 V DC** (commonly **5 V** for 8051 kits) - **Operating Current:** **10 mA to 30 mA** (typical **15 mA** at 5 V) - **Sound Pressure Level (SPL):** **85 dB to 105 dB at 10 cm** - **Resonant Frequency (f₀):** **2.0 kHz to 4.0 kHz** (human ear is most sensitive in **2 kHz – 4 kHz** range) - **Operating Temperature:** **−20 °C to +70 °C** - **Standard Color Code:** Black casing = Active; Blue/Silver casing = Passive (varies by vendor) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight — Why ~2.73 kHz is the "Golden Frequency":** > Human hearing is most sensitive at approximately **2.73 kHz** (per the **Fletcher-Munson equal-loudness contour**). This is why most KTU lab buzzers are tuned near this frequency — it produces the *loudest perceived sound* for the *least electrical power*. # Module 3: Buzzer — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Piezoelectric Buzzer — Operational Principle A **piezo buzzer** exploits the **inverse piezoelectric effect**, discovered by *Pierre and Jacques Curie* in 1880. When a voltage is applied across a piezoelectric ceramic disc (commonly *Lead Zirconate Titanate* — **PZT**), the disc mechanically deforms (bends or vibrates). This mechanical vibration is coupled to a metallic diaphragm, which displaces air molecules to produce sound waves. ### 2.1.1 Active vs Passive Piezo Buzzer — Distinction > [!IMPORTANT] > **ACTIVE BUZZER** (Self-Oscillating) > - Contains an internal **astable multivibrator** (built-in oscillator) > - Sounds when a **steady DC voltage** is applied — no PWM required > - GPIO output: `HIGH` → ON, `LOW` → OFF > - Cannot change tone/pitch > - KTU-typical use: simple ON/OFF alarms > > **PASSIVE BUZZER** (Externally Driven) > - **No** internal oscillator > - Requires an **external square wave** (PWM) of desired frequency > - GPIO toggles at the required frequency → ON when toggling, OFF when held LOW > - Pitch/tone *can* be changed by varying frequency > - KTU-typical use: melody generation, multi-tone alerts ### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Working Logic (Active Buzzer) 1. MCU sets GPIO pin to logic **HIGH (5 V)**. 2. Current flows through current-limiting resistor **R** into base of switching transistor **Q** (e.g., **2N2222** NPN BJT). 3. Transistor **Q** saturates → collector pulls low → current flows from Vcc (e.g., +5 V or +9 V) through buzzer coil/element to ground. 4. Inside the active buzzer, the internal **astable multivibrator** generates an oscillating signal at the resonant frequency of the piezo element (~2.73 kHz). 5. The piezo disc vibrates → sound is produced. 6. MCU sets GPIO pin to **LOW** → transistor OFF → buzzer silenced. ### 2.1.3 Step-by-Step Working Logic (Passive Buzzer with PWM) 1. MCU configures a **timer** in *PWM output compare mode* (e.g., **8051 Timer 0** in Mode 1). 2. Timer is loaded with a **reload value** that determines the toggle frequency. 3. The timer overflows → GPIO toggles (HIGH ↔ LOW) → a square wave of **50% duty cycle** is generated. 4. The square wave drives the piezo element directly (or through transistor). 5. The piezo vibrates **at the square wave's frequency** → produces a tone. 6. To change pitch, the **timer reload value is changed** → frequency changes. ## 2.2 Interfacing Buzzer with 8051 / ARM Microcontroller — Circuit Design ### 2.2.1 Why a Transistor Driver is Required > [!WARNING] > **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often connect the buzzer *directly* to a GPIO pin. This is **incorrect** because: > 1. A typical MCU GPIO can source/sink only **10 mA – 20 mA**. > 2. A buzzer may require **20 mA – 30 mA** continuous. > 3. Driving the buzzer directly can **damage the MCU pin** over time. > 4. **Back-EMF** from the buzzer (especially magnetic type) can destroy the GPIO. The **2N2222 NPN BJT** acts as a **low-side switch**, where the MCU GPIO drives the base through a current-limiting resistor, and the buzzer is connected between Vcc and the collector. ### 2.2.2 Base Resistor Calculation — The "Magic Formula" To switch the transistor into **saturation**, the base current (I_B) must satisfy: $$I_B \geq \frac{I_C}{\beta_{DC}}$$ Where: - **I_C** = collector current = buzzer current (e.g., **25 mA**) - **β_DC** = DC current gain of 2N2222 (typically **100**) - **I_B** = required base current For safety, **double the calculated I_B** (overdrive factor of 2): $$R_B = \frac{V_{OH} - V_{BE(sat)}}{I_B \times 2}$$ Where: - **V_OH** = MCU logic HIGH voltage (typically **5 V** for 8051, **3.3 V** for ARM) - **V_BE(sat)** = base-emitter saturation voltage (**0.7 V**) ### 2.2.3 Worked Example — Base Resistor Selection Given: V_OH = 5 V, V_BE(sat) = 0.7 V, I_C = 25 mA, β_DC = 100. **Step 1:** Calculate minimum base current. $$I_{B(min)} = \frac{I_C}{\beta_{DC}} = \frac{25 \text{ mA}}{100} = 0.25 \text{ mA}$$ **Step 2:** Apply overdrive factor of 2. $$I_{B(design)} = 2 \times 0.25 \text{ mA} = 0.5 \text{ mA}$$ **Step 3:** Calculate base resistor. $$R_B = \frac{V_{OH} - V_{BE(sat)}}{I_{B(design)}} = \frac{5 \text{ V} - 0.7 \text{ V}}{0.5 \text{ mA}} = \frac{4.3 \text{ V}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}}$$ $$R_B = 8.6 \text{ k}\Omega$$ **Step 4:** Select nearest **standard E12 value** → **R_B = 8.2 kΩ** (or **10 kΩ** for safety margin). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Notes | |---|---------------------|------------|-------|-------| | 1 | Buzzer current (active) | $I_{buzz} = 10$ to $30$ | mA | At 5 V Vcc | | 2 | Required base current | $I_B = \dfrac{I_C}{\beta_{DC}}$ | mA | Minimum | | 3 | Overdriven base current | $I_{B(design)} = 2 \times I_{B(min)}$ | mA | Design rule | | 4 | Base resistor | $R_B = \dfrac{V_{OH} - V_{BE(sat)}}{I_{B(design)}}$ | $\Omega$ | 2N2222 driver | | 5 | Tone frequency (PWM) | $f_{tone} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{2 \times N \times (256 - TH)}$ | Hz | 8051, Mode 1 | | 6 | Timer reload value | $TH = 256 - \dfrac{f_{osc}}{2 \times N \times f_{tone}}$ | decimal | N = prescaler | | 7 | Period of tone | $T_{tone} = \dfrac{1}{f_{tone}}$ | seconds | For delay loops | | 8 | SPL (inverse-square) | $SPL(d) = SPL_0 - 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right)$ | dB | d₀ = 10 cm | | 9 | Piezo resonance | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Hz | Lumped model | | 10 | Power consumed | $P_{buzz} = V_{cc} \times I_{buzz}$ | mW | At rated voltage | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** Formula 5 is the **most-tested formula** for buzzer-related programming questions. The factor of 2 in the denominator arises because the timer must **toggle twice per full cycle** (once for HIGH→LOW and once for LOW→HIGH). Confusing "toggle" with "full period" is the #1 reason students lose marks. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry Domain | Application | Why Buzzer is Used | |-----------------|-------------|--------------------| | **Automotive** | Reverse parking alerts, seatbelt reminder | Loud, attention-grabbing, low cost | | **Medical Devices** | Infusion pump alarms, ECG beeps | Fail-safe audible warning | | **Home Appliances** | Microwave "done" beep, washing machine end-tone | Simple, reliable, no display needed | | **Industrial IoT** | Machine fault alarm, conveyor jam alert | Works in noisy factory environment | | **Security Systems** | Intrusion alarm, fire alarm | SPL > 100 dB can be heard over background | | **Consumer Electronics** | Keyboard click feedback, phone notifications | Haptic-style audio feedback | # Module 3: Buzzer — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Timer Reload Value for a Desired Tone Frequency (8051 Microcontroller) ### 3.1.1 Problem Statement > A 8051 microcontroller with a **crystal oscillator of 11.0592 MHz** is used to drive a **passive piezo buzzer** through a GPIO pin toggled by **Timer 0 in Mode 1 (16-bit)**. The desired tone frequency is **f_tone = 2 kHz**. Calculate the value to be loaded into the **TH0 register** (assuming TL0 = 0x00, i.e., the timer counts from 0x0000 to the reload value). ### 3.1.2 Given Data - **f_osc = 11.0592 MHz** (standard 8051 crystal) - **f_tone = 2 kHz = 2000 Hz** (desired audible tone) - **Mode 1 (16-bit timer)**: Timer counts from TH0:TL0 up to **0xFFFF**, then overflows to 0x0000. - **One machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods** (8051 architecture). - **Timer increments by 1 every machine cycle.** ### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Compute the machine cycle frequency (f_MC).** $$\begin{aligned} f_{MC} &= \frac{f_{osc}}{12} \\ &= \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}}{12} \\ &= 921{,}600 \text{ Hz} \end{aligned}$$ *Textual Logic:* The 8051 divides the crystal frequency by 12 to produce the internal machine cycle rate, because each machine cycle is 12 clock periods long. --- **Step 2 — Compute the time period of one machine cycle (T_MC).** $$\begin{aligned} T_{MC} &= \frac{1}{f_{MC}} \\ &= \frac{1}{921{,}600} \text{ seconds} \\ &\approx 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \\ &= 1.085 \text{ μs} \end{aligned}$$ *Textual Logic:* This is the smallest time-resolution available to the timer; every increment of the timer register represents 1.085 μs of real time. --- **Step 3 — Relate tone frequency to timer overflow frequency.** Since the GPIO pin must **toggle** to generate one full cycle of the square wave, the **timer overflow rate must be twice the desired tone frequency**. $$\begin{aligned} f_{overflow} &= 2 \times f_{tone} \\ &= 2 \times 2000 \text{ Hz} \\ &= 4000 \text{ Hz} \end{aligned}$$ *Textual Logic:* Every overflow triggers a GPIO toggle. Two toggles (HIGH→LOW and LOW→HIGH) make one full square-wave cycle. Therefore, the overflow rate is double the audible tone frequency. --- **Step 4 — Compute the time period between consecutive overflows (T_overflow).** $$\begin{aligned} T_{overflow} &= \frac{1}{f_{overflow}} \\ &= \frac{1}{4000} \text{ s} \\ &= 250 \text{ μs} \end{aligned}$$ *Textual Logic:* The timer must overflow once every 250 μs so that the GPIO toggles every 250 μs, producing a 500 μs (= 1/2000 Hz) full period. --- **Step 5 — Compute the number of timer increments per overflow (N).** $$\begin{aligned} N &= \frac{T_{overflow}}{T_{MC}} \\ &= \frac{250 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}} \\ &\approx 230.4 \end{aligned}$$ *Textual Logic:* The timer needs to count approximately 230 machine cycles between reloads. Since 230.4 is not an integer, the closest valid count is **230** (which gives a slightly different actual tone — acceptable for a buzzer). --- **Step 6 — Compute the reload value (TH0:TL0 = 16-bit starting value).** In Mode 1, the timer counts **up** from the reload value to **0xFFFF (65535)**, then overflows. $$\begin{aligned} \text{Reload value} &= 65536 - N \\ &= 65536 - 230 \\ &= 65306 \end{aligned}$$ *Textual Logic:* The 16-bit timer rolls over at 65536 (2^16). To count up by N, we start at (65536 − N). --- **Step 7 — Split into TH0 and TL0 bytes.** $$\begin{aligned} \text{TH0} &= \frac{65306}{256} \text{ (integer division)} \\ &= 255 \text{ (i.e., } 0xFF \text{)} \\ \\ \text{TL0} &= 65306 \mod 256 \\ &= 65306 - (255 \times 256) \\ &= 65306 - 65280 \\ &= 26 \text{ (i.e., } 0x1A \text{)} \end{aligned}$$ *Textual Logic:* The 16-bit reload value is split into a high byte (TH0) and a low byte (TL0). The high byte goes into the TH0 register and the low byte into the TL0 register of Timer 0. --- **Step 8 — Verify the actual tone frequency produced.** $$\begin{aligned} f_{actual} &= \frac{f_{MC}}{2 \times N} \\ &= \frac{921{,}600}{2 \times 230} \\ &= \frac{921{,}600}{460} \\ &\approx 2003.5 \text{ Hz} \end{aligned}$$ *Textual Logic:* The actual tone is 2003.5 Hz, which is within 0.2% of the desired 2000 Hz — well within the audible detection threshold of the human ear. ### 3.1.4 General-Purpose Formula (Already in Cheat Sheet) The general formula derived above can be expressed as: $$\begin{aligned} N &= \frac{f_{osc}}{2 \times 12 \times f_{tone}} = \frac{f_{osc}}{24 \times f_{tone}} \\ \\ TH0 &= 256 - \left\lfloor \frac{65536 - N}{256} \right\rfloor \quad \text{(high byte)} \\ TL0 &= (65536 - N) \mod 256 \quad \text{(low byte)} \end{aligned}$$ ### 3.1.5 Summary Table — Tone Frequencies for Common Musical Notes (C4 to C5) | Note | Frequency (Hz) | Timer Count (N) | TH0 (hex) | TL0 (hex) | |------|----------------|------------------|-----------|-----------| | **C4 (Middle C)** | **261.6** | 1761 | 0xFB | 0x1F | | **D4** | **293.7** | 1568 | 0xFC | 0x66 | | **E4** | **329.6** | 1398 | 0xFD | 0x2C | | **F4** | **349.2** | 1319 | 0xFD | 0x7D | | **G4** | **392.0** | 1175 | 0xFD | 0xB7 | | **A4** | **440.0** | 1047 | 0xFE | 0x0B | | **B4** | **493.9** | 933 | 0xFE | 0x5C | | **C5** | **523.3** | 880 | 0xFE | 0x9D | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** These values are derived for **f_osc = 11.0592 MHz**, the most common 8051 crystal in KTU labs. If a different crystal is used, recalculate using the formulas above. Do **not** memorize these values blindly — derive them. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: SPL Decay with Distance ### 3.2.1 Problem Statement > A buzzer has a rated SPL of **95 dB at 10 cm**. What is the SPL at **1 m** and at **5 m** (free-field, no reflections)? ### 3.2.2 Derivation **Step 1 — Inverse-Square Law in decibels.** $$\begin{aligned} SPL(d) &= SPL(d_0) - 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) \end{aligned}$$ Where d₀ = 10 cm = 0.1 m. **Step 2 — At d = 1 m.** $$\begin{aligned} SPL(1 \text{ m}) &= 95 - 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{0.1}\right) \\ &= 95 - 20 \log_{10}(10) \\ &= 95 - 20 \times 1 \\ &= 75 \text{ dB} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — At d = 5 m.** $$\begin{aligned} SPL(5 \text{ m}) &= 95 - 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{5}{0.1}\right) \\ &= 95 - 20 \log_{10}(50) \\ &= 95 - 20 \times 1.699 \\ &= 95 - 33.98 \\ &\approx 61 \text{ dB} \end{aligned}$$ *Textual Logic:* Doubling the distance reduces SPL by 6 dB. The 1 m → 5 m span is 5× (≈ 2.32 doublings), so SPL drops by 2.32 × 6 ≈ 14 dB from 75 dB to ~61 dB. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Buzzer Type for a KTU Project | Design Constraint | Active Buzzer | Passive Buzzer | Verdict | |-------------------|---------------|----------------|---------| | Single "beep" needed | ✅ Ideal | ⚠️ Overkill | **Active** | | Multi-tone melody | ❌ Cannot vary pitch | ✅ Required | **Passive** | | No PWM hardware available | ✅ Works on plain GPIO | ❌ Needs Timer/PWM | **Active** | | Minimum code complexity | ✅ `digitalWrite(HIGH)` | ❌ Requires ISR | **Active** | | Battery-powered application | ⚠️ Higher quiescent current | ✅ Lower power if duty-cycled | **Passive** | | Cost-sensitive (₹10 – ₹30) | ✅ Cheaper | ⚠️ Slightly costlier | **Active** | | Frequency precision < 1% | ⚠️ Toleranced ±10% | ✅ Crystal-controlled | **Passive** | | KTU lab kit default | ✅ Most kits ship with active | ❌ Rare in starter kits | **Active** | # Module 3: Buzzer — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Buzzer Interfacing with 8051 Microcontroller ```mermaid flowchart LR MCU1["8051 MICROCONTROLLER
PORT 1.0 GPIO"] R1["BASE RESISTOR RB
8.2 KOHM"] Q1["NPN TRANSISTOR 2N2222
LOW SIDE SWITCH"] BZ1["BUZZER MODULE
PIEZO ELEMENT"] OSC["INTERNAL ASTABLE
MULTIVIBRATOR"] VCC["VCC PLUS 5V DC"] GND["GROUND 0V"] SPK["AUDIBLE SOUND
OUTPUT"] CPU["CPU EXECUTES
SETB P1.0"] CPU --> MCU1 VCC --> R1 R1 --> MCU1 MCU1 -->|GPIO LOGIC HIGH| Q1 Q1 -->|COLLECTOR LOW| BZ1 BZ1 --> OSC OSC -->|OSCILLATES AT 2.73 KHZ| SPK BZ1 --> GND VCC -->|FEED SUPPLY| BZ1 Q1 -->|EMITTER| GND ``` **Reading Guide:** - The **CPU** executes the instruction `SETB P1.0`, which sets **Port 1, Pin 0** of the 8051 to logic HIGH. - The base resistor **R_B (8.2 kΩ)** limits the base current to a safe value (~0.5 mA). - The **2N2222 NPN transistor** acts as a switch in *saturation mode*, connecting the buzzer to ground when activated. - The **internal astable multivibrator** (inside the active buzzer) generates the ~2.73 kHz oscillation. - The piezo element vibrates at this frequency, producing **audible sound**. ## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Program Flow for Buzzer Beep Generation ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant CPU as 8051 CPU participant T0 as TIMER 0 participant GPIO as PORT 1.0 participant Q1 as 2N2222 BJT participant BZ as BUZZER CPU->>T0: INITIALIZE TIMER 0 MODE 1 CPU->>T0: LOAD TH0 AND TL0 WITH RELOAD VALUE CPU->>T0: START TIMER TR0 EQUALS 1 loop EVERY OVERFLOW INTERRUPT T0-->>CPU: TIMER OVERFLOW FLAG TF0 CPU->>GPIO: TOGGLE P1.0 GPIO->>Q1: BASE CURRENT ON OR OFF Q1->>BZ: CONNECT OR DISCONNECT TO GROUND BZ-->>BZ: PRODUCE OR SILENCE TONE end CPU->>T0: STOP TIMER TR0 EQUALS 0 CPU->>GPIO: CLEAR P1.0 LOGIC LOW ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Architecture: Complete Embedded Buzzer Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph POWER_DOMAIN["POWER SUPPLY DOMAIN"] PSU["PLUS 5V REGULATED
FROM 7805 IC"] GNDREF["GROUND REFERENCE"] end subgraph CONTROL_DOMAIN["MICROCONTROLLER DOMAIN"] ALU["CPU AND ALU"] TMR["TIMER SUBSYSTEM
16 BIT MODE 1"] GPIOA["GPIO PORT 1"] MEM["CODE MEMORY
FIRMWARE"] end subgraph DRIVER_DOMAIN["TRANSISTOR DRIVER DOMAIN"] RB["RB 8.2 KOHM"] SW["2N2222 NPN
SATURATION SWITCH"] end subgraph ACTUATOR_DOMAIN["BUZZER ACTUATOR DOMAIN"] ASTBL["ASTABLE
MULTIVIBRATOR"] PIEZO["PIEZO CERAMIC DISC
PZT ELEMENT"] DIAPH["METALLIC DIAPHRAGM"] AIR["ACOUSTIC WAVES
IN AIR"] end PSU --> SW SW --> ASTBL ASTBL --> PIEZO PIEZO --> DIAPH DIAPH --> AIR GNDREF --> SW GNDREF --> PIEZO MEM --> ALU ALU --> TMR TMR --> GPIOA GPIOA --> RB RB --> SW ``` **Architectural Reading Guide:** The system is partitioned into **four functional domains**: 1. **POWER DOMAIN:** Provides regulated +5 V via **7805 linear regulator** (input 7 V – 12 V DC). 2. **CONTROL DOMAIN:** The 8051 MCU runs firmware stored in code ROM, which configures Timer 0 to toggle GPIO P1.0 at the desired rate. 3. **DRIVER DOMAIN:** The BJT switches the higher-current buzzer load using a low-current GPIO signal. 4. **ACTUATOR DOMAIN:** The active buzzer contains an internal astable oscillator and a PZT piezo disc that mechanically vibrates a diaphragm to produce sound. ## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram: Buzzer Operational States ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> IDLE IDLE --> ACTIVE: GPIO SET HIGH IDLE --> SILENT: GPIO REMAINS LOW ACTIVE --> SILENT: GPIO CLEARED LOW SILENT --> ACTIVE: GPIO SET HIGH AGAIN ACTIVE --> FAULT: OVERCURRENT DETECTED FAULT --> IDLE: SYSTEM RESET note right of ACTIVE: PRODUCES 2.73 KHZ TONE note right of SILENT: NO SOUND OUTPUT note right of FAULT: TRANSISTOR OR MCU SHUTDOWN ``` # Module 3: Buzzer — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Part A > **Differentiate between an active buzzer and a passive buzzer. Which one is suitable for generating multiple musical tones, and why?** **Model Answer:** An **active buzzer** contains an internal **astable multivibrator** and produces sound when a **steady DC voltage** is applied; it cannot vary its tone. A **passive buzzer** has **no internal oscillator** and requires an **external square wave (PWM)** of the desired frequency to produce sound. **A passive buzzer is suitable for generating multiple musical tones** because its output frequency equals the frequency of the input square wave, which the microcontroller can vary in real-time using timer reload values, allowing different musical notes (C4, D4, E4, etc.) to be played sequentially. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — Part A > **Why is a transistor driver (e.g., 2N2222) required between an 8051 GPIO pin and a buzzer, instead of connecting the buzzer directly?** **Model Answer:** A transistor driver is required for the following reasons: 1. **Current Limitation:** An 8051 GPIO pin can source/sink only ~10–15 mA, while a buzzer typically requires 20–30 mA. A transistor (2N2222) can switch the higher current safely. 2. **Protection from Back-EMF:** Magnetic buzzers generate voltage spikes when switched off; the transistor isolates the MCU from these transients. 3. **Voltage Level Shifting:** The buzzer may operate at 9 V or 12 V, which exceeds the MCU's 5 V GPIO rating. The transistor allows a low-voltage control signal to switch a higher-voltage load. 4. **Reduced Power Dissipation on MCU:** Driving a high-current load directly would cause excessive heating in the MCU's output driver transistors. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] > **With a neat circuit diagram, explain the interfacing of a piezoelectric buzzer with the 8051 microcontroller. Write an 8051 assembly program to generate a 2 kHz tone on the buzzer using Timer 0 in Mode 1. Assume crystal frequency = 11.0592 MHz.** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Circuit Diagram & Explanation **Circuit Components:** 1. **8051 Microcontroller** (e.g., AT89C51) 2. **Piezo Buzzer** (Active type, 5 V, 25 mA) 3. **2N2222 NPN Transistor** (low-side switch) 4. **Base Resistor R_B = 8.2 kΩ** (connected between P1.0 and transistor base) 5. **Free-wheeling diode 1N4007** (across buzzer, cathode to Vcc) — for magnetic buzzers only 6. **Pull-up resistor 10 kΩ** on P1.0 (optional, for noise immunity) 7. **Crystal 11.0592 MHz** with two 33 pF loading capacitors 8. **Reset circuit:** 10 µF electrolytic + 10 kΩ resistor + manual push-button **Connection Description:** - **P1.0** of 8051 → **R_B (8.2 kΩ)** → **Base** of 2N2222. - **Collector** of 2N2222 → **Negative terminal of buzzer**. - **Positive terminal of buzzer** → **+5 V Vcc**. - **Emitter** of 2N2222 → **GND**. - The transistor acts as a **low-side switch**: when P1.0 is HIGH, the transistor saturates and the buzzer is connected to GND → current flows → sound produced. When P1.0 is LOW, transistor OFF → no current → silence. **Working Principle:** The 8051 CPU executes the `SETB P1.0` instruction, which sets the P1.0 output latch to logic HIGH. The voltage at P1.0 (~5 V) drives current through R_B into the base of the 2N2222 transistor, turning it ON (saturation region). This creates a low-resistance path from the collector to the emitter, completing the circuit for the buzzer connected between Vcc and the collector. The active buzzer's internal oscillator (typically ~2.73 kHz) drives its piezo element, producing sound. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: 8051 Assembly Program for 2 kHz Tone **Pre-computed Reload Values (from Section 3.1.7):** - **N = 230** (timer increments per overflow) - **Reload value = 65536 − 230 = 65306 = 0xFF1A** - **TH0 = 0xFF, TL0 = 0x1A** ```assembly ; Program: Generate 2 kHz tone on Buzzer via P1.0 using Timer 0 Mode 1 ; Crystal: 11.0592 MHz ; Tone frequency: 2 kHz ORG 0000H LJMP MAIN ORG 000BH ; Timer 0 ISR vector address CPL P1.0 ; Toggle P1.0 (buzzer ON/OFF) MOV TH0, #0FFH ; Reload high byte MOV TL0, #01AH ; Reload low byte RETI ORG 0030H MAIN: MOV TMOD, #01H ; Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit) MOV TH0, #0FFH ; Initial high byte MOV TL0, #01AH ; Initial low byte SETB ET0 ; Enable Timer 0 interrupt SETB EA ; Enable global interrupt SETB TR0 ; Start Timer 0 HERE: SJMP HERE ; Infinite loop (tone continues) END ``` **Program Explanation:** | Line | Purpose | |------|---------| | `LJMP MAIN` | Jump to main program, skipping the ISR | | `ORG 000BH` | ISR entry point (Timer 0 overflow vector) | | `CPL P1.0` | Complement P1.0 — generates the square wave | | `MOV TH0, #0FFH` | Reload high byte for next overflow | | `MOV TL0, #01AH` | Reload low byte | | `MOV TMOD, #01H` | Configure Timer 0 in 16-bit Mode 1 | | `SETB ET0 / SETB EA` | Enable Timer 0 and global interrupts | | `SETB TR0` | Start the timer counting | | `SJMP HERE` | Main loop — does nothing; tone is generated by ISR | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Forgetting to **reload TH0 and TL0 inside the ISR**. In Mode 1 (16-bit), the timer auto-resets to 0x0000 on overflow — if not reloaded, the next overflow will take 65536 counts (not 230), producing a very low ~7 Hz "click" instead of 2 kHz tone. > > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** Confusing `CPL P1.0` (complement/toggle) with `SETB P1.0` (set HIGH). Toggle is essential for a square wave; SETB would keep the pin HIGH continuously. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam – May 2023] > **Explain the construction and working principle of a piezoelectric buzzer. With a neat diagram, describe its interfacing with an ARM Cortex-M0 microcontroller. Calculate the base resistor value for a 2N2222 driver if V_OH = 3.3 V, I_C = 25 mA, and β_DC = 100.** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Construction & Working Principle of Piezo Buzzer **Construction:** A piezoelectric buzzer consists of the following layers (from top to bottom): 1. **Plastic Casing (Resonator Chamber):** Provides mechanical rigidity and acoustic coupling. 2. **Metallic Diaphragm:** A thin brass or stainless-steel disc that vibrates to displace air. 3. **Piezo Ceramic Disc (PZT — Lead Zirconate Titanate):** The active element, sandwiched between two silver electrode layers. 4. **Oscillator Circuit Board (Active type only):** Contains a CMOS astable multivibrator (typically using a 555 timer IC or transistor-based multivibrator) tuned to the resonant frequency of the piezo disc. 5. **Lead Wires / Pin Terminals:** + (positive) and − (negative) for external DC power. 6. **Sound Hole:** Small aperture on top of the casing that allows sound to escape. **Working Principle — Step-by-Step:** 1. When a **DC voltage** is applied to the buzzer terminals (active type), the internal **astable multivibrator** generates an oscillating square wave at a frequency matching the **mechanical resonance** of the piezo disc (typically 2.0 kHz to 4.0 kHz). 2. The oscillating voltage is applied across the **piezo ceramic disc**, which exhibits the **inverse piezoelectric effect** — it mechanically deforms (bends) in proportion to the applied voltage. 3. The piezo disc is mechanically bonded to the **metallic diaphragm**. As the disc bends, it forces the diaphragm to vibrate at the same frequency. 4. The vibrating diaphragm **compresses and rarefies** the surrounding air molecules, creating **longitudinal pressure waves** — i.e., sound. 5. The **resonance** between the electrical drive frequency and the mechanical natural frequency of the diaphragm produces a **loud, efficient tone** (most energy is converted to sound rather than heat). **Mathematical Resonance Condition:** $$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_{eq} C_{eq}}}$$ Where **L_eq** and **C_eq** are the equivalent electrical inductance and capacitance of the piezo element at resonance. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: ARM Cortex-M0 Interfacing + Base Resistor Calculation **ARM Cortex-M0 Interfacing Diagram (in text form):** - **ARM Cortex-M0 GPIO Pin (e.g., P0.4):** Output pin configured as push-pull. - **Base Resistor R_B:** Connected between GPIO pin and 2N2222 base. - **2N2222 NPN Transistor:** Emitter to GND, Collector to buzzer negative terminal. - **Buzzer Positive Terminal:** Connected to +5 V (external supply, since ARM may run at 3.3 V logic but buzzer may need 5 V). - **Flyback Diode 1N4007:** Across buzzer (cathode to +5 V) — for magnetic buzzers, to suppress back-EMF. **Key Differences from 8051 Interfacing:** | Aspect | 8051 (5 V) | ARM Cortex-M0 (3.3 V) | |--------|-----------|----------------------| | GPIO HIGH voltage | **5.0 V** | **3.3 V** | | GPIO source current | ~10–15 mA | ~4–8 mA | | Logic level to 2N2222 | Direct (V_OH > V_BE) | Direct (3.3 V > 0.7 V) | | R_B calculation formula | Same | **Same, but V_OH = 3.3 V** | **Base Resistor Calculation:** **Given:** - V_OH = 3.3 V - V_BE(sat) = 0.7 V - I_C = 25 mA - β_DC = 100 **Step 1 — Minimum base current:** $$I_{B(min)} = \frac{I_C}{\beta_{DC}} = \frac{25 \text{ mA}}{100} = 0.25 \text{ mA}$$ **Step 2 — Overdriven base current (factor of 2):** $$I_{B(design)} = 2 \times 0.25 \text{ mA} = 0.5 \text{ mA}$$ **Step 3 — Base resistor:** $$\begin{aligned} R_B &= \frac{V_{OH} - V_{BE(sat)}}{I_{B(design)}} \\ &= \frac{3.3 \text{ V} - 0.7 \text{ V}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}} \\ &= \frac{2.6 \text{ V}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}} \\ &= 5200 \text{ }\Omega \\ &= 5.2 \text{ k}\Omega \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Standard E12 nearest value:** **R_B = 5.6 kΩ** (or 4.7 kΩ for tighter saturation). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3:** Many students use **V_OH = 5 V** for ARM calculations. This is **wrong** for Cortex-M0, which operates at **3.3 V logic**. Using 5 V gives R_B = 8.6 kΩ, which is **not valid** for ARM. Always check the V_OH based on the MCU family stated in the question. > > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #4:** Forgetting the **overdrive factor of 2**. Using I_B(min) directly gives R_B = 10.4 kΩ, which barely saturates the transistor and results in V_CE(sat) > 0.4 V, reducing buzzer loudness by ~20%. Examiners specifically look for the overdrive factor. --- ## 5.3 KTU Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CARD — BUZZER (MODULE 3)** - ✅ A **buzzer** is an **electro-acoustic transducer** that converts electrical energy into audible sound. - ✅ Two main types: **Active** (built-in oscillator, DC drive) and **Passive** (requires external square wave, can vary pitch). - ✅ Active buzzers need only a **logic HIGH** on GPIO; passive buzzers need a **PWM/square wave**. - ✅ Active buzzers are used for **simple ON/OFF alerts**; passive buzzers for **multi-tone melodies**. - ✅ A **transistor driver (2N2222 NPN)** is **mandatory** — never connect a buzzer directly to an MCU GPIO. - ✅ The driver circuit is a **low-side switch**: load between Vcc and collector, emitter to GND. - ✅ Base resistor formula: $R_B = (V_{OH} - V_{BE(sat)}) / I_{B(design)}$ with **2× overdrive** on I_B. - ✅ **8051 logic HIGH = 5 V; ARM Cortex-M0 logic HIGH = 3.3 V** — affects R_B calculation. - ✅ Tone frequency is generated by **toggling a GPIO pin at twice the desired audio frequency** (toggle = ½ cycle). - ✅ 8051 timer formula: $N = f_{osc} / (24 \times f_{tone})$;
