## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of the **ADC0808**.

**Model Answer:**

The ADC0808 is an 8-bit CMOS successive approximation Analog-to-Digital Converter. It consists of three major blocks:

1. **8-channel analog multiplexer** — Selects one of 8 input channels (IN0–IN7) using three address lines ADDA, ADDB, ADDC.
2. **8-bit SAR (Successive Approximation Register)** — Performs binary search on the input voltage using an internal R-2R ladder and comparator. Total conversion time is about **100 μs** at 640 kHz clock.
3. **Tri-state output latch** — Holds the 8-bit result on D0–D7 when OE (Output Enable) is HIGH.

**Working:** Channel address is latched by ALE, START initiates conversion, EOC goes LOW when conversion is complete, and OE HIGH presents the digital code on the data bus.

> **Marks Allocation:** Block diagram = 1.5 marks, working = 1 mark, key signals = 0.5 marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define **PWM** and write its applications in embedded systems.

**Model Answer:**

**Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** is a technique in which the width of a digital pulse is varied to control the average power delivered to a load, while the frequency remains constant.

**Key Parameter:** Duty Cycle $D = \frac{T_{on}}{T} \times 100\%$

**Applications:**
1. **DC Motor Speed Control** — varying average voltage to motor terminals.
2. **LED Dimming** — varying perceived brightness through duty cycle.
3. **Servo Motor Control** — 50 Hz PWM with 1–2 ms pulse for 0–180° rotation.
4. **Switch-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS)** — high-efficiency voltage regulation.
5. **Audio Generation** — Class-D amplifiers.
6. **Communication** — IR remote control encoding.

> **Marks Allocation:** Definition + formula = 1 mark, applications = 2 marks (any 4).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **interfacing of DAC0808 with an 8051 microcontroller** with a neat diagram. Derive the output voltage expression for a digital input of 10000000₂.

**(b)** [7 Marks] What is **PWM**? Explain how PWM is generated using the **Arduino UNO's Timer/Counter** to control the brightness of an LED. Include the relevant register configurations.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (a):

**DAC0808 Interfacing with 8051:**

The DAC0808 is an 8-bit current-output DAC. To produce a voltage, it must be cascaded with an op-amp (LF351) configured as a current-to-voltage converter.

**Interfacing Steps:**
- Connect the 8 data lines of Port P1 (P1.0–P1.7) of the 8051 to the digital inputs A1–A8 of DAC0808.
- Connect the I_out pin of DAC0808 to the inverting input of the op-amp.
- Connect R_f between op-amp output and inverting input.
- Connect V_ref through R_14 to pin 14 of DAC0808 (sets reference current).
- Tie the non-inverting input of op-amp to ground.

**Derivation of Output Voltage for B = 10000000₂ = 128₁₀:**

**Step 1 — Compute I_ref:**

$$
I_{ref} = \frac{V_{ref}}{R_{14}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute I_out:**

$$
I_{out} = I_{ref} \times \frac{B}{2^n} = \frac{V_{ref}}{R_{14}} \times \frac{128}{256} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2 R_{14}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute V_out across R_f (op-amp acts as transimpedance amplifier):**

$$
V_{out} = -I_{out} \times R_f = -\frac{V_{ref} \times R_f}{2 R_{14}}
$$

**Numerical Example:** If V_ref = 10 V, R_14 = R_f = 5 kΩ:

$$
V_{out} = -\frac{10 \times 5\text{k}}{2 \times 5\text{k}} = -5.0 \text{ V}
$$

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (b):

**PWM Generation using Arduino UNO Timer:**

The ATmega328P (Arduino UNO's MCU) has **three timers**: Timer0 (8-bit), Timer1 (16-bit), Timer2 (8-bit). Arduino's `analogWrite()` uses **Fast PWM** mode on these timers to generate a PWM signal on pins 3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11.

**Internal Block Diagram of PWM Generation:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[TCNTn Counter] --> B{TCNTn < OCRx?}
    B -->|Yes| C[OCx Pin HIGH]
    B -->|No| D[OCx Pin LOW]
    E[Clock Source Prescaler] --> A
    F[analogWrite pin value] --> G[OCRx Register]
    G --> B
```

**Working Principle:**
- The hardware counter TCNTn increments from 0 to 255 (or TOP value) at the timer clock rate.
- A second register **OCRx** holds the user-set value (0–255).
- While TCNTn < OCRx, the output pin is HIGH. When TCNTn ≥ OCRx, the output is LOW.
- This creates a PWM waveform whose duty cycle is `value/255`.

**Arduino Sketch for LED Brightness Control:**

```
int ledPin = 9;          // PWM pin (Timer1, 490 Hz)
int brightness = 0;
int fadeStep = 5;

void setup() {
  pinMode(ledPin, OUTPUT);
}

void loop() {
  analogWrite(ledPin, brightness);
  brightness = brightness + fadeStep;
  if (brightness <= 0 || brightness >= 255) {
    fadeStep = -fadeStep;        // reverse direction
  }
  delay(30);
}
```

**Register-Level Configuration (for advanced students):**

| Register | Purpose | Example Value |
|:--------:|:--------|:--------------|
| TCCR1A | Waveform Generation Mode bits | `0b10000001` (Fast PWM, 8-bit) |
| TCCR1B | Clock Select bits | `0b00000011` (Prescaler = 64) |
| OCR1A / OCR1B | Compare Match value | 0–255 for 8-bit PWM |
| TIMSK1 | Interrupt mask (optional) | — |
| DDRB | Pin 9 (PB1) as output | `0b00000010` |

**Average Voltage Across LED:**

$$
V_{avg} = D \times 5\text{V} = \frac{\text{value}}{255} \times 5\text{V}
$$

> **Marks Allocation:** Explanation of Fast PWM = 3 marks, register/function flow = 2 marks, sketch = 1 mark, average voltage formula = 1 mark.

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Describe the working of the **ADC0808**. List its salient features and the function of each control signal.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Arduino Cloud Editor** with its features, architecture, and a step-by-step procedure to upload a sketch from the cloud to a connected Arduino board.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (a):

**ADC0808 Working — Detailed Explanation:**

The ADC0808 operates on the **Successive Approximation** principle. Internally, it contains a 256-resistor R-2R ladder, a comparator, and a Successive Approximation Register (SAR). The block diagram is shown below:

**Block Diagram (textual representation):**

```
   IN0 ─┐
   IN1 ─┤
   IN2 ─┤  8-to-1     ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────────┐    ┌──────────┐
   IN3 ─┤  Analog  ──▶│ SAR +   │───▶│ Output Latch│───▶│ D0  ...  │
   IN4 ─┤  MUX        │ R-2R +  │    └─────────────┘    │ D7 Outputs│
   IN5 ─┤             │ Comp.   │            ▲            └──────────┘
   IN6 ─┤             └─────────┘            │
   IN7 ─┘              ▲                     │ OE
   ADDA,ADDB,ADDC ───┘ │
                        │ START
                        │ CLK (640 kHz)
                        │
                       EOC → goes LOW when done
```

**Salient Features (must memorize):**
- **Resolution:** 8 bits
- **Channels:** 8 single-ended analog inputs
- **Conversion Time:** 100 μs (typical at 640 kHz clock)
- **Total Unadjusted Error:** ±½ LSB and ±1 LSB
- **Output:** Tri-state, TTL compatible, latched
- **Single Supply:** 5 V DC
- **Low Power:** 15 mW typical

**Control Signal Functions:**

| Signal | Direction | Function |
|:------:|:---------:|:---------|
| ALE | Input | Address Latch Enable — Latches ADDA–C into the channel decoder on HIGH-to-LOW edge |
| START | Input | Starts conversion on HIGH-to-LOW edge (pulse width ≥ 100 ns) |
| EOC | Output | End of Conversion — goes LOW when conversion is finished |
| OE | Input | Output Enable — enables tri-state output buffers (HIGH = enabled) |
| CLK | Input | External clock, 10–1280 kHz (typical 500–640 kHz) |
| V_ref+ / V_ref- | Input | Reference voltages defining input range |

**Working Cycle (4 phases):**
1. **Address Phase** — Apply channel address; pulse ALE HIGH to latch.
2. **Conversion Phase** — Pulse START; SAR runs for 8 clock cycles (~100 μs); EOC is HIGH during this phase.
3. **Wait Phase** — CPU polls EOC or waits for interrupt on EOC falling edge.
4. **Read Phase** — Set OE HIGH; read 8-bit data from D0–D7.

#### Model Solution for Question B (b):

**Arduino Cloud Editor — Detailed Explanation:**

The **Arduino Cloud Editor** is a cloud-based development environment accessible at `create.arduino.cc/editor`. It mirrors the functionality of the desktop Arduino IDE 2.x but runs entirely in a browser.

**Key Features:**
1. **Browser-based IDE** — No installation required; works on Chromebooks, tablets, and locked-down enterprise PCs.
2. **Cloud Sketchbook** — All sketches are auto-saved to the user's Arduino account; accessible from any device.
3. **Built-in Library Manager** — Same as desktop IDE; libraries are cloud-cached for fast install.
4. **Web Serial Monitor** — Real-time debugging via browser-based terminal.
5. **Project Sharing** — Sketches can be shared via a public URL for collaboration.
6. **OTA Updates** — Supports Over-The-Air programming for Wi-Fi-enabled boards.
7. **Tight IoT Cloud Integration** — One-click transition from sketch to IoT "Thing" with cloud variables and dashboards.

**Architecture:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BR[Browser Client] --> WS[WebSocket to Cloud Server]
    BR --> SA[Serial Agent Local Daemon]
    SA --> USB[USB Driver]
    USB --> MCU[Arduino Board]
    CS[Cloud Server] --> SB[Sketchbook Database]
    CS --> LIB[Library Cache]
    CS --> IOT[IoT Cloud MQTT Broker]
    IOT --> DASH[Web Dashboard]
```

**Step-by-Step Procedure to Upload a Sketch via Cloud Editor:**

1. **Sign in** to `create.arduino.cc/editor` using an Arduino account.
2. **Install the Arduino Create Agent** on the local PC (one-time). This agent bridges the cloud to USB.
3. **Connect the Arduino board** via USB. The agent detects it and the editor shows "Connected to [Board Name]".
4. **Create a new sketch** (or open an existing one from the Sketchbook).
5. **Write the code** using the browser's Monaco-based editor (with syntax highlighting, intellisense).
6. **Click the Upload button**. The cloud compiler (avr-gcc or arm-gcc) compiles the `.ino` into a `.hex` binary.
7. **Agent receives the binary** via WebSocket and streams it to the board over USB using `avrdude` or `bossac` (depending on MCU).
8. **Board runs the new sketch** immediately after upload completes.
9. **Open the Web Serial Monitor** to view `Serial.print()` output in the browser tab.

> **Marks Allocation:** Features = 2 marks, Architecture = 2 marks, Upload procedure = 2 marks, Diagram = 1 mark.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in DAC0808 op-amp output → lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing `analogWrite()` with true analog output** — it is PWM, not DAC. Losing 2 marks.
> 3. **Not stating conversion time (100 μs)** for ADC0808 → lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing up EOC active level** (it is *active LOW*, not HIGH) → lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Omitting the Arduino Create Agent** in cloud-editor explanations → lose 1 mark (it is the critical bridge).
> 6. **Using wrong n in formulas** (e.g., n=10 instead of n=8 for ADC0808) → lose 1 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **ADC0808 is 8-bit, 8-channel, SAR-type** with **100 μs** conversion time.
- [x] **Resolution formula:** $\Delta V = V_{ref} / 2^n$ (use n=8 for ADC0808).
- [x] **Control signals:** ALE (latch address), START (begin), EOC (end — *active LOW*), OE (output enable).
- [x] **ADC0808 EOC goes LOW when conversion is complete** — common KTU question.
- [x] **DAC0808 is a current-output device** — op-amp (LF351) is required for voltage output.
- [x] **DAC0808 output equation:** $V_{out} = -\frac{V_{ref} \cdot R_f}{R_{14}} \cdot \frac{B}{2^n}$ — note the negative sign.
- [x] **Settling time of DAC0808** is approximately 150 ns.
- [x] **PWM = Pulse Width Modulation** — varies duty cycle, not amplitude or frequency.
- [x] **Duty Cycle** $D\% = (T_{on} / T) \times 100\%$.
- [x] **Average voltage** $V_{avg} = D \times V_{peak}$; **RMS voltage** $V_{rms} = V_{peak} \times \sqrt{D}$.
- [x] **Arduino UNO PWM pins:** 3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11 — all 8-bit, default ~490 Hz (pins 5,6 = ~980 Hz).
- [x] **`analogWrite(pin, value)` value range = 0 to 255** — sets the compare-match register.
- [x] **`analogWrite()` produces PWM, NOT a true analog voltage** — there is no hardware DAC on ATmega328P.
- [x] **Arduino Cloud Editor** is a web-based IDE — requires the local **Arduino Create Agent** to access USB.
- [x] **Cloud Editor supports OTA** updates for Wi-Fi boards (ESP32, MKR series, Nano 33 IoT).
- [x] **Cloud Editor integrates directly with Arduino IoT Cloud** for cloud-synced variables and dashboards.
- [x] For **DC motor PWM**, frequency should be **>20 kHz** to avoid audible whine.
- [x] For **servo motors**, use **50 Hz PWM (T = 20 ms)** with pulse width **1 ms (0°) to 2 ms (180°)**.
